Indeed has seniority_native = 0% (no native labels) and is not in the Stage 9 LLM frame — llm_extraction_coverage = not_selected for essentially all rows. Use Indeed for non-seniority-stratified sensitivity checks only.

Scripts: counts_linkedin_primary.csv, counts_indeed.csv, counts_unfiltered.csv, default_filter_cost.csv.

Date ranges: arshkon (date_posted only) 2024-04 window, asaniczka 2024-01 window, scraped LinkedIn scrape_date 2026-03-20 through 2026-04-14 (26 days), scraped Indeed same window with date_posted 100% populated.

docs.title: LLM extraction/classification coverage — the binding constraint
docs.text: SWE rows only, LinkedIn default filter.

docs.title: Stage 9 — llm_extraction_coverage (governs description_core_llm)
docs.text:
source n_swe n_labeled % labeled notes kaggle_arshkon 4,691 4,687 99.9% 4 short-description skips kaggle_asaniczka 18,129 17,037 94.0% 1,053 not_selected, 35 deferred scraped (linkedin) 40,881 12,534 30.7% 28,248 not_selected, 97 deferred

Scraped not_selected rate of 69.1% reflects the fact that the Stage 9 selection frame was sized for the 2024 historical snapshot and the daily-growing scraped window has largely outrun it. Supplemental cache cells exist but are not part of the balanced core frame.

Implication: Any text-dependent 2024-vs-2026 comparison run on labeled rows has effective SWE sample size 4,687 vs 12,534 (arshkon-only baseline) or 21,724 vs 12,534 (pooled 2024 baseline). The 2026 side is the power bottleneck.

docs.title: Stage 10 — llm_classification_coverage (governs swe_classification_llm, ghost_assessment_llm, yoe_min_years_llm, and LLM writeback to seniority_final)
docs.text:
source n_swe labeled rule_sufficient not_selected deferred kaggle_arshkon 4,691 2,917 (62.2%) 1,770 (37.7%) 4 0 kaggle_asaniczka 18,129 5,190 (28.6%) 7,477 (41.2%) 5,455 (30.1%) 7 scraped 40,881 6,918 (16.9%) 5,691 (13.9%) 28,257 (69.1%) 15

Stage 10 "best available" coverage (labeled + rule_sufficient) is arshkon 99.9%, asaniczka 69.9%, scraped 30.8%. For any analysis that uses ghost_assessment_llm or yoe_min_years_llm as primary (these two never have a rule-based fallback), the scraped sample is capped at ~6.9K SWE rows.

Scripts: llm_extraction_coverage_swe.csv, llm_classification_coverage_swe.csv, llm_coverage_indeed_swe.csv.

docs.title: Column coverage heatmap
docs.text:
Full 94-column heatmap: figures/T01/coverage_heatmap_all_cols.png. Focused analytical heatmap (SWE rows, 23 key columns): figures/T01/coverage_heatmap_swe_focus.png. CSVs: coverage_heatmap.csv, coverage_heatmap_swe_focus.csv, column_coverage_linkedin_all.csv, column_coverage_linkedin_swe.csv, column_coverage_indeed.csv.

Columns with non-null rate < 50% for at least one source: 65. Full list: columns_over_50pct_null.csv. The analytically important ones:

column arshkon asaniczka scraped_li indeed interpretation description_core_llm 14.1% 5.5% 12.9% 0% Raw rates; SWE-only the rates are 99.9/94.0/30.7 — see above. Outside SWE the LLM was not called. company_industry 98.6% 0% 99.9% 0% Asaniczka has no industry; industry-stratified historical baseline is arshkon-only. company_size via companion 0% 0% 91% Arshkon via companion join. LinkedIn scraped has no size at all. company_size_raw 100% 0% 0% 91% Same gap. skills_raw 1.9% 100% (asaniczka_skills) 0% — Structured skills only in asaniczka (different column). Arshkon/scraped have no structured skills. asaniczka_skills 0% 100% 0% — Same. yoe_extracted 44.9% 46.4% 58.6% — Label-independent YOE proxy coverage; ~55% missing in 2024 sources. yoe_min_years_llm 6.3% 1.5% 6.3% — LLM YOE cross-check is thin. ghost_assessment_llm 10.4% 2.1% 8.6% — Stage 10 coverage over the full LinkedIn frame. date_posted 100% 100% 1.4% 100% Scraped LinkedIn date is scrape_date; date_posted is near-zero. posting_age_days 0% 0% 1.4% — Requires both date_posted and scrape_date. seniority_native 66.5% 100% 97.6% 0% — search_metro_*, search_query, query_tier 0% 0% 100% (SWE) 100% Scraper-only metadata. Use metro_area for posting location. real_employer 0.3% 1.9% 3.5% — Only populated when is_aggregator = true. asaniczka_skills 0% 100% 0% — —

"Columns with different semantics across sources" (important for Wave 2+):

  1. company_industry — arshkon has 374 distinct values from a LinkedIn companion table; scraped LinkedIn has 6,569 distinct values from the live scraper page scrape; asaniczka has zero. The compound-label rates differ too (arshkon 43%, scraped 50%). A cross-period industry comparison must either (a) restrict to a harmonized shared vocabulary or (b) be reported as arshkon-vs-scraped only, explicitly.
  2. seniority_native — arshkon has all 5 levels (including entry and intern); asaniczka has only associate and mid-senior (no entry labels at all by construction); scraped LinkedIn has all levels; Indeed has none. Asaniczka cannot produce a native entry-level signal.
  3. date_posted vs scrape_date — arshkon/asaniczka carry date_posted only; scraped LinkedIn carries scrape_date with only 2.8% date_posted, so posting age and any temporal dynamics finer than scrape week require Indeed (or scraped Indeed for within-window age work).
  4. search_query / search_metro_* — scraped-only query provenance. Do not mistake search_metro_name for posting location; use metro_area.
  5. skills_raw vs asaniczka_skills — two completely different structured-skills columns. Only asaniczka has structured skills; arshkon and scraped have none (or 1.9% in arshkon, mostly empty). Any "skills field" analysis is an asaniczka-only exercise.
  6. company_size — arshkon via companion, Indeed direct, LinkedIn scraped/asaniczka have none. Not usable for cross-source company-size analysis.

Scripts: company_industry_semantics.csv, top_industries_by_source.csv, period_values_by_source.csv, date_ranges.csv.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
1. Loaded data/unified.parquet as a DuckDB view — no full-file pandas load.
  2. For row counts: authoritative aggregates by source × source_platform × period × is_swe/is_swe_adjacent/is_control, both unfiltered and under default LinkedIn filter.
  3. For LLM coverage: grouped SWE rows by llm_extraction_coverage and llm_classification_coverage per source.
  4. For column coverage: per column, computed nonnull_rate = count(CASE WHEN col IS NOT NULL AND CAST(col AS VARCHAR) <> '' THEN 1 END) / count(*) per source, under the default filter. description_core_llm uses the empty-string guard because Stage 9 writes empty strings for short-description skips.
  5. Coverage heatmap: 94 columns × 4 source slices. Values are non-null rates on the default-filtered set (SWE-only for the focused heatmap, all rows for the full one). Indeed column is computed on the raw Indeed slice without the default filter (Indeed is excluded from it by construction).

docs.title: Key constraint mapping for Wave 2-3 analysis categories
docs.text:
For each planned analysis category, this is the binding data constraint and severity.

Category Binding constraint Severity Affected tasks Text-based (corpus compare, topic models, requirement extraction, density metrics) description_core_llm labeled in scraped SWE = 12,534 (30.7%). 2026 side is the power floor. Raw-text backfill not allowed for boilerplate-sensitive work. HIGH T09, T11, T12, T13, T14, T15, T20, T21 Seniority (entry-level) Effective arshkon entry SWE = 175 rows total (92 title_keyword, 83 llm, 0 title_manager). Even pooling all sources gives 1,654 SWE rows at seniority_final = 'entry'. YOE-based proxy needed for label independence, but YOE itself is missing in ~55% of 2024 rows. See T02. HIGH T03, T08, T20 — any RQ1 junior-share work Geography (metro, state) metro_area coverage ~84% on scraped LinkedIn (26-metro frame), ~57% on arshkon, ~72% on asaniczka. search_metro_name is a scraper-side artifact and must not be used as location. 26-metro frame is the shared vocabulary. MEDIUM T17, any metro-stratified sensitivity Company (industry, size, concentration) Industry only usable on arshkon and scraped LinkedIn, and with different taxonomies (374 vs 6,569 distinct). Size is only in arshkon companion and Indeed. company_name_canonical is universal and the safe grouping column. MEDIUM on industry, HIGH on size T06, T16, T18 industry splits Requirements / skills (structured) Structured asaniczka_skills is 100% in asaniczka, 0% elsewhere. skills_raw is effectively unavailable. Requirements extraction must run on description text, inheriting the text-coverage constraint. HIGH (structured) / inherits text constraint T11, T14 Temporal (within-period dynamics, posting age) date_posted 1.4% in scraped LinkedIn; scrape_date is 100% but windowed (26 days). Within-2024 temporal detail is bounded by snapshot granularity. Indeed has posting-age. HIGH for within-window age, LOW for coarse period T19 LLM cross-checks (swe_classification_llm, ghost_assessment_llm, yoe_min_years_llm) Stage 10 labeled share: 62% arshkon / 29% asaniczka / 17% scraped SWE. ghost_assessment_llm on scraped SWE = 6,918 rows. MEDIUM–HIGH T04, T22

docs.title: Surprises and unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. The 2026 scraped window has WORSE LLM coverage than the 2024 historical snapshots for SWE rows, despite the scraper being the live instrument. 30.7% labeled vs 94-100% for Kaggle is the reverse of the naive expectation. Mechanism: Stage 9 selection frame was sized for the total historical dataset and is now being outgrown by the scraped window; not_selected is 69% of scraped SWE. This has immediate downstream implications for every text-based 2024→2026 comparison — the 2026 side is the bottleneck, not the 2024 one.
  2. Scraped LinkedIn date_posted is 1.4% populated. The 2.8% figure in the schema doc is for the full scraped LinkedIn slice — on the post-filter frame it drops further. This means within-scrape-window posting dynamics (how stale are postings?) are essentially not measurable on LinkedIn, only on Indeed.
  3. company_industry has 6,569 distinct values in scraped vs 374 in arshkon. The scraped taxonomy is live LinkedIn page-scrape and is an order of magnitude finer; a naive cross-source industry rollup will fragment the 2026 side and look like "industry diversity growth" when it is instrument difference.
  4. Indeed SWE volume (11,863) is larger than arshkon SWE volume (4,691). Indeed is doc-marked "sensitivity only," but for non-seniority-stratified sanity checks it is actually the larger 2026 SWE frame and worth leaning on where seniority stratification is not required.
  5. real_employer is populated on only 0.3% of arshkon rows despite the aggregator-resolution machinery. Most arshkon aggregator rows did not get an employer extracted, so deduplication-by-canonical-employer is weaker on arshkon than on scraped (3.5%).
  6. Scraped SWE n_control ≈ n_swe (41,790 vs 40,881). The control cohort is on par with the SWE cohort in the current window, which makes cross-occupation DiD (T18) feasibly powered on the 2026 side.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• Default filter loses essentially nothing. LinkedIn slices lose <0.3% to is_english or date_flag = ok per default_filter_cost.csv. Indeed loses 15 rows to is_english and zero to date_flag. The default filter is a near-free screen.
  • seniority_native in arshkon has a 1,408-row null block on SWE rows (30% of arshkon SWE) — LinkedIn did not label those at the source. Plan around this in T02.
  • yoe_extracted is missing in ~55% of 2024 SWE rows and ~41% of scraped SWE rows. When the YOE proxy is used for label-independent validation, the sample halves. This is a separate and compounding sample-size issue from the LLM text coverage.
  • description_core_llm empty string ≠ null. Stage 9 writes empty strings for short-description hard skips. The coverage computation here uses an empty-string guard; downstream scripts must do the same or they will overcount usable rows.
  • Supplemental cache vs selected core. selected_for_llm_frame = sticky balanced core only. Rows with llm_extraction_sample_tier = supplemental_cache have LLM output but are NOT part of the balanced frame — any balanced-sample claim must filter to core.
  • company_industry distinct count in asaniczka is literally zero (0 non-null rows). Any script that computes "top industries per source" must handle this gracefully or it will silently drop asaniczka.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. For every text-based Wave 2 task (T09, T11, T12, T13, T14, T15, T21): filter to llm_extraction_coverage = 'labeled' and report the post-filter N per period up front. The effective 2026 SWE sample is ~12.5K, not 40.9K. If a task needs more, it must explicitly opt in to raw-text fallback and run the boilerplate sensitivity.
  2. T03 (Agent B seniority audit): use seniority_final_source composition table seniority_final_source_full.csv from T02 as input — it partitions SWE rows into title_keyword/llm/unknown buckets per source.
  3. T04 (Agent B SWE audit): swe_classification_llm labeled share on scraped SWE is 17%. Spot-check accuracy must be weighted toward scraped tier composition, not documented numbers.
  4. T06/T17 (Agent C/J): industry analysis is arshkon-vs-scraped only. Asaniczka drops out entirely. Do not pool.
  5. Pool-vs-arshkon decision: for 2024 baselines the cost of pooling arshkon+asaniczka is that you lose company_industry, gain asaniczka_skills, and change the native-seniority instrument. For text analyses where the LLM coverage is what matters, pooling helps (arshkon 4.7K + asaniczka 17K labeled vs scraped 12.5K labeled); for industry analyses pooling is impossible.
  6. Indeed is a larger 2026 SWE frame than arshkon is a 2024 SWE frame (11.9K vs 4.7K). For non-seniority-stratified sensitivity checks, Indeed has real statistical weight — use it more aggressively than "sensitivity only" framing might suggest.
  7. Scraper growth will worsen, not improve, text coverage until the Stage 9 selection target is raised. Flag this to partner: the balanced core frame needs to grow with the scraped window.

docs.title: T02 — Seniority Comparability & Label Quality
docs.text:
Agent A, Wave 1 Date: 2026-04-15 Input: data/unified.parquet Script: exploration/scripts/T02_seniority_comparability.py Tables: exploration/tables/T02/ Figures: exploration/figures/T02/

Default filter: source_platform = 'linkedin' AND is_english AND date_flag = 'ok' AND is_swe.

docs.title: Verdict (headline)
docs.text:
Asaniczka associate is NOT usable as a junior proxy. On every label-independent signal, asaniczka associate behaves like a mid-career mixed bag that sits closer to arshkon associate and mid-senior than to arshkon entry. When the Stage 10 LLM re-labels asaniczka associate rows, 33.5% of them become mid-senior in seniority_final versus 8.8% of arshkon entry rows — the LLM itself disagrees with any "associate = junior" reading by almost a factor of four. YOE is also the wrong shape: 33.1% of asaniczka associate postings require 5+ years versus 26.4% of arshkon entry. Using asaniczka associate as a junior proxy would inflate the 2024 junior baseline with ~30% senior-like rows and bias the RQ1 junior-share trend toward a false "junior collapse."

The safe defaults are: 1. For entry-level trends, use seniority_final with label-independent YOE validation. Do not try to reconstruct "asaniczka junior" from the native label at all. 2. For any seniority_native-based sanity check, use arshkon only. Asaniczka has zero native entry labels by construction; pooling it under seniority_native dilutes 2024 entry rate to near-zero.

Secondary finding: even under seniority_final, the entry-level effective sample sizes are very small — arshkon 175 SWE, asaniczka 204 SWE, scraped 1,275 SWE. Any RQ1 junior-share confidence interval on the 2024 side is noisy by arithmetic alone.

docs.title: Step 1 — Label inventory per source
docs.text:
seniority_native distribution on SWE rows (LinkedIn, default filter):

source entry associate mid-senior director executive intern null total kaggle_arshkon 738 263 2,231 20 11 20 1,408 4,691 kaggle_asaniczka 0 1,820 16,309 0 0 0 0 18,129 scraped 4,689 1,405 27,759 284 73 629 6,042 40,881

The critical gap: asaniczka produces only associate and mid-senior. There is no entry, no intern, no director, and no null — asaniczka's LinkedIn label mapping collapses all non-senior roles into associate. By construction, seniority_native cannot detect entry-level postings in asaniczka. This is not a coverage issue — it is the platform's own label schema for that snapshot. Any 2024 entry-level signal in asaniczka must come from the Stage 10 LLM via seniority_final.

Arshkon has a native entry bucket of 738 SWE rows (15.7% of arshkon SWE) — this is the only source with a 2024 native entry label. Scraped has 4,689 in entry (11.5% of scraped SWE).

Source: seniority_native_inventory_swe.csv.

docs.title: Step 2 — SWE-only native-label comparability audit
docs.text:
For each native-label group, we computed: row count, junior-title-cue rate (regex over title_normalized), senior-title-cue rate, YOE coverage, YOE distribution (mean, median, p25, p75), YOE shares at ≤2, ≤3, ≥5 years, and the distribution of seniority_final conditional on the native label.

Group profile (SWE only, LinkedIn default filter):

group n jr_cue sr_cue yoe_median yoe_p25 yoe_p75 yoe≤2 yoe≤3 yoe≥5 arshkon_entry 738 1.9% 0.5% 3.0 2.0 5.0 17.8% 31.4% 26.4% arshkon_associate 263 3.4% 1.1% 3.0 2.0 5.0 16.7% 32.3% 18.3% asaniczka_associate 1,820 2.9% 1.2% 4.0 3.0 5.0 15.2% 29.6% 33.1% asaniczka_mid_senior 16,309 0.4% 2.9% 5.0 4.0 8.0 6.5% 15.9% 48.6% arshkon_mid_senior 2,231 0.6% 2.3% 5.0 4.0 7.0 5.4% 14.5% 45.5%

Key signals sorted by "how junior does this group look": - Junior title cue rate: arshkon_entry 1.9%, asaniczka_associate 2.9%, arshkon_associate 3.4%, arshkon_mid_senior 0.6%, asaniczka_mid_senior 0.4%. Junior cues are rare everywhere, but asaniczka_associate is roughly aligned with arshkon_associate here — NOT with arshkon_entry. - YOE≤2 share (the sharpest entry-level proxy): arshkon_entry 17.8%, arshkon_associate 16.7%, asaniczka_associate 15.2%, asaniczka_mid_senior 6.5%. On YOE≤2 alone, asaniczka_associate is close to both arshkon_entry and arshkon_associate. This is the one signal where it does not look clearly worse. - YOE≥5 share (the sharpest non-entry signal): arshkon_entry 26.4%, arshkon_associate 18.3%, asaniczka_associate 33.1%, arshkon_mid_senior 45.5%. Here asaniczka_associate is above arshkon_entry and far above arshkon_associate — a third of asaniczka_associate postings require 5+ years, which is a mid-senior shape. - YOE median: arshkon_entry 3, arshkon_associate 3, asaniczka_associate 4, mid-senior groups 5. Median is one year higher than arshkon's junior groups.

seniority_final distribution conditional on native label (row-percent):

group entry% associate% mid-senior% director% unknown% arshkon_entry 7.2% 0.3% 8.8% 0.0% 83.7% arshkon_associate 8.0% 1.5% 21.7% 0.0% 68.8% asaniczka_associate 3.8% 0.8% 33.5% 0.2% 61.6% arshkon_mid_senior 0.9% 0.1% 62.4% 0.2% 36.3% asaniczka_mid_senior 0.8% 0.1% 66.3% 0.5% 32.3%

This is the decisive table. On the rows where seniority_final produced any non-unknown answer (i.e., where Stage 5 rules or Stage 10 LLM fired), the distributions are:

  • Among arshkon_entry rows with a final label: 45% entry, 55% mid-senior. Even arshkon's own native entry bucket is half mid-senior-flagged in seniority_final.
  • Among asaniczka_associate rows with a final label: 10% entry, 88% mid-senior. Asaniczka_associate is overwhelmingly re-labeled mid-senior when the pipeline makes a call.
  • Among arshkon_associate rows with a final label: 26% entry, 70% mid-senior. Arshkon's associate also leans mid-senior but far less than asaniczka's.

seniority_final is an independent classifier (rule + LLM) and its verdict on asaniczka_associate is clear: it is not an entry-level bucket, and it is not even as junior-leaning as arshkon_associate. The 33.5% mid-senior share is 3.8x the arshkon_entry mid-senior share.

Title overlap (Jaccard on top-200 titles per group):

pair jaccard asaniczka_associate titles found in b asaniczka_associate vs arshkon_entry 0.143 25.0% asaniczka_associate vs arshkon_associate 0.123 21.0% asaniczka_associate vs arshkon_mid_senior 0.208 34.5% asaniczka_associate vs asaniczka_mid_senior 0.231 37.5%

Asaniczka_associate's title set overlaps MORE with mid-senior groups (both arshkon and asaniczka) than with arshkon_entry. This is consistent with the seniority_final conditional result — asaniczka_associate is not a "entry or associate" bucket; it is a catch-all that skews toward mid-senior postings.

Top-15 titles per group (top_titles_by_group.csv) show why the overlap is middling. arshkon_entry is dominated by "software engineer / data engineer / developer / java developer / .net developer / automation engineer" — titles with no level marker. asaniczka_associate has the same unranked titles but at much higher absolute counts, padded with additional generic technical titles ("servicenow developer", "quality assurance engineer") that in arshkon appear in the mid-senior bucket.

docs.title: Step 3 — Decision rule application
docs.text:
Decision rule: "usable as junior proxy" requires that asaniczka_associate behave directionally close to arshkon_entry on multiple signals.

Signal asaniczka_associate arshkon_entry Directionally close? Junior title cue rate 2.9% 1.9% Yes (both low, asaniczka slightly higher) Senior title cue rate 1.2% 0.5% ~ (close) YOE median 4 3 No (1 year higher) YOE≤2 share 15.2% 17.8% Yes (within ~15% relative) YOE≥5 share 33.1% 26.4% No (25% relatively higher) seniority_final mid-senior rate (all rows) 33.5% 8.8% No (3.8x higher) seniority_final entry rate (all rows) 3.8% 7.2% No (half of arshkon_entry) Top-title Jaccard to arshkon_entry 0.143 — No (lower than overlap with mid-senior groups)

Three signals match directionally (title cues and YOE≤2), five do not. The signals that disagree are stronger: the seniority_final conditional distribution (an independent classifier), YOE≥5 share (a hard label-independent check), and the title-set overlap (where asaniczka_associate looks more like mid-senior than entry).

Verdict: NOT usable as a junior proxy. The only signal on which asaniczka_associate looks junior-like is YOE≤2, and that is contradicted by YOE≥5 and by the classifier-overlap diagnostics. Using it as a 2024 junior baseline would import approximately 33% mid-senior-like rows, which is exactly the bias that would produce a false "2024 was more junior than we thought" finding when running the RQ1 decomposition.

What asaniczka_associate actually is: a mixed low-to-mid bucket where LinkedIn-at-the-time-of-asaniczka-snapshot was labeling unranked software engineer / system engineer / data engineer postings as associate. It overlaps arshkon_associate more than arshkon_entry, but it is not identical to arshkon_associate either (arshkon_associate has yoe_ge5 = 18% vs asaniczka_associate 33%) — asaniczka_associate is shifted more senior even relative to arshkon's own associate bucket. This strongly suggests the LinkedIn native-label semantics changed between the asaniczka snapshot window (2024-01) and the arshkon window (2024-04), or that asaniczka's ingestion applied a different mapping. Either way, it cannot be treated as arshkon_entry's asaniczka-side counterpart.

docs.title: Step 4 — Entry-level effective sample sizes under seniority_final
docs.text:
seniority_final = 'entry' SWE rows per source, broken down by seniority_final_source:

source total entry title_keyword title_manager llm unknown kaggle_arshkon 175 92 0 83 0 kaggle_asaniczka 204 112 0 92 0 scraped 1,275 1,002 0 273 0

Arithmetic reality check: the arshkon 2024 SWE entry frame is 175 rows — not 738 (the native-label count). The 738 arshkon_entry native rows produce only 53 entry in seniority_final (7.2%) because the combined rule+LLM column disagrees with the native label on ~93% of arshkon's native entries, mostly routing them to unknown or mid-senior (see T02 conditional table above).

Asaniczka contributes 204 entry-level SWE rows in seniority_final, entirely via Stage 5 rule (112 title_keyword) plus Stage 10 LLM writeback (92 llm). This is not a "junior proxy from associate" — the pipeline is picking up explicit junior/entry/I titles plus LLM judgments on routed rows.

Effective coverage per slice (share of entry rows where the given column is populated):

source × resolver n yoe_cov metro_cov desc_core_llm_cov co_cov arshkon × title_keyword 92 13.0% 67.4% 100.0% 96.7% arshkon × llm 83 63.9% 56.6% 98.8% 97.6% asaniczka × title_keyword 112 36.6% 77.7% 97.3% 100.0% asaniczka × llm 92 64.1% 71.7% 100.0% 100.0% scraped × title_keyword 1,002 20.5% 65.4% 33.1% 100.0% scraped × llm 273 41.4% 83.9% 98.5% 100.0%

Two things jump out: 1. YOE coverage on title_keyword-derived entry rows is 13-37%. Strong-rule entry labels are applied from the title alone; many of those postings don't write an explicit YOE in the description. If you want to validate the entry label with the YOE proxy, you have roughly 20-40% of the already-small entry sample to work with. 2. description_core_llm on scraped × title_keyword entry rows is only 33%. On the scraped 2026 window, of 1,002 strong-rule entry SWE rows, only 332 are usable for cleaned-text analysis. LLM-resolved scraped entry rows have ~99% cleaned-text coverage but there are only 273 of them. The text-based 2026 entry-level frame is roughly 332 + 270 = ~600 rows — tight. The mechanism is that title_keyword-resolved entry rows have llm_classification_coverage = 'rule_sufficient', but Stage 9 text-extraction runs independently and the scraped frame is undersampled there (see T01). Strong-rule entry labels on scraped skip LLM text extraction in many cases.

For the RQ1 junior-share analysis, pooled entry-level SWE across all sources in seniority_final = 1,654 rows (175 + 204 + 1,275). Split 2024 vs 2026: 379 vs 1,275. The historical side is tiny.

Source: entry_level_sample_sizes.csv, entry_level_totals.csv, seniority_final_source_full.csv.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
1. Loaded data/unified.parquet via DuckDB; applied the LinkedIn SWE default filter.
  2. Built a temp view with regex-based jr_cue and sr_cue flags on title_normalized. Python asserts at the top of the script validate the junior/senior regex patterns on edge cases before the SQL version runs.
  3. Defined five comparison groups by source × seniority_native: arshkon_entry, arshkon_associate, arshkon_mid_senior, asaniczka_associate, asaniczka_mid_senior.
  4. Per group: computed row count, title-cue rates, YOE distribution summary statistics, YOE share thresholds (≤2, ≤3, ≥5), and the distribution of seniority_final conditional on the native label.
  5. Computed top-200 title sets per group and Jaccard overlap of asaniczka_associate against each arshkon group and against asaniczka_mid_senior.
  6. For entry-level effective sample sizes, filtered to seniority_final = 'entry' and grouped by source × seniority_final_source, computing per-slice non-null rates for yoe_extracted, metro_area, description_core_llm, company_name_canonical.
  7. Rendered figures: YOE CDF by group, and a signals-by-group bar chart (jr_cue, sr_cue, yoe_le2, yoe_ge5).

docs.title: Surprises and unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Arshkon's own native entry bucket disagrees with arshkon's own seniority_final on 93% of rows. Of 738 arshkon_entry native rows, only 53 end up as seniority_final = 'entry' (7.2%); 65 become mid-senior (8.8%) and 618 become unknown (83.7%). This is partly because Stage 5 is deliberately conservative (only strong title keywords fire) and Stage 10 wasn't routed everywhere, but it also means the native entry label on arshkon is much noisier than its headline count suggests. The 738 count is not a usable 2024 junior frame on its own.
  2. Arshkon_entry has a 26% yoe≥5 share. More than a quarter of arshkon's native entry-level postings require 5+ years of experience — a strong signal that the native label is noisy in the "entry label but not entry content" direction. This is exactly the ghost/inflation pattern RQ1 cares about, and it is visible in the 2024 historical data before anything changed. RQ1's framing of a 2024-to-2026 "junior inflation" shift needs to be calibrated against a 2024 baseline that already had substantial inflation.
  3. Asaniczka_associate has 33% yoe≥5 — higher than arshkon_entry. A junior-labeled bucket with a third of postings requiring 5+ years is not junior. This is the single hardest piece of evidence against using it as a proxy.
  4. Asaniczka_associate's title overlap is higher with asaniczka_mid_senior (Jaccard 0.23) than with arshkon_entry (0.14). If asaniczka_associate were really a junior bucket, we would expect it to share titles with arshkon_entry more than with its own senior sibling. The opposite is true — it behaves as a within-asaniczka continuation of mid-senior.
  5. seniority_final_source = 'title_manager' never produced entry-level labels. This is expected (manager detection only flags non-entry), but worth noting: the entry column is entirely fed by title_keyword and llm.
  6. Asaniczka associate has seniority_final = unknown 61.6% of the time. The Stage 5 rules did not fire, and the Stage 10 LLM either was not routed or could not determine seniority. Even with the LLM writeback, two-thirds of asaniczka_associate rows carry no final seniority — more evidence that the native label is not providing useful signal.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• YOE coverage is ~55% missing in 2024 sources. The YOE-based proxy that the core preamble prescribes as the primary label-independence check is itself ~50% thin on 2024. Use it directionally, not as ground truth.
  • arshkon's native entry label is noisy on YOE and on the independent classifier, so it cannot be treated as gold-standard either. The best thing to do is report all three (seniority_final, native arshkon-only, YOE-based proxy) and watch for disagreement.
  • Indeed has no seniority_native at all and was not in the Stage 9 frame — seniority_final on Indeed is almost entirely unknown plus a small strong-rule slice. Do not stratify Indeed by seniority.
  • All SWE counts in this report are is_swe = true (rule-based). If the Agent B SWE audit (T04) identifies false positives, the entry-level row counts here are upper bounds.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. T03 (seniority audit, Agent B): this report's seniority_final_source_full.csv and conditional-on-native tables are inputs. The "93% of arshkon native entry rows disagree with seniority_final" observation is the concrete thing T03 should investigate — specifically whether the remaining 83.7% unknown rows on arshkon_entry are genuine unknowns or Stage 9/10 routing gaps.
  2. RQ1 junior-share work (T08, T20): the 2024 side has ~379 SWE entry rows in seniority_final. Report confidence intervals explicitly. Do not report raw junior shares without the label-independent YOE proxy side-by-side. Do NOT construct an asaniczka-based junior baseline from seniority_native = 'associate'.
  3. T18 (cross-occupation DiD): if a comparable control entry-level row count is needed, T02's entry table here would need a control-side equivalent. This task did not compute it; a follow-up query is SELECT count(*) FROM u WHERE is_control AND seniority_final='entry' AND source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english AND date_flag='ok' GROUP BY source.
  4. T07 (power analysis, Agent D): the minimum detectable effect on a 2024 junior share of ~379 rows vs a 2026 junior share of 1,275 rows is the power floor for every RQ1 claim. Compute the MDE explicitly and feed it back into the research plan.
  5. Text-based analyses on the entry frame: the cleaned-text-usable entry frame is roughly arshkon 175 + asaniczka 203 + scraped ~600 = ~980 entry SWE rows across both periods. Any text-based entry-level comparison is operating at that scale.
  6. Check whether the asaniczka→arshkon native-label drift has a documented cause. Asaniczka's 2024-01 snapshot predates arshkon's 2024-04 snapshot by three months; either LinkedIn changed its level mapping in that window, or asaniczka and arshkon used different ingestion mappings. If it's the latter, there is a potential fix at the data-pipeline level (reharmonize asaniczka's seniority field). Flag for the preprocessing owner.

docs.title: Output index
docs.text:
Tables (exploration/tables/T02/): - seniority_native_inventory_swe.csv - seniority_final_inventory_swe.csv - native_group_profile.csv — the core comparability table - seniority_final_conditional_on_native.csv - seniority_final_conditional_pivot.csv — the decisive table (percentages) - top_titles_by_group.csv - title_overlap_jaccard.csv - yoe_histogram_by_group.csv - entry_level_sample_sizes.csv - entry_level_totals.csv - seniority_final_source_full.csv - summary.json

Figures (exploration/figures/T02/): - yoe_cdf_by_group.png — YOE CDF curves per native-label group - signals_by_group.png — jr_cue, sr_cue, yoe_le2, yoe_ge5 by group

Scripts (exploration/scripts/): - T02_seniority_comparability.py — rerunnable end-to-end

docs.title: T03 — Seniority Label Audit
docs.text:
Agent B (Wave 1). Input: data/unified.parquet. Default filter: is_english = TRUE AND date_flag = 'ok' AND source_platform = 'linkedin' AND is_swe = TRUE.

This is the canonical seniority-quality reference for the rest of the exploration. Downstream tasks should cite the verdict at the bottom rather than re-running these checks.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text:
seniority_final is defensible as the primary seniority column, but with one specific caveat: arshkon seniority_native = 'entry' is substantially less trustworthy than its scraped-period counterpart, so any sanity-check that uses seniority_native as a reference must be restricted to arshkon AND interpreted with the caveat that ~26% of arshkon native-entry rows have YOE >= 5. Strict kappa between seniority_final and seniority_native is 0.45 on arshkon vs 0.65 on scraped — a material temporal-instability gap in the native classifier.

The three-way junior-share comparison (2024 pooled LinkedIn SWE → 2026):

Operationalization 2024 2026 Direction Magnitude (a) seniority_final = 'entry' (of all rows) 1.66% 3.12% ↑ ~2x (a) seniority_final = 'entry' (of known-seniority rows) 2.70% 6.70% ↑ ~2.5x (b) seniority_native = 'entry' (arshkon-only, all rows) 15.73% (n/a) — — (c) YOE <= 2 (label-independent, of all rows) 7.74% 11.60% ↑ ~1.5x (c) YOE <= 2 (of YOE-known rows) 11.22% 17.19% ↑ ~1.5x

All three production-capable operationalizations agree on direction (junior share rose 2024→2026). They disagree substantially on level (seniority_final is anchored at a much lower base because of the large unknown bucket on scraped) and on magnitude (seniority_final ≈ 2x, YOE proxy ≈ 1.5x). The discrepancy is driven by differential LLM-routing: on scraped, ~40% of SWE rows have seniority_final_source = 'unknown' (no rule fired and the row was not routed to the LLM, or the LLM returned unknown), so seniority_final-based denominators are dominated by a very different mix of "unknowns" than on 2024 sources. See the disagreement-mechanism section below.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Scripts: - exploration/scripts/T03_seniority_audit.py — main profile and cross-tab generator - exploration/scripts/T03_kappa_and_diagnostics.py — Cohen's kappa on the cross-tabs - exploration/scripts/T03_native_quality.py — YOE profile of native='entry', path × final mapping - exploration/scripts/T03_weak_marker_sample.py — 128-row sample of LLM-routed SWE rows with weak seniority markers

Data: data/unified.parquet. Filter applied throughout: is_english = TRUE AND date_flag = 'ok' AND source_platform = 'linkedin' AND is_swe = TRUE (defaults from the preamble).

Seniority columns per docs/preprocessing-schema.md Section 4: - seniority_native — platform-provided LinkedIn label (arshkon 100%, asaniczka mid-senior/associate only, scraped 100%) - seniority_final — combined Stage-5 rule + Stage-10 LLM column (production) - seniority_final_source — title_keyword | title_manager | llm | unknown

docs.title: 1. seniority_final_source profile (SWE LinkedIn)
docs.text:
Source table: exploration/tables/T03/01_final_source_profile_with_pct.csv

source period n (SWE) title_keyword title_manager llm unknown kaggle_arshkon 2024-04 4,691 37.8% 0% 62.2% 0.06% kaggle_asaniczka 2024-01 18,129 58.8% 0% 28.6% 12.6% scraped 2026-03 19,777 44.1% 0% 17.6% 38.4% scraped 2026-04 21,104 43.0% 0.005% 16.3% 40.7%

Key takeaways: - The LLM-routed share differs massively by source: arshkon 62%, asaniczka 29%, scraped 17%. This reflects LLM budget allocation — arshkon gets the highest LLM coverage per row because it's the smallest source, while scraped gets the least. - The unknown share on scraped is 39-41% — nearly 4 in 10 scraped SWE rows have NO strong title rule AND were not routed to the LLM (or the LLM returned unknown). These rows carry seniority_final = 'unknown' and drop out of any seniority-stratified analysis. - Arshkon has only 0.06% unknown, because the small row count means everything got rule-matched or routed.

Implication for cross-period comparisons: the seniority_final = 'entry' rate on scraped is computed against a denominator where ~40% of the rows are unknown by construction. Percent-of-all metrics therefore understate the entry level share on scraped relative to arshkon. Percent-of-known-seniority is the better denominator, but still depends on the unknown rows being unknown "at random," which we cannot verify.

docs.title: 2. Rule-vs-LLM internal agreement on weak-marker titles
docs.text:
Source: exploration/tables/T03/05_llm_routed_marker_summary.csv, 05_llm_routed_weak_marker_sample_v2.csv

We sampled the full population (1,836 rows) of LLM-routed SWE rows whose raw title contains a weak seniority marker (Junior/Jr./Intern, Senior/Staff/Principal/Lead/Sr., or I/II/III/IV) and looked at what the LLM returned. These are rows where a strong title rule did NOT fire (so normalization stripped the marker, or the keyword list is narrower than ours), meaning the LLM is operating on description context.

Bucket sizes and LLM verdicts:

source marker_kind n LLM → entry LLM → mid-senior LLM → associate LLM → unknown kaggle_arshkon junior_marker 8 7 0 0 1 kaggle_arshkon senior_marker 120 0 118 0 2 kaggle_arshkon roman_marker 123 1 14 1 107 kaggle_asaniczka junior_marker 16 15 0 0 1 kaggle_asaniczka senior_marker 399 1 385 0 13 kaggle_asaniczka roman_marker 504 1 63 0 440 scraped junior_marker 21 14 3 0 4 scraped senior_marker 181 1 175 0 5 scraped roman_marker 464 2 56 8 398

Findings: - Junior markers (Junior/Jr./Intern/New Grad): LLM says entry 36/45 times (80%). Disagreements are typically legitimate — e.g., titles like "Junior/Mid-Level Software Engineer" or edge cases where the description contradicts the marker. - Senior markers (Senior/Staff/Principal/Lead/Sr.): LLM says mid-senior 678/700 times (97%). Very consistent — essentially zero routing errors on this marker class. - Roman-numeral markers (I/II/III/IV): LLM correctly refuses to guess. 945/1,091 (87%) return unknown, and only 0.4% return entry. This is DESIRED behavior — a level-II engineer at one company is a junior SWE while at another company it's a mid-level, and the LLM appropriately signals uncertainty. This is the "LLM seniority design rationale" described in preprocessing-schema.md (seniority_final_source = 'llm' AND seniority_final = 'unknown' is valid output, not a defect).

Routing-error rate estimate: <5%. The LLM's routing is consistent with a strong rule everywhere a rule would have produced a confident answer. The only concern is the Junior marker disagreements (~20%), but manual inspection of a handful shows they are genuinely ambiguous titles.

docs.title: 3. Cross-tabs and Cohen's kappa — seniority_final vs seniority_native
docs.text:
Sources: exploration/tables/T03/03_crosstab_arshkon.csv, 03_crosstab_scraped_linkedin.csv, 03_kappa_summary.csv

Two regimes: - Strict — restricts to rows where native is known AND seniority_final != unknown. Measures the agreement on the subset the production pipeline confidently labeled. - Lenient — counts seniority_final = unknown as a disagreement. Measures the full agreement including coverage loss.

Native labels intern and executive were mapped to entry and director respectively for the comparison.

Tag N strict κ strict κ lenient Entry recall (strict) Entry recall (lenient) Mid-senior recall (strict) arshkon 1,667 0.454 0.184 52.1% 9.6% 98.0% scraped_linkedin 15,982 0.646 0.135 81.3% 18.8% 98.2%

Two critical findings:

  1. Temporal instability in LinkedIn's native classifier. Strict kappa is 0.65 on scraped but 0.45 on arshkon — a 0.20 gap. Entry-level recall (strict) is 81% on scraped but only 52% on arshkon. This is not a production pipeline defect; the production pipeline agrees with native labels well on scraped but struggles with arshkon because arshkon's native entry labels are themselves noisy. See section 4.

  2. Coverage collapse on scraped (lenient kappa). Lenient kappa is 0.13 on scraped — worse than arshkon's 0.18 — because ~47% of native-labeled scraped SWE rows fall into seniority_final = unknown. This is the ~40% "scraped unknown" bucket showing up in kappa terms.

Associate recall is near-zero (1.5% strict arshkon, 1.8% strict scraped). This reflects a design decision: the LLM prompt does not routinely output associate as a category, so the rare cases where native says associate almost always map to something else in final.

Director recall is 22-26% strict — the rule-based pipeline does have director detection but it's narrower than LinkedIn's director definition (which seems to include a lot of VP/Director-titled engineering leaders that our pipeline calls mid-senior).

docs.title: 4. Native-label quality diagnostic — the arshkon entry-label problem
docs.text:
Source: exploration/tables/T03/07_native_entry_yoe_profile.csv

For rows where seniority_native = 'entry', what does yoe_extracted look like?

source period n_native_entry mean YOE median % with YOE % YOE ≤ 2 % YOE ≥ 5 % YOE ≥ 7 kaggle_arshkon 2024-04 738 4.18 3 62.1% 17.8% 26.4% 9.5% scraped 2026-03 2,264 2.34 2 39.6% 32.3% 4.2% 1.6% scraped 2026-04 2,425 2.37 2 38.0% 30.7% 4.0% 1.7%

This is the most important data-quality finding of T03. Arshkon's native-entry rows have mean YOE of 4.18 and 26% of them have YOE >= 5. These are not entry-level roles by any reasonable definition — they are a mix of actual juniors, associate/mid-level, and even some senior roles that happen to be tagged "entry" by LinkedIn's 2024-era labeler.

Scraped 2026 native-entry rows look much cleaner — mean YOE 2.34, median 2, only 4% ≥ 5 YOE. This is consistent with what one would expect from a native "entry" label.

Mechanism hypothesis: LinkedIn appears to have changed how it assigns the 'entry' label between early 2024 and 2026. In the 2024 arshkon snapshot, 'entry' captured a much broader pool that included a lot of mid-level roles. By 2026, 'entry' tracks YOE much more tightly. This is not something our preprocessing pipeline can fix — it is a property of the upstream label.

Implication: Any analysis that uses seniority_native = 'entry' as a ground-truth reference on arshkon will OVERESTIMATE the 2024 entry share (because the label is polluted with mid-level rows). This partially explains why the seniority_native arshkon-only 2024 entry share (15.7%) is so much higher than both seniority_final 2024 (2.7% of known) and the YOE proxy 2024 (11.2% of YOE-known) — the 2024 native label simply does not mean what it says.

Implication for downstream tasks: Do NOT treat arshkon seniority_native = 'entry' as ground truth. Use it only as a diagnostic to flag where the production pipeline disagrees. The YOE proxy is a better label-independent anchor for cross-period entry-share comparisons.

docs.title: 5. Junior share under three operationalizations (detailed)
docs.text: Sources: exploration/tables/T03/04*_junior_share*.csv

docs.title: 5b. Pooled 2024 vs 2026
docs.text: period bucket n n_entry_final % entry_final % entry of known YOE ≤ 2 % YOE ≤ 2 % YOE ≤ 2 of YOE known 2024 22,820 379 1.66% 2.70% 1,766 7.74% 11.22% 2026 40,881 1,275 3.12% 6.70% 4,742 11.60% 17.19%

docs.title: 5c. arshkon-only native entry baseline (2024-04)
docs.text: source n n_native_entry % native entry (all) % of known arshkon 2024-04 4,691 738 15.73% 22.48%

docs.title: Do they agree?
docs.text:
Direction: YES — seniority_final (all three denominators) and the YOE proxy all show a rise from 2024 to 2026. Magnitude differs but direction is unanimous.

Magnitude: - seniority_final (of all rows): 1.66% → 3.12% (×1.9) - seniority_final (of known seniority): 2.70% → 6.70% (×2.5) - YOE ≤ 2 (of all rows): 7.74% → 11.60% (×1.5) - YOE ≤ 2 (of YOE-known rows): 11.22% → 17.19% (×1.5)

Levels differ by ~5x between seniority_final and the YOE proxy. The YOE proxy is anchored at a much higher rate because "YOE ≤ 2" includes a lot of roles that require 1-2 YOE minimum but are not labeled entry-level by title or description. These are mid-career roles with flexible YOE floors, not junior roles. The YOE proxy is therefore a loose upper bound on juniors, while seniority_final is a tight lower bound.

The seniority_native arshkon-only figure (15.7%) is entirely inconsistent with the other two and — per section 4 — is best explained by the arshkon native classifier being too generous. Downstream tasks should not rely on arshkon seniority_native='entry' as a 2024 baseline.

docs.title: 6. Mechanism investigation — why do the levels differ?
docs.text:
The seniority_final vs YOE-proxy level gap (~5x) is driven by:

  1. The scraped "unknown" bucket. 39-41% of scraped SWE rows have seniority_final = 'unknown'. These rows DO have YOE extracted in some cases, so they show up as numerator in the YOE proxy but not in the seniority_final='entry' numerator. This drives the YOE proxy higher.

  2. YOE "at most 2" captures broad mid-level roles. Many mid-level postings specify a YOE floor of 2 or 3. The yoe_extracted <= 2 filter does not distinguish "1-2 YOE requirement for an entry role" from "2+ YOE minimum for a mid role." The LLM pipeline correctly distinguishes them (so seniority_final is more conservative) while the YOE proxy does not.

  3. The arshkon native label is polluted but the production pipeline handles it gracefully. Of 738 arshkon native-entry rows, 618 (84%) end up as seniority_final = 'unknown' — the LLM correctly refuses to call them entry when the description evidence is ambiguous. A further 53 map to entry, 65 to mid-senior, and 2 to associate. The production pipeline is more conservative than the native label in 2024, which is the right behavior given the native label's noise.

Bottom line on the magnitude disagreement: both operationalizations are measuring different things. seniority_final measures "postings with explicit entry-level signals"; the YOE proxy measures "postings with low experience requirements." Both rose from 2024 to 2026. The direction is robust.

docs.title: 7. Recommendation — is seniority_final defensible as primary?
docs.text:
YES. seniority_final is defensible as the primary seniority column for the rest of the exploration. Key reasons:

  1. It is conservative where the data is noisy (arshkon native 'entry' → most become 'unknown' because the LLM cannot confirm, which is the right behavior).
  2. It agrees with the label-independent YOE proxy on direction in cross-period analyses.
  3. LLM routing errors are <5% on weak-marker titles.
  4. Rule and LLM halves are internally consistent where both fire.

Required caveats for downstream use:

  1. Report results both "of all rows" and "of known-seniority rows." The scraped ~40% unknown bucket means the "of all rows" denominator is not comparable across sources. Always report both denominators.

  2. Always pair with the YOE proxy. Any seniority-stratified finding must show seniority_final primary + YOE proxy as label-independence check. If they disagree on direction, DO NOT pick a side without investigating (as per the core preamble).

  3. Do not use seniority_native='entry' as a 2024 ground-truth baseline on arshkon. The arshkon entry label has mean YOE 4.18 and 26% of rows have YOE ≥ 5; it is too noisy to anchor a cross-period comparison. Use arshkon seniority_native only as a diagnostic, not a reference.

  4. Interpret cross-period entry-share rises conservatively. The rise is real (direction agrees under all three ops) but part of it is mechanically driven by LLM coverage differences between sources (arshkon LLM coverage 62% vs scraped 17%). The LLM can only re-label rows it sees, so arshkon's lower unknown rate may mean it has more entry rows surfaced via the LLM than scraped does, inflating the 2024 denominator structure.

No remediation needed. The pipeline behavior is correct given the upstream data quality. The downstream protocol is the mitigation, not a code fix.

docs.title: Surprises and unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Arshkon's native entry label is the noisy one, not scraped's. Going in, I assumed 2024 native labels would be cleaner (older data, more curation) and 2026 scraped labels would be the unreliable ones. The reverse is true: arshkon entry has mean YOE 4.18, scraped entry has mean YOE 2.34. This is a big update for how I think about the 2024 baseline.

  2. The LLM returning 'unknown' on Roman-numeral titles is a feature, not a bug. 87% of Engineer II/III/IV titles get final='unknown'. This is the design working correctly — company-internal level numbers don't map cleanly to junior/mid/senior without context. Downstream tasks must not interpret this as a data-quality defect.

  3. The LLM was called but Stage 5 title-keyword rules still dominate asaniczka (58.8% keyword vs 28.6% LLM). This is higher than I expected given asaniczka's large size. The title-keyword classifier is catching a lot of Senior/Lead/Principal/Staff roles in asaniczka.

  4. Zero title_manager rows almost everywhere — only 1 in scraped 2026-04. This may suggest the manager-title classifier is under-utilized; worth a one-line follow-up.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. title column in unified.parquet contains some rows with invalid UTF-8 byte sequences — DuckDB's regex engine raises InvalidInputException when combined with window functions on these rows. Work-around: use LIKE patterns instead of regexp_matches, or normalize via Python after fetching with encode(). This is a preprocessing issue worth fixing in a future pipeline run (a one-line replace_invalid_utf8 during ingestion would be sufficient).

  2. title_normalized strips seniority markers (Junior/Senior/Jr/Sr), so marker-based sampling must use the raw title column. This is by design but worth documenting for other agents.

  3. Stage 9/10 LLM budgets differ across sources by a factor of ~4x in terms of coverage (arshkon 62% LLM-routed vs scraped 17%). Any cross-source comparison of seniority classification quality has to account for this.

  4. The Indeed subset (not under the default filter) has seniority_native = NULL entirely, so cross-validation on Indeed is not possible for the native-label audit.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. Use seniority_final as the primary seniority column. Do not re-derive seniority or apply alternative imputation schemes unless the task explicitly argues for one.
  2. Always pair seniority_final-based results with the YOE ≤ 2 label-independent check. Report both. Disagreement on direction is a finding; disagreement on magnitude usually is not.
  3. Never report seniority_native='entry' rates on arshkon as a cross-period baseline without the caveat that arshkon's 2024 entry label has mean YOE 4.18 and is demonstrably noisy.
  4. For percent-of-known calculations, always report the unknown fraction alongside. ~40% of scraped SWE rows are unknown — downstream tasks need to know how much of the denominator is being dropped.
  5. When computing the 2024→2026 entry-share change, report it under both seniority_final and the YOE proxy. Both show the rise, but magnitudes differ by ~60%. Treat the YOE proxy as a loose upper bound and seniority_final as a tight lower bound.
  6. Consider filing a preprocessing follow-up for (a) invalid UTF-8 in raw title and (b) the very low title_manager hit rate (effectively unused).

docs.title: T04 — SWE Classification Audit
docs.text: Agent B (Wave 1). Input: data/unified.parquet. Default filter: is_english = TRUE AND date_flag = 'ok' AND source_platform = 'linkedin'.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text:
SWE classification is reliable enough to use as primary. The regex tier (88-93% of is_swe=TRUE rows) is essentially noise-free, the dual-flag invariant holds perfectly, and the boundary categories (ML/Data/DevOps/Security) partition cleanly with only modest period drift. The main risk is the title_lookup_llm tier (5-11% of SWE rows): manual labeling of 50 borderline rows from this tier estimates 24% hard false-positive and 28% borderline-ambiguous, weighted to an aggregate FP contribution of roughly 1.3-2.7% of the full SWE sample. False negatives in the "contains engineer/developer/software" non-SWE pool are rare (~6% hard FN in 50 samples from a much larger pool), with most missed cases being DevSecOps / low-code platform developers / web-performance engineers.

Recommendation: Use is_swe as-is for primary analyses. For any headline finding whose magnitude could plausibly be driven by a 2-3% classification FP, rerun with tier title_lookup_llm excluded and report the delta (sensitivity dimension g in the preamble).

docs.title: 1. Tier breakdown (SWE, LinkedIn default filter)
docs.text:
Source: exploration/tables/T04/01b_swe_tier_breakdown_pct.csv

source period tier n % kaggle_arshkon 2024-04 regex 4,158 88.6% kaggle_arshkon 2024-04 title_lookup_llm 456 9.7% kaggle_arshkon 2024-04 embedding_high 77 1.6% kaggle_asaniczka 2024-01 regex 15,775 87.0% kaggle_asaniczka 2024-01 title_lookup_llm 1,968 10.9% kaggle_asaniczka 2024-01 embedding_high 386 2.1% scraped 2026-03 regex 18,468 93.4% scraped 2026-03 title_lookup_llm 1,018 5.2% scraped 2026-03 embedding_high 291 1.5% scraped 2026-04 regex 19,663 93.2% scraped 2026-04 title_lookup_llm 1,141 5.4% scraped 2026-04 embedding_high 300 1.4%

Observations: - The regex tier is dominant everywhere (87-93%). This is the strongest classification path and behavior-checked sampling suggests near-zero FP here. - title_lookup_llm share drops from 10-11% in 2024 to 5-5% in 2026. This is likely because the regex rules have been tuned against the 2024 vocabulary and are catching more titles cleanly on scraped data. (Or scraped titles are more standardized.) - embedding_high is small (1-2%) and essentially a safety net.

Confidence distribution (source: 03_swe_conf_by_tier.csv): confidence is effectively constant per tier (regex=1.0, title_lookup_llm=0.9, embedding_high~0.89). There are zero rows with swe_confidence in the [0.3, 0.7] range across all tiers — confidence is a tier indicator, not a continuous score. The borderline sample therefore has to be drawn from the title_lookup_llm tier, which is by construction the borderline category.

docs.title: 2. Dual-flag invariant
docs.text:
Source: exploration/tables/T04/02_dual_flag_check.csv

Metric Value Rows with >1 of (is_swe, is_swe_adjacent, is_control) 0 Rows with 0 of the three flags 968,658 n is_swe 63,701 n is_swe_adjacent 23,408 n is_control 155,745 n (LinkedIn default filter) 1,211,512

Invariant holds. Zero dual-flag violations. The 968K "none" rows are correct — they are postings outside all three target categories (finance, healthcare, retail, miscellaneous jobs that the pipeline explicitly did not flag).

docs.title: 3. Borderline SWE sample (50 rows, tier=title_lookup_llm)
docs.text:
Sampled 50 rows from the title_lookup_llm tier (since there is no confidence-in-[0.3,0.7] subpopulation). Manually labeled each as SWE / BORDERLINE / NOT_SWE based on title + first 250 chars of description. Full labels: exploration/tables/T04/04_borderline_swe_sample_labeled.csv.

Label Count SWE (true positive) 24 BORDERLINE (QA / test / firmware / cybersecurity) 14 NOT_SWE (false positive) 12

Clear false positive examples in the sample: - Computer Engineer IV — Combat vehicle systems engineer, hardware-adjacent (not SWE) - Lead Test Engineer — Aerospace systems engineer at Leidos (not SWE) - Quality Assurance Engineer — Magnera (materials manufacturer) QA (not SWE) - Automation Engineer — Food manufacturing industrial automation (not SWE) - Sr Automation Engineer — Electronic circuits / manufacturing (not SWE) - Senior Systems Integration Engineer — Medtronic medical device systems (not SWE) - Reliability Engineer — Bulk manufacturing (not SWE) - Principal Systems Test Engineer — DoD hardware test

These share a pattern: "Engineer" titles in industrial/aerospace/medical-device companies where the role is not writing software. The title_lookup_llm path catches the generic "Engineer" token without sufficient description context.

Borderline examples (ambiguous): - QA Automation / Test Engineer / Software Quality Engineer — debatable whether these are SWE or SWE_adjacent - DevSecOps Engineer II, Senior DevSecOps Engineer — I labeled these SWE (they write automation code) but reasonable people could call them adjacent - FPGA Firmware Engineer, Senior Firmware Engineer — embedded systems dev, borderline - Junior QA Engineer — pure QA, borderline

FP contribution estimate: - Hard FP rate within title_lookup_llm tier: 24% (12/50) - Borderline rate: 28% (14/50), which if half should really be adjacent ≈ 14% additional leakage - Title_lookup_llm share of total SWE: 5-11% across sources - Aggregate hard-FP contribution to SWE sample: 24% × (5-11%) = 1.3-2.7% - Aggregate soft-FP contribution: add ~1-2% for borderline leakage → ~2.3-4.7% total

This is within the 5% tolerance typical for production NLP classifiers. It is NOT large enough to invalidate cross-period comparisons, but it IS large enough that any headline finding whose effect size is smaller than 5% should be rerun with swe_classification_tier = 'title_lookup_llm' excluded (sensitivity dimension g).

docs.title: 4. Borderline non-SWE sample (50 rows, title contains engineer/developer/software, is_swe=FALSE)
docs.text:
Sampled 50 rows. Full labels: exploration/tables/T04/05_borderline_nonswe_sample_labeled.csv.

Label Count Legitimately NOT_SWE 35 BORDERLINE (ambiguous adjacent/SWE) 12 FALSE_NEGATIVE (should be SWE) 3

Clear false negatives identified: - FLEX Principal Engineer - Web Performance (Marriott) — This is clearly a front-end web performance SWE role. Classified as none (not SWE, not adjacent, not control). (swe_confidence 0.53 from the LLM backup path.) - Senior Pega Developer — Pega is a low-code platform but Pega developer is a real developer role. Classified as none. - Senior Cloud DevSecOps Engineer — Federal cloud DevSecOps (Applied Insight). Classified as none despite swe_confidence of 0.64.

Borderline cases worth noting: - Workday Financials Developer/Analyst, Sr Workday Payroll Engineer — Workday is platform config work; debatable whether it counts as SWE - Dashboard BI Developer — BI/dashboarding is borderline - Sr. Software Cybersecurity Engineer — Abbott medical cybersecurity, could be SWE or SWE_adjacent (correctly tagged adjacent) - Senior Commissioning Engineer - Robotics — Robotics with smart control code, borderline - Quality Engineer 1 — "Software Quality Engineer 1" with automated testing, borderline adjacent vs SWE

FN rate estimate: - Hard FN rate in this pool: 6% (3/50) - Borderline rate: 24% (12/50) - This sample is biased toward finding FNs because we filtered the non-SWE pool to rows whose title contains "engineer/developer/software" — the base rate of SWE in the remaining ~950K non-SWE postings (that do NOT contain these words) is much lower. - The title-filter subset contains roughly 150K-200K rows, so ~6% FN × 150K ≈ 9K missed SWE rows across all sources. Spread over 4 period-buckets, that's ~2K per bucket — non-trivial but small relative to the 19K-21K SWE per scraped bucket.

The FN pattern has an interpretable structure: the missed cases are overwhelmingly in newer specialized roles (DevSecOps, Pega platform dev, web-performance engineering) or platform-config work (Workday, Salesforce, BI). The rule-based regex was designed for 2024 vocabulary and does not fully capture the 2026 taxonomy. This is a known-weakness the title_lookup_llm tier is supposed to catch; its 90% confidence threshold is conservative enough that these slip through.

docs.title: 5. is_swe_adjacent and is_control profiles
docs.text:
Sources: exploration/tables/T04/06_adjacent_top_titles.csv, 07_control_top_titles.csv

Top adjacent titles (top 10): | title_normalized | n | |---|---:| | quality engineer | 1,407 | | data scientist | 1,368 | | network engineer | 1,258 | | data analyst | 1,110 | | architect | 466 | | solutions architect | 370 | | security engineer | 320 | | systems administrator | 259 | | system administrator | 192 | | data architect | 171 |

Top control titles (top 10): | title_normalized | n | |---|---:| | accountant | 7,770 | | registered nurse | 3,674 | | electrical engineer | 3,237 | | financial analyst | 2,558 | | mechanical engineer | 2,476 | | accounting manager | 2,371 | | nurse practitioner | 1,122 | | civil engineer | 931 | | tax accountant | 643 | | travel nurse - rn | 408 |

Assessment: - Adjacent is a coherent "technical, some code, not primary SWE" set: QA/test, data analyst/scientist, network/security, sys admin, architect, DevOps-adjacent. Clean. - Control is a clean non-tech baseline: accounting, nursing, electrical/mechanical/civil engineering. Clean. The EE/ME/civil presence is valuable — it shows the pipeline is correctly distinguishing "engineer" in other disciplines from software engineers.

No concerning patterns in either category's top-50.

docs.title: 6. Boundary-case analysis
docs.text:
Source: exploration/tables/T04/08_boundary_classification.csv

For roles that commonly straddle the SWE / adjacent boundary, how does the classifier split them?

Category source period n_total n_swe n_adjacent n_control n_other ML Engineer arshkon 2024-04 92 91 0 0 1 ML Engineer asaniczka 2024-01 216 208 0 0 8 ML Engineer scraped 2026-03 786 761 8 0 17 ML Engineer scraped 2026-04 862 835 10 0 17 Data Engineer arshkon 2024-04 366 340 9 0 17 Data Engineer asaniczka 2024-01 1,076 970 46 0 60 Data Engineer scraped 2026-03 1,572 1,501 38 0 33 Data Engineer scraped 2026-04 1,671 1,607 25 0 39 DevOps Engineer arshkon 2024-04 253 247 4 0 2 DevOps Engineer asaniczka 2024-01 526 520 4 0 2 DevOps Engineer scraped 2026-03 909 901 6 0 2 DevOps Engineer scraped 2026-04 963 956 3 0 4 Platform Engineer arshkon 2024-04 44 39 2 0 3 Platform Engineer asaniczka 2024-01 239 187 45 0 7 Platform Engineer scraped 2026-03 322 285 17 0 20 Platform Engineer scraped 2026-04 364 327 16 2 19 Security Engineer arshkon 2024-04 243 4 226 0 13 Security Engineer asaniczka 2024-01 517 3 472 0 42 Security Engineer scraped 2026-03 978 29 925 0 24 Security Engineer scraped 2026-04 1,018 18 970 0 30 QA Engineer arshkon 2024-04 372 57 31 2 282 QA Engineer asaniczka 2024-01 2,060 188 86 18 1,768 QA Engineer scraped 2026-03 526 186 114 6 220 QA Engineer scraped 2026-04 549 202 98 4 245

Observations:

  1. ML Engineer → SWE consistently. 96-99% of ML Engineer titles are classified as is_swe across all sources. Cross-period drift is minimal. No concern.

  2. Data Engineer → SWE with a small adjacent slice. 93-96% SWE, 2-4% adjacent. Slight drift: arshkon 93%, scraped 96% — scraped is slightly more aggressive about calling Data Engineers SWE. This is compatible with real vocabulary shift (Data Engineering is more code-heavy in 2026 job descriptions).

  3. DevOps Engineer → overwhelmingly SWE. 97-99% across all sources. Tight.

  4. Platform Engineer is a concern. Asaniczka has 81% SWE / 19% adjacent, but scraped has 89% SWE / 5% adjacent. Cross-period drift of ~8 pp on the SWE share. This is the boundary-case role most at risk of differential classification between periods. Action: downstream tasks that include Platform Engineers should run a sensitivity with platform-engineer-only tagged separately.

  5. Security Engineer → adjacent consistently. 96-97% adjacent, 2-3% SWE. The small SWE share likely reflects roles titled "Security Software Engineer" that the classifier correctly identifies as SWE. Scraped SWE share is slightly higher (2.8-3.0% vs 0.6-1.6% on 2024) — this may reflect real growth in product-security-engineering roles or minor classifier drift. Too small to move aggregate numbers.

  6. QA Engineer split is the noisiest boundary. Asaniczka classifies 9% as SWE / 4% adjacent / 86% other (i.e., most asaniczka "QA Engineer" titles don't match the SWE regex narrowly enough and fall through into the unclassified pool). Scraped is 35-37% SWE / 18-21% adjacent / 42-45% other — a major jump in the SWE share. This is significant and is almost certainly due to the 2026 scraped pool having more "QA Software Engineer" / "SDET" / "QA Automation Engineer" variants that trip regex patterns.

  7. This last pattern — QA Engineer SWE share jumping from 9% to 37% between asaniczka and scraped — is a genuine classifier drift concern. If downstream tasks compare "SWE share of QA" or related metrics, this drift should be controlled for. The aggregate impact on total is_swe counts is small (~500 extra SWE rows in scraped vs a baseline of 19K-21K), but the share of QA roles flagged as SWE is period-dependent.

docs.title: 7. LLM cross-check — swe_classification_llm on is_swe=TRUE rows
docs.text:
Source: exploration/tables/T04/09_swe_classification_llm_on_is_swe.csv, 10_is_swe_vs_llm.csv

Where Stage 10 LLM classification was labeled (not all rows), what did the LLM return on is_swe=TRUE rows?

source period LLM=SWE LLM=SWE_ADJ LLM=NOT_SWE LLM=NULL n_is_swe arshkon 2024-04 2,283 269 365 1,774 4,691 asaniczka 2024-01 3,568 548 1,074 12,939 18,129 scraped 2026-03 2,912 308 254 16,303 19,777 scraped 2026-04 2,900 301 243 17,660 21,104

Among rows where the LLM did provide a classification, rate of LLM saying NOT_SWE (possible FP indicator):

  • arshkon: 365 / (2,283+269+365) = 12.5% LLM says NOT_SWE
  • asaniczka: 1,074 / 5,190 = 20.7%
  • scraped 2026-03: 254 / 3,474 = 7.3%
  • scraped 2026-04: 243 / 3,444 = 7.1%

Asaniczka has the highest LLM-disagreement rate (20.7%), consistent with its larger title_lookup_llm share and looser title filtering. Scraped has the lowest (7%). This is a reasonable upper bound on classifier FP: 7-21% of the LLM-labeled subset. But the LLM is only called on a subset of rows, and those are rows the rule-based pipeline was uncertain about, so this FP rate does NOT apply to the full SWE sample — just to the LLM-labeled subset, which is 18-29% of total SWE.

Combining with the tier breakdown: regex-tier FP ~0%, title_lookup_llm-tier FP ~24%, weighted by population share → aggregate FP ~1.3-2.7% (consistent with the borderline sample analysis in Section 3).

docs.title: Surprises and unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. The swe_confidence column is not a continuous score. It takes exactly three values (1.0 for regex, 0.9 for title_lookup_llm, ~0.89 for embedding_high). The task spec said to sample "confidence 0.3-0.7 or tier=title_lookup_llm" but the former is empty. This is not a bug — it reflects the tier-based design — but it means the borderline sample had to be drawn purely from title_lookup_llm.

  2. QA Engineer SWE-share jumps from 9% (asaniczka) to 37% (scraped). This is the only boundary case that shows substantial cross-period drift. Probably reflects real 2026 vocabulary shift (more "Software QA" / "SDET" postings) but worth flagging for downstream work.

  3. Security Engineer is almost never SWE (2-3%), even though "Security Software Engineer" titles exist. The adjacent classification is correct, but it means any task looking at "security-related SWE roles" cannot just filter is_swe — it needs to explicitly include is_swe_adjacent for security-engineer capture.

  4. Control category is cleaner than expected. Accountants, nurses, EEs, MEs — no contamination with SWE-adjacent roles in the top 50.

  5. False negatives in the non-SWE pool are concentrated in 2026 newer roles (DevSecOps, Pega, web-performance engineers). The classifier was tuned on 2024 vocabulary and misses some 2026-native role names. Aggregate impact is small (~6% of the filtered subset, ~1-2% of the true non-SWE pool).

  6. 3 false negatives in 50 non-SWE samples is higher than the ~1% I expected from a "carefully designed" classifier. But all 3 are edge cases (Pega, web perf, DevSecOps) that legitimate SWE rosters might disagree on — not egregious misses.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. swe_confidence is effectively a tier code. Sampling "confidence in [0.3, 0.7]" returns 0 rows. Document this for future agents so nobody writes a filter that expects continuous confidence values.

  2. The title_lookup_llm tier has a 0.9 blanket confidence. The LLM was called only when the rule-based stage could not decide; it doesn't produce a granular confidence. The tier is the signal.

  3. Non-English and non-OK rows were excluded by the default filter. Classification quality on non-English or date-flag-bad rows is not assessed here and is irrelevant to the primary analysis.

  4. Borderline sample size is 50 per direction (100 total). The 95% Wilson CI on a 24% proportion with n=50 is roughly [14%, 37%]. Don't over-interpret the exact number; the takeaway is "order of magnitude 20-30% within title_lookup_llm tier."

  5. Boundary case counts use simple LIKE matching on title. Titles with unusual phrasings (e.g., "Machine Learning Platform") may not match. The boundary analysis is indicative, not exhaustive.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. Use is_swe as primary. FP rate is ~2-3% aggregate, well within acceptable bounds.

  2. For effect sizes <5%, rerun with swe_classification_tier != 'title_lookup_llm' (sensitivity dimension g). Report the delta. If the finding survives, it's robust to SWE FP.

  3. Treat the QA boundary as period-drift-risky. When a task compares a metric across periods that includes QA Engineers, rerun once excluding rows whose title normalizes to *quality* or *qa* and report the delta.

  4. Treat Platform Engineer similarly. The 81% → 89% SWE-share drift between asaniczka and scraped is small but non-zero.

  5. Consider a Stage 5 rule refresh to catch 2026 vocabulary (DevSecOps, Pega Developer, Web Performance Engineer, Cloud Platform Engineer, Forward Deployed Engineer). This is the main FN pattern. Not required for Wave 1 analysis, but a high-value preprocessing improvement for a future pipeline run.

  6. Do NOT apply a confidence threshold filter — confidence is constant within tier so any such filter reduces to a tier filter. Use the tier column directly.

docs.title: T05 — Cross-dataset comparability
docs.text: Agent: Wave 1 Agent C Scope: SWE postings, LinkedIn only, is_english = true, date_flag = 'ok' Inputs: data/unified.parquet Script: exploration/scripts/T05_cross_dataset_comparability.py

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
• Description length: large, real shift (scraped ~4.9 K chars vs ~3.3 K arshkon vs ~3.9 K asaniczka). KS statistic arshkon↔scraped = 0.296 (p≈0). Signal-to-noise (cross-period / within-2024) = 2.29 — above but not far above instrument noise, driven partly by markdown preservation in the scraped text.
  • AI mention rate (binary): 2.81% (arshkon) → 2.80% (asaniczka) → 18.8% (scraped). Within-2024 noise ≈ 0. SNR = 925. This is the cleanest cross-period change in the dataset.
  • Entry share under seniority_final: arshkon 7.72%, asaniczka 1.74%, scraped 6.70%. Within-2024 gap (5.98 pp) is ~3× larger than the cross-period gap. SNR = 0.33 — well below the instrument-noise threshold. The scraped entry share is almost identical to the historical arshkon baseline. Any "entry-share declining" story cannot be told from seniority_final pooled over 2024 sources.
  • YOE ≤ 2 share: arshkon 15.0%, asaniczka 10.4%, scraped 17.2%. Within-2024 noise (4.6 pp) ≈ cross-period effect (4.5 pp). SNR = 0.98. Also below the threshold.
  • Mean YOE: arshkon 5.22, asaniczka 5.80, scraped 5.05. SNR = 0.80. Also indistinguishable from noise.
  • Geographic: state distributions differ significantly in every pair (chi² p ≈ 0 for all three). The within-2024 arshkon-vs-asaniczka chi² is 373; arshkon-vs-scraped is 2596 and asaniczka-vs-scraped is 6212 — so the cross-period geographic shift is much larger than the within-2024 variation but it is still substantial within 2024, and two different platforms/collection methods can look very different even in the same year. Interpret geographic shifts with care.
  • Industry composition (arshkon vs scraped): systematically different, but sample bias in arshkon (small, recruiting/consulting-heavy) is the likely dominant driver — see the aggregator profile in T06.
  • Company overlap: Jaccard(arshkon, scraped) = 0.109; Jaccard(asaniczka, scraped) = 0.135. ~44% of arshkon companies appear in the scraped window; only 13% of scraped companies are in arshkon. This asymmetry matters for the T06 panel (125 companies with ≥5 SWE in both arshkon and scraped).
  • Title vocabulary: Jaccard(arshkon, scraped) = 0.026 (very low). Most titles are unique to one window — title-level comparisons must collapse to families.

docs.title: 1. Description length
docs.text:
source n mean median p25 p75 std kaggle_arshkon 4691 3306 2974 1534 4749 2142 kaggle_asaniczka 18129 3881 3733 2288 5250 2079 scraped 40881 4912 4862 3171 6455 2343

KS-pair (all p ≈ 0): - arshkon ↔ scraped: D = 0.296 - asaniczka ↔ scraped: D = 0.198 - arshkon ↔ asaniczka: D = 0.144

Artifact diagnostic: the scraped source preserves markdown formatting (bullets, bold headers, nested lists) whereas the Kaggle sources are HTML-stripped. A meaningful portion of the ~1.6 K-char jump is almost certainly re-inserted whitespace and markdown tokens rather than real additional content. Use description_core_llm (Stage 9 cleaned text) for any finding that depends on length, and report a boilerplate-stripped sensitivity.

See exploration/figures/T05/desc_length_hist.png.

docs.title: 2. Company overlap
docs.text:
pair n_a n_b intersection jaccard overlap(a→b) overlap(b→a) arshkon↔scraped 2298 7940 1004 0.109 0.437 0.126 asaniczka↔scraped 4948 7940 1529 0.135 0.309 0.193 arshkon↔asaniczka 2298 4948 742 0.114 0.323 0.150

Top-50 overlap: 21 of arshkon's top 50 appear in scraped's top 50 (Jaccard 0.27); 15 of asaniczka's top 50 (Jaccard 0.18); 12 shared between arshkon and asaniczka top 50 (Jaccard 0.14). The big players repeat across sources, but the long tail does not — any source-restricted finding is really about a specific mix of employers.

docs.title: 3. Geographic (state)
docs.text:
Chi-squared (pooled state counts, multi-location excluded):

pair chi² df p arshkon ↔ scraped 2596 39 0 asaniczka ↔ scraped 6212 47 0 arshkon ↔ asaniczka 373 47 2e-52

All pairs reject the null at any threshold. Within-2024 chi² is ~14% the size of arshkon↔scraped, so cross-period geographic shift is real but not massively larger than within-2024 variability.

Top-10 state shares per source are in exploration/tables/T05/top10_states.csv.

docs.title: 4. Seniority distributions (seniority_final, unknown excluded)
docs.text:
level arshkon asaniczka scraped arshkon share asa share scr share entry 175 204 1275 0.077 0.017 0.067 mid-senior 2064 11426 17418 0.910 0.973 0.915 director 13 92 295 0.006 0.008 0.015 associate 15 24 52 0.007 0.002 0.003

Chi² pairwise: all significant (arshkon↔scraped chi² = 26, asaniczka↔scraped chi² = 433, arshkon↔asaniczka chi² = 275). The within-2024 gap in entry share (7.72 → 1.74, 5.98 pp) is 3× the cross-period gap (6.70 → average of the two 2024 baselines ≈ 4.73, a 1.97 pp difference). The "entry share" comparison cannot survive pooling 2024 sources — it is dominated by asaniczka's near-zero native entry rate, which is a known classifier-coverage artifact (asaniczka has no native entry labels; all its entry signal comes from Stage 10 LLM backfill on selected rows).

docs.title: 5. Title vocabulary
docs.text:
pair n_a n_b intersection jaccard arshkon ↔ scraped 2162 18321 517 0.026 asaniczka ↔ scraped 7577 18321 927 0.037 arshkon ↔ asaniczka 2162 7577 449 0.048

The scraped title vocabulary is 6-8× larger. Titles unique to 2026 are dominated by company-prefixed job titles and hyper-specific roles; titles unique to 2024 are dominated by recruiting-firm stock language. See titles_unique_2026.csv / titles_unique_2024.csv.

docs.title: 6. Industry (arshkon vs scraped)
docs.text: See exploration/tables/T05/industry_dist.csv. Arshkon is disproportionately "Staffing & Recruiting" (a side-effect of the Kaggle snapshot oversampling recruiting agencies), while scraped tilts toward Software Development and Financial Services. This is a composition difference, not a market shift.

docs.title: 7. Artifact diagnostic — which comparisons are trustworthy?
docs.text:
metric direction survives pooling? within-2024 SNR verdict AI mention rate yes 925 trustworthy — real, enormous mean desc length yes 2.29 trustworthy but inflated by markdown reinsertion; use description_core_llm geographic mix yes ~7 real but reflects collection-method differences too seniority_final entry share no 0.33 not above noise — do not pool 2024 sources for this YOE ≤ 2 share marginal 0.98 not above noise mean YOE marginal 0.80 not above noise title vocabulary yes (divergence large) ~2 structural instrument difference — collapse to title families company overlap yes (divergence large) similar instrument-driven long tail

Primary takeaway: the most-cited cross-period RQ1 outcomes (entry share, YOE, mean YOE) cannot be asserted naively by pooling the 2024 sources. The arshkon-only vs scraped comparison is the safer default; asaniczka is useful for power but not for entry-level directionality.

docs.title: 8. Within-2024 calibration table
docs.text:
See signal_to_noise.csv. Full table:

metric arshkon asaniczka scraped within-2024 cross-period SNR mean_desc_len 3306 3881 4912 576 1318 2.29 entry_share 0.0772 0.0174 0.0670 0.0598 0.0197 0.33 yoe_le2_share 0.1498 0.1036 0.1719 0.0461 0.0452 0.98 mean_yoe 5.22 5.80 5.05 0.58 0.46 0.80 ai_mention_rate 0.0281 0.0280 0.1878 0.0002 0.1597 925

Rule of thumb (from preamble): SNR < 2 ⇒ flag as potentially within instrument noise.

docs.title: 9. Platform labeling stability test
docs.text:
Top-20 SWE titles that appear in both arshkon and scraped (see top20_title_seniority_final.csv, top20_title_yoe.csv, top20_title_seniority_native.csv).

Disagreement pattern for the top title "software engineer": - seniority_final entry share: 6.5% (arshkon) → 4.9% (scraped). Decline. - yoe_extracted <= 2 share: 18.2% (arshkon) → 20.2% (scraped). Increase. - Mean YOE: 4.83 → 5.00 (stable).

Same disagreement at most top titles: "devops engineer" goes from 0% entry (final) to 4.6%, while YOE ≤ 2 goes from 2.1% to 11.5%. "machine learning engineer" entry share goes from 4.0% to 0.5% but YOE ≤ 2 stays ~15%. "ai engineer" has almost no arshkon baseline. The two operationalizations frequently disagree in sign.

Interpretation options (non-exclusive): 1. LLM classifier conservatism. Stage 10's LLM is stricter than arshkon's native label, so a posting that says "2-3 yrs" and arshkon tagged entry is now mid-senior. That would push seniority_final down while YOE stays put. 2.
