Platform label drift. LinkedIn may be tagging fewer postings as "Entry level" in 2026 than in 2024 (so seniority_native shifts), and Stage 10's LLM is not fully offsetting that. 3. Real scope inflation at the same YOE level. A company writes "entry level" but asks for 2+ years and calls it a mid role — consistent with RQ1.

The arshkon-only native comparison (top20_title_seniority_native.csv) is mostly sparse because the scraped source has very few seniority_native = 'entry' rows (asaniczka has zero by construction, and scraped's native labels are concentrated in mid_senior). This ambiguity is exactly why T06's within-vs-between decomposition needs both operationalizations — see T06.

Indeed cross-validation (indeed_vs_linkedin_entry.csv):

platform n entry_share_of_known yoe_le2_share indeed 11860 0.0661 0.1806 linkedin (scraped) 40881 0.0670 0.1719

Indeed and LinkedIn scraped give essentially identical entry shares and YOE distributions. The LinkedIn-platform-relabeling hypothesis weakens substantially: whatever is driving seniority_final at 6.7% in the 2026 scraped data is also driving it at 6.6% on an entirely different platform pulled through the same harmonizer. That is a strong signal that the Stage 5/10 pipeline is measuring the same thing across platforms, and that the within-2024 SNR shortfall is a real label-coverage problem (asaniczka missing entry labels) rather than a cross-platform artifact.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Entry share survives Indeed cross-validation. LinkedIn scraped and Indeed scraped agree on entry share to 0.1 pp. This rules out the "LinkedIn platform relabeling" worry for RQ1-related metrics as long as arshkon (not asaniczka) is the 2024 baseline.
  2. The SNR table is more punishing than I expected for the headline RQ metrics. Entry share, YOE-≤2, mean YOE all fail the SNR = 2 cutoff once you pool 2024 sources. This has to be flagged before the formal analysis.
  3. Within top-20 titles, seniority_final and YOE proxy frequently disagree in sign, not just magnitude. The direction of the "junior share" finding depends on which one you use. See T06 decomposition for the full story.
  4. Top-50 company overlap is only 21 companies between arshkon and scraped top-50. The "big-employer" lists that dominate corpus-level analyses have a 58% replacement rate across sources.
  5. Cross-company description collisions are rare — the top collision is 6 companies sharing a hash (Stage 4 aggregator-group collapse working as intended, see T06 §3).

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• KS-test p-values hit floating-point zero because of large N — do not interpret p-values, interpret the D statistic magnitude.
  • yoe_extracted is a regex-derived proxy; it is available for ~60-70% of postings with large coverage differences across sources.
  • All "entry share" numbers exclude seniority_final = 'unknown'. Unknown shares differ across sources (~1% arshkon, ~50% asaniczka pre-LLM, ~53% scraped pre-LLM), and seniority_final_source is worth profiling before any formal comparison.
  • Indeed's seniority_final only has Stage 5 strong-rule labels (no LLM frame) — treat the Indeed 0.066 number as a floor, not a direct comparable.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• For RQ1 (junior share): default to arshkon vs scraped, do not pool asaniczka, and present both seniority_final and YOE ≤ 2. Disagreement in direction is itself a finding to investigate (see T06 §5). The within-2024 calibration should appear in every table that compares 2024 to 2026.
  • For T08/T09 (distribution profiling, topic models): use description_core_llm with llm_extraction_coverage = 'labeled' for any length-sensitive metric. Raw-length findings are confounded with the Kaggle→scraped formatting shift.
  • For text-based analyses: cap at 20-50 postings per company_name_canonical (see T06 §1) — the scraped top-20 take 18% of the corpus.
  • For the feasibility agent (T07): the arshkon ↔ scraped company overlap panel is 125 companies at ≥5 SWE; at ≥3 SWE the panel is likely 300-400. Power on within-company effects is workable for length/AI but weaker for entry share.

docs.title: T06 — Company concentration deep investigation
docs.text: Agent: Wave 1 Agent C Scope: SWE postings, LinkedIn only, is_english = true, date_flag = 'ok' Inputs: data/unified.parquet Script: exploration/scripts/T06_company_concentration.py, T06_figures.py

docs.title: Headline (read this section before Wave 2)
docs.text:
1. Company concentration is moderate-to-high in every source (scraped Gini ≈ 0.69, top-20 share 18%). It is actively high enough to drive corpus-level term frequencies, topic models, and anything that aggregates mentions-per-posting.
  2. Entry-level posting is highly concentrated on a small subset of companies. 79% of arshkon companies, 89% of asaniczka companies, and 79% of scraped companies with ≥5 SWE postings post ZERO entry-labeled roles (under seniority_final). The aggregate "entry share" is driven by a small group of entry-active companies, not a market-wide behavior.
  3. The entry-share change on the arshkon ↔ scraped overlap panel flips sign depending on operationalization. Under seniority_final: arshkon 6.65% → scraped 5.74%, a -0.9 pp DECLINE with within-company component = -3.2 pp. Under YOE ≤ 2: arshkon 12.3% → scraped 19.7%, a +7.4 pp INCREASE with within-company component = +1.1 pp (the rest is composition). These are the same companies. The operationalization is what changes the sign. Downstream agents MUST report both.
  4. Stage 4 fixed the duplicate-template problem. No within-source company has a dup_ratio that violates the ≈1.0 target after multi-location collapse. The prior "6 companies with 4-25 identical descriptions" issue is gone. Residual dup_ratios in scraped (Turing 3.0, DataAnnotation 2.7, Mercor 1.8) are small and are real repostings over the scrape window, not group artifacts.
  5. Cross-company hash collisions are small — top collision is 6 companies sharing one description (likely an aggregator reposting under alternate company labels not caught by the canonicalizer). Worth noting but not structural.
  6. Top-6 scraped entry-final companies account for 18.8% of the entry pool (vs the prior run's 23%). TikTok + ByteDance alone are 10.4% of the entry pool. The prior run's finding was substantially driven by description duplication; the current finding is driven by genuine entry-poster concentration plus one ByteDance-group spike. Treat the scraped entry pool as a small-number problem even after the fix.

docs.title: 1. Concentration metrics per source
docs.text:
concentration_metrics.csv. All SWE/LinkedIn/English/date_flag=ok.

source excl agg n_companies n_postings HHI Gini top-1 top-5 top-10 top-20 top-50 singletons arshkon no 2298 4646 15.6 0.434 1.1% 4.6% 7.6% 12.3% 20.9% 69.0% arshkon yes 2235 4216 13.3 0.399 1.2% 4.0% 6.8% 10.4% 18.3% 69.7% asaniczka no 4948 18129 54.4 0.641 4.9% 12.2% 16.0% 21.1% 30.6% 58.9% asaniczka yes 4814 15186 19.1 0.588 1.9% 5.8% 9.1% 14.1% 23.5% 59.1% scraped no 7940 40881 36.1 0.693 4.2% 9.4% 12.9% 18.1% 26.9% 49.1% scraped yes 7241 34081 23.6 0.672 2.2% 7.2% 11.1% 16.6% 25.1% 50.5%

Key points: - Scraped has the lowest singleton share (49%) — more companies have multiple postings in the scraped window. Good for a panel but bad for corpus-weighted aggregates. - Asaniczka's HHI drops from 54 to 19 once aggregators are removed, because ClearanceJobs alone contributed ~5% of the asaniczka corpus. - Scraped top-1 is "Jobs via Dice" (an aggregator, 4.2%), dropping to 2.2% once aggregators are excluded. "Jobs via Dice" = 1711 postings = largest single concentration mass in the dataset. - See exploration/figures/T06/lorenz_curves.png for Lorenz curves with/without aggregators.

docs.title: 2. Top-20 employer profile per source
docs.text: Full tables: top20_kaggle_arshkon.csv, top20_kaggle_asaniczka.csv, top20_scraped.csv.

docs.title: Top-20 scraped (SWE, LinkedIn) — headline rows
docs.text:
# company n share agg mdl yoe entry_share (final) yoe_le2_share 1 Jobs via Dice 1711 4.2% yes 5208 4.90 5.7% 19.9% 2 Google 757 1.9% no 4768 2.75 1.8% 52.4% 3 JPMorgan Chase 657 1.6% no 5820 5.12 0.0% 4.5% 4 Microsoft 386 0.9% no 6429 3.68 0.4% 38.6% 5 AWS 339 0.8% no 6847 3.78 0.0% 7.3% 6 Amazon 335 0.8% no 5631 3.37 16.7% 14.9% 7 Booz Allen 303 0.7% no 5634 4.04 2.2% 30.8% 8 Wells Fargo 288 0.7% no 5889 3.71 0.0% 27.8% 9 Anduril 255 0.6% no 8356 4.88 1.3% 12.9% 10 Leidos 245 0.6% no 7433 7.08 24.1% 21.5% 11 Actalent 238 0.6% yes 4841 4.54 6.1% 21.3% 12 Walmart 233 0.6% no 8725 2.15 0.0% 73.8% 13 Haystack 232 0.6% no 2012 5.35 9.8% 14.8% 14 Motion Recruitment 229 0.6% yes 2311 4.98 1.8% 10.8% 15 Capital One 223 0.5% no 6540 4.22 0.0% 10.8% 16 hackajob 216 0.5% no 5945 5.42 2.8% 12.0% 17 NVIDIA 188 0.5% no 4126 6.39 1.3% 6.7% 18 Uber 187 0.5% no 3614 4.48 2.7% 20.1% 19 General Motors 182 0.4% no 7202 5.28 6.7% 11.1% 20 Peraton 181 0.4% no 5880 5.86 17.1% 18.5%

Critical drivers hiding in this table: - Walmart alone drives 73.8% of its YOE ≤ 2 rate → 172 yoe-le2 rows. This one company contributes ~3.6% of the entire scraped YOE ≤ 2 pool while contributing only 0.6% of postings. - Google has 52.4% YOE ≤ 2 → 391 yoe-le2 rows ≈ 8.2% of the scraped YOE ≤ 2 pool from a single company with 1.9% of postings. - These are the companies driving the "juniors rising" story when measured by the YOE proxy. Without Walmart + Google the scraped yoe_le2 share falls by ~2.2 pp (back into noise range). - Under seniority_final, 8 of the top 20 companies (AWS, JPMorgan, Wells Fargo, Capital One, Walmart, Microsoft at 0.4%, AWS 0%, Cap One 0%) post essentially zero entry-labeled roles. Entry share is driven by a tail, not by the top of the distribution.

docs.title: Top-20 arshkon — observations
docs.text: Dominated by recruiting agencies (Motion Recruitment, TEKsystems, Actalent, Robert Half, Apex Systems, Open Systems Technologies, Experis, Insight Global, Akkodis). 14 of 20 are staffing firms, which is a baseline composition that doesn't reappear in scraped top-20 — this contributes to the "industry shift" in T05 §6.

docs.title: Top-20 asaniczka — observations
docs.text: Includes ClearanceJobs at 898 postings (~5% of asaniczka). Asaniczka top-20 is dominated by aggregators and recruiting firms more than either other source.

docs.title: 3. Duplicate-template audit (verification)
docs.text:
dup_templates_*.csv, cross_company_hash_collisions.csv.

Within-source verification:

source top dup_ratio company posts / distinct arshkon 1.15 maven 15 / 13 asaniczka 1.64 Forward 18 / 11 scraped 3.00 Turing 42 / 14

Scraped residuals ≥ 1.2×: Turing (3.0, 42/14), DataAnnotation (2.7, 71/26), Mercor (1.8, 73/40), YO IT Consulting (1.8, 27/15), ExecutivePlacements.com (2.2, 11/5), Pittsburgh Robotics Network (1.4, 27/19), Stealth Startup (1.4, 41/29), Pure Storage (1.4, 24/17), CHAOS Industries (1.4, 21/15), Crossing Hurdles (1.3, 25/19). None of these collapsed onto a 4-25×-identical description the way the prior run's six entry employers did. Turing and DataAnnotation posting the same job repeatedly across weeks is real reposting behavior, not a harmonizer bug — Stage 4 collapses same-snapshot multi-location duplicates, it does not (and should not) collapse reposts across time.

Cross-company hash collisions (top): largest collision is 6 companies sharing one description_hash; next two are 5 each. Total collisions with ≥2 companies sharing an exact description: 24 hashes in the top 30. Sample inspection (not done here) would likely show these are aggregator relabeling — a single employer reposted under different aggregator brand names that the canonicalizer treats as distinct. Action item: sample 5 of these hashes and manually confirm they're aggregator relabels; if so, extend the canonicalizer with an alternate-name table.

Verification verdict: Stage 4 multi-location collapse is working. Entry-specialist companies in the scraped source have dup_ratio = 1.0 (every entry post is a distinct hash). The prior-run surprise is fixed at the pipeline level.

docs.title: 4. Entry-level posting concentration (CRITICAL)
docs.text:
entry_poster_concentration.csv, entry_share_distribution_*.csv, entry_specialists_*.csv, figure top15_entry_final_pool_scraped.png, entry_share_within_company.png.

source total companies any-entry-final any-entry share ≥5 SWE cos zero-entry @ ≥5 zero-entry share zero-yoe≤2 @ ≥5 specialists (>50% entry, ≥5 SWE) specialist share arshkon 2298 147 6.4% 164 130 79.3% 93 56.7% 3 asaniczka 4948 145 2.9% 632 562 88.9% 367 58.1% 3 scraped 7940 664 8.4% 1500 1191 79.4% 796 53.1% 59

This is the most important diagnostic in this report. Entry-level posting is not a market-wide behavior. In every source, only 6-8% of companies post any entry-labeled rows at all. Among companies with substantive presence (≥5 SWE postings), 80-89% post zero entry. The "entry share" aggregate is dominated by a small entry-active subset.

Scraped entry-share distribution (companies with ≥5 SWE that post any entry):

entry-share range companies 0 - 5% 28 5 - 10% 34 10 - 20% 69 20 - 30% 53 30 - 50% 43 50 - 75% 42 75 - 100% 40

Of the 309 scraped companies with ≥5 SWE and at least one entry post, 40 are >75% entry, 42 are 50-75% entry, and 129 are ≤20% entry. The distribution is bimodal — a long right tail of entry-specialists plus a broad "occasional entry poster" left tail. The 59 specialist companies (>50% entry) are listed in entry_specialists_scraped.csv; inspection shows many are junior-focused programs (SynergisticIT, WayUp, SkillStorm, Brooksource, Tinder (???), IXL Learning, university-recruiting brands).

Top 15 scraped companies by entry_final count (distinct-description, post-Stage-4):

company entry_n distinct descs cum share of entry pool TikTok 85 85 6.7% ByteDance 48 48 10.4% Jobs via Dice 41 41 13.6% Leidos 28 28 15.8% IBM 21 21 17.5% SynergisticIT 17 17 18.8% Amazon 16 16 20.1% WayUp 16 16 21.3% Lensa 13 13 22.4% Peraton 12 12 23.3% Cisco 12 12 24.2% Lockheed Martin 11 11 25.1% General Motors 10 10 25.9% Emonics LLC 9 9 26.6% MetLife 7 7 27.1%

Top-6 share 18.8% (vs the 23% "6 companies × identical descriptions" from the prior run — the difference is that Stage 4 collapsed the multi-location duplicates, so the concentration is now measured on unique postings). TikTok and ByteDance alone = 10.4% of the entry_final pool. Treat any entry-level finding as a small-number result that can move by several percentage points if one or two companies shift hiring posture.

docs.title: 5. Within-company vs between-company decomposition
docs.text:
decomposition_overlap_panel.csv, overlap_panel_info.csv, figure within_vs_between_decomposition.png.

Panel: 125 companies with ≥5 SWE in BOTH arshkon and scraped. Total arshkon rows in panel = 1381. Total scraped rows in panel = 9060. This is an asymmetric panel — the same company has ~6.6× more postings in 2026 than 2024, so aggregate means shift with the weight change even when within-company metrics don't.

metric n companies arshkon panel mean scraped panel mean total change within between residual entry share (seniority_final) 115 0.0665 0.0574 -0.0091 -0.0320 +0.0229 0 entry share (YOE ≤ 2) 121 0.1228 0.1965 +0.0737 +0.0112 +0.0626 0 mean desc length 125 3489 5255 +1766 +963 +803 0 AI mention rate 125 0.0275 0.1832 +0.1557 +0.1477 +0.0080 0

docs.title: Reading the decomposition
docs.text:
Entry share — sign flips. - Under seniority_final: within-company entry share fell 3.2 pp, but the mix of companies in the panel shifted toward higher-entry companies, bringing the aggregate only -0.9 pp. If you held composition constant, every company on average hired fewer entry-labeled roles. - Under YOE ≤ 2: within-company rose only 1.1 pp; the overwhelming driver is composition (+6.3 pp). If you held composition constant, the YOE ≤ 2 share barely moved. - The two operationalizations disagree on the direction of within-company change at the same company-year panel. This is the most important disagreement in the entire Wave 1 exploration.

Most likely mechanisms (to be investigated in Wave 2): 1. Classifier shift. Stage 5 title keywords changed between pipeline versions, or Stage 10's LLM is stricter than the arshkon native classifier. A row that arshkon labeled entry at YOE ≤ 2 now gets mid-senior under seniority_final. This would simultaneously decrease seniority_final entry share and leave YOE ≤ 2 unchanged. This is consistent with the pattern. 2. Employer relabeling. Companies post the same YOE range under a mid-level title now that they used to post under an entry title. This would also decrease seniority_final entry share and increase or leave flat YOE ≤ 2, which is what we see. 3. Real scope inflation. Companies ask for 2 YOE but call the role "Software Engineer II" now that they used to call "New Grad." This is RQ1 in its pure form and also fits the pattern.

Nothing in the data distinguishes these three. All three predict the observed disagreement. Wave 2 needs a title×YOE contingency for the panel companies to pick between them.

Description length. Within-company (+963) is larger than between-company (+803) and accounts for 55% of the total change. This is good news — it means length inflation is a within-company phenomenon, not a composition effect. Same Anduril, same JPMorgan, same Wells Fargo write longer descriptions now than they did in 2024.

AI mention rate. Within-company (+14.8 pp) is 95% of total change. AI mentions are overwhelmingly within-company; composition contributes essentially nothing. The "AI mentions are exploding" story is robust to composition.

docs.title: Implication
docs.text: Any Wave 2 finding that depends on entry share direction must cite both operationalizations and both decomposition components. The agent recommends: report seniority_final as the primary result, report YOE ≤ 2 as the proxy, and report the within-company decomposition on the overlap panel as the cleanest single estimate of the effect. Headline: under seniority_final, within-company entry share dropped 3.2 pp at the same 115 companies that appear in both panels; under YOE ≤ 2, it rose 1.1 pp at 121 companies. The direction flip is itself the finding.

docs.title: 6. Aggregator profile
docs.text:
aggregator_profile.csv.

source agg? n mdl entry share yoe≤2 mean yoe ai rate arshkon no 4261 3359 7.8% 15.5% 5.21 2.9% arshkon yes 430 2780 6.4% 9.0% 5.36 1.6% asaniczka no 15186 3805 2.0% 11.0% 5.76 2.9% asaniczka yes 2943 4276 0.7% 6.9% 5.99 2.5% scraped no 34081 5113 6.5% 17.9% 4.98 19.1% scraped yes 6800 3905 8.3% 13.2% 5.46 17.1%

Aggregator share is 16.6% of scraped, 16.2% of asaniczka, 9.2% of arshkon. Scraped aggregator entry share is higher than non-aggregator entry share (!) — this is driven by Jobs-via-Dice and WayUp (both aggregators with entry-heavy feeds). Excluding aggregators changes both the numerator and denominator in opposite directions, which is why concentration metrics drop so much under exclusion (§1). Use is_aggregator = false as a sensitivity for any aggregate entry-share finding and report the split.

docs.title: 7. New entrants profile
docs.text:
new_entrants_profile.csv. A "new 2026 company" is a scraped company_name_canonical that does not appear in arshkon or asaniczka SWE/LinkedIn data.

bucket n companies n posts mdl entry share yoe≤2 AI rate aggregator share returning 2024 22564 5142 6.2% 19.1% 17.3% 13.9% new 2026 5916 18317 4629 7.4% 14.6% 20.6% 20.0%

New 2026 companies are: - More numerous (5916 vs 2024 — the scraped window sees 3× the company universe of returning employers) - Slightly shorter descriptions (4629 vs 5142) - Slightly higher entry share - Lower YOE ≤ 2 share (14.6% vs 19.1%) — which explains part of the YOE ≤ 2 composition component from §5: returning companies have more yoe≤2 in 2026, but new entrants have less, so composition pulls in opposite directions. - Higher AI rate (20.6% vs 17.3%) — new entrants are more AI-mention-dense, consistent with 2026 startups writing modern job ads. - More aggregator-heavy (20.0% vs 13.9%) — the expansion in company universe is partially driven by aggregators relabeling postings under new brand names.

docs.title: 8. Per-finding concentration prediction table (MOST IMPORTANT OUTPUT)
docs.text:
exploration/tables/T06/concentration_predictions.csv (written below). Downstream Wave 2/3 agents should read this before running any corpus-level analysis.

Columns: - metric: the downstream analysis category - unit: row, company, company-term, etc. - concentration_risk: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH — how much a handful of companies can swing the aggregate - direction_risk: can the direction flip under a reasonable re-weighting? - recommended_default: what the agent should do as primary specification - essential_sensitivity: what alternative must be reported alongside - evidence: T06 section that justifies the classification

metric unit concentration_risk direction_risk recommended_default essential_sensitivity evidence entry share (seniority_final) row HIGH YES (flips sign vs YOE proxy) arshkon-vs-scraped on overlap panel, within-company decomposition report YOE ≤ 2 side-by-side; cap at 50/company as robustness §4, §5 entry share (YOE ≤ 2) row HIGH partial (sign doesn't flip but magnitude moves ~60% between with/without top-2 companies) overlap panel, within-component report seniority_final; exclude Walmart/Google/Microsoft as sensitivity §2, §5 AI mention rate (binary) row LOW no (SNR=925, within-component 95% of total) arshkon vs scraped, all rows aggregator exclusion §5 mean description length row MEDIUM no (within-component = 55% of total) arshkon vs scraped; within-panel as sensitivity use description_core_llm; cap at 50/company §5, T05 §1 term frequency (corpus) company-term HIGH yes (top company contributes 4% of corpus, top-20 contribute 18%) cap at 50 postings per company, aggregate at company-term level, re-normalize no-cap sensitivity; company-name stoplist (preamble) §1, §2 topic model prevalence row HIGH yes (topic scores are corpus-weighted) cap at 30-50 postings per company before fitting; report topic × company Gini no-cap sensitivity on topic size ordering §1, §2 co-occurrence network edges company-term-pair HIGH yes (single company's boilerplate template produces a dense internal clique) binary per-posting, then collapse to company-level edge before aggregation show edge weight distribution across companies contributing to each edge §2, §3 tech stack prevalence (binary mentions per posting) row MEDIUM partial (top-20 companies tilt toward specific stacks) cap at 50/company report per-company presence (did any of >=5 posts mention X) §2 "scope inflation" indicators (ownership, lead, end-to-end) row HIGH yes (3 top-20 companies have boilerplate "ownership" language) cap at 50/company + validate keyword on 50-row sample per period preamble §6 (keyword validation) §2, T05 §8 "management language" indicators row HIGH yes (same 3 companies + Leidos template drive the signal) cap at 50/company; exclude aggregators validation sample §2 ghost-job rate row MEDIUM partial (aggregators have inflated ghost rates) exclude aggregators as primary all-rows sensitivity §6 geographic / metro distribution row MEDIUM no (states differ cross-source but ordering is stable) as-is with multi-location excluded — T05 §3 YOE distribution (mean, median, quantiles) row HIGH yes (Walmart 73.8% yoe≤2 = 3.6% of entire yoe≤2 pool) cap at 50/company as primary, no-cap sensitivity report YOE distribution per top-20 company §2 company industry mix company LOW no (company-level unit of analysis) as-is — T05 §6 seniority_final × title cells row HIGH yes (title vocabulary turns over 97%; per-title cells are tiny) collapse titles to 20-30 families before comparing report within-family n T05 §5 aggregator-adjusted entry share row HIGH partial report both is_aggregator=true and false splits; never combine without showing — §6 new-entrant vs returning comparison company MEDIUM no present separately from returning; do not pool — §7

Top-3 findings most likely to be concentration-driven and flip direction under a reasonable re-weighting, plus recommended handling:

  1. Entry share cross-period change. Sign depends on operationalization and on whether you use the overlap panel vs. aggregate. Recommendation: present seniority_final and YOE ≤ 2 side by side on the 125-company overlap panel, with within-company decomposition as the primary estimate. Absolutely do not present aggregate entry share pooled across 2024 sources (T05 SNR = 0.33).

  2. Corpus term frequencies for "junior/entry/scope inflation" language. A single well-known company with a boilerplate template can move a term frequency by several percentage points. Recommendation: cap at 50 postings per company_name_canonical as primary; run once without capping as sensitivity; use company-name stoplist from preamble.

  3. Topic model topic ordering and prevalence. BERTopic/NMF topic sizes reflect corpus weights. With scraped top-20 = 18% of corpus, a topic can be "the Walmart topic" or "the Jobs-via-Dice topic" with high probability. Recommendation: fit on a capped corpus (50/company), report topic × top-company concentration before interpreting any topic as a "market signal."

docs.title: Essential sensitivities applied
docs.text:
• (a) Aggregator exclusion — applied throughout §1, §6, and within the prediction table.
  • (b) Company capping — this task's subject; not re-applied as sensitivity. The prediction table is the deliverable for when capping is appropriate downstream.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. The entry-share sign flip under seniority_final vs YOE proxy survives the overlap-panel decomposition. This is not a sampling artifact; it is a classifier-vs-proxy disagreement on the same 115 companies. Wave 2 needs to figure out which side is right (it probably cannot, without hand-labeling).
  2. Walmart contributes 73.8% of its own YOE ≤ 2 rate, accounting for ~3.6% of the entire scraped YOE ≤ 2 pool. I did not expect one retailer to be this dominant in a "junior share" metric.
  3. 79-89% of substantive-presence companies post zero entry-labeled roles in every source. This is the single most important framing for RQ1: "entry share" is a metric about a small subset of the employer population, not the median company.
  4. Stage 4 fix worked cleanly. Dup_ratio = 1.0 for all scraped entry-posting companies. The prior-run's "6 companies with identical descriptions" is no longer visible, which means the concentration pattern we see now (top-6 = 18.8% of the entry pool) is the genuine concentration pattern.
  5. TikTok + ByteDance alone = 10.4% of the scraped entry pool. This is a single corporate family. If ByteDance corporate policy changes, the scraped entry pool moves several percentage points.
  6. AI mention rate is the one finding that concentration does not threaten. Within-component is 95% of the total change, and the effect is 50×-400× larger than any other metric. This is the most concentration-robust finding in the dataset.
  7. Cross-company hash collisions are small (max 6 companies) but real. Probably aggregator relabeling under alternate brand names. A follow-up canonicalizer pass should fold them.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• company_name_canonical is imperfect — some of the "new 2026" 5916 companies are almost certainly returning companies under a different brand label. This would inflate the between-company composition component.
  • seniority_final is unknown for a substantial share of rows, and the unknown share differs by source. All entry-share numbers exclude unknowns; the unknown share itself is diagnostic and should be tracked separately.
  • Aggregator detection depends on Stage 4 rules; false negatives are possible (e.g., small aggregators not in the rule list).
  • YOE extraction via regex covers only ~60-70% of postings; the yoe_le2 share is always computed over the YOE-present subset and is sensitive to which companies write explicit YOE floors.

docs.title: Action items for Wave 2
docs.text:
• T08 (distribution profiling) — use the per-finding prediction table as the master reference. Any finding in the HIGH-risk rows must report the company-capped sensitivity before the uncapped primary.
  • T09 (topic discovery) — fit on 50-posting/company cap; report topic concentration.
  • Any term-frequency or co-occurrence agent — apply company-name stoplist + 50/company cap. Never present corpus-wide term frequency without it.
  • For RQ1 — default overlap-panel decomposition with both seniority_final and YOE ≤ 2. Do not report a single entry-share number without the decomposition.
  • Canonicalizer improvement — investigate the 24 cross-company hash collisions as a follow-up maintenance task.

docs.title: T07 — External benchmarks & power/feasibility analysis
docs.text:
Agent D, Wave 1. Run date: 2026-04-15. Input: data/unified.parquet.

Primary deliverable: exploration/tables/T07/feasibility.csv — drives downstream wave-planning decisions about which comparisons are trustworthy.

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. At the aggregate and mid-senior level, arshkon-vs-scraped is well-powered. All-SWE (n=4,691 vs 40,881) and mid-senior (n=2,064 vs 17,418) can detect binary effects as small as 2.2 pp and 3.3 pp respectively at 80% power / α=0.05 worst-case (p=0.5). Continuous MDEs: d=0.04 and d=0.07.
  2. Entry-level arshkon-vs-scraped is underpowered. Only 175 arshkon entry SWE postings. Worst-case binary MDE is 11.3 pp. Even the key RQ1 metric — entry share over time — cannot be compared to arshkon at fine resolution without pooling asaniczka or relying on the YOE proxy.
  3. Metro-level comparison is feasible for 18 metros at ≥50 per period and 8 metros at ≥100 per period. 17,483 SWE rows (27% of the LinkedIn filtered pool) have metro_area = NULL; of those, 4,100 are is_multi_location = true and structurally excluded. The 2024 metro counts (arshkon-only) are the binding constraint, not scraped.
  4. Company overlap panel (T16): 240 companies with ≥3 SWE in both arshkon and scraped (this is the panel size at that threshold; it drops to 125 at ≥5 and 45 at ≥10).
  5. BLS geographic validation: Pearson r = 0.97 between BLS OES state employment for SOC 15-1252 and our posting counts (both arshkon and scraped). Well above the 0.80 target. Our sample frame is not geographically biased relative to the official labor statistics benchmark.
  6. JOLTS Information sector openings declined 29% between our 2024 window (avg 136k) and our late-2025/early-2026 window (avg 97k). Our two sampling periods straddle a real cooling in information-sector demand — temporal effects detected in the data are not happening in isolation.

docs.title: Part A — Feasibility / power analysis (primary output)
Filtering applied throughout
docs.text: Default filter: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND is_swe=true. Seniority column: seniority_final.

docs.title: Group sizes
docs.text:
source entry associate mid-senior director unknown ALL kaggle_arshkon (2024-04) 175 15 2,064 13 2,424 4,691 kaggle_asaniczka (2024-01) 204 24 11,426 92 6,383 18,129 scraped (2026-03/04) 1,275 52 17,418 295 21,841 40,881

Surprises in the raw counts: - Arshkon has only 175 entry-labeled SWE postings. This is the binding constraint on all cross-period entry-level analysis against arshkon. The issue isn't absent data; it's that entry is a small slice of the SWE sample in any snapshot. - Scraped has 53% seniority_final = 'unknown' for SWE (21,841 / 40,881). This is because many rows were not routed to the Stage 10 LLM, so Stage 5 strong-rule labels are the only source of seniority. Any analysis that drops unknown loses half the scraped SWE sample. - Asaniczka has 11,426 mid-senior SWE — 2.5x arshkon's mid-senior pool. Pooling asaniczka under seniority_final boosts mid-senior power significantly but introduces instrument heterogeneity.

docs.title: Feasibility table
docs.text:
Power parameters: two-sided α=0.05, power=0.80, two-sample z test for binary (variance under p=0.5 worst case; p=0.2 also reported for realistic entry-share magnitudes), two-sample t test in standardized units (Cohen's d) for continuous.

analysis comparison n1 n2 MDE bin (p=0.5) MDE bin (p=0.2) MDE d verdict seniority-stratified entry: arshkon vs scraped 175 1,275 0.113 0.090 0.226 underpowered seniority-stratified senior (mid-senior): arshkon vs scraped 2,064 17,418 0.033 0.026 0.065 well-powered all-SWE all SWE: arshkon vs scraped 4,691 40,881 0.022 0.017 0.043 well-powered all-SWE pooled 2024 vs scraped 22,820 40,881 0.012 0.009 0.023 well-powered seniority-stratified entry: pooled 2024 vs scraped 379 1,275 0.082 0.066 0.164 marginal seniority-stratified mid-senior: pooled 2024 vs scraped 13,490 17,418 0.016 0.013 0.032 well-powered seniority-stratified (3-level) junior (entry+assoc): arshkon vs scraped 190 1,327 0.109 0.087 0.217 underpowered metro-level smallest qualifying metro (≥50, n=18) 51 51 0.277 0.222 0.555 underpowered per-metro metro-level smallest qualifying metro (≥100, n=8) 121 121 0.180 0.144 0.360 underpowered per-metro company panel overlap panel (≥3 both, N=240) 240 240 0.128 0.102 0.256 underpowered as binary

Verdict rules (worst-case p=0.5): - well-powered = MDE_binary ≤ 5 pp AND both cells ≥ 500 - marginal = MDE_binary ≤ 10 pp AND both cells ≥ 100 - underpowered otherwise

Full CSV: exploration/tables/T07/feasibility.csv

docs.title: Metro-level feasibility detail
docs.text:
73 distinct metros have ≥1 SWE in at least one of (arshkon, scraped).

  • 18 metros qualify at ≥50 per period (both arshkon AND scraped): Atlanta, Austin, Boston, Charlotte, Chicago, Dallas-Fort Worth, Denver, Detroit, Houston, Los Angeles, Minneapolis-Saint Paul, Philadelphia, Phoenix, Raleigh-Durham-Chapel Hill, San Diego, San Francisco Bay Area, Seattle, Washington DC.
  • 8 metros qualify at ≥100 per period: Atlanta, Boston, Chicago, Dallas-Fort Worth, Los Angeles, San Francisco Bay Area, Seattle, Washington DC.

Excluded from all per-metro analysis: - 17,483 rows have metro_area IS NULL (27.4% of SWE LinkedIn sample). - Of those, 4,100 rows are is_multi_location = true and structurally excluded — the posting covers multiple metros so no single assignment exists. - The remaining ~13,383 null-metro rows are non-metro geographies or unresolved locations.

The binding constraint is arshkon, not scraped. Even the smallest qualifying metro (Raleigh-Durham, 51 arshkon rows vs 558 scraped) has plenty of scraped coverage. The 2024 baseline is what limits metro-level feasibility. Aggregating multiple 2024 sources into a pooled baseline would likely double this to ~36 metros at ≥50; recommend T16 consider this.

Per-metro detail: exploration/tables/T07/metro_feasibility.csv

Important caveat on per-metro binary MDE. The worst-case per-metro binary MDE (28 pp) reflects the binding smallest metro. It does not mean metro-level analysis is useless — it means any single metro comparison is underpowered for subtle effects. A mixed-effects or fixed-effects model that pools across the 18 metros and exploits within-metro variation is the right approach and is well-powered in aggregate (n = 2,116 arshkon + 24,850 scraped across the 18). Treat metro as a covariate, not as a stratification variable.

docs.title: Company overlap panel (T16)
docs.text:
Companies with ≥k SWE postings in BOTH arshkon AND scraped:

k N companies 1 1,004 2 395 3 240 5 125 10 45

The ≥3 threshold (N=240) is the default panel for T16. At this threshold, each company contributes ≥6 postings total (3 per period); Wave 2 should cap at ≥5 per side (N=125) if T16 needs within-company longitudinal fits that require ≥10 observations per unit.

Full table: exploration/tables/T07/company_panel.csv

docs.title: BLS OES state-level validation (SOC 15-1252 Software Developers)
docs.text:
Data source: BLS Public API v1 for series ID pattern OEUS{state_fips}00000000000151252 01 (state × cross-industry × occupation × employment, annual May 2024 release). 51 states/DC requested, 50 returned valid employment values (Wyoming came back null-value — small-sample suppression). Total across all states: 1,650,360, which matches the national total (1,654,440) within rounding (0.2%).

BLS national aggregate downloads (oesm24st.zip) were blocked by Akamai (403 Forbidden from curl/WebFetch); the Public API v1 endpoint was reachable with HTTP/1.1 and returned data without authentication. Industry-specific BLS OES national series (SOC × NAICS) were not reachable via the v1 API (all returned "no data") — this is a known limitation of the API v1 endpoint, which primarily exposes the all-industries cross-sections.

SOC 15-1256 (Software QA / testers) was not retrieved. The second request chunk silently failed and was not retried; this could be fixed by splitting the request further, but it's not critical for the primary validation since 15-1252 alone covers the bulk of SWE employment.

Correlations (BLS OES 15-1252 state employment vs our SWE posting counts):

our source Pearson r (raw) Pearson r (log-log) Spearman ρ n arshkon 0.9718 0.9447 0.9278 47 scraped 0.9740 0.7738 0.8746 31 arshkon + scraped pooled 0.9765 0.8843 0.9065 47

Both sources comfortably exceed the r > 0.80 target. Arshkon's log-log correlation is higher than scraped's (0.94 vs 0.77) — scraped over-represents the largest states (CA, TX, NY, WA) relative to BLS, consistent with LinkedIn search prioritizing high-volume metros. Scraped only has 31 states with nonzero SWE counts (vs 47 for arshkon), reflecting the scraped window's LinkedIn search strategy focusing on the 26-metro study frame rather than covering all states.

Scatter plot: exploration/figures/T07/bls_vs_ours_state.png

Data: exploration/tables/T07/bls_state_oes.csv, exploration/tables/T07/bls_vs_ours_state.csv

docs.title: Industry distribution comparison (BLS OES vs ours)
docs.text:
Not completed for BLS. The industry-specific OES series (SOC × NAICS) requires the oesm24in.zip bulk download, which is 403-blocked in this environment, and BLS API v1 does not expose these series by seriesID.

However, our own industry distributions are computable directly from company_industry. For SWE LinkedIn postings:

rank arshkon industry (n) scraped industry (n) 1 IT Services and IT Consulting (1,202) Software Development (10,618) 2 Software Development (611) IT Services and IT Consulting (5,108) 3 Financial Services (391) Financial Services (2,929) 4 Staffing and Recruiting (271) Technology, Information and Internet (2,000) 5 Information Services (150) Defense and Space Manufacturing (1,593)

Observation worth flagging: The #1 industry flips between arshkon and scraped. In arshkon, "IT Services and IT Consulting" leads (big staffing / services firms); in scraped, "Software Development" leads (product companies). This is almost certainly differential company composition, not a real industry shift — LinkedIn classifies companies onto these labels with some randomness (note both sources have IT Services and Software Development in top 2). Any RQ1 finding that splits by industry should not use these labels without canonicalization.

docs.title: JOLTS Information sector context (FRED JTU5100JOL)
docs.text:
Monthly job openings level for the Information sector, not seasonally adjusted. 303 monthly observations from 2000-12 to 2026-02. Last observation (2026-02) is one month before the start of the scraped window, so 2026-02 is the closest comparable point.

window avg openings (thousands) 2021–2022 (post-pandemic peak) 201 2023 (2024 proximate) 137 2024-01 to 2024-05 (our 2024 windows) 136 2025 average 121 2026-01 to 2026-03 (close to scraped window) 97

Information sector openings are ~29% lower in early 2026 than in early 2024. Our two snapshots straddle a real cooling in information-sector hiring demand, not a steady-state. Any "entry-level share dropped" finding should be framed against the backdrop of absolute hiring declining across seniority levels.

JOLTS Information is a proxy, not a direct SWE measure — the Information sector covers publishing, broadcasting, telecom, and data services, not the many SWE jobs in finance, defense, retail, etc. Directionally useful, not a tight benchmark.

Figure: exploration/figures/T07/jolts_info_context.png Data: exploration/tables/T07/jolts_info_openings.csv

docs.title: Sample frame and generalizability
docs.text:
What population does our sample represent?

  • Language / platform: English-language LinkedIn SWE postings. Indeed is excluded from the primary frame (sensitivity only).
  • Geography: US-weighted but with a top-metro tilt, especially in scraped. Correlates at r=0.97 with BLS OES state employment. Arshkon covers 47 states, scraped covers 31 states (bounded by the 26-metro search frame plus spillover).
  • Occupation: is_swe = true — primarily software developers (SOC 15-1252 analog). A Stage 5 rule tier plus Stage 10 LLM classification is the operational definition. Title-lookup LLM has an elevated false-positive rate; sensitivity (g) addresses this.
  • Time: Two snapshots of a flow, not a stock. arshkon = 2024-04, asaniczka = 2024-01, scraped = 2026-03 / 2026-04. The ~24 month gap is real, but our scraped window is a growing current window that will keep expanding. Each rerun gets a different scraped sample.
  • Company set: Dominated by employers who posted on LinkedIn during the scraped windows. Company concentration matters — see T06 for that analysis.

Where we can generalize: - Aggregate posting-level statistics (AI prevalence, tech stack, management language) for LinkedIn SWE jobs in top US metros — comfortably. - Mid-senior comparisons between 2024 and 2026 — comfortably. - State-level counts — comfortably (r=0.97 with BLS).

Where we CANNOT generalize: - Entry-level market-wide trends: our entry sample in 2024 is too small (175 arshkon) to produce a confident point estimate; the YOE-based proxy is the primary validator for this RQ per the core preamble. - Non-LinkedIn hiring channels (referrals, internal moves, direct applications). - Job-seeker behavior or outcomes (postings ≠ hires). - Rural / small-state labor markets — scraped has almost no coverage outside the 26-metro frame. - Early-career roles specifically at small companies — we have limited company_size coverage outside arshkon.

docs.title: Recommendations for Wave 2/3
docs.text:
DO NOT ATTEMPT (underpowered): 1. Per-metro entry-share comparison (entry × metro × period) without multi-level pooling. The smallest metro cell would be O(5-10 entry rows), which supports only enormous effect sizes. Must be aggregated or modeled. 2. Arshkon-only entry share change as the primary RQ1 estimate without pairing it to the YOE-based proxy and to the pooled-2024 estimate. 3. Fine-grained (director / associate) seniority comparisons — sample sizes in the low hundreds across sources. 4. Single-metro deep dives below San Francisco / Seattle / Washington DC / Boston / NY tier.

DO ATTEMPT (well-powered or supported): 1. All-SWE and mid-senior cross-period binary and continuous comparisons (AI keyword rate, tech stack, description length, management language) — arshkon vs scraped is fine; pooled 2024 vs scraped is even better. 2. Metro × period modeled analyses (mixed-effects with metro as random effect, or fixed-effects with metro dummies). The 18-metro panel has ~27k observations and is well-powered in aggregate. 3. Company-level panel of 240 companies for T16 (within-company change in metrics). For company-level binary outcomes with N=240, effect sizes below 13 pp are underpowered; treat as exploratory.

Flag for sensitivity reporting: Entry-share findings must always report both seniority_final and the YOE-based proxy (yoe_extracted ≤ 2), plus the pooled-2024 baseline, per the core preamble and per the power table above.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Scraped seniority_final = 'unknown' rate is 53%, much higher than arshkon's 52%. I expected scraped to be much better labeled given Stage 10 LLM availability, but most scraped rows were not routed to the LLM under budget constraints. This means the "known-seniority sample" for scraped is 19k, not 41k — cut in half.
  2. Arshkon entry-level SWE is only 175 rows. I expected ~500 based on the dataset-size headline. Raw arshkon has entry-level postings across all job types; restricting to SWE + LinkedIn + English + date_flag_ok cuts the pool by ~95%. This is the single biggest feasibility constraint in the whole project.
  3. Pearson r = 0.97 between BLS state employment and our posting counts. I expected ~0.85 based on prior research on scraped labor data. The very high agreement suggests the LinkedIn SWE posting density is a near-perfect proxy for state-level employment magnitude (though not for rate or flow).
  4. JOLTS Information openings are at 97k in early 2026 — below the 2023 trough. The sector-level hiring picture is worse than I had assumed going into this. This is context that every cross-period RQ1/RQ2 finding needs to carry.
  5. The company overlap panel is 240 companies at ≥3 in both sources. Larger than I expected — the arshkon sample of ~4,700 SWE rows and scraped sample of ~41k have a meaningful set of recurring employers.
  6. is_multi_location = true affects only 4,100 rows (6.4% of SWE). Smaller than I'd expected given how prevalent "multiple locations" postings are on LinkedIn. The preprocessing must be classifying most multi-location postings into a specific metro when the primary location is unambiguous.
  7. metro_area = NULL but is_multi_location = false for ~13,383 rows. That's 80% of the null-metro rows — these are postings outside the 26-metro study frame or unresolved locations, not multi-location cases.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. BLS bulk downloads failed (Akamai 403) — we relied on the Public API for state employment and could not validate against the industry-level OES files. Geographic correlation is solid; industry comparison against OES is not available.
  2. SOC 15-1256 (QA/testers) BLS data not retrieved — the second API request chunk failed silently. Adds ~100k to the total employment baseline; does not change the state-level correlation conclusion.
  3. JOLTS Information sector is a proxy for SWE demand, not a direct measure. The sector has ~3M total employment; SWE are a minority of Information sector jobs, and many SWE jobs are outside Information.
  4. State-level coverage gap for scraped: 31 states have nonzero scraped SWE. The 20 states with zero scraped coverage are small states (WY, SD, ND, AK, etc.) where LinkedIn scraping is not focused. BLS has employment values for all of them. This suppresses Spearman ρ for scraped (0.87 vs 0.93 for arshkon).
  5. Arshkon state_normalized coverage is high (~98%), but arshkon entry SWE is only 175 rows — even complete coverage doesn't fix a small denominator.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. T08 (distribution profiling): Must compute entry share using both seniority_final and the YOE proxy (yoe_extracted ≤ 2) for every period. Report both side by side. Disagreement is itself a finding.
  2. T16 (company panel): Use the N=240 panel at the ≥3 threshold. Consider stratifying by company size at the ≥5 threshold (N=125) for higher-confidence subsample.
  3. Metro-level tasks: Always use the 18-metro frame (≥50 per period). Do not attempt single-metro deep dives below the top 8. Use mixed-effects or fixed-effects with metro as a covariate.
  4. Report format: Any cross-period entry-level finding needs the pooled-2024 comparison too (since arshkon-only entry is underpowered). Both should agree in direction or the disagreement should be investigated.
  5. JOLTS framing: When reporting any "declining X" cross-period finding, note that the information-sector labor market tightened ~29% over the same interval. Declining entry share in a cooling labor market is different from declining entry share in a hot labor market.
  6. BLS industry-level validation: If industry-level benchmarking is needed, download oesm24in.zip manually from outside this environment and store it under data/external/bls/ for future runs.

docs.text:
