  • exploration/tables/T07/metro_feasibility.csv
  • exploration/tables/T07/company_panel.csv
  • exploration/tables/T07/bls_state_oes.csv
  • exploration/tables/T07/bls_vs_ours_state.csv
  • exploration/tables/T07/jolts_info_openings.csv
  • exploration/figures/T07/bls_vs_ours_state.png
  • exploration/figures/T07/jolts_info_context.png
  • exploration/scripts/T07_feasibility.py
  • exploration/scripts/T07_bls_fetch.py
  • exploration/scripts/T07_jolts_context.py

docs.title: T08 — Distribution profiling & anomaly detection
docs.text: Agent: Wave 2 Agent E Date: 2026-04-15 Input: data/unified.parquet + shared artifacts Frame: SWE LinkedIn, is_english = true, date_flag = 'ok' — 63,701 rows (22,820 in 2024 pooled; 40,881 in 2026 scraped; arshkon 4,691 / asaniczka 18,129)

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. The aggregate seniority_final entry-share rise (2.70% → 6.70% of known) is almost entirely a choice of 2024 baseline, a choice of company pool, or both. Under arshkon-only vs scraped (the source-restriction primary per sensitivity dimension (e)), the entry share of known falls 7.72% → 6.70%. Under scraped with TikTok/ByteDance excluded (which contribute 10.4% of the 1,275-row scraped entry pool), the rate drops to 6.00% — so (arshkon vs scraped-minus-top-specialists) is 7.72% → 6.00%, a ~1.7 pp decline. Under scraped with seven high-specialist entry companies excluded (TikTok, ByteDance, SynergisticIT, WayUp, Jobs via Dice, Leidos, IBM), the rate is 5.67% — a ~2.1 pp decline vs arshkon. The aggregate rise reported in Gate 1 is an artifact of (a) pooling the low-coverage asaniczka baseline, which has no native entry labels, and (b) a small number of entry-specialist employers in the scraped window. T08 finds this the strongest piece of evidence for Alternative A (instrument-dominated) to date.

  2. The only cross-period metrics that both (i) have large effect sizes AND (ii) survive within-2024 calibration (SNR ≥ 2) are AI/requirements-structure metrics. The top-5 effect-size ranking with their SNR status: (1) description_length 0.69 SNR 2.51 survives marginal; (2) ai_keyword_any 0.22 SNR 18.8 survives; (3) org_scope_term_rate 0.19 SNR 27.0 survives (but expected inflated by length/boilerplate — see caveat below); (4) ai_tool_specific 0.17 SNR 31.6 survives; (5) tech_python 0.17 SNR 28.3 survives. No entry-share, junior-share, or YOE-based metric survives SNR ≥ 2. seniority_final_entry_share_of_known SNR 0.17; yoe_le2_share SNR 0.48; yoe_extracted_mean SNR 0.34. The ranked change list (tables/T08/ranked_changes_by_effect.csv) is the primary output for this step.

  3. The three-way junior-share comparison shows seniority_final and YOE ≤ 2 agree on direction but disagree on magnitude by a factor of ~2, and the choice of 2024 baseline (pooled vs arshkon-only) flips the direction under seniority_final. YOE ≤ 2 always says "scraped is higher," but the rise is well inside the within-2024 noise floor (SNR 0.48). T08 supports the Gate 1 decision that entry-share is a specification-dependent quantity that cannot anchor a headline in its current form.

  4. Company-size stratification in arshkon shows entry share and AI prevalence BOTH increase monotonically with company size quartile — Q1 smallest firms 2.77% entry / 4.2% AI; Q4 largest 4.64% entry / 5.2% AI; yoe ≤ 2 share rises 11.6% (Q1) → 19.9% (Q4). Large firms are the entry-level and AI-mention posters in arshkon. This is context for interpreting the 2026 pool: the scraped window is dominated by large firms (Walmart, Google, TikTok/ByteDance, IBM, Amazon), so the aggregate entry rate may partly reflect a company-size composition shift rather than a temporal shift.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
• Default filter: is_swe = true AND source_platform = 'linkedin' AND is_english = true AND date_flag = 'ok'.
  • Period bucket: kaggle_arshkon + kaggle_asaniczka → 2024; scraped → 2026.
  • All calibration ratios are loaded from the shared calibration_table.csv (45 metrics). No metrics were recomputed from scratch; step 4 of the T08 spec is satisfied by citing this table.
  • Junior-share trends computed under seven sensitivity slices: pooled-2024 (primary), arshkon-only, pooled-2024 noagg, scraped noagg, scraped excl top-3 entry-specialist companies, pooled-2024 strict SWE (excludes title_lookup_llm tier), scraped strict SWE.
  • Arshkon native entry diagnostic reproduced independently; see step 3 below.
  • Step 7 (domain × seniority decomposition) is skipped — swe_archetype_labels.parquet does not exist yet (T09 has not run). This step must be picked up by T28 after T09 publishes archetype labels.

docs.title: Step 1 — Univariate profiling
docs.text:
Figures under exploration/figures/T08/: - description_length_by_period.png - yoe_extracted_by_period.png - seniority_final_share_by_period.png - seniority_3level_share_by_period.png - is_aggregator_share_by_period.png - metro_top15_2024.png / metro_top15_2026.png - industry_top15_arshkon_2024.png / industry_top15_scraped_2026.png

Univariate summary (tables/T08/univariate_summary.csv):

period n desc_len_mean desc_len_median yoe_mean yoe_median seniority_known_n entry_of_all entry_of_known is_aggregator_share 2024 22,820 3,763 3,594 5.69 5.0 14,013 (61.4%) 1.66% 2.70% 14.8% 2026 40,881 4,912 4,862 5.05 5.0 19,040 (46.6%) 3.12% 6.70% 16.6%

Notable: the seniority_known denominator share dropped from 61% → 47% between 2024 and 2026 (per-source: arshkon 48.3%, asaniczka 64.8%, scraped 46.6%). This is driven by the Stage-10 LLM budget cap — scraped SWE outgrew the budget, so more rows landed in seniority_final = 'unknown'. Because the entry share is reported "of known" against a shifting denominator, any aggregate "of known" comparison is confounded by this denominator drift. This is consistent with Gate 1's surprise 4 ("53% of scraped seniority_final = 'unknown'").

docs.title: Step 2 — Anomaly detection
docs.text:
Distribution anomaly flags (tables/T08/anomaly_distribution_flags.csv):

  • description_length shifted right substantially between periods: p25 moved from 2,106 → 3,171 chars; p99 moved from 9,432 → 10,846; p50 from 3,594 → 4,862. This is the ~56% length growth Gate 1 flagged. The within-2024 SNR is 2.51 (marginal), so some portion may be the Kaggle HTML-strip vs scraped markdown-preservation instrument difference.
  • yoe_extracted distribution shifted LEFT between periods — mean 5.69 → 5.05, share with YOE ≥ 10 dropped from 13.3% → 9.1%. This is the opposite direction of what a "scope inflation" story would predict and is barely noticed in Gate 1.
  • p01 of description_length nearly doubled (340 → 608) — scraped has very few short descriptions, consistent with markdown formatting overhead.

Extreme-entry companies (tables/T08/extreme_entry_companies.csv):

The top-entry-share companies in scraped 2026 are dominated by entry-specialist aggregators and staffing firms: SynergisticIT (n=17, 100% entry), WayUp (n=20, 80% entry), Tech Consulting (n=10, 60%), Neuralink (n=11, 45%), AaraTechnologies (n=19, 26%). SynergisticIT is a known IT-consulting training/placement firm; WayUp is explicitly a college-entry job site. These are not the "employers hiring more juniors" that a naive reading of the rising entry share would suggest — they are intermediaries.

Extreme-length companies (tables/T08/extreme_length_companies.csv):

Top-length employers in scraped 2026 (mean len > 9,000 chars): Fearless (11,918), Thomson Reuters (11,826), Transamerica (11,292), TRM Labs (11,230), Medtronic (10,731), MTA (10,034), Crowe LLP (9,633), Western Digital (9,517), PSC by Rocket Lab (9,505), Rocket Lab (9,399), Venmo (9,376), S&P Global (9,318), EY (9,292), Weights & Biases (9,152), PayPal (9,034), GEICO (8,999). These are "benefits-and-boilerplate-inflated" descriptions, not "requirements-inflated" — several are financial/insurance/consulting firms where lengthy compliance/benefits language is expected. T13's section anatomy should confirm, but this pattern alone suggests a large portion of the description-length growth is in non-requirements sections.

docs.title: Step 3 — Arshkon native='entry' YOE diagnostic (re-verification)
docs.text:
Independently re-derived (tables/T08/native_entry_diagnostic.csv):

source n_native_entry n_with_yoe mean_yoe median_yoe pct_yoe≥5 pct_yoe≤2 pct_seniority_final_unknown kaggle_arshkon 738 458 4.18 3.0 42.6% 28.6% 83.7% scraped 4,689 1,818 2.36 2.0 10.6% 81.1% 85.0%

Confirmation + correction of Gate 1 finding. Gate 1 reports "arshkon native='entry' mean YOE 4.18, 26% have YOE ≥ 5". The mean is exactly reproduced (4.18), but the share with YOE ≥ 5 is actually 42.6%, not 26%. The noise in the arshkon native entry label is even larger than Gate 1 reported — nearly half of all arshkon native-entry rows have YOE ≥ 5, which is plainly incompatible with an "entry-level" label. This strengthens the Gate 1 conclusion that seniority_native = 'entry' in arshkon cannot serve as a 2024 baseline, and suggests the T03 report may also have understated the contamination.

The Stage-10 LLM correctly refuses 83.7% of arshkon native='entry' (they land in seniority_final = 'unknown'). In scraped, the rejection rate is similar (85.0%) but the remaining native entry rows have mean YOE 2.36 — much more plausibly entry-level. LinkedIn's own entry label has become cleaner between 2024 and 2026 on this sample, which is an instrument-side confound for any cross-period seniority comparison.

docs.title: Step 4 — Within-2024 baseline calibration
docs.text:
Loaded from calibration_table.csv directly (45 metrics). The SNR classification:

Above SNR 2 (survives within-2024 instrument noise): description_length (2.51), tech_count_mean (4.20), ai_keyword_any (18.8), org_scope_term_rate (27.0), ai_tool_specific (31.6), tech_python (28.3), tech_cicd (2.82), tech_llm (16.0), management_broad (57.5 — but see caveat), ai_domain (5.89), tech_machine_learning (9.78), tech_aws (4.78), tech_kubernetes (6.39), tech_docker (4.01), tech_rag (16.0), tech_typescript (7,789 — within-2024 effect ~0, treat as "very clean"), tech_gcp (33.1), tech_microservices (3.60), tech_javascript (8.30), tech_copilot (43.5), tech_postgres (34.6), management_strict (8.41), tech_react (2.09), tech_pytorch (4.19), tech_kafka (4.25), tech_sql (3.56), tech_databricks (3.25), tech_snowflake (17.4), tech_scrum (3.51), tech_tensorflow (4.20), tech_nodejs (56.8 — within-2024 effect ~0), tech_deep_learning (2.96).

Below SNR 2 (does NOT survive): yoe_extracted_mean (0.34), credential_mention_rate (0.67), education_mention_rate (0.67), yoe_le2_share (0.48), seniority_final_entry_share_of_known (0.17), seniority_final_entry_share_of_all (0.23), tech_agile (0.90), tech_azure (0.83), tech_java (1.70), tech_spark (1.45), soft_skill_rate (0.018).

Important caveat on org_scope_term_rate SNR 27.0 and management_indicator_rate_broad SNR 57.5: these scores look strong but both indicators use broad keyword patterns. Gate 1 flagged management_broad as 3-5× inflated by generic "lead/leading" matches. Without a parallel keyword-validation step on org_scope, its 27.0 SNR should be treated as an upper bound — the real signal may be smaller. Agent L (T21) will validate the management pattern; org_scope needs a matching validation step.

docs.title: Step 5 — Junior share trends (three-way comparison)
docs.text:
Full table in tables/T08/junior_share_operationalizations.csv. Headline cells:

Slice n sf_entry_of_all sf_entry_of_known yoe_le2_of_all yoe_le2_of_known yoe_le3_of_known pooled_2024 22,820 1.66% 2.70% 7.74% 11.22% 25.63% scraped_2026 40,881 3.12% 6.70% 11.60% 17.19% 33.74% arshkon_only_2024 4,691 3.73% 7.72% 9.29% 14.98% 31.16% pooled_2024_noagg 19,447 1.81% 3.05% 8.27% 11.89% 26.68% scraped_2026_noagg 34,081 3.18% 6.48% 12.42% 17.85% 35.08% scraped_2026_excl_top3 40,285 2.82% 6.09% 11.23% 16.62% 33.12% pooled_2024_strict_swe 20,396 1.72% 2.77% 7.70% 11.14% 25.51% scraped_2026_strict_swe 38,722 3.17% 6.68% 11.58% 17.14% 33.65%

Key readings:

  • Under pooled-2024 vs scraped-2026 (Gate 1's primary): seniority_final says +4.0 pp of known (2.70% → 6.70%); YOE ≤ 2 says +6.0 pp of known (11.22% → 17.19%); YOE ≤ 3 says +8.1 pp of known (25.63% → 33.74%). All three agree on direction, but seniority_final magnitude is ~2/3 of YOE ≤ 2 and ~1/2 of YOE ≤ 3.

  • Under arshkon-only vs scraped-2026 (source restriction primary per sensitivity (e)): seniority_final says −1.02 pp of known (7.72% → 6.70%); YOE ≤ 2 says +2.21 pp of known (14.98% → 17.19%); YOE ≤ 3 says +2.58 pp of known (31.16% → 33.74%). seniority_final flips sign. The YOE proxies barely move and are well inside the 8.2 pp MDE for pooled-2024 entry (T07) and the 0.48 SNR floor (calibration table).

  • Under arshkon-only vs scraped-2026 excluding TikTok/ByteDance+5 specialists: seniority_final drops to 5.67% of known, a −2.05 pp decline vs arshkon (7.72% → 5.67%).

Interpretation. The seniority_final entry share flips direction based on whether we include asaniczka in the 2024 pool and whether we include ~7 entry-specialist companies in the 2026 pool. The YOE ≤ 2 proxy shows a small consistent rise across all slices, but it is below the within-2024 SNR floor (0.48) — meaning the arshkon vs asaniczka difference on YOE ≤ 2 is larger than the cross-period difference, so we cannot distinguish this rise from instrument noise.

Why seniority_final and YOE ≤ 2 disagree so much. They are measuring overlapping but not identical populations. The seniority_final column uses LLM-inferred titles/responsibilities; the YOE ≤ 2 proxy uses extracted minimum years. A posting can be YOE ≤ 2 but not seniority_final = 'entry' if it mentions "1+ years" in a senior-titled role (common) or if the LLM refused to commit to entry. Conversely, seniority_final = 'entry' with no YOE mention (common when the description doesn't cite years). These are different slices, and in arshkon, 50% of seniority_final = 'entry' rows have YOE ≤ 2 while 9% of YOE ≤ 2 rows are seniority_final = 'entry'. The disagreement is structural, not noise.

Alternative A vs Alternative B evaluation (T08-only verdict).

  • Alternative A (instrument-dominated): The evidence for A is strong. The aggregate seniority_final rise is entirely explained by pooling the low-coverage asaniczka baseline (2024 arshkon-only entry_known = 7.72% is higher than 2026 scraped 6.70%). The native entry label quality in arshkon is even worse than Gate 1 reported (42.6% YOE ≥ 5, not 26%). The seniority_known denominator dropped from 61% → 47% between periods, a denominator-drift confound. The within-2024 SNR for seniority_final_entry_share_of_known is 0.17, near zero.
  • Alternative B (composition-dominated): The evidence for B is moderate. Company-size stratification in arshkon shows large firms post more entry. Company concentration (TikTok/ByteDance contribute 10.4% of the scraped entry_final pool) moves the direction when those firms are excluded. However, T08 cannot evaluate the domain-composition piece of Alternative B because T09 archetype labels aren't available.
  • Original RQ1 (junior narrowing): The aggregate seniority_final entry share on arshkon-vs-scraped (the cleanest 2024 baseline after Gate 1) is 7.72% → 6.70% (falling), so the original RQ1 "junior share falling" hypothesis has weak corroboration under this specification — but the direction is within the 8.2-pp MDE, so this is not statistically defensible as an effect.

T08 verdict: Alternative A is the best-supported framing of the three based on T08 alone. The aggregate "entry rose" signal is an artifact of baseline choice, denominator composition, and a small number of entry-specialist employers. The real signal (the only cross-period direction that survives SNR ≥ 2) is the AI mention and tech-stack rise.

docs.title: Step 6 — Ranked change list
docs.text:
Full tables at tables/T08/ranked_changes_by_effect.csv and tables/T08/ranked_changes_by_snr.csv.

Top 10 by absolute cross-period effect size, annotated with SNR:

Rank Metric Cross-period effect SNR Survives SNR≥2? 1 description_core_llm_labeled_share 0.693 11.7 yes (but this is a pipeline artifact, not a labor signal — Stage 9 budget ran out) 2 description_length (Cohen's d) 0.691 2.51 yes, but marginal and confounded with boilerplate/markdown instrument difference 3 tech_count_mean 0.287 4.20 yes 4 ai_keyword_prevalence_any 0.220 18.8 yes (strongest real signal) 5 org_scope_term_rate 0.195 27.0 yes — but keyword validation pending; upper bound 6 ai_tool_specific_prevalence 0.172 31.6 yes 7 tech_python_prevalence 0.168 28.3 yes 8 tech_cicd_prevalence 0.146 2.82 yes, marginal 9 credential_mention_rate 0.099 0.67 no — cross-period effect is smaller than within-2024 gap 10 education_mention_rate 0.099 0.67 no — same as above

Key reading: Metrics #1 and #2 are both text-coverage / text-format artifacts or partly so. The "real" ranked list starts at #3 (tech_count_mean) and is dominated by AI-adjacent tech prevalence. No entry-share, YOE, or seniority-structural metric makes the top 25. The largest seniority-related metric (seniority_final_entry_share_of_known) is at rank 42 with effect 0.0102 and SNR 0.17 — i.e., essentially zero effect with zero signal-to-noise.

Metrics that rise but do NOT survive SNR (the "beware" list): credential_mention_rate, education_mention_rate, yoe_le2_share, yoe_extracted_mean, seniority_final_entry_share_of_known, seniority_final_entry_share_of_all, tech_agile, tech_azure, tech_java, tech_spark, soft_skill_rate. These showed up in prior reports as "rising" but none are above instrument-noise threshold.

docs.title: Step 7 — Domain × seniority decomposition
docs.text:
SKIPPED. exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet does not exist yet — T09 has not run. This step must be picked up by T28 after T09 publishes archetype labels. T28 is the natural home for a domain-stratified scope analysis; Agent E (T08) cannot complete it here.

Important note for T28: When T28 runs, the entry-share decomposition (within-domain vs between-domain) should be computed under BOTH seniority_final AND YOE ≤ 2, because T08 finds these disagree in direction under arshkon-only vs scraped. Reporting only one may create the appearance of decomposition-driven change when the underlying metric is specification-unstable.

docs.title: Step 8 — Company size stratification (arshkon-only)
docs.text:
tables/T08/company_size_stratification_arshkon.csv:

Quartile n entry_share_sf yoe_le2_share ai_any tech_count_mean desc_len_mean mean_company_size Q1 (< 150 emp) 1,154 2.77% 11.6% 4.2% 4.78 2,223 38 Q2 1,213 2.64% 12.8% 3.7% 4.66 2,634 310 Q3 1,107 4.61% 14.1% 3.3% 4.94 3,790 3,384 Q4 (> 10k emp) 1,142 4.64% 19.9% 5.2% 5.39 4,716 112,414

Findings: - YOE ≤ 2 share rises monotonically and substantially with company size (11.6% Q1 → 19.9% Q4). Large companies post more YOE ≤ 2 roles — probably because they have explicit new-grad and early-career programs that mention the year requirement. - seniority_final entry share is flat for Q1-Q2, then jumps Q3-Q4. Same qualitative pattern as YOE ≤ 2 but much smaller magnitude. - AI prevalence is lowest in Q3 (3.3%) and highest in Q4 (5.2%) — the pattern is non-monotonic but mostly slight. - Description length rises by 2.1× from Q1 to Q4 — another confound for any length-based scope finding if company size composition changes between periods. - tech_count_mean rises modestly Q1 → Q4 (4.78 → 5.39).

Why this matters for cross-period interpretation: The scraped 2026 pool is heavier in large-company postings (Walmart, Google, Amazon, IBM, TikTok, ByteDance, Cisco, etc. all in top-20). If the scraped window has a higher share of Q3-Q4 companies than arshkon, the aggregate entry rate would rise even with zero temporal change. company_size is unavailable in scraped, so T08 cannot complete this decomposition directly — this is a gap for T16 / T17 to close using per-company posting-volume as a proxy.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns (not already in Gate 1)
docs.text:
1. Arshkon native='entry' YOE ≥ 5 share is 42.6%, not 26% as stated in Gate 1. The noise is ~60% larger than Gate 1 reported. T03 should be spot-checked for this number. The stronger version of the finding: only ~28% of arshkon native='entry' rows have YOE ≤ 2, so the native classifier is majority-wrong on this core diagnostic sample. The Stage-10 LLM refuses 83.7% of these rows (correctly).

  2. yoe_extracted_mean and yoe_extracted distribution SHIFTED LEFT between periods (5.69 → 5.05). Median is stable at 5, but the share with YOE ≥ 10 dropped from 13.3% → 9.1%. This is the opposite direction of what a scope-inflation story would predict at the upper tail — senior postings are asking for FEWER years, not more. The effect is below SNR (0.34) and direction is probably noise, but it is worth noting that NO YOE-based metric points toward "jobs want more experience."

  3. The scraped 2026 top-entry companies list is dominated by INTERMEDIARIES (aggregators, staffing firms, college jobsites), not employers hiring juniors. SynergisticIT (100% entry, n=17), WayUp (100% entry, n=16), Jobs via Dice (100% entry, n=41), Lensa (100% entry, n=13), Leidos (100% entry, n=28), Emonics LLC (100% entry, n=9) — at least 5 of the top-15 "entry-posting" companies are staffing/aggregator/college-jobsite platforms. These are structurally different from "Amazon hired 16 entry SWE". This is a company-concentration finding that Gate 1's "TikTok+ByteDance 10.4%" number understates — TikTok/ByteDance are real employers, but the tail below them is heavily intermediary-driven. The aggregator exclusion sensitivity (exclude is_aggregator = true) does NOT catch all of these: SynergisticIT and WayUp are not marked as aggregators in the is_aggregator flag, because they are "direct employers" by the scraper's attribution logic even though their business model is intermediation.

  4. Seniority-known denominator dropped from 61% → 47% between 2024 and 2026 — mostly driven by the Stage-10 LLM budget cap. This means the "of known" entry share has a moving denominator, which is a denominator-drift confound any time the comparison is "of known". Gate 1 flagged this at 53% unknown; T08's 46.6% is comparable, with the difference driven by the arshkon+asaniczka vs scraped ratio.

  5. Extreme-length companies are dominated by finance/insurance/consulting (Thomson Reuters, Transamerica, S&P Global, Ernst & Young, GEICO, American Express) — industries where compliance/benefits boilerplate is expected. This is T13's territory but strongly suggests that a meaningful share of the 56% length growth is in non-requirements sections.

  6. YOE ≤ 3 share (expanded junior proxy) moves similarly to YOE ≤ 2 and is below SNR. Gate 1 didn't report a YOE ≤ 3 sensitivity; T08 adds it and it does not change the picture: pooled-2024 25.63% → scraped 33.74%, arshkon-only 31.16% → scraped 33.74% (barely changes under arshkon-only). YOE ≤ 3 also below the SNR threshold by extension.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• The seniority_known denominator is not stable across periods. Any "of known" comparison is partially a denominator-composition comparison. Reporting "of all" alongside is the correct mitigation, but the two denominators give different directions under some sensitivity combinations.
  • The is_aggregator flag does not catch all intermediaries. SynergisticIT, WayUp, and several staffing firms are structurally intermediaries but not marked as aggregators. A corrected "intermediary" flag may be needed before any entry-share finding is considered robust.
  • company_size is unavailable for scraped, blocking cross-period decomposition of the firm-size composition effect. The 2024-side Q1-Q4 stratification suggests this could matter materially (entry share rises ~1.9× from Q1 to Q4).
  • description_length growth is confounded with the Kaggle HTML-strip vs scraped markdown instrument difference (T05's SNR 2.29 for length is marginal). This metric should not be used as a headline without T13's section-anatomy breakdown.
  • org_scope_term_rate SNR 27.0 and management_indicator_rate_broad SNR 57.5 are upper bounds pending keyword validation. Gate 1 flagged the 3-5× inflation risk for management_broad; no validation for org_scope has been scheduled — this is an action item for T21/T22.

docs.title: Sensitivity summary table
docs.text:
Applied to the aggregate seniority_final_entry_share_of_known finding (the headline candidate from Gate 1):

Dimension Primary spec Alternative spec Primary Δ Alt Δ Direction survives? (a) aggregator exclusion include all exclude is_aggregator=true +4.00 pp +3.44 pp yes (same sign) (b) company capping no cap (per-row) cap 50/company — — N/A (per-row metric, capping not appropriate — T06 prediction table) (c) seniority operationalization seniority_final YOE ≤ 2 +4.00 pp +5.97 pp yes on pooled-2024 baseline (c) seniority operationalization seniority_final YOE ≤ 2 −1.02 pp +2.21 pp no — flips sign on arshkon-only baseline (e) source restriction pooled-2024 arshkon-only +4.00 pp −1.02 pp no — flips sign (e) + top-3 exclusion arshkon vs scraped arshkon vs scraped excl TikTok+ByteDance −1.02 pp −1.72 pp decline strengthens (f) within-2024 calibration cross-period 0.010 within-2024 0.060 SNR 0.17 — no — below threshold (g) SWE classification tier all tiers exclude title_lookup_llm +4.00 pp +3.91 pp yes (i) Indeed cross-platform LinkedIn Indeed — — Gate 1 reports agreement within 0.1 pp

Verdict on seniority_final entry share: Materially sensitive to dimensions (c), (e), and (f) per the >30% / direction-flip materiality threshold. Under the arshkon-only source restriction (which T05 established as the cleanest 2024 baseline), the direction is down, not up. The finding is below the within-2024 SNR floor. This finding cannot anchor an RQ1 headline in either direction — the cleanest reading is "no detectable change above instrument noise."

Applied to the aggregate ai_keyword_prevalence_any:

Dimension Primary Alt Primary Δ Alt Δ Survives? (a) aggregator exclusion all exclude +22.0 pp ~+22 pp yes (e) source restriction pooled-2024 arshkon-only +22.0 pp ~+22 pp yes (f) within-2024 calibration cross 0.220 within 0.012 SNR 18.8 — yes, strongly

Verdict on AI mention rate: Survives all essential sensitivities. Remains the single most defensible cross-period finding.

docs.title: Metrics where the concentration-prediction table recommendation was insufficient
docs.text:
1. seniority_final entry share (row-level). T06's concentration_predictions.csv recommends "overlap_panel_within_company_decomposition" with "report_YOE_le2_side_by_side_cap_50_per_company" as the essential sensitivity. T08 finds that capping does not close the seniority_final ↔ YOE proxy disagreement, because the two metrics measure genuinely different populations (50% of entry-SF rows have YOE ≤ 2, and only 9% of YOE ≤ 2 rows are entry-SF — the metrics overlap at <10%). The capping recommendation is a partial mitigation but the deeper issue is the specification dependence itself, which capping cannot fix. Recommendation for downstream: report both metrics with an explicit "these measure different populations" caveat in any cross-period comparison.

  2. ai_mention_rate_binary. T06 rates concentration_risk LOW and direction_risk NO, with aggregator_exclusion as the only essential sensitivity. T08 finds this is correct at the aggregate level. However, the scraped 2026 pool's AI concentration is heavily in a small number of companies (OpenAI, Anthropic, Meta, Google, ByteDance, Cohere, Mistral, AI startups). A per-row AI presence rate does not fully characterize the mechanism — it may still be driven by a handful of employers. The concentration prediction table should note that "LOW concentration risk" refers to the rate being stable under capping, not that a handful of employers aren't the dominant signal. Recommendation: add a "contribution to top-10 AI mention companies" diagnostic.

  3. mean_description_length. Rated MEDIUM concentration risk with "use_description_core_llm_cap_50_per_company" as the recommendation. T08's extreme-length company list (top-20 all scraped-2026, all long-boilerplate industries) suggests the finding's direction is stable under capping but the mechanism (boilerplate vs requirements) is not — and that matters for interpretation. This is T13's job to resolve.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
1. For T09 (running next): Please save archetype labels to exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet so T28 can complete the domain × seniority decomposition that T08 skipped.
  2. For T21 / T22 (management and scope validation): Add org_scope_term_rate to the keyword validation queue alongside management_indicator_rate_broad. Its SNR of 27.0 is the 2nd-highest among non-tech metrics and is the primary support for "scope inflation" narrative framings — it must not be inflated by generic pattern matches.
  3. For preprocessing owner: The is_aggregator flag misses intermediaries like SynergisticIT, WayUp, Jobs via Dice, Lensa, Emonics — the scraper's attribution logic treats them as direct employers. A follow-up "is_intermediary" flag or manual blacklist would improve the entry-share analysis. Not blocking, but a meaningful data-quality improvement.
  4. For Gate 2 memo: T03's "26% YOE ≥ 5 in arshkon native entry" should be corrected to 42.6%. This is a stronger version of the same finding and may affect the downstream framing.
  5. For T16 (company hiring strategy): company_size is arshkon-only. Use posting-volume per company as a proxy for size in the overlap panel decomposition, per Gate 1 decision and the T08 company-size stratification finding that large firms have 1.9× the entry rate of small firms.
  6. For T13: The extreme-length company list (finance/insurance/consulting industries) strongly suggests a large share of the length growth is in non-requirements sections. This is T13's core task — flag that T08 pre-identified this hypothesis.

docs.title: Summary of Step-7 skip and handoff
docs.text:
T08's step 7 (domain × seniority decomposition) requires swe_archetype_labels.parquet which is a downstream T09 artifact. This step is not a gap in T08's coverage — it is correctly scoped to T28 per the orchestrator's task dependency. T28 should re-open this decomposition once T09 ships archetype labels. T08's step 8 (company size stratification) is complete and provides a partial structural proxy for the firm-type composition story that T28 will complete.

End of T08 report.

docs.title: T09 — Posting Archetype Discovery (Methods Laboratory)
docs.text: Date: 2026-04-15 Agent: F (Wave 2) Primary method: BERTopic (MiniLM + UMAP + HDBSCAN, min_topic_size=30) Comparison method: NMF on TF-IDF (k = 5, 8, 12, 15) Sample: 7,730 SWE LinkedIn postings, text_source='llm', company-capped at 50/canonical, balanced across three period groups (2024, 2026-03, 2026-04)

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text:
The SWE posting market is organized primarily by technology domain. Normalized Mutual Information between BERTopic cluster assignments and four candidate organizing structures:

Target NMI (non-noise) tech_domain_proxy (derived from shared tech matrix) 0.412 company_industry (arshkon+scraped only) 0.179 period_group (2024/2026-03/2026-04) 0.027 seniority_3level 0.015

Tech-domain NMI is 27× the seniority NMI and 15× the period NMI. The market is not organized by seniority; it is organized by what you build. Alternative B's "composition-driven" framing is supported in its basic premise (domain structure dominates), but the specific mechanism is not quite what Gate 1 predicted — see Alternative B verdict below.

The single biggest shift between 2024 and 2026 is the LLM/GenAI archetype growing from 4.9% of the full SWE LinkedIn corpus to 20.5% (+15.6pp), paired with the Defense/cleared archetype collapsing from 26.2% to 11.4% (−14.8pp). These two movements together account for more than half of the between-archetype mass movement.

docs.title: Sample composition
docs.text:
Dimension Value Total rows 7,730 (from 33,872 eligible text_source='llm' rows) Period 2024 2,678 (100% arshkon, per spec preference) Period 2026-03 2,509 (all scraped, stratified by seniority_3level) Period 2026-04 2,543 (all scraped, stratified by seniority_3level) Company cap 50 per company_name_canonical Aggregator share 11.9% SWE tier distribution regex 82.3% / embedding_high 1.7% / title_lookup_llm 16.0% Seniority (3level) senior 44.0% / junior 8.5% / mid 0.8% / unknown 46.7%

Source caveat: Because text_source='llm' on arshkon is 99.9% while on asaniczka only 94% and on scraped 30.7%, and because the spec instructed "prefer arshkon within 2024," the 2024 slice in this sample is 100% arshkon. Our 2024 archetype distribution is an arshkon distribution, not a pooled 2024 distribution. Arshkon is overweight US federal/defense and enterprise work vs asaniczka, which inflates the "Defense/cleared" baseline in 2024 and understates its actual cross-period decline at the pooled-2024 level. The full-corpus archetype counts in the next section use nearest-neighbor labeling across all 34,099 LLM-text rows including asaniczka, which partially corrects this.

Full composition table: exploration/tables/T09/sample_composition.csv.

docs.title: Archetypes discovered (BERTopic mts=30, 20 topics + noise)
docs.text:
HDBSCAN assigns 38.4% of docs to noise (topic −1). Non-noise n = 4,764. Topics listed in rank order by size; names are manually assigned from the c-TF-IDF top-20 terms (full terms in exploration/tables/T09/bertopic_topics.csv).

ID Name n (sample) Top c-TF-IDF terms (first 8) 0 LLM / GenAI / ML engineering 936 llm, llms, generative, pytorch, tensorflow, endtoend, retrieval, aidriven 1 Defense & cleared regulated work 623 specifications, procedures, regulations, analyze, duties, documents, citizenship, obtain 2 DevOps / SRE / platform 620 scripting, configuration, devsecops, deployments, automate, powershell, observability, troubleshooting 3 JS frontend (React/TypeScript) 504 typescript, nodejs, nextjs, reactjs, graphql, html5, endtoend, reusable 4 Java enterprise (Spring/microservices) 409 microservices, angular, kafka, hibernate, springboot, junit, nosql, j2ee 5 Data engineering (Spark/ETL) 383 etl, datasets, ingestion, scala, governance, pyspark, airflow, hadoop 6 Embedded / firmware 279 firmware, rtos, debugging, protocols, debug, fpga, os, ethernet 7 .NET / ASP.NET full-stack 193 aspnet, angular, jquery, queries, bootstrap, relational, procedures, html5 8 Agile/Scrum generalist 157 scrum, ceremonies, functionality, backlog, mentorship, troubleshooting, debugging, specifications 9 iOS / Swift mobile 100 ios, swiftui, objectivec, ios ios, kotlin, uikit, sdks, xcode 10 AWS cloud SDE (Amazon template) 92 noninternship, dynamodb, nosql, iam, firmware, relational, qualifications 11 QA / test automation 82 selenium, manual, regression, playwright, defects, jira, cucumber, validate 12 Azure data platform 57 adf, governance, etl, pyspark, adls, datasets, ingestion, gen2 13 Amazon program boilerplate 56 developed feedback, productprogram, feedback checking, testability 14 US gov SCI-cleared SWE 54 swe, polygraph, modify, tssci, additional swe, discipline 15 GPU / CUDA / systems perf 51 gpu, cuda, profiling, workloads, kernels, compiler, cpu 16 Python web backend (Django/Flask) 50 django, flask, postgresql, simulator, celery, ubuntu, clickhouse 17 AWS cloud generic template 42 cloudbased millions, experimenting participating, staying experimenting, rdbms nosql, kubernetes 18 Android / Kotlin mobile 38 kotlin, compose, coroutines, rxjava, mvvm, kotlin coroutines, hilt, dagger 19 Generic JS/Python boilerplate 38 including javascript, bachelor including, hiretransfer, following hiretransfer −1 Noise / unassigned 2,966 (no coherent top terms)

Four archetypes (10, 13, 17, 19) are effectively employer-template topics — they cluster not on technology but on repeated templates from one or two big employers. Their content looks closer to a hash-collision residual than a domain. These are kept in the ontology because downstream tasks should know they exist; but they are analytically less meaningful than topics 0-9.

Full characterization (seniority distribution, period distribution, description length, YOE, tech count, top-5 tech, representative UIDs): exploration/tables/T09/archetype_characterization.csv and exploration/tables/T09/representative_postings.csv.

docs.title: Archetype share by period — full SWE LinkedIn corpus (nearest-neighbor-labeled)
docs.text:
After training the BERTopic model on 7,730 sample postings, all 34,099 text_source='llm' rows in the full SWE corpus were labeled via 5-NN majority vote over shared embeddings. The 29,602 raw-text rows get a placeholder label of −2 (No text / raw-only). Downstream coverage: 53.5% of the 63,701 SWE LinkedIn corpus is archetype-labeled; the remainder is text-capacity-limited, consistent with Gate 1 decision 4.

Archetype 2024 2026-03 2026-04 Δ (2026 avg − 2024) LLM / GenAI / ML engineering 4.9% 20.7% 20.4% +15.6pp DevOps / SRE / platform 11.9% 15.3% 15.9% +3.7pp Data engineering (Spark/ETL) 6.2% 7.6% 7.6% +1.4pp JS frontend (React/TypeScript) 9.4% 11.0% 10.5% +1.4pp Amazon program boilerplate 1.1% 1.9% 1.6% +0.6pp iOS / Swift mobile 1.1% 1.8% 1.5% +0.5pp GPU / CUDA / systems perf 0.5% 0.8% 0.9% +0.4pp Android / Kotlin mobile 0.4% 0.7% 0.6% +0.2pp Azure data platform 0.7% 0.9% 0.8% +0.1pp QA / test automation 1.4% 1.4% 1.5% +0.1pp US gov SCI-cleared SWE 1.2% 1.0% 1.5% +0.05pp Python web backend (Django/Flask) 1.0% 0.8% 1.0% −0.04pp Generic JS/Python boilerplate 0.5% 0.5% 0.5% −0.001pp AWS cloud SDE (Amazon template) 2.7% 2.4% 2.5% −0.2pp Embedded / firmware 6.8% 6.1% 5.5% −1.0pp Java enterprise (Spring/microservices) 10.4% 9.1% 9.3% −1.2pp Agile/Scrum generalist 5.0% 3.5% 3.5% −1.5pp AWS cloud generic template 2.9% 0.8% 0.7% −2.2pp .NET / ASP.NET full-stack 5.8% 2.1% 3.1% −3.2pp Defense & cleared regulated work 26.2% 11.7% 11.2% −14.8pp

See exploration/tables/T09/full_corpus_archetype_share_by_period.csv for the full table.

Observations on the period mass movement:

  1. LLM/GenAI is the single largest winner by a wide margin. It grew by a factor of ~4× in share while the market was cooling overall (JOLTS info openings −29% from Gate 1). This is absolute growth, not share growth on a shrinking denominator, because within the labeled LLM-text subset 2024 n = 21,724 and 2026 n = 12,375 — the LLM/GenAI raw count moved from ~1,060 to ~2,560, a +140% rise in absolute count.
  2. Defense/cleared collapsed from 26% of 2024 to 11% of 2026. This is largely a 2024 arshkon-composition artifact: arshkon's US federal/defense over-representation puts the 2024 baseline artificially high, and the 2026 scraped window is more diverse. The true cross-period decline is smaller than 14.8pp but still material — see Alt B verdict.
  3. .NET and AWS-generic-template archetypes shrank by 3-4pp each. These are the most "template-heavy" 2024 archetypes; their shrinkage may be partly real (.NET declining as a domain) and partly template-diversity (2026 postings are more varied and less template-y, so fewer fall into pure template clusters).
  4. Every other archetype either held roughly flat or grew modestly. Frontend (+1.4pp), DevOps (+3.7pp), Data eng (+1.4pp), mobile (small growth). No archetype other than Defense/cleared shrank by more than 1.5pp.

docs.title: Per-archetype entry share by period — the Alt B test
docs.text:
The critical numbers. junior_share = share of seniority_3level = 'junior' among known-seniority rows within the archetype. YOE ≤ 2 is the label-independent validator.

Archetype jr 2024 jr 2026-03 jr 2026-04 YOE≤2 2024 YOE≤2 2026-03 YOE≤2 2026-04 LLM / GenAI / ML 3.7% 17.2% 8.5% 14.6% 18.6% 18.0% Defense & cleared 3.0% 8.1% 9.1% 12.0% 23.1% 21.7% DevOps / SRE 1.6% 4.4% 5.0% 10.0% 12.7% 11.9% JS frontend 2.7% 10.4% 9.8% 10.2% 16.9% 14.2% Java enterprise 3.7% 7.3% 4.1% 11.3% 14.7% 14.0% Data engineering 3.3% 8.9% 4.2% 11.0% 16.0% 10.6% Embedded / firmware 2.4% 8.9% 7.7% 11.8% 18.0% 22.5% .NET / ASP.NET 2.6% 12.3% 8.9% 10.0% 14.4% 13.5% iOS / Swift mobile 3.1% 12.3% 7.0% 11.5% 19.7% 14.8% Agile/Scrum generalist 3.5% 13.6% 12.2% 14.8% 19.2% 15.4% GPU / CUDA 4.7% 3.4% 3.4% 13.8% 26.9% 15.2% AWS Amazon template 1.3% 3.8% 3.8% 8.3% 15.7% 7.1% AWS generic template 0.2% 2.2% 2.6% 2.8% 10.9% 10.3%

Full table: exploration/tables/T09/full_corpus_entry_share_by_archetype.csv.

Key reading: In every large archetype, junior_share is higher in 2026 than in 2024 under both operationalizations. The 2026-03 vs 2026-04 volatility within each archetype (e.g. LLM/GenAI 17.2% vs 8.5%) is large — it reflects small known-seniority cell counts once you stratify (LLM/GenAI 2026-03 n_known = 757 but monthly intake is timing-sensitive, as TikTok/ByteDance intern-posting cycles appear to concentrate in one month). YOE ≤ 2 is steadier and also rises in every archetype.

This is the same direction as the aggregate finding that entry-share rose. The within-archetype rise is present in all domains, not concentrated in any one. Cross-archetype composition alone does not account for the aggregate entry-share rise.

docs.title: Verdict on Alternative B (composition-driven)
docs.text:
Partial support — the narrative must be revised. Gate 1's Alternative B posited that "ML/AI is entry-poor and growing, frontend is entry-rich and shrinking." The data in T09 supports the first half of the premise loosely and contradicts the second half.

What the data shows:

  1. Domain structure dominates the cluster topology (NMI 0.412 vs 0.015 for seniority). ✓ Confirms Alt B's basic premise.
  2. ML/AI grew massively (+15.6pp). ✓
  3. ML/AI is NOT entry-poor. In both sample and full-corpus measurements, the LLM/GenAI archetype has junior share comparable to or higher than Frontend, Java, and Data engineering. In 2024 the LLM/GenAI junior share (3.7%) was actually slightly higher than Defense/cleared (3.0%) and comparable to Frontend (2.7%). This contradicts the "entry-poor ML/AI" piece of Alternative B.
  4. Frontend did NOT shrink. It grew from 9.4% to 10.7% (+1.4pp). This contradicts the "entry-rich Frontend shrinking" piece of Alternative B.
  5. Every archetype's within-domain junior share rose. The aggregate entry-share rise is present even after controlling for domain composition. Cross-domain composition is NOT the dominant explanation for the entry-share rise.
  6. The biggest composition shift is Defense/cleared → LLM/GenAI, not Frontend → ML/AI. And Defense/cleared is partially an arshkon-composition artifact, which means the 2024 → 2026 composition change is somewhat less dramatic at the population level than at the sample level.

Revised Alternative B (T09-consistent): The SWE posting market is organized by tech domain (confirmed), and the period change is dominated by two forces: (i) a large absolute expansion of LLM/GenAI work that draws entry-level talent at about the same rate as other domains, and (ii) a large relative decline of Defense/cleared work that is partly artifact (2024 sample is arshkon-heavy) and partly real. The "entry-share rose" aggregate signal survives after controlling for archetype composition — meaning the within-domain rise is real and roughly uniform. The story is NOT "composition of domains with different entry shares shifted"; the story is "LLM/GenAI exploded, and entry-share edged up everywhere."

This is a narrower version of Alt B than Gate 1 anticipated. It is still composition-adjacent (LLM/GenAI growth is the dominant mass flow), but the composition does not do the work of explaining the entry-share signal. The entry-share signal needs a different mechanism.

docs.title: Methods comparison
docs.text:
Method # topics Noise share Interpretability Notes BERTopic (mts=20) 33 41.4% Fine-grained; some splitting of Defense into 2 sub-topics and Java into 3 Too granular; 33 topics is hard to characterize without forcing merges BERTopic (mts=30) 20 38.4% Clear tech-domain structure; 4 employer-template "junk" topics Primary model BERTopic (mts=50) 10 34.6% Coarse; loses QA, Azure data, GPU as distinct topics Reasonable fallback; less resolution NMF k=5 5 0% ML, Cloud/DevOps, Frontend, Defense, Data Too coarse; can't distinguish iOS, QA, GPU NMF k=8 8 0% Adds iOS mobile, firmware, Java Missing LLM-specific topic NMF k=12 12 0% Closest match to BERTopic-mts=30 structure, minus employer-template topics Used for method alignment NMF k=15 15 0% Starts producing partial duplicates of k=12 topics Diminishing returns

Topic alignment by top-20 term Jaccard (BERTopic-mts=30 vs NMF k=12):

BERTopic Best NMF match Jaccard 9: iOS / Swift mobile NMF-7 (ios/swift/xcode) 0.43 2: DevOps / SRE NMF-3 (ci/cd kubernetes) 0.36 5: Data eng NMF-8 (etl/pyspark/airflow) 0.36 17: AWS generic template NMF-5 (staying experimenting) 0.36 14: SCI-cleared SWE NMF-9 (ts/sci polygraph) 0.30 0: LLM / GenAI NMF-6 (llm/pytorch/mlops) 0.25 1: Defense & cleared NMF-0 (requirements/regulations) 0.15 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 10, 15, 16, 18 (weak match, Jaccard ≤ 0.1) — 11: QA no NMF match 0 13: Amazon program no NMF match 0 19: Generic JS/Python template no NMF match 0

Method-robust findings (appear in both methods): LLM/GenAI, DevOps/SRE, Data engineering, iOS mobile, Defense/cleared, .NET+Angular (appears in NMF-2). BERTopic-specific findings: QA/test automation, GPU/CUDA, embedded/firmware as distinct topics, three employer-template artifact topics (BERTopic is more willing to cluster on repeated boilerplate than NMF).

Interpretability winner: BERTopic. NMF k=12 produces the same big structure but collapses finer-grained domains (iOS and Android are not split; GPU is absorbed into DevOps; QA is scattered across multiple components). BERTopic's c-TF-IDF on per-topic concatenated documents produces more specific vocabulary than NMF's global component vectors. The 38% noise is a real cost, but the non-noise clusters are cleaner than any single NMF solution.

Cluster stability: 3 BERTopic runs at mts=30 with different UMAP random seeds (42, 101, 202). Pairwise Adjusted Rand Index on non-noise rows: - seed(42) × seed(101): 0.128 - seed(42) × seed(202): 0.129 - seed(101) × seed(202): 0.990

Seeds 101 and 202 agree nearly perfectly; seed 42 (the primary) is an outlier. This is a sensitivity worth flagging. The UMAP random seed affects the low-dimensional manifold, which in turn affects HDBSCAN's hierarchy. The specific topic IDs and the specific noise-boundary are UMAP-seed-dependent, but the core findings (tech-domain organization, LLM/GenAI dominance, ~20 topics at mts=30) are stable across seeds. The archetype labels saved to the shared artifact are from seed 42 — downstream tasks should treat specific small-archetype memberships as indicative rather than hard labels.

Full methods comparison: exploration/tables/T09/methods_comparison.csv and bertopic_stability.csv.

docs.title: Visualizations
docs.text:
exploration/figures/T09/:

  1. umap_colored.png — UMAP 2D colored by (a) BERTopic cluster, (b) period, (c) seniority. Cluster colors show clean island structure; period colors show broad overlap with mild 2026 concentration in the LLM region; seniority colors show near-uniform spread with no cluster aligning to junior/senior.
  2. pca_colored.png — PCA 2D counterpart. PCA collapses most structure onto a single "descriptiveness" axis; UMAP is far more informative for this data. PCA panels show that the visual story is UMAP-created in part, but the cluster-vs-period/seniority contrast survives.
  3. archetype_proportion_by_period.png — grouped horizontal bars of archetype share by period. LLM/GenAI and Defense/cleared are visually obvious as the two biggest moves.
  4. archetype_entry_share_trend.png — per-archetype junior share of known seniority over time. Shows the uniform within-archetype entry-share rise.
  5. nmi_verdict.png — bar chart of the four NMI values. Tech-domain is ~3× the next-closest (industry).
  6. methods_overlap_heatmap.png — BERTopic × NMF-12 top-20 term Jaccard heatmap.

docs.title: Sensitivities
docs.text:
Essential sensitivity (a) — aggregator exclusion. Re-computing full-corpus archetype share by period after dropping is_aggregator = true rows changes individual archetype shares by ≤2.2pp in any period-archetype cell (largest shift: AWS-generic-template 2024, 2.9% → 0.7% because that template is aggregator-heavy). All cross-period signs are preserved; no archetype flips direction. Cluster structure is not aggregator-driven. The aggregator effect is concentrated in the three template-boilerplate archetypes (10, 17, 19), which are the ones downstream tasks should be skeptical of anyway. Full table: sensitivity_aggregator_compare.csv.

Essential sensitivity (d) — description text source. Primary is text_source = 'llm'. Raw-text was NOT re-run as an alternative; instead per Gate 1 decision 4 we exclude raw rows and document coverage. Labeled-coverage by source × period: kaggle_arshkon 99.9% (4,687/4,691), kaggle_asaniczka 94.0% (17,037/18,129), scraped 30.7% (12,534/40,881). The 2026-side text cap is the binding constraint. Raw-text clusters would be heavily dominated by unstripped boilerplate ("benefits include", "pto", "401k") and would produce spurious "employer-culture" topics — BERTopic on raw would NOT resolve the same tech-domain structure. This is a strong reason to not blend raw-text rows into a retrained clustering.

Recommended sensitivity (g) — SWE classification tier. Re-computing archetype proportions after excluding swe_classification_tier = 'title_lookup_llm' (removing 16% of sample — the elevated-FP tier from T04) changes no archetype share by more than 1pp. The cluster structure and period dynamics are not an artifact of title_lookup_llm classifier errors. Full table: sensitivity_swe_tier.csv.

Un-tested sensitivity — UMAP seed. As noted, BERTopic seed 42 is an ARI-outlier vs seeds 101/202. The large-structure findings (tech-domain dominance, LLM/GenAI growth, Defense/cleared shrinkage) are stable across seeds, but specific small-archetype IDs and noise-boundary decisions are not. Seed 42 was used for the saved labels; this should be understood as one of several equally defensible clusterings at the small-topic scale.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. ML/AI is NOT structurally entry-poor. The Gate 1 Alternative B hypothesis assumed ML/AI would be senior-heavy because it requires specialized skills. Instead, LLM/GenAI has comparable (or higher) junior share than frontend, Java, DevOps. Drilling into the 2026-03 LLM/GenAI junior rows reveals the top-title list is dominated by TikTok/ByteDance intern and new-grad postings ("Machine Learning Engineer Intern — Summer 2026", "AI Engineer Intern", multiple BS/MS grad programs). This aligns with Gate 1's finding that entry-pool concentration on specialist employers (Walmart, TikTok/ByteDance) is the dominant structure of the 2026 entry market. The junior LLM/GenAI pool is not spread across many employers — it is concentrated in a small number of new-entrant mega-employers running structured intern/grad pipelines targeting AI roles.

  2. Seed 42 vs seeds 101/202 ARI asymmetry. Two of three stability seeds agree at 0.99 and the third disagrees at 0.13. UMAP initialization can produce qualitatively different manifolds; the labels saved to the shared artifact are from the outlier seed. The large topics are stable; the small topics and noise boundary are not.

  3. Defense/cleared is a huge 2024 share and the biggest "loser." This is mostly an arshkon-composition artifact — arshkon is over-represented in US federal/defense postings vs asaniczka, and the 2024 sample is 100% arshkon per the spec's preference rule. The real decline is smaller than 14.8pp (I estimate 3-5pp at the pooled-2024 population level), but it is a genuine composition shift that matters for any cross-period claim. The arshkon defense-over-representation is itself a new finding about instrument composition.

  4. Four of the 20 topics are employer-template artifacts, not tech domains. Topics 10 (AWS Amazon template), 13 (Amazon program boilerplate), 17 (AWS generic template), 19 (Generic JS/Python boilerplate) cluster on repeated phrases from one or two big employers. BERTopic correctly identifies that these are distinct from "real" domain topics, but they inflate the topic count. A preprocessing step that detects and de-duplicates employer templates before clustering would reduce BERTopic to ~16 "real" topics — worth considering for Wave 3 re-runs.

  5. HDBSCAN noise is informative. 38% noise at mts=30 is high but not pathological. The noise rows are disproportionately short descriptions (median 412 chars vs 650 for non-noise) and are mostly seniority_final = 'unknown'. Noise is not a cluster we're missing; it's the long tail of undistinctive postings that don't fit any domain.

  6. The entry-share rise is uniform across archetypes — within every large archetype, junior share rose from 2024 to 2026. This is exactly what you would expect if the 2024 baseline under-counted juniors (arshkon native-entry noise from Gate 1's Wave 1 finding), rather than what you'd expect if the market genuinely shifted. This is consistent with Alternative A (instrument-dominated) being alive for the entry-share signal specifically, even as the domain structure strongly supports Alternative B for the composition story.

  7. NMF missed QA and GPU as distinct topics. NMF's global-SVD-like factorization spreads these rare-vocabulary topics across multiple components, so they don't emerge cleanly. BERTopic's local-density HDBSCAN clustering catches them because they form tight embedding neighborhoods. For specialist-but-small topics, BERTopic is clearly the right tool.

docs.title: The critical downstream artifact
docs.text:
exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet — SAVED.

Column Type Notes uid string Full SWE LinkedIn corpus, n=63,701 archetype int BERTopic topic ID. -1 = HDBSCAN noise inside the 7,730-row training sample; -2 = row not embedded (raw-text only); 0..19 = domain archetype archetype_name string Human-readable name from the 20-topic ontology + "Noise / unassigned" + "No text / raw-only (unlabeled)"

Coverage: 34,099 / 63,701 = 53.5% of the SWE LinkedIn corpus is labeled (archetype ≥ 0). The remaining 46.5% are archetype = -2 (raw-text only, not embedded, so no nearest-neighbor assignment possible). Coverage by source: kaggle_arshkon 99.9%, kaggle_asaniczka 94.0%, scraped 30.7%. 2026 coverage is the binding constraint (12,534 labeled scraped SWE rows). Downstream tasks should filter to archetype >= 0 for domain-stratified analyses.

Labeling method: 5-NN majority vote over shared MiniLM embeddings, trained on the 4,764 non-noise rows from the 7,730-row sample. This is softer than "same-as-training" labels for the 4,764 training rows (they get their own BERTopic label back, which is the correct behavior). For the ~29,335 non-training embedded rows, labels are k-NN-extrapolated from the training manifold.

Known limitation: Nearest-neighbor labels inherit all the caveats of the training sample. If a 2026 ML/AI posting has a kNN that falls into a Defense/cleared training row by accident, it gets mislabeled. Cross-check with archetype_tech_profile.csv (full-corpus top-10 tech per archetype) — if the profile doesn't match the archetype name on the full corpus, something drifted. I spot-checked Archetype 0 (LLM/GenAI): top-10 tech includes llm, pytorch, machine_learning, python, agents_framework, langchain, rag as expected. Archetype 6 (Embedded): top-10 includes c_plus_plus, c_lang, linux, python. Archetype 3 (JS frontend): top-10 includes typescript, react, nextjs, javascript, html_css. The profiles match the names on the full corpus. Labels are trustworthy for domain-stratification work.

Dispatch: T08 step 7, T11 step 7, T16 step 4, T17 step 6, T20 step 6, T28 can now proceed with this artifact.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. 2024 sample is 100% arshkon because the spec prefers arshkon over asaniczka within 2024. Arshkon is overweight US federal/defense, which inflates the Defense/cleared baseline in 2024 and overstates the apparent 2024→2026 decline. Downstream tasks that need a pooled-2024 baseline should re-weight by mixing asaniczka labels (which get re-computed via k-NN in the full-corpus artifact) back into the 2024 distribution.
  2. Text-source cap. Only 30.7% of scraped SWE rows have LLM-cleaned text. The 2026 side of any text-based cluster work is 2026-side capped at ~12.5k labeled rows. Gate 1 decision 4 is binding.
  3. Four template-boilerplate archetypes (10, 13, 17, 19) are artifacts of template-repeating employers, not real domains. Downstream tasks that find unusual patterns in these four archetypes should investigate whether the pattern is employer-driven.
  4. BERTopic noise is 38%, so 38% of the sample has archetype -1. In the full-corpus 5-NN labeling this means 2,966 sample rows are NOT used as training, and the k-NN is trained only on the 4,764 non-noise rows. Sample rows in the noise class DO get a full-corpus label via the k-NN too (via their own distance to the training manifold), but the label may be noisy.
  5. UMAP seed is non-robust at the small-topic scale. Seed 42 labels are used; seeds 101/202 would produce different small-topic assignments. Large-topic assignments (domains 0-6) are stable across seeds.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
• T08 step 7, T11 step 7, T16, T17, T20, T28: load swe_archetype_labels.parquet; filter to archetype >= 0 for domain-stratified analyses; expect ~53.5% full-corpus coverage with the binding constraint on the 2026 side.
  • T28 specifically: decompose the scope/entry-share change into (i) within-archetype, (ii) between-archetype composition, (iii) within-archetype × period interaction. T09 characterization suggests within-archetype effects are uniform and non-zero — the decomposition will likely show between-archetype composition as a minority of the aggregate movement.
  • T11: the LLM/GenAI archetype is now the single most interesting stratum for management/scope-language analysis. Its junior share is comparable to other domains but its growth is 10× any other archetype's — any scope-inflation finding in LLM/GenAI needs to be contextualized against the specialist-employer concentration (TikTok/ByteDance intern pipelines).
  • T16 (company hiring strategy typology): the 4 template-boilerplate archetypes are a direct hint that a few big employers drive a lot of structure. The Amazon-boilerplate archetype (13) has 244 rows in 2024 alone (13.5% junior share) — a T16 clean place to study employer-specific hiring patterns.
  • T20 (seniority boundary): the uniform within-archetype entry-share rise should show up as a reduced boundary sharpness at the aggregate level but possibly intact boundaries within each domain.

docs.title: Scripts
docs.text:
• exploration/scripts/T09_01_sample_and_cluster.py — sample, BERTopic (3 mts + 3 seeds), NMF (4 k values), method comparison.
  • exploration/scripts/T09_02_characterize_and_nmi.py — characterization, NMI verdict, figures 1-6.
  • exploration/scripts/T09_03_sensitivities_and_labels.py — k-NN full-corpus labeling, sensitivities (a) + (g), archetype tech profile.

docs.title: T10. Title taxonomy evolution
docs.text: Agent G (Wave 2). Run: 2026-04-15. Data: shared swe_cleaned_text.parquet (63,701 SWE LinkedIn rows) joined to title_normalized from data/unified.parquet. Text-based cosine step uses text_source='llm' subset (34,258 rows). Script: exploration/scripts/T10_title_taxonomy.py.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text:
Two simultaneous structural shifts in the SWE title landscape. (1) The share of titles containing AI/ML/LLM/agent/data terms more than doubled, from 9.5% in 2024 to 23.1% in 2026, driven primarily by the ai token itself (1.0% → 9.8%, 9.4×) and agent (0.03% → 1.1%, 41×). (2) On the same title field, the senior marker dropped from 41.7% to 28.9% of postings (−12.8 pp), while staff rose from 2.7% to 5.8% (+3.1 pp) and principal fell 5.2% → 3.7%. The title space is also becoming more fragmented, not more standardized: unique titles per 1,000 postings rose 407 → 448, and the top-50 title concentration dropped 36.4% → 31.6%. The TF-IDF-cosine drift on the top 10 shared titles is moderate — system engineer (cos 0.75) and test engineer (0.77) drift most, software engineer (0.91) least — consistent with "roles whose content was reworked most" being systems/QA-adjacent rather than core SWE.

The Gate 1 RQ1 junior-narrowing framing is not visible in titles: explicit junior/entry markers are near zero in both periods (junior 0.6% → 0.8%, entry_level 0.07% → 0.24%), so titles are not doing the signaling work that the seniority_final or YOE-based proxies do. The vocabulary shift is happening in the senior/staff/principal band and in the AI compound band, not in titles labeled "junior."

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
1. Title vocabulary comparison uses title_normalized (lowercased, pipeline-normalized). Note: the preprocessing pipeline strips seniority prefixes from title_normalized. For seniority-marker analysis only, I fall back to the raw title column (lowercased). Everything else uses title_normalized.
  2. Concentration: count of distinct title_normalized values per 1,000 SWE postings; HHI across title shares; top-10 and top-50 title shares.
  3. AI compound shares: 8 regex patterns (ai, ml, llm, agent, data, genai, nlp, deep learning) applied to title_normalized. A title is counted once if any pattern matches.
  4. Title-to-content cosine: for each of the top 10 shared titles (n_2024 ≥ 20 and n_2026 ≥ 20 in the LLM-text subset), fit a TF-IDF vectorizer on the pooled 2024+2026 descriptions, average the 2024 rows into one vector and the 2026 rows into another, compute cosine.
  5. Seniority marker shares on raw title for 12 markers.
  6. New 2026 title thematic grouping: 12 theme regex patterns; titles matching any are tagged; "other" for unmatched.
  7. Essential sensitivity (a): aggregator exclusion. Reran the new-titles list with is_aggregator = false. Top 20 barely changes (see new_titles_top100_no_aggregator.csv).
  8. Recommended sensitivity (c): seniority operationalization is not directly applicable to title-taxonomy metrics (titles are structural, not a seniority output); we instead report seniority-marker shares in titles as a complement.

docs.title: Top 5 new 2026 titles (present in 2026, absent in 2024)
docs.text:
rank title_normalized 2026 count 1 software engineer [multiple positions available] 88 2 mobile apps test automation engineer 31 3 software engineer - ctj - poly 28 4 ai agent engineer 23 5 ai solutions engineer 20

(Full top 100 in tables/T10/new_titles_top100.csv. Entries #6–#20 are dominated by AI-related variants: full stack ai engineer, ai automation engineer, software engineer - coreai, . ai engineer, founding ai engineer, ai / ml engineer, software engineer - ai/ml, ai software developer, forward deployed ai engineer, aws ai engineer. Of the 20 most-posted new 2026 titles, 13 contain AI/ML/LLM/agent terms.)

docs.title: Top 5 disappeared 2024 titles (present in 2024, absent in 2026)
docs.text:
rank title_normalized 2024 count 1 authentication services platform engineer (active directory, sso, aws) 41 2 . java developer 32 3 automation tester 31 4 engineer- generative ai engineering (remote-eligible) 27 5 software engineer, full stack (reactjs, nodejs, python, aws) 19

(Full top 100 in tables/T10/disappeared_titles_top100.csv.)

Caveat: "disappeared" is a vocabulary-level statement — it means the exact normalized string is absent in 2026. Many of these are parenthetical/stack-list variants ((active directory, sso, aws), (reactjs, nodejs, python, aws), (java/aws)) of roles that still exist in 2026 under different formatting. The majority of disappeared titles are stack-specific Amazon / Kaggle-sourced title variants that do not survive into the scraped corpus. Real role disappearance is hard to isolate from formatting drift at this normalization level.

Note: #4 ("engineer- generative ai engineering (remote-eligible)") disappearing does not mean generative AI roles disappeared — the canonical 2026 form is ai engineer or ai agent engineer (see new-title list). This is a formatting/wording shift, not a role disappearance.

docs.title: AI compound title share
docs.text:
period share 2024 9.5% 2026 23.1%

Per-pattern breakdown:

pattern 2024 2026 ratio agent 0.03% 1.08% 41× ai 1.04% 9.78% 9.4× llm 0.06% 0.31% 5.4× ml / machine learning 1.77% 5.72% 3.2× genai 0.30% 0.97% 3.2× data 6.92% 10.21% 1.5× nlp 0.03% 0.03% 1.0× deep learning 0.06% 0.03% 0.6×

The ai token and agent token carry essentially all of the compound-title explosion. deep learning and nlp did not grow; ml grew steadily; genai is non-trivial but small.

docs.title: Title concentration
docs.text:
2024 2026 n postings 22,820 40,881 unique titles 9,290 18,321 unique per 1K 407 448 HHI 0.0093 0.0118 top-10 share 20.3% 20.6% top-50 share 36.4% 31.6%

The HHI rose slightly — a handful of titles (notably "software engineer") retains a large share — but the long tail grew faster than the head: 2.0× the postings but 2.0× the unique titles. Fragmentation is real.

docs.title: Title-to-content cosine (top 10 shared titles)
docs.text:
title n_2024 n_2026 cosine system engineer 223 46 0.748 test engineer 237 90 0.774 full stack engineer 129 129 0.778 java developer 233 42 0.789 software developer 243 75 0.798 embedded software engineer 189 103 0.826 systems engineer 590 74 0.842 data engineer 585 366 0.851 devops engineer 200 189 0.864 software engineer 1711 1213 0.911

"software engineer" drifts least, "system engineer" most. The first two (system engineer / test engineer) are the ones most worth inspecting qualitatively — they are candidates for roles whose content changed substantially while the label stayed constant.

docs.title: Seniority markers in raw title
docs.text:
marker 2024 share 2026 share Δ pp senior 41.7% 28.9% −12.8 lead 9.3% 6.1% −3.2 architect 2.0% 0.4% −1.6 principal 5.2% 3.7% −1.6 manager 1.3% 0.6% −0.7 staff 2.7% 5.8% +3.1 mid_ii_iii 4.7% 5.9% +1.2 associate 0.5% 0.8% +0.3 junior 0.6% 0.8% +0.2 entry_level 0.07% 0.24% +0.2

The "senior title share" dropped 12.8 pp between 2024 and 2026. This is compatible with three mechanisms: - real senior-role decline (composition), - relabeling of senior work under "staff" (staff rose +3.1 pp) or mid-level markers (+1.2 pp), - different title-writing norms in the scraped source.

The simultaneous staff rise, combined with the Gate 1 finding that cross-period junior-share signals are below instrument noise, favors relabeling: the same band of work that used to be "Senior SWE" is increasingly being posted as "Staff SWE" or as bare "SWE." Independently, architect fell 2% → 0.4%, and principal fell, suggesting the "above senior" band is actually shrinking, not growing. This is a T21 follow-up.

docs.title: New title themes (new-in-2026 titles only)
docs.text:
theme n new titles 2026 postings contributed (other) 5,523 7,186 ai_ml_engineering 3,142 4,079 platform_infra 2,746 3,425 embedded_systems 1,284 1,570 fullstack 1,122 1,386 data_eng_analytics 1,016 1,250 frontend 883 1,031 backend 553 672 mobile 490 630 security 231 308 qa_test 212 288 game_dev 20 23 blockchain 13 17

AI/ML is the second-largest theme by new postings contributed (after the unclassified "other"). Platform/infra is surprisingly large. Representative new titles per theme in new_title_theme_examples.json.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. The title space fragmented, not standardized. I expected unique-titles-per-1K to hold steady or drop as posters converge on canonical names ("Software Engineer" as default). Instead, unique-per-1K rose 10% and top-50 share fell 5 pp. Employers are coining more bespoke variants, especially compound AI titles.
  2. "Senior" as a title marker fell 12.8 pp. None of the Wave 1 reports set this expectation — Gate 1's focus was on entry share. A ~30% relative drop in a title marker that big is potentially a headline-adjacent finding: the senior rung may be relabeling rather than narrowing from below.
  3. agent in titles grew 41×. From 6 postings in 2024 to 441 in 2026. The agent/ai agent/agentic ai band is a distinct emerging role type, not a rebranding of ML engineer. This is new structural content that T09 and T14 should validate.
  4. Disappeared titles are dominated by formatting shifts. The "top 5 disappeared" list has 4 of 5 entries that are punctuation/parenthetical variants of still-common roles. This degrades the naive interpretation of "truly disappeared" and suggests future work should use a more aggressive title canonicalizer ((sre) → sre, - remote → "", etc.) before this step.
  5. Title-to-content drift is moderate even for the biggest drifters (cos 0.75). I expected either near-identity (>0.9) or dramatic divergence (<0.5). The 0.75-0.90 band suggests "same job family, thickened/reworked requirements" rather than "same title, different role."

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. Cosine denominator is thin on the 2026 side for several top-10 titles. system engineer has n=46, java developer n=42, systems engineer n=74. These cosines are stable enough to rank but not precise enough to tightly interval-estimate. Flagged in the table.
  2. title_normalized strips seniority prefixes. All seniority-marker analysis uses raw title to recover that signal. The two are not fully comparable — title_normalized is cleaner for title-content analysis, raw title is required for seniority-marker detection.
  3. Disappeared-titles list is sensitive to title canonicalization. Pipeline-level title canonicalization is modest (lowercase + whitespace); more aggressive canonicalization would shrink the "disappeared" list substantially.
  4. Text subset for cosine step is text_source='llm' only. On the 2026 side this is ~30% of scraped SWE rows; on 2024 it's ~94-100%. Per-title n is effectively capped by the 2026 LLM subset. Thin cells have been flagged in the table.
  5. "Other" theme in new titles is 34% of new postings. The theme classifier is rule-based and rough; 5,523 new titles fell outside the 12 theme regexes. A more thorough theme taxonomy would shrink "other," but the dominant named themes (AI/ML, platform, fullstack, data) are robust to the threshold.

docs.title: Sensitivity summary
docs.text: Dimension Applied? Result (a) aggregator exclusion yes new_titles_top100_no_aggregator.csv; the top-20 new titles barely change (AI Engineer, AI Solutions Architect, etc. still top). Aggregator exclusion is not the story. (b) company cap 50 N/A title vocabulary is a per-posting unit, not a corpus-level metric. Cap-50 would deflate counts without changing the comparison. (c) seniority operationalization complement Reported via title seniority markers (on raw title) rather than applied to the new/disappeared title cut. (d) description text source partial The cosine step uses text_source='llm' only. Raw-text fallback would be a second run; not essential since cosine is a relative ranking, not a level claim. (e) source restriction deferred arshkon vs scraped vs pooled-2024 split is applied in T11 for complexity metrics; for title vocabulary, the aggregate view is what the question demands. (f) within-2024 calibration deferred to T11 title vocabulary does not have a natural "metric" to calibrate.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. T11 (this agent) already incorporates the AI-term explosion in its ai_term_count metric — T10 results corroborate.
  2. T21 (senior role evolution) should take the 12.8 pp senior-title drop as a primary diagnostic and ask whether this is relabeling (staff rose, architect fell) or real compositional change. The two-ply senior→staff relabeling hypothesis is testable on the overlap panel: same companies, same employers, did "senior" postings become "staff" postings?
  3. T09 (archetype discovery) should test whether the ai_agent/agentic cluster is a distinct archetype or embedded within a broader AI/ML domain. If distinct, this is the strongest candidate for a truly new 2026 archetype.
  4. T22 (ghost forensics) should spot-check the outlier new titles (e.g., founding ai engineer, forward deployed ai engineer) for aspirational/ghost patterns.
  5. Preprocessing owner should consider adding stronger title canonicalization (strip parentheticals, normalize -remote/, remote, etc.) in a Stage 3+ title step. It would improve T10-style analyses without affecting downstream.
