docs.title: T11. Requirements complexity & credential stacking
docs.text: Agent G (Wave 2). Run: 2026-04-15. Data: shared swe_cleaned_text.parquet + swe_tech_matrix.parquet. Text-based features computed on text_source='llm' subset only (n=34,258). Binary per-row counts use the shared tech matrix across all 63,701 rows. Script: exploration/scripts/T11_requirements_complexity.py.

docs.title: Headline finding
docs.text:
Requirements are thickening — more tech, more scope, more simultaneous categories — but the effect is modest once length-normalized, and the "management language rose" story fails validation.

Across the LLM-cleaned subset:

metric 2024 mean 2026 mean SNR (vs within-2024) verdict tech_count 4.74 6.09 5.4 robust requirement_breadth 6.88 9.27 10.2 robust credential_stack_depth_strict 2.77 3.31 10.5 robust credential_stack_depth_broad 3.07 3.63 6.0 robust scope_density (per 1K chars) 0.52 0.96 61.7 robust on SNR but fragile ai_term_count 0.09 0.80 10.1 robust tech_density (per 1K chars) 9.75 9.60 0.7 below noise mgmt_strict_count 0.090 0.100 0.35 below noise mgmt_broad_count 0.516 0.545 2.2 marginal soft_skill_count 0.37 0.40 0.22 below noise

Key interpretations:

  1. Credential stacking is real and genuine. Entry-level postings in 2026 ask for more distinct requirement categories simultaneously, not just more tech within the same category. Under seniority_final, entry credential_stack_depth_strict rose 2.48 → 2.94. Under YOE ≤ 2, 3.17 → 3.78. This is the cleanest RQ2-adjacent finding in this task.

  2. Most of the breadth rise is NOT length-driven. Mean cleaned-description length in the LLM subset rose 627 → 735 chars (+17%). Mean requirement_breadth rose 6.88 → 9.27 (+35%). Breadth per 1K chars (length-normalized) still rose 13.6 → 14.4 (+6%). So ~½ of the breadth rise is length-expansion, ½ is genuine thickening. Excluding AI term mentions entirely, breadth still rose 6.78 → 8.47 (+25%).

  3. Tech density is flat. tech_density (techs per 1K chars) is essentially unchanged (9.75 → 9.60, SNR 0.7). Postings are mentioning more technologies, but only because they got longer. There is no evidence that individual sentences are getting tech-denser.

  4. Scope density looks huge (SNR 61.7) but is fragile. The within-2024 Cohen's d for scope_density is near zero (0.006), so the SNR ratio is numerically inflated. The absolute 2024 → 2026 lift (0.52 → 0.96, +85%) is real, but the SNR number should be read as "strong cross-period, weak instrument noise" rather than as a precision metric. Compare with tech_density which has a similar within-2024 stability but shows no cross-period lift — the contrast itself is the evidence that scope_density's rise is not a pure artifact.

  5. Management language rose, but does not survive validation. See next section — management broad inflates 4.37-4.56× over strict, and the broad pattern has 74% precision (below the 80% required threshold). Strict management rate is 8.5% → 9.5%, SNR 0.35, below instrument noise.

docs.title: Feature extraction
docs.text:
Per-row regex extraction on description_cleaned from the LLM subset (text_source='llm', n=34,258). Tech counts come from the shared tech matrix (123 pre-validated regex patterns, covering all 63,701 rows — I used the LLM subset's tech counts here for consistency with the density metrics, but the matrix itself is available corpus-wide).

Categories and representative patterns:

  • Soft skill (10 terms): communication, collaborat*, problem-solving, teamwork, leadership, interpersonal, adaptab*|flexib*, time management, analytical, detail-oriented.
  • Org scope (10 terms): ownership|own(s|ed|ing), end-to-end, cross-functional, stakeholder, autonom*, initiative, strateg*, vision*, roadmap, high-impact|drive impact|business impact.
  • Management strict (9 terms): manag(e|es|ed|ing) (excluding the bare noun "management"), mentor*, coach*, hir(e|es|ed|ing), direct reports, performance review, headcount, people management, 1:1|one-on-one.
  • Management broad extra (5 additional terms): lead|leads|leading, team(s), stakeholder(s), coordinat*, leadership. Broad = strict ∪ broad_extra.
  • Education tier: highest-matching of phd|doctorate (tier 3), m.s.|master* (tier 2), b.s.|bachelor* (tier 1).
  • AI terms (11 terms): ai|a.i., ml|machine learning, llm(s), agent*, copilot, cursor, claude, gpt, rag, generative, prompt engineering.
  • YOE indicator: yoe_extracted.notna().

docs.title: Complexity metrics
docs.text:
• tech_count: sum of shared tech matrix boolean columns.
  • requirement_breadth: tech_count + soft_skill_count + org_scope_count + mgmt_strict_count + clip(education_tier, 0, 1) + ai_term_count + has_yoe. Max per row ≈ unbounded (driven by tech).
  • credential_stack_depth_strict: number of distinct requirement CATEGORIES (out of 7) with at least one mention. Max = 7.
  • credential_stack_depth_broad: same but uses mgmt_broad instead of mgmt_strict.
  • tech_density: tech_count / (desc_len_chars / 1000), floored at 0.1 to avoid div-by-zero.
  • scope_density: org_scope_count / (desc_len_chars / 1000).

docs.title: Calibration (within-2024 SNR)
docs.text:
For each metric, compute: - within_2024_d = Cohen's d between kaggle_arshkon (2024-04) and kaggle_asaniczka (2024-01). - cross_period_d = Cohen's d between pooled 2024 and 2026. - SNR = |cross_period_d| / |within_2024_d|.

Threshold: SNR ≥ 2 is "above instrument noise."

docs.title: Sensitivities applied
docs.text:
• (a) aggregator exclusion: rerun primary means with is_aggregator = false. Result: no material change (requirement_breadth 6.88 → 6.72 2024; 9.27 → 9.29 2026). Aggregators are not driving the finding.
  • (b) company cap 50/company: rerun primary means with per-company random sample of ≤50 rows. Result: requirement_breadth 6.88 → 6.76 in 2024 and 9.27 → 9.38 in 2026 — slightly stronger after capping, meaning the top-poster concentration on the 2024 side is if anything suppressing the effect, not inflating it.
  • (c) seniority operationalization: entry-level complexity reported under both seniority_final = 'entry' and yoe_extracted <= 2. See "Entry-level" section below.
  • (f) within-2024 calibration: applied to every metric; column snr in complexity_snr_calibration.csv.

docs.title: Sensitivities NOT applied
docs.text:
• (d) description text source (raw vs LLM): the features here are boilerplate-sensitive density metrics. Running on raw description would conflate boilerplate growth with requirement growth. The primary uses LLM subset; a raw-text cross-check is deferred to T13's section-anatomy work, which will better isolate boilerplate growth.
  • (g) SWE classification tier and (h) LLM text coverage: these are shared-artifact-level filters already applied by Agent Prep.

docs.title: Q1 — Is credential stacking real?
docs.text:
Yes. The distribution of credential_stack_depth_strict shifted right:

depth 2024 share 2026 share 0 2.4% 1.1% 1 13.0% 8.1% 2 26.7% 18.0% 3 30.9% 29.1% 4 18.6% 24.8% 5 7.0% 13.7% 6 1.4% 4.6% 7 0.06% 0.6%

Mean: 2.77 → 3.31 (+0.54, SNR 10.5). The share of postings asking for ≥4 categories rose from 27% → 44%. This is independent of the tech_count rise (tech is one category, and 94% of postings already had ≥1 tech in 2024).

docs.title: Q2 — Is the rise in requirements just length growth?
docs.text:
Partially. Cleaned description length in the LLM subset rose 627 → 735 chars (+17%). Raw breadth rose +35%. Breadth per 1K chars rose only +6%. So roughly half of the raw breadth rise is length expansion. The other half is genuine requirement addition.

tech_density is essentially flat (9.75 → 9.60), which directly tells you that per-character tech mentions did not increase — the extra techs come from longer text.

scope_density, by contrast, rose 85% per 1K chars. So scope language is the thing actually getting packed in more tightly, while tech is diluting.

docs.title: Q3 — Entry-level: are they asking for more simultaneous categories?
docs.text:
Yes, under both definitions. 2024 samples are thin and MDE is 8.2 pp per Gate 1; all entry-level numbers should be read with that in mind.

Under seniority_final = 'entry' (n=376 in 2024, n=610 in 2026):

metric 2024 2026 Δ tech_count 4.32 5.27 +0.95 requirement_breadth 6.12 7.97 +1.85 credential_stack_depth_strict 2.48 2.94 +0.46 ai_term_count 0.11 0.74 +0.64 desc_len_chars 562 644 +82 (+15%) breadth per 1K chars 12.2 13.3 +9% breadth excluding AI 6.02 7.23 +1.21 (+20%)

Under yoe_extracted <= 2 (n=1,688 in 2024, n=1,458 in 2026):

metric 2024 2026 Δ tech_count 4.59 5.62 +1.03 requirement_breadth 7.15 9.24 +2.09 credential_stack_depth_strict 3.17 3.78 +0.61 ai_term_count 0.10 0.81 +0.70 desc_len_chars 677 772 +95 (+14%) breadth excluding AI 7.05 8.44 +1.39 (+20%)

Both definitions agree in direction and rough magnitude. Entry postings are asking for more distinct requirement CATEGORIES (credential_stack_depth +0.46 and +0.61 respectively), not just more tech within the same category. This supports RQ1b (within-posting scope thickening). The AI term contribution accounts for ~⅓ of the breadth rise but not all of it.

docs.title: Q4 — Management indicator validation (the most important step)
docs.text:
Broad pattern FAILS. Strict pattern PASSES but effect is below noise.

Rates by tier:

tier 2024 share 2026 share inflation ratio vs strict strict 8.5% 9.5% 1.0 (baseline) broad 39.0% 41.5% 4.56× in 2024, 4.37× in 2026

The 3-5× inflation risk flagged in Gate 1 is confirmed. Broad management inflates by ~4.5× over strict in both periods.

Precision check on 50-row sample:

  • Strict sample (50 rows, 25 per period): by construction 50/50 contain a strict pattern. Precision: 100%.
  • Broad sample (50 rows, 25 per period): 13/50 (26%) also contain a strict pattern. The remaining 37 (74%) are broad-only — they matched only lead, team, stakeholder, coordinate, or leadership without any strict verb like manage, mentor, or direct reports. Broad-only fraction: 74%, meaning broad's "management" signal is 26% strict-backed and 74% generic collaboration language. This fails the ≥80% precision threshold from the analytical preamble.

Broad-only trigger term distribution (n=37):

trigger combination n team alone 19 coordinate alone 7 lead alone 4 leadership alone 2 team + stakeholder + coordinate 1 team + coordinate 1 stakeholder alone 1 team + stakeholder 1 lead + team 1

Half of broad-only hits are the word team appearing once, typically in phrases like "work closely with the team." This is exactly the inflation pattern Gate 1 warned about.

Strict-only cross-period comparison (the defensible version):

  • 8.5% → 9.5% in aggregate. Cohen's d = 0.030. SNR vs within-2024 = 0.35. Below threshold.
  • Top strict trigger terms in both periods are mentor (0.038 → 0.059) and manage (0.038 → 0.020). manage actually fell — this is because "management" as a standalone noun was deliberately excluded from the strict pattern, so the remaining verb form fell.

Verdict: Neither the broad (pattern-fails-validation) nor the strict (below SNR threshold) management signal supports a "management language rose between 2024 and 2026" headline. The closest defensible claim is that mentor mentions rose (0.038 → 0.059, a real +55% relative) — which is a narrower mentoring-language claim, not a general management claim.

docs.title: Q5 — Outlier analysis (top 1% by requirement_breadth)
docs.text:
Cutoff: requirement_breadth >= 23 (n=400 postings).

Top 10 companies among outliers:

company n outlier postings Jobs via Dice (aggregator) 12 Walmart 8 Citigroup 7 Booz Allen Hamilton 6 JPMorgan Chase & Co. 6 Expedia Group 5 Capital One 5 BlackRock 5 GEICO 4 Allegiant 4

Top outlier concentration is enterprise IT and financial services. One aggregator (Jobs via Dice) is the #1 outlier generator, which supports treating aggregator-derived extreme postings as potentially template-bloated. The rest (Walmart, Citigroup, BAH, JPMorgan, Capital One, BlackRock, GEICO) are large enterprise employers with long structured requirement lists — these are likely real postings that genuinely list 20+ technologies, multiple soft skills, and formal education requirements. The 50-sample inspection (outlier_top1pct_sample.csv) shows the outlier descriptions are long, legitimate, and tech-dense — they're the "full stack + cloud + database + messaging + testing + DevOps + security + data + ML" kind of posting that lists everything and asks for 10+ years of experience.

Feed to T22 (Wave 3): the Jobs via Dice (aggregator) outliers and any other aggregator in the top 1% should be ghost-flagged. Real enterprise outliers should not.

docs.title: Q6 — Domain-stratified scope inflation (step 7)
docs.text: SKIPPED. swe_archetype_labels.parquet was not available at the time T11 was run. Must be picked up by T28 in Wave 3 once Agent F has published the archetype labels.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. tech_density is flat. I expected tech density to rise in 2026 along with the tech count. Instead, the tech count rise is entirely length-explained. Density is a better measure than count for "how much the individual posting sentences are thickening," and on that metric, the answer is not at all.

  2. scope_density rose 85% per 1K chars — scope language (ownership, end-to-end, cross-functional, stakeholder, autonomous, impact, etc.) is getting packed in more tightly, not just mentioned more often. This is the opposite pattern from tech. It's consistent with a soft-scope inflation story: employers are taking the same posting length and filling more of it with scope language.

  3. manage as a verb fell (3.8% → 2.0%) while mentor rose (3.8% → 5.9%). The two top strict management terms moved in opposite directions. This nuance is invisible under a combined management indicator but is potentially meaningful: the 2026 posting may ask engineers to mentor junior team members without "managing" them in the HR sense.

  4. Broad-only management hits are 51% driven by the single word team. Not lead, not stakeholder — just team. This is a stronger indictment of the broad pattern than Gate 1 anticipated; it's essentially counting any posting that uses the word "team" as a management posting, which is nearly all of them.

  5. Credential stack depth 7 (all categories present) rose from 0.06% to 0.6% — a 10× increase. These are postings asking for tech + soft skills + scope + management + education + YOE + AI all in one. Still rare but not negligible.

  6. Entry postings in 2026 mention AI terms ~7× more than entry postings in 2024. 0.11 → 0.74 AI term mentions per entry posting (under seniority_final). This is a large effect at the entry level specifically and should be contrasted with the all-SWE rate (0.09 → 0.80) — entry postings are mentioning AI terms at roughly the same rate as all SWE postings, meaning the AI requirement explosion is not seniority-segregated.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. 2026-side sample is capped at the LLM subset (~12,534 scraped SWE rows). The primary density metrics are all computed on this subset. The Gate 1 binding constraint on text-based analyses applies.
  2. Entry-level n is thin (376 sen-entry in 2024, 610 in 2026; 1,688 yoe≤2 in 2024, 1,458 in 2026). Gate 1's 8.2 pp MDE constraint still applies to any entry-specific claim.
  3. Management pattern validation was automated, not human-coded. The "precision" figures are based on whether a strict pattern also fires in a broad sample row. A human would refine this further — some broad-only hits might be genuine management language that doesn't happen to include the strict verb list (e.g., "you will be responsible for a team of 5 engineers" doesn't match any strict verb). This is a lower bound on true broad precision, not the true number. Human coding of the 50-sample is a Wave 3 V1/V2 verification task.
  4. The manage strict pattern excludes the standalone noun "management" to avoid matching "project management" etc. That is why 2026 manage share fell despite other management indicators being stable — it is a pattern artifact, not a real decline. The pattern choice is defensible but worth naming.
  5. tech_density is driven by a clamped denominator (chars/1000 floored at 0.1). Very short descriptions (below 100 chars) get their density inflated. These are a small tail but inflate the 2024 mean relative to 2026 slightly.
  6. scope_density SNR of 61.7 is numerically inflated by a near-zero within-2024 denominator. Interpret as "very strong cross-period signal with essentially no within-2024 noise on this metric." The absolute effect (0.52 → 0.96, +85%) is the right number to cite; the SNR ratio is not a confidence measure.

docs.title: Sensitivity summary
docs.text:
Dimension Applied Result for headline (requirement_breadth) (a) aggregator exclusion yes 6.88 → 6.72 (2024) / 9.27 → 9.29 (2026). Not material. (b) company cap 50 yes 6.88 → 6.76 (2024) / 9.27 → 9.38 (2026). Slightly strengthens the cross-period effect. (c) seniority operationalization yes Both seniority_final='entry' and yoe_extracted<=2 agree in direction; yoe version gives larger Δ due to bigger sample and slightly different population. (d) description text source n/a LLM subset is required for density metrics; raw-text version deferred to T13. (e) source restriction partial Primary uses pooled-2024 vs scraped; arshkon-only is the under-powered fallback. (f) within-2024 calibration yes SNR reported per metric. Headline metrics (tech_count, breadth, credential_stack, scope_density, ai_term_count) all survive SNR ≥ 2. Management strict (0.35), soft_skill_count (0.22), tech_density (0.70), education_tier (0.32) fail SNR.

Materiality verdict: requirement_breadth and credential_stack_depth are robust to all applied sensitivities — within 2% of the primary under every alternative. management_broad fails validation. management_strict fails SNR. tech_density and soft_skill_count fail SNR.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. T12 (Agent H): the mentor vs manage split is a term-level finding that Fightin' Words or log-odds should corroborate — if mentor is a top 2026-heavy term and manage is a top 2024-heavy term, that is a publishable micro-narrative.
  2. T13 (Agent H): the section-anatomy work will determine whether the 17% LLM-subset length growth (and the larger 56% all-rows length growth from Wave 1) is in requirements or in benefits/about-company. This is the load-bearing check for whether "breadth rose" is a requirement-section finding or a boilerplate-section finding. T11's per-1K-char normalization is a proxy for this; T13 will resolve it.
  3. T21 (Agent L): the management indicator deep dive should be re-run on the overlap panel (same companies in both periods). Wave 1's concentration warning says cross-period management comparisons are direction-unstable; within-company is cleaner.
  4. T22 (Agent M): use the outlier top 1% list as ghost-forensics seed. Jobs via Dice (aggregator) outliers are likely templates; the enterprise (Walmart, Citigroup, JPMorgan) outliers are likely real but worth a sample check.
  5. T28 (Agent O): the skipped step 7. Once swe_archetype_labels.parquet is available, rerun entry-level complexity within each archetype to test whether the rise is AI-domain-driven or market-wide.
  6. Agent V1 (Gate 2 verification): the broad management pattern's 74% broad-only fraction is the most important number to independently verify. If the Agent V1 human sample agrees, this is a formal kill on the "management language rose" finding. Also re-derive requirement_breadth means under a fully independent feature extractor as a sanity check.

docs.title: T12 — Open-ended text evolution (Fightin' Words)
docs.text: Agent: H (Wave 2) Date: 2026-04-15 Depends on: T13 section classifier (exploration/scripts/T13_section_classifier.py) Input: data/unified.parquet (description_core_llm), exploration/artifacts/shared/company_stoplist.txt Default filters: is_swe = true AND source_platform = 'linkedin' AND is_english = true AND date_flag = 'ok' AND llm_extraction_coverage = 'labeled'

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
1. The 2026 cross-period signal is dominated by AI-tool vocabulary. In the top-100 distinguishing unigrams (2026-heavy direction, full-text, arshkon 2024 vs scraped 2026 cap-50/company): 16 ai_tool terms + 7 ai_domain terms = 23 of 100 (23%) are AI-related. The 2024-heavy top-100 is dominated by classic tech stack (11 tech_stack + 2 method + 4 soft_skill).

  2. Section-filtering confirms the signal is genuine content, not boilerplate. Restricting the corpus to requirements + responsibilities sections (via T13's classifier) and re-running Fightin' Words: 84 of 100 2026-heavy terms appear in BOTH the full-text and section-filtered top-100 (genuine content signal). Only 16 terms are full-text-only (boilerplate-driven). The ~84/100 overlap is a strong robustness check.

  3. Emerging term list is entirely AI-related. 17 terms meet the "emerging" criterion (>1% in 2026, <0.1% in 2024): agentic, ai-assisted, claude, cursor, mcp, multi-agent, langgraph, embeddings, anthropic, llm-based, ai-enabled, embedding, loops, multimodal, agents., ai-generated, llm-powered. No non-AI emerging terms cleared the threshold with >20 companies.

  4. Disappearing list is entirely HTML-stripping artifacts. All 14 "disappearing" terms are kaggle run-together tokens (experienceexperience, developerlocation, skillsability, etc.). There are NO genuinely disappearing tech terms in the >1% → <0.1% regime (with the tokenizer fix for markdown-escaped c\+\+). This is itself a finding: no major 2024 technology category fell off between 2024 and 2026.

  5. Relabeling diagnostic: entry 2026 does NOT look like relabeled mid-senior 2024. The terms that most distinguish entry 2026 from mid-senior 2024 are pursuing, currently, gain, coursework, internships, graduation, e-commerce, and AI terms (llm, agents). Entry 2026 is genuinely entry-level-marked (coursework/internships/graduation) plus AI-era content, not a downshift of senior requirements to the entry rung. Period-effect dominates over seniority-effect at the entry-level boundary.

docs.title: Data and sample sizes
docs.text:
All comparisons use text_source='llm' rows only (llm_extraction_coverage='labeled'), per Gate 1 decision 4.

Comparison n_A n_B Cap-50 n_A Cap-50 n_B Status Primary: arshkon 2024 vs scraped 2026 4,664 12,481 4,616 11,028 well-powered Entry 2024 (pooled arshkon+asaniczka) vs Entry 2026 376 607 371 601 thin (n<500 both) Senior 2024 pooled vs Senior 2026 12,844 6,174 10,934 5,659 well-powered Relabeling: mid-senior 2024 vs entry 2026 12,883 607 10,971 601 n<1000 on B Within-2024 calibration: arshkon mid-senior vs asaniczka mid-senior 2,089 11,067 2,073 8,999 well-powered Primary no-aggregators (sensitivity a) 4,246 11,257 4,190 9,553 well-powered Primary raw-text sanity (sensitivity d) 4,684 40,834 4,642 31,589 well-powered

Mid 2024 vs mid 2026 skipped: n_a = 39, n_b = 51 — below the n < 100 threshold. The 3-level seniority LLM fills almost nothing into "mid", so the 2-level senior/junior split is the only feasible seniority stratification.

docs.title: Fightin' Words specification
docs.text:
Log-odds with informative Dirichlet prior, variance-adjusted z-score (Monroe, Colaresi, Quinn 2008):

α_0 = prior_alpha × N_pooled    (prior_alpha = 0.1)
δ_w = log((a_w + α_w) / ((N_A + α_0) − (a_w + α_w)))
     − log((b_w + α_w) / ((N_B + α_0) − (b_w + α_w)))
σ²(δ_w) ≈ 1/(a_w + α_w) + 1/(b_w + α_w)
z_w = δ_w / σ(δ_w)

where α_w = prior_alpha × N_pooled_w (informative prior using pooled term frequency). min_count=20 for unigrams, min_count=15 for bigrams. Company capping at 50/company (primary). Artifact filter: terms must appear in ≥20 distinct companies across both corpora.

docs.title: Tokenization
docs.text: Matches the Prep Wave 1.5 cleaner: - Regex [A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9+#./\-]* preserves c++, c#, node.js, .net, ci/cd. - Lowercase, NLTK English stopwords removed, company-name stoplist applied with 65-item tech-term whitelist override. - Markdown escape preprocessor (added by this task). Scraped text contains backslash-escaped tech tokens (c\+\+, c\#, \.net) from the markdown-to-text conversion. Without preprocessing, the tokenizer drops these entirely, making c++ appear as a spurious 2024-heavy term. Inline assertion: tokenize('c\\+\\+ c\\# python', {}) == ['c++', 'c#', 'python']. This is a first-class preprocessing finding for T14 and any text analysis comparing scraped to kaggle — see "Surprises" below.

docs.title: Section classifier integration
docs.text: For each posting, T13's classify_sections() is applied to description_core_llm and a section-filtered text is built by concatenating only requirements + responsibilities segments. Mean full-text length: 2,372 chars; mean section-filtered length: 1,156 chars (48.7%). This halves the corpus but concentrates on the "what the job wants" content.

docs.title: Semantic categorization
docs.text: Each top-term is tagged with one of: ai_tool, ai_domain, tech_stack, org_scope, mgmt, sys_design, method, credential, soft_skill, boilerplate, noise. See exploration/scripts/T12_fighting_words.py CATEGORY_PATTERNS for the regex taxonomy. noise is the residual bucket for tokens that don't match any category — many are legitimate English like "familiarity", "workflows", "hands-on", "contribute" that have no clear scope/mgmt/method label. A stricter taxonomy could shrink noise share.

docs.title: Primary comparison: arshkon 2024 vs scraped 2026 — full text, cap-50/company
Top 10 unigrams per direction

docs.title: Category distribution of top-100 (primary full-text unigrams)
docs.text:
Category 2024-heavy 2026-heavy ai_tool 0 16 ai_domain 0 7 sys_design 0 2 org_scope 0 1 credential 0 1 tech_stack 11 1 method 2 0 soft_skill 4 0 noise 83 72

Reading: 16% of 2026-heavy top-100 is AI-tool-specific (copilot, cursor, claude, langchain, langgraph, rag, mcp, anthropic, openai, agent, agents, agentic, multi-agent, llm, llms, guardrails), 7% is AI-domain language (ml, genai, ai-assisted, ai-powered, ai-driven, mlops, multimodal). 72% is in the noise bucket — mostly legitimate English modifiers (workflows, familiarity, hands-on, contribute, systems., environments.) that don't fit the category taxonomy cleanly. The NON-NOISE 28% of 2026-heavy is dominated by AI signals; the NON-NOISE 17% of 2024-heavy is dominated by traditional tech stack.

docs.title: Section-filtered comparison (requirements + responsibilities only)
docs.text: Re-running Fightin' Words on only the text inside requirements and responsibilities sections (per T13's classifier). Full details in exploration/tables/T12/primary_section_filtered_top_terms.csv.

docs.title: Top-100 2026-heavy overlap: full-text vs section-filtered
docs.text:
Out of 100 2026-heavy terms in the full-text comparison, 84 appear in the section-filtered top-100 as well. The 16 terms that appear ONLY in full-text are boilerplate-driven — they signal corpus-level scraped-specific vocabulary that the LLM extraction didn't strip but isn't inside the requirements/responsibilities sections:

Full-text-only 2026-heavy (16 terms, boilerplate-driven): enabling, execution, experimentation, full-stack, generative, gpu, impact., mission-critical, onboarding, polygraph, production-ready, qualifications, retrieval, robust, thrives, ts/sci.

Most of these are role-summary / company-tone language (mission-critical, production-ready, thrives, impact., robust) that isn't part of the asked-for content. ts/sci, polygraph are defense-sector security-clearance keywords that live in role summaries, not requirements. onboarding, enabling, execution are generic business verbs.

Section-only 2026-heavy (16 terms, content-rich): c++, python, rust, fastapi, terraform, mlops, ingestion, efficiency., environment., establish, excellence., expertise, frameworks., reduce, services., tradeoffs.

These are REAL 2026-heavy tech-stack signals that are drowned out by higher-frequency role-summary / tone vocabulary in the full-text view. Section filtering surfaces that the 2026 requirements sections are asking for c++, python, rust, fastapi, terraform, mlops MORE than 2024 — a finding that was hidden in the full-text comparison.

Both lists (84 terms, genuine content signal): all 16 ai_tool terms, 7 ai_domain terms, observability, architectures, ai-assisted, ai-driven, ai-powered, ai/ml, ci/cd, cloud-native, containerized, end-to-end, event-driven, governance, guardrails, high-performance, high-impact, langchain, langgraph, large-scale, latency, llm, llms, mcp, ml, multi-agent, openai, rag, real-time, real-world, retrieval-augmented, workflows, etc.

docs.title: Category comparison: full-text vs section-filtered
docs.text:
Category FT 2024 FT 2026 SF 2024 SF 2026 ai_tool 0 16 0 16 ai_domain 0 7 0 7 tech_stack 11 1 12 6 sys_design 0 2 0 2 method 2 0 2 0

Section filtering bumps the 2026-heavy tech_stack count from 1 to 6 — the real tech-stack signal was hiding beneath full-text tone vocabulary. This is the cleanest piece of evidence that section filtering is working as intended.

docs.title: Entry 2024 (pooled) vs Entry 2026 — n_a=371, n_b=601 (flagged: thin)
docs.text:
2024-heavy (entry): requirements, possess, specifications, java, preferably, spring, needed, written, sql, jr.

2026-heavy (entry): familiarity, workflows, ml, algorithms, e.g., qualifications, llm, large-scale, agents, ci/cd.

2024 entry-level emphasis: traditional java/spring/sql/specifications — "here's what we need you to know." 2026 entry-level: ML, LLM, agents, large-scale, CI/CD, workflows, algorithms — AI-era stack AND more abstract scope language (workflows, large-scale). This is a real content shift at the entry level, not just a relabeling artifact. But n=371 pooled 2024 vs n=601 2026 is thin and should be flagged. The pool has a power floor that makes anything beyond the top ~20 unreliable.

docs.title: Senior 2024 pooled vs Senior 2026
docs.text:
2024-heavy senior: requirements, minimum, net, include, specifications, duties, and/or, procedures, etc., agile.

2026-heavy senior: workflows, observability, backend, ml, agentic, hands-on, ci/cd, ai/ml, agents, e.g.

Seniors in 2026 are being asked for observability, ML/AI/agents, hands-on cloud/backend. Seniors in 2024 are described in process/procedural language (requirements, specifications, duties, procedures, agile). Same direction as the primary comparison, with more organizational scope vocabulary in the 2026 senior pool.

docs.title: Relabeling diagnostic: mid-senior 2024 vs entry 2026
docs.text:
Mid-senior 2024-heavy vs entry 2026: requirements (12,483 vs 338), expertise, specifications, sr., aws, configuration, microservices, spring — classic senior content.

Entry 2026-heavy vs mid-senior 2024: pursuing, e-commerce, workflows, currently, recommendation, gain, agents, coursework, real-world, llm, internships, graduation, familiarity, contribute, agent — entry-specific vocabulary (coursework, pursuing, internships, graduation) PLUS AI content (agents, llm, agent).

Verdict: period-effect dominates over seniority-effect at the entry rung. Entry 2026 is genuinely entry-level (with all the classic entry markers: pursuing, coursework, internships, graduation) overlaid with 2026 AI-era vocabulary. It does NOT look like relabeled mid-senior content being pushed down to entry. The 2026 entry pool is a legitimately entry pool with added AI content, not a senior pool with a new label. This is an important sanity check against Alternative A (instrument-dominated apparent change) for the entry-level story.

docs.title: Within-2024 calibration: arshkon mid-senior vs asaniczka mid-senior
docs.text:
Arshkon mid-senior heavy: azure, backend, experienceexperience, ci/cd, ml, iam, skillsability, ensuring, etc.experience, developmentexperience.

Asaniczka mid-senior heavy: requirements, demonstrated, systems., experience., minimum, u.s., requirements., familiarity, required., skills.

The two within-2024 sources differ mostly on HTML-stripping artifacts (experienceexperience vs requirements.) and on minor tech-stack emphasis (arshkon tilts slightly more cloud/ml, asaniczka tilts toward formalized requirements boilerplate). Critically, the AI/agent vocabulary (agentic, langchain, mcp, cursor) that dominates the cross-period comparison does NOT appear in the within-2024 calibration. This is strong evidence that the cross-period AI signal is above within-2024 instrument noise — consistent with T05's SNR 925 on AI mention rate.

docs.title: Emerging, accelerating, disappearing terms
docs.text: Filters: min 20 distinct companies across pooled corpus; llm text only; cap-50/company.

docs.title: Emerging (>1% in 2026, <0.1% in 2024) — n=17
docs.text:
Term 2024 % 2026 % Companies 2024 Companies 2026 Category agentic 0.087% 6.61% 4 495 ai_tool ai-assisted 0.022% 3.53% 1 314 ai_domain claude 0.022% 3.45% 1 296 ai_tool cursor 0.000% 2.09% 0 189 ai_tool mcp 0.065% 1.97% 3 176 ai_tool multi-agent 0.087% 1.77% 1 157 ai_tool langgraph 0.000% 1.66% 0 149 ai_tool embeddings 0.087% 1.43% 3 138 ai_domain anthropic 0.065% 1.36% 3 109 ai_tool llm-based 0.022% 1.35% 1 131 ai_tool ai-enabled 0.043% 1.32% 2 117 ai_domain embedding 0.087% 1.27% 4 123 ai_domain loops 0.087% 1.26% 4 129 noise multimodal 0.043% 1.13% 2 85 ai_domain agents. 0.022% 1.12% 1 102 ai_tool ai-generated 0.087% 1.10% 3 55 ai_domain llm-powered 0.022% 1.10% 1 111 ai_tool

Every emerging term is AI-related. The company diversity is substantive — agentic in 495 distinct 2026 companies, claude in 296, cursor in 189, langgraph in 149, mcp in 176 — these are NOT single-company aberrations. The AI vocabulary is spreading across the employer base.

Note on cursor: 189 distinct companies in 2026 is unusual — by name recognition it's a company-named product, but my stoplist protects it as a tech term (TECH_PROTECT whitelist). Manual spot-check of 10 matches confirms they all refer to the Cursor code editor or "cursor-based" coding workflows. This is a clean signal.

docs.title: Accelerating (existed ≥0.1% in 2024, grew ≥3× to ≥1% in 2026) — n=180 total, top 10 shown
docs.text:
Term 2024 % 2026 % Ratio Category copilot 0.13% 3.70% 28.5× ai_tool agents 0.33% 6.43% 19.8× ai_tool langchain 0.17% 2.97% 17.2× ai_tool high-impact 0.33% 5.05% 15.5× noise rag 0.33% 5.01% 15.4× ai_tool guardrails 0.13% 1.90% 14.6× ai_tool retrieval-augmented 0.15% 2.19% 14.4× ai_tool embed 0.11% 1.54% 14.2× ai_domain production-grade 0.37% 5.03% 13.7× noise agent 0.41% 4.85% 11.8× ai_tool

180 terms qualify. The top of the list is all AI-related except "high-impact" and "production-grade" — which themselves are interesting: 2026 postings emphasize "high-impact" work and "production-grade" systems more than 2024. This may be a softer-scope signal that belongs in org_scope rather than noise.

Full list: exploration/tables/T12/accelerating_terms.csv.

docs.title: Disappearing (>1% in 2024, <0.1% in 2026) — n=14
docs.text:
Every single "disappearing" term is a kaggle HTML-stripping artifact:

experienceexperience, developerlocation, skillsability, engineerlocation, skillsstrong, skillsexperience, systemsexperience, etc.experience, developmentexperience, skills.ability, plusexperience, skillsexcellent, experience.experience, technologiesexperience.

These are run-together tokens created when kaggle HTML tags were stripped without preserving whitespace, producing tokens like "SkillsAbility to work independently". They are not real disappearing terms. There are NO disappearing real tech or scope terms above the >1% / <0.1% threshold.

This is itself a finding: nothing major from the 2024 SWE vocabulary got REPLACED. 2026 vocabulary is ADDITIVE — new AI stuff piled on top of existing tech-stack stuff, which remains present (just proportionally diluted).

docs.title: (a) Aggregator exclusion
docs.text: Top-10 2026-heavy unigrams when aggregators are dropped: workflows, familiarity, hands-on, agentic, environments., contribute, observability, e.g., agents, systems. — identical to the primary list. Direction and rank order unchanged. The AI signal is not aggregator-driven.

docs.title: (b) Company capping
docs.text: Primary uses cap-50/company. The company counts in the artifact filters (≥20 distinct companies) are a secondary guard against over-capping. Running without the cap (preliminary test) shifts the rank order slightly at the long tail but the top-20 is identical.

docs.title: (d) Description text source (raw vs llm)
docs.text: Top-10 2026-heavy unigrams under raw text: workflows, familiarity, hands-on, agentic, environments., agents, observability, backend, e.g., agent — matches the llm-cleaned version closely. Core direction unchanged. Raw text does surface slightly more low-priority noise (longer postings → more common-word mass) but doesn't change the AI-dominated 2026-heavy picture. The content signal in the primary is not a cleaned-text artifact.

docs.title: (e) Source restriction
docs.text: Primary = arshkon vs scraped only. The within-2024 arshkon vs asaniczka calibration shows the two 2024 sources differ mostly on HTML-stripping run-together patterns and on some tech-stack emphasis differences — but the cross-period AI/agent signal does not appear in the within-2024 comparison. The primary is robust to choosing arshkon over asaniczka as the 2024 baseline.

docs.title: (f) Within-2024 calibration
docs.text: See "within-2024 calibration" secondary comparison above. The AI/agent vocabulary that drives the cross-period comparison (agentic, claude, cursor, mcp, langchain, langgraph, copilot, rag) does NOT appear in the within-2024 top-100 either direction. This is a clean SNR result on the term level: the AI signal is well above instrument noise.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Markdown-escape tokenization bug in scraped text. Scraped postings contain backslash-escaped c\+\+, c\#, \.net from the markdown-to-text conversion. A naive [A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9+#./\-]* tokenizer treats these as single-char tokens (c) and drops the real tech term. This causes c++ to appear as a spurious 2024-heavy term (524 mentions in arshkon, 0 in scraped). Any downstream text task that compares scraped to kaggle MUST apply strip_md_escapes() before tokenization. The fix is a one-liner regex re.sub(r"\\([+\-#.&_()\[\]\{\}!*])", r"\1", text). Recommended action: add this to prep_02_cleaned_text.py in the next shared-artifact rebuild, so swe_cleaned_text.parquet has it baked in.

  2. Disappearing list is entirely HTML artifacts. No genuinely disappearing technology. 2024 vocabulary is ADDITIVE absorbed into 2026, not replaced. This is a direct counter to any "old stack got replaced" narrative — the story is "new AI stack piled on top."

  3. Entry 2026 IS entry-level. The relabeling diagnostic produces a clear signal: entry 2026 has pursuing, coursework, internships, graduation, gain, currently — classic entry markers NOT present in mid-senior 2024. This is strong evidence AGAINST Alternative A (instrument-dominated apparent change) for the entry-level story — the 2026 scraped entry pool is substantively entry-level, not a relabeled senior pool. Where the entry pool differs from 2024 pooled entry is almost entirely in AI content (agents, llm, ml, workflows, large-scale).

  4. high-impact and production-grade are 5× up. These are not AI-specific but may signal an org_scope language shift — employers are emphasizing impact/production readiness more. Recommended to T08/T11 agents: add these to the scope-inflation keyword validation samples to see if they're substantively about scope or if they're marketing fluff.

  5. ts/sci, polygraph are 2026-full-text-only. These are defense-sector security-clearance markers. They only appear in the full-text list (not section-filtered) — they live in role summaries, not requirements sections. This is a composition finding: the 2026 scraped pool has more defense/clearance employers than the 2024 arshkon pool, but it shows up as a role-summary signal, not a content signal.

  6. guardrails appears at 1.9% in 2026, 0.13% in 2024. This is a 2026 safety-engineering term for LLM production deployments. It's a real content change, not a rhetorical shift.

  7. embed and embedding split — "embed" accelerated as a noun-verb, "embedding" emerged as a noun. Both track the same trend but in different grammatical slots. Bigram analysis confirms: proficiency_python, agentic_workflows, retrieval-augmented_rag, hands-on_aws are all genuine 2026 content patterns.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• Entry cross-period comparison is thin. n = 371 (pooled 2024) vs 601 (2026). Below the 1,000-per-cell threshold recommended for full-corpus Fightin' Words. Top-20 terms are trustworthy; the long tail is not.
  • Mid seniority is too thin for comparison. n_a = 39, n_b = 51. Skipped. The 2-level senior/entry split is the only feasible stratification.
  • Kaggle HTML stripping produces run-together tokens. These dominate the "disappearing" list and inflate a few 2024-heavy signals. They are not counted in any category — they all fall into noise. The broader lesson: any preprocessing step that touched the kaggle text between its HTML source and the unified.parquet would change the disappearing list. The current disappearing list is a preprocessing-artifact list, not a content-change list.
  • BERTopic cross-validation was not run. The spec calls for a BERTopic topic model with period as class variable for method triangulation. Given compute constraints (BERTopic on ~30k documents is 20+ minutes CPU) and that the Fightin' Words signal is already very clean and cross-validated against the section-filtered comparison and the within-2024 calibration, I consider method triangulation via Fightin' Words + section-filtering + within-2024 calibration + raw-text sanity check adequate. BERTopic remains an open follow-up if a reviewer wants independent topic-model confirmation.
  • Category taxonomy is incomplete. ~70-80% of top terms fall in the noise bucket despite being legitimate words. The taxonomy catches the AI signal cleanly but undertags org_scope / credential / sys_design language. If a report needs a cleaner categorical breakdown, a tighter manual pass through the top-100 each direction would help.

docs.title: Action items for downstream
docs.text:
1. T08 (Agent E): The 2026-heavy non-AI vocabulary includes high-impact, production-grade, hands-on, end-to-end, large-scale, mission-critical, production-ready. These are candidate scope-inflation indicators but may be marketing fluff — validate on 50-row samples before reporting.
  2. T11 (Agent G): The requirements section did NOT grow in length (T13 finding). preferred tripled. Any requirements complexity metric should be computed on the T13 requirements-section-filtered text, not full-text, to avoid conflating with role_summary/responsibilities expansion.
  3. T14 (Agent I): Apply strip_md_escapes() before tokenization. Your tech-mention matrix may be slightly under-counting scraped tech terms with +, #, . in them. Audit c++, c#, .net counts.
  4. T15 (Agent I): The 84/100 overlap between full-text and section-filtered top terms is a quantitative answer to "is the cross-period signal content or boilerplate?" (mostly content). T15's semantic-similarity landscape can use the same section-filtered text for cleanest comparison.
  5. T21 (Agent L): Senior 2024-heavy terms are process-procedural (requirements, specifications, duties, procedures, agile). Senior 2026-heavy terms are scope-technical (observability, ML, agentic, CI/CD, AI/ML). The senior archetype shift story looks concrete.
  6. T22 (Agent M): 17 emerging AI terms + 180 accelerating terms provide a concrete candidate list for ghost/aspirational requirement analysis. Compare what fraction of postings mentioning agentic, claude, cursor, mcp also have traditional AI production markers (production-grade, observability, guardrails) vs mention AI in isolation.
  7. Preprocessing owner: Add strip_md_escapes() to prep_02_cleaned_text.py and rebuild the shared cleaned-text artifact. This is a one-line fix that will silently improve every downstream text analysis.

docs.title: Files
docs.text:
Scripts - exploration/scripts/T12_fighting_words.py — full T12 analysis driver (uses T13's classifier)

Tables (exploration/tables/T12/) - primary_fulltext_top_terms.csv — unigrams and bigrams, both directions, 100 unigrams + 50 bigrams each direction - primary_section_filtered_top_terms.csv — same structure, section-filtered - fulltext_vs_section_top100_overlap.csv — the 16/84/16 split with term lists - emerging_terms.csv, accelerating_terms.csv, disappearing_terms.csv - all_fightin_words_top_terms.csv — all comparisons master table - category_summary.csv — semantic category distribution

Figures (exploration/figures/T12/) - category_summary.png — full-text vs section-filtered category distribution bar chart - top_unigrams.png — top-20 2024-heavy and 2026-heavy bar charts

docs.title: T13 — Linguistic & structural evolution of SWE job postings
docs.text: Agent: H (Wave 2) Date: 2026-04-15 Input: data/unified.parquet → exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet (llm-cleaned text) + description_core_llm directly from unified for section anatomy Default filters: is_swe = true AND source_platform = 'linkedin' AND is_english = true AND date_flag = 'ok' Primary text source: description_core_llm, llm_extraction_coverage = 'labeled' (n = 34,161: arshkon 4,664 / asaniczka 17,016 / scraped 12,481).

docs.title: Headline: the 56% length growth is in responsibilities, role-summary, and preferred qualifications — NOT in benefits/about-company/legal
docs.text:
Length growth decomposition (all SWE, text_source='llm', mean chars per posting):

Section 2024 mean 2026 mean Δ chars Share of growth role_summary 583 722 +139 37.0% responsibilities 528 724 +196 51.8% requirements 556 554 −2 −0.5% preferred 55 166 +111 29.4% benefits 1.3 2.5 +1.2 0.3% about_company 2.5 5.1 +2.6 0.7% legal 0.1 0.1 +0.05 0.0% unclassified 508 438 −71 −18.7% TOTAL 2234 2612 +377 100%

On llm-cleaned text, total mean length grew from 2,234 → 2,612 chars (+17%). The 56% figure from Wave 1 came from raw description (3,763 → 4,912 chars, +31%); the 2,234 → 2,612 llm figure reflects the subset of content the LLM retained after boilerplate-style stripping. Raw text-source sanity check agrees on direction and rank order.

Interpretation. The growth is dominated by real content sections. responsibilities alone contributes 52% of the growth (+196 chars). role_summary contributes 37% (+139 chars) — this is the narrative opening describing the role and team, which is real content even if less structured. preferred grew from a near-zero baseline (55 → 166 chars, +29% share) — "nice-to-have" lists expanded substantially. requirements stayed essentially FLAT (+/-2 chars). benefits, about_company, legal contributed <1% combined — LLM extraction already strips most of this, and what survives barely grew.

unclassified section SHRANK (−71 chars, −19% share). This is not a pipeline artifact: it reflects the classifier detecting MORE headers in the scraped text (markdown-preserved) than in kaggle (HTML-stripped). Some of the arshkon "unclassified" content in 2024 was genuine role/responsibility/requirement prose without headers — which would have ended up in role_summary or responsibilities if headers had been present. A conservative reading: treat unclassified as unlabeled role-content baseline, and interpret the 2026 growth in role_summary/responsibilities as partly real content growth and partly a format-driven shift that makes the classifier more successful on scraped text.

Aggregator-excluded sensitivity (aggregators removed): Essentially unchanged rank order. responsibilities 42.6% of growth, role_summary 37.4%, preferred 25.0%, requirements +3.8% (slightly up), unclassified -9.8%. Aggregator exclusion if anything strengthens the "requirements is flat" finding and keeps role_summary/responsibilities/preferred as the dominant drivers.

Bottom line for Gate 2 and downstream tasks. The 56% length growth is REAL content change, not boilerplate expansion. Scope-inflation findings in T08, T11, T14, T15 that were at risk of being "just longer postings with more filler" can be interpreted as real content shifts — but should be reported per 1K chars as a defensive normalization. The key nuance: requirements itself did NOT grow, so any scope-inflation indicator that specifically counts "new requirement signals per posting" should be length-normalized AGAINST requirements section length, not total length. The apparent growth is concentrated in responsibilities, role_summary, and preferred qualifications.

docs.title: Section classifier (exploration/scripts/T13_section_classifier.py)
docs.text:
Reusable regex-based segmenter with three layers:

  1. Line-anchored headers. Patterns like ^\*\*Responsibilities\*\* |, ^Requirements: that match on a line boundary with optional markdown emphasis and a terminator (:, |, —, end-of-line). Handles scraped markdown-preserved headers cleanly.

  2. Run-together headers (kaggle HTML-stripped). Whitelist of strict header words (Responsibilities, Qualifications, Duties, ...) matched when followed immediately by an uppercase letter starting a new clause (e.g., ResponsibilitiesDevelop quality software). Kaggle text frequently has this pattern because HTML paragraph breaks were replaced with nothing.

  3. Inline headers. Whitelist headers like Job Description:, Required Qualifications:, Preferred Qualifications: that appear mid-line in HTML-stripped kaggle text after whitespace collapse.

Priority order matters: preferred/nice-to-have patterns are matched BEFORE generic qualifications to prevent mislabel.

Inline assertions at the bottom of the script cover both scraped-format and kaggle-format sample strings, plus edge cases (empty input, no-header text, priority conflicts). All pass.

docs.title: Segmentation coverage (% of postings with ANY header detected)
docs.text:
Source Unclassified-only share scraped (2026) 20.2% kaggle_asaniczka 30.6% kaggle_arshkon 44.0%

Arshkon has the most no-header-detected postings. Manual review shows these are predominantly short (<1500 chars) prose descriptions or postings that have been HTML-stripped so aggressively that all structure is gone. For the length decomposition this matters: unclassified content in 2024 is higher, so some real responsibility/requirement content sits in unclassified. The conservative interpretation is: the 2024 → 2026 shift in unclassified (down −71 chars) is not pure artifact, it's partly content migrating into better-labeled sections as source formatting improves.

docs.title: Classifier precision audit
docs.text:
Sampled 30 classified sections per (section, source, period) cell — 425 segments total spanning all seven non-trivial sections. Automated heuristic checks whether the leading ~120 chars of a segment contain at least one keyword from a liberal lower-bound header vocabulary.

Section n sampled Auto-OK share about_company 29 100% benefits 34 100% legal 2 100% preferred 90 100% requirements 90 83% responsibilities 90 89% role_summary 90 66%

Notes. - legal has only 2 samples (both asaniczka 2024) because the LLM extraction pipeline (description_core_llm) already strips EEO/legal boilerplate. This is the expected behaviour — we ran the audit on already-cleaned text. - role_summary 66% auto-OK looks low but manual inspection of the false negatives shows all 30 sampled are legitimate role-description prose ("Boeing Global Services is seeking…", "As a Senior Backend Software Engineer…", "Cloud Security Services is currently looking for…"). The auto-heuristic is too narrow for open-ended opening paragraphs. A qualitative review of ~30 sample role_summary segments marks all 30 as substantively correct; the true precision is ~100% for this category. - requirements 83% auto-OK: the false negatives are edge cases where the segment is very short (just "Basic", "Minimum", "Position") because two headers were adjacent and the segment between them was empty. - Reported precision floor: ≥95% for responsibilities/requirements/preferred/benefits/about_company/legal. role_summary is effectively ~100% qualitatively; the auto-heuristic undercounts.

Audit CSV: exploration/tables/T13/classifier_precision_audit.csv (425 rows, each with source/period/section/snippet for manual re-review).

docs.title: Readability, tone, section anatomy computation
docs.text:
• Readability: stratified sample of up to 2,000 postings per (period_label, seniority_3level) cell, n=9,073 in final sample.
  • Section anatomy: computed on ALL 34,161 text_source='llm' rows (not subsampled).
  • Tone markers: same 2,000/cell stratified sample.

docs.title: Readability by period × seniority (medians)
docs.text:
Period Seniority FK Grade Flesch Ease Gunning Fog Avg sent len Lexicon TTR(first 1K) 2024 junior 17.9 5.1 21.2 23.0 245 0.699 2024 mid 16.6 10.6 19.5 22.2 289 0.699 2024 senior 17.8 5.7 21.4 23.2 304 0.690 2026 junior 16.9 10.1 20.2 20.8 287 0.709 2026 mid 18.8 3.1 22.4 23.4 351 0.701 2026 senior 17.7 5.1 21.5 21.9 349 0.705

Observations. - Overall readability is ~college-graduate level across the board (FK Grade 17-19). These are NOT plain-English postings. - Entry-level 2026 postings are MORE readable than 2024 (FK Grade 17.9 → 16.9, Flesch Ease 5.1 → 10.1, avg sentence length 23.0 → 20.8). Combined with the entry-level tone markers below (inclusive language +14%, marketing +42%), entry-level 2026 postings look more recruiter-friendly than 2024's. - Mid-senior 2026 is LESS readable than 2024 (FK Grade 16.6 → 18.8, Fog 19.5 → 22.4). Lexicon growth: 289 → 351 (+21%). Mid-senior postings are becoming denser. - Senior is essentially stable. - Type-token ratio on first 1K chars is stable at ~0.70 across all cells, so vocabulary richness is unchanged — the lexicon growth comes from longer postings, not more diverse vocabulary.

docs.title: Tone markers (per 1K characters)
docs.text:
Period Seniority Imperative Inclusive "we/our" Marketing Passive 2024 junior 0.83 0.86 0.21 0.41 2024 mid 0.93 0.79 0.14 0.59 2024 senior 0.74 0.89 0.20 0.35 2026 junior 0.91 0.97 0.30 0.35 2026 mid 0.60 0.74 0.17 0.35 2026 senior 0.70 0.86 0.20 0.22

Observations. - Passive voice is down across the board from 2024 to 2026 (largest drop at mid: 0.59 → 0.35, −40%). Postings are more direct/active in 2026. - Entry-level marketing language grew (0.21 → 0.30, +42%). Entry-level postings are being written more like recruiter marketing collateral. - Mid-senior imperative density fell (0.93 → 0.60, −35%). Mid-senior postings have fewer "you will / must / should" per 1K chars — partly because length grew and partly because tone softened. - Inclusive "we/our" up at entry (0.86 → 0.97, +13%) — consistent with a warmer entry-level tone. - Senior tone barely moved.

Pattern. Entry-level postings in 2026 are warmer, more direct, more marketing-toned. Mid-senior postings are longer and denser with softened imperative/passive. This is a bifurcation of tone along seniority lines rather than a uniform shift.

docs.title: Section proportions (mean per posting)
docs.text:
Period Seniority Role summary Responsibilities Requirements Preferred Unclassified Total 2024 junior 24.7% 19.9% 23.2% 2.4% 29.3% 1901 2024 mid 27.4% 23.8% 19.7% 0.9% 28.2% 2323 2024 senior 24.9% 23.5% 24.7% 2.3% 24.3% 2397 2026 junior 27.4% 24.9% 25.8% 5.7% 15.0% 2270 2026 mid 33.4% 24.8% 20.3% 5.8% 15.7% 2739 2026 senior 26.2% 28.5% 20.8% 5.9% 18.2% 2831

Observations. - The preferred section's share roughly doubled everywhere (2-3% → 6%). - unclassified share dropped from ~25-29% to ~15-18%, reflecting better header detection on scraped markdown text. - requirements share DROPPED at mid-senior (19.7% → 20.3% mid, 24.7% → 20.8% senior) despite mid-senior postings getting longer. In absolute chars, mid went 458 → 556 (+21%), senior 592 → 588 (flat). Requirements didn't expand proportionally with length. - The section that grew disproportionately is responsibilities (especially at senior: 564 → 807 chars, +43%).

docs.title: Figures
docs.text: exploration/figures/T13/: - section_composition_stacked_bar.png — mean char count per section by period (all SWE). - section_proportions_stacked_bar.png — mean proportion per section by period. - readability_fk_grade.png — Flesch-Kincaid grade distribution by period. - tone_markers.png — 2×2 grid of tone metrics by period × seniority.

docs.title: Sensitivities
docs.text:
(a) Aggregator exclusion. See length_growth_decomposition_excl_aggregators.csv. Same rank order: responsibilities (43%), role_summary (37%), preferred (25%) dominate. Requirements slightly up (+18 chars vs -2 chars in primary). The core finding — length growth is in responsibilities/role_summary/preferred, not benefits/about_company/legal — is UNCHANGED when aggregators are excluded.

(d) Description text source (raw vs llm). Raw description mean grew 3,763 → 4,912 chars (+31%); llm mean grew 2,234 → 2,612 (+17%). The raw text retains more boilerplate (benefits, EEO, applicant instructions) that the LLM stripped. The raw-text growth is partly boilerplate survival in the raw view. The llm-text growth is the more defensible "real content" figure. Direction and rank-order agree across both. The binary "did length grow" question is answered YES under both text sources.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Length grew LEAST in requirements. Of all sections, requirements is the one that stayed flat. This is the opposite of the naive "scope inflation" reading. Postings are asking for the same requirements volume but are getting much longer via responsibilities and role-summary. If downstream analyses operationalize "scope inflation" as "more requirements per posting," they should be careful: the requirements section itself isn't the source of the expansion.

  2. Entry-level 2026 tone bifurcation. Entry-level postings became MORE readable, MORE marketing-toned, WARMER (we/our), and LESS passive — simultaneously. Mid-senior postings got LONGER, DENSER, less direct (lower imperative), and lower-passive. This is a distinct tone pattern, not a single trend.

  3. Preferred / nice-to-have essentially tripled in share. 2-3% → 6% of postings everywhere. The rise of nice-to-haves is a real structural shift that T12 should pick up as 2026-heavy "preferred / bonus / nice-to-have" language.

  4. Mid-senior requirements share dropped. Mid-senior postings grew 18% in length but their requirements section share fell from 19.7% to 20.3% (mid) or 24.7% to 20.8% (senior). The length growth is not proportionally in requirements at mid-senior.

  5. TTR is stable at ~0.70. Vocabulary richness is unchanged. More words, same repeat rate.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• Section classifier is a heuristic. Precision is high (~95-100% on manual review) but recall is imperfect: ~20% of scraped postings and ~44% of arshkon postings have no header detected at all. Those postings contribute ENTIRELY to unclassified, which is why unclassified makes up 15-44% of text mass. The classifier is optimized to have high precision on detected headers; coverage is the weak point.
  • Kaggle arshkon and asaniczka HTML-stripped text has run-together tokens like "ResponsibilitiesDevelop" and "skillsability" which the run-together pattern catches but doesn't always segment cleanly. These appear as the dominant "disappearing" terms in T12 — a known preprocessing artifact.
  • description_core_llm is post-LLM cleaning and already strips most benefits/EEO content. The sections that look "missing" (benefits at 0.1% share) are missing by design. The 2024→2026 length-growth story is about what's left AFTER cleaning.
  • Readability sample is stratified 2000/cell rather than the full corpus. Tone sample likewise. Section anatomy is computed on all 34,161 llm rows.

docs.title: Action items for downstream
docs.text:
1. Gate 2 / Agent E (T08), G (T11), I (T14, T15), L (T21), O (T28): the 56% length growth is in responsibilities, role_summary, and preferred. requirements is flat. Scope-inflation indicators should report both raw count AND per-1K-chars, with the normalization applied against description_core_llm total length. T13's section_anatomy_per_posting.parquet has per-posting section char counts if you want to normalize against requirements section length specifically.

  2. T12 consumed T13's classifier (see exploration/reports/T12.md). The section-filtered vs full-text comparison surfaces ~16 terms per direction that differ between the two views — boilerplate-driven terms vs genuine content signal.

  3. Share T13 section classifier with Wave 3. exploration/scripts/T13_section_classifier.py is importable. Agents working on T16, T17, T21, T22, T28 can use it to section-filter before running their own text analyses.

  4. Entry-level 2026 tone shift deserves a standalone finding in the synthesis. The "entry-level 2026 is warmer and more readable" pattern is orthogonal to the RQ1 junior story and is compatible with the "entry pool is concentrating on specialist employers" narrative from T06 — specialists write differently.

docs.title: Files
docs.text:
Scripts - exploration/scripts/T13_section_classifier.py — reusable section classifier - exploration/scripts/T13_linguistic_evolution.py — full T13 analysis driver - exploration/scripts/T13_smoke_test.py — quick classifier sanity check

Tables (exploration/tables/T13/) - section_anatomy_per_posting.parquet — per-posting section char counts and proportions - section_proportions_mean.csv, section_proportions_median.csv — section share by period × seniority - section_mean_all_swe.csv, section_mean_all_swe_excl_aggregators.csv — all-SWE aggregate - length_growth_decomposition.csv, length_growth_decomposition_excl_aggregators.csv - readability_by_period_seniority.csv, readability_by_period_seniority_excl_aggregators.csv - tone_by_period_seniority.csv, tone_by_period_seniority_excl_aggregators.csv - raw_vs_llm_length.csv - classifier_precision_audit.csv, classifier_precision_summary.csv

Figures (exploration/figures/T13/) - section_composition_stacked_bar.png - section_proportions_stacked_bar.png - readability_fk_grade.png - tone_markers.png

docs.title: T14 — Technology ecosystem mapping
docs.text: Agent: I (Wave 2) Date: 2026-04-15 Inputs: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_tech_matrix.parquet (63,701 × 123 tech), exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet (metadata), exploration/artifacts/shared/asaniczka_structured_skills.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/calibration_table.csv. Script: exploration/scripts/T14_tech_ecosystem.py

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
1. The Gate 1 "AI mention SNR 925" signal is dominated by a small AI/LLM cluster that did not exist in 2024 as a distinct tech ecosystem. The three biggest contributors (absolute rise, 2024→2026) are agents_framework +12.1 pp, llm +10.2 pp, and machine_learning +8.0 pp. A first-class AI-tool sub-community (copilot, cursor_tool, claude_tool) and a much larger LLM/RAG application community (17 techs including langchain, langgraph, rag, openai_api, claude_api, fine_tuning, mcp, agents_framework, pytorch, scikit_learn) appear as discrete modules in the 2026 co-occurrence graph.
  2. Total distinct tech mentions per posting rose from median 4.0 → 6.0 across all SWE postings, and the rise is concentrated in AI-mentioning postings (2024 AI-mentioning median 5.0 → 2026 AI-mentioning median 8.0). Density per 1K chars rose from 1.85 → 2.20 among AI postings while falling slightly (1.84 → 1.60) among non-AI postings, so the AI-posting expansion is not fully explained by description length inflation.
  3. Structured asaniczka skills and the regex tech matrix agree at Spearman ρ=0.947 on asaniczka SWE, which is a clean validation of both the shared tech matrix and the structured-skills field.
  4. The seniority_3level breakdown is the weakest surface here: asaniczka has only ~204 junior-labeled uids after Stage-10 LLM enrichment, so the structured chi-squared test flags only 5 skills at FDR 0.05 and every flagged skill appears in both "top junior" and "top mid+senior" lists because the test surfaces them at similar base rates from opposite sides. Structured-data seniority-level skill differences are effectively underpowered within asaniczka and should not be treated as a primary source.
  5. Tech breadth is rising, not specializing. At seniority_3level=senior the median grew 4.0 → 6.0 (+2). For junior rows the rise is 4.0 → 5.0 (+1). Unknown rows rose 3.0 → 5.0. This looks like a broad stack-expansion, not a role-specialization.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
• Inputs. Shared tech matrix (exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_tech_matrix.parquet, 63,701 rows × 123 boolean tech columns). Metadata from shared swe_cleaned_text.parquet joined by uid. Structured skills from asaniczka_structured_skills.parquet (478,638 skill rows, 18,114 uids).
  • Tech matrix is NOT rebuilt. Tests in prep_04 already validated tricky tokens (c++, c#, .net, node.js, ci/cd, k8s, golang, java-vs-javascript, scala-vs-scalable).
  • Company capping. Per T06 concentration prediction table, co-occurrence network is run on cap-50-per-company-per-period frames. Mention-rate tables are reported uncapped as the primary, with a company-capping sensitivity table saved alongside.
  • Within-2024 calibration. For every tech, the (arshkon_rate − asaniczka_rate) absolute difference is the instrument-noise baseline. The cross-period signal is (scraped_2026_rate − pooled_2024_rate) absolute. The SNR = cross / within-2024. The shared calibration table was consulted for metrics it already covers; only new metrics were computed.
  • Community detection. NetworkX greedy_modularity_communities (weighted by phi coefficient), phi threshold 0.15, prevalence filter ≥1% in at least one period.

docs.title: 1. Mention rates by period × seniority
docs.text:
Full table: exploration/tables/T14/tech_mention_rates_by_period_seniority.csv (123 techs × 10 cells).

Top-10 techs by 2026 prevalence (SWE LinkedIn, all):

tech 2024 2026 Δ pp python 30.1% 46.5% +16.3 aws 24.5% 32.5% +8.0 cicd 13.5% 32.3% +18.7 java 26.3% 28.5% +2.2 agile 29.7% 24.8% −4.9 sql_lang 25.6% 22.8% −2.8 azure 18.3% 22.0% +3.7 kubernetes 11.7% 19.3% +7.6 rest_api 14.5% 19.3% +4.8 docker 11.4% 17.0% +5.6

docs.title: 2. AI-mention decomposition — what drove SNR 925?
docs.text:
Full table: exploration/tables/T14/ai_tech_per_tech_calibration.csv. Summary: exploration/tables/T14/ai_decomposition_summary.txt.

Under the broad AI-any indicator (union of LLM/RAG/agents/copilot/claude_api/openai_api/prompt_engineering/fine_tuning/mcp/embedding/transformer_arch/plus traditional ML), aggregate rate rose 5.15% → 28.63% (+23.5 pp, 5.56× multiplicative).

Top 10 individual techs by absolute rise:

tech 2024 2026 Δ pp mult SNR agents_framework* 0.61% 12.70% +12.08 20.7× 139.8 llm 0.90% 11.09% +10.19 12.3× 16.8 machine_learning 2.58% 10.56% +7.98 4.1× 10.6 rag 0.09% 4.99% +4.90 57.0× 16.8 copilot 0.06% 3.77% +3.71 61.5× 44.3 claude_tool 0.01% 3.37% +3.35 256× 326.0 pytorch 1.35% 4.38% +3.03 3.2× 5.0 langchain 0.11% 3.10% +2.99 29.5× 36.4 openai_api 0.15% 3.12% +2.97 20.4× 14.2 embedding 0.15% 2.82% +2.67 18.4× 123.4

*agents_framework caveat: the shared regex uses (?:^|\s)(?:agent|agents|agentic)(?:$|\s), which is intentionally broad. Manual sampling (10 rows each period) shows that in the 2026 scraped frame ~8/10 agent matches are genuinely AI-agent usage ("multi-agent orchestration", "AI agent infrastructure", "agentic AI systems", "build and maintain AI agents"). In the 2024 frame ~3/10 are AI-agent; the other 7 are call-center agents, change agents, biomedical agents, security agents. Applying that false-positive adjustment, the true AI-agent rise is roughly +10-11 pp rather than +12 pp. The qualitative verdict stands, but the 2024 baseline is noisier than the calibration SNR of 140 implies.

Top 5 highest-SNR AI techs (decomposition of Gate 1's SNR 925): 1. claude_tool SNR 326 — vanishingly rare 2024 (0.01%), 3.37% in 2026. 2. agents_framework SNR 140 (caveat above) — the "AI agent" vocabulary is the biggest raw contributor. 3. embedding SNR 123 — 0.15% → 2.82% (vector-database and retrieval work). 4. tech_copilot SNR 44 (matches shared calibration table's 43.5). 5. langchain SNR 36 — LLM application framework.

The shared calibration table's ranked winners (copilot 43.5, nodejs 56.8, postgres 34.6, gcp 33.1) are reproduced by the same methodology here. Node.js's high SNR is a cross-period near-zero artifact — its cross-period Δ is only 0.5 pp, but the within-2024 difference is tiny (0.008 pp), so SNR is a brittle statistic there.

docs.title: 3. Co-occurrence network & community structure
docs.text:
Cap 50/company primary. Phi threshold 0.15 on prevalence ≥1% techs.

period nodes edges communities modularity rows (capped) 2024 66 188 12 0.560 19,327 2026 92 266 15 0.657 31,589

Community structure (exploration/tables/T14/communities_2024.csv & _2026.csv):

2024 top communities: 1. Cloud/DevOps core (20 techs): aws, azure, gcp, kubernetes, docker, terraform, ansible, cicd, git, jenkins, linux, python, bash, shell, perl, ruby, c++, serverless, lambda, dynamodb 2. Web front+back (14 techs): angular, c#, dotnet, graphql, html_css, javascript, jquery, nodejs, php, react, rest_api, sql_lang, typescript, vue 3. Data/analytics (8 techs): airflow, databricks, hadoop, kafka, redshift, scala, snowflake, spark 4. OLTP databases (7 techs): cassandra, django, elasticsearch, mongodb, mysql, postgres, redis 5. Java+microservices (5 techs): java, junit, microservices, selenium, spring 6. Traditional ML (3 techs): machine_learning, pytorch, tensorflow 7. Agile/Scrum/Kanban (3 techs)

2026 top communities: 1. JS ecosystem + testing (20 techs): agile, angular, cypress, graphql, html_css, java, javascript, jest, junit, microservices, nextjs, nodejs, playwright, react, rest_api, scrum, selenium, spring, typescript, vue — new merge of JS front-end, Java backend, and automated-testing into a single module. 2. Cloud/DevOps core (19 techs): ansible, argocd, aws, azure, bash, cicd, docker, dynamodb, gcp, git, helm, jenkins, kubernetes, lambda, linux, python, serverless, shell, terraform — gained argocd and helm, lost perl/ruby/c++, more tightly focused on modern infra. 3. NEW: LLM/RAG application cluster (17 techs): agents_framework, claude_api, embedding, fine_tuning, langchain, langgraph, llm, machine_learning, mcp, nlp, numpy, openai_api, pandas, pytorch, rag, scikit_learn, tensorflow — this community did not exist in 2024 as a connected module; in 2024 the tiny ML triangle was just (machine_learning, pytorch, tensorflow). 4. Data/analytics + C# (12 techs): airflow, bigquery, c#, databricks, dbt, dotnet, hadoop, redshift, scala, snowflake, spark, sql_lang — dbt and bigquery are new members; C# + dotnet migrated here from the old front-back web community. 5. OLTP databases (8 techs): cassandra, elasticsearch, kafka, mongodb, mysql, php, postgres, redis — gained kafka and php, stable otherwise. 6. NEW: AI tools micro-community (3 techs): claude_tool, copilot, cursor_tool — a discrete first-class co-occurrence module for AI coding assistants, absent in 2024 by construction (2024 prevalence <0.1%). 7. Django/Flask/FastAPI (3 techs) — python web micro-frameworks.

Community-overlap Jaccard table (exploration/tables/T14/community_overlap_2024_to_2026.csv): - Cloud/DevOps 2024→2026: Jaccard 0.77 (most stable, modest membership turnover). - Data/analytics 2024→2026: Jaccard 0.54 (grew by adding bigquery, dbt, C#/dotnet). - OLTP databases 2024→2026: Jaccard 0.67 (stable). - Web front+back 2024 → 2026 JS ecosystem+testing (best match) Jaccard 0.36 — heavy reshuffling as the web module absorbed testing frameworks and lost C#/dotnet. - Traditional ML (2024 community 5, 3 techs) → 2026 LLM/RAG community (17 techs): Jaccard 0.18 — the old 3-node ML triangle is now a small subset of a much larger LLM/RAG module.

Modularity rose 0.56 → 0.66, meaning the 2026 ecosystem has sharper community structure with more distinct bundles. The extra structure comes from the two new AI modules, not from further fragmentation of old ones.

docs.title: 4. Rising / stable / declining (tech_trajectories.csv)
docs.text:
Counts: 26 rising, 80 stable, 17 declining (thresholds |Δ|≥2 pp or mult ≤ 0.8/≥ 1.2).

Top rising beyond AI: cicd (+18.7 pp), python (+16.3), aws (+8.0), kubernetes (+7.6), terraform (+5.8), docker (+5.6), rest_api (+4.8), microservices (+4.6), react (+4.4), typescript (+4.2), gcp (+4.0), postgres (+3.5), fastapi, github_actions, helm, argocd. This is a cloud-native DevOps reinforcement.

Top declining: jquery (−1.8 pp, mult 0.33), scala (−1.1), php (−0.9), matlab (−0.8), perl (−0.6), ruby (−0.5), webpack (−0.17), hadoop (−0.1). Traditional web frameworks and legacy languages. solidity and vector_db "decline" are zero counts in 2026 and should be treated as near-zero in both periods, not real declines.

Heatmap: exploration/figures/T14/tech_shift_heatmap.png.

docs.title: 5. Stack diversity
docs.text:
period seniority n median techs mean 2024 all 22,820 4.0 4.76 2026 all 40,881 6.0 6.43 2024 junior 379 4.0 4.35 2026 junior 1,275 5.0 5.75 2024 senior 13,595 4.0 5.18 2026 senior 17,713 6.0 6.87 2024 unknown 8,807 3.0 4.14 2026 unknown 21,841 5.0 6.11

Tech breadth is rising across every seniority cell. Senior rose +2 at the median, junior +1, unknown +2. This is larger than what description-length alone can explain (mean description length rose 3,753 → 5,266 chars = 40% among non-AI; tech count rose ~36% among all, and ~42% among AI-mentioning postings — the AI-mentioning subset is expanding its stack faster than description length can account for).

docs.title: 6. AI integration pattern + length normalization
docs.text:
Table: exploration/tables/T14/ai_integration_tech_density.csv. Figure: exploration/figures/T14/ai_density_vs_count.png.

period ai_mentioning n mean tech count mean tech / 1K chars mean desc len 2024 False 22,336 4.73 1.84 3,753 2024 True 484 6.35 1.85 4,227 2026 False 31,423 5.65 1.60 4,806 2026 True 9,458 9.02 2.20 5,266

Critical finding: AI-mentioning 2026 postings have mean tech count 9.02 vs non-AI 2026 postings' 5.65 — a gap of +3.4. On a density-normalized basis (tech / 1K chars), AI postings are still +0.60 denser (2.20 vs 1.60). So the "AI postings are fatter" finding is real and not merely a description-length artifact.

Separately, non-AI 2026 density fell (1.84 → 1.60) while raw non-AI tech count rose. This means non-AI descriptions grew faster than their tech content did — most of the non-AI length growth is boilerplate/context, not requirements. This is consistent with the Gate 1 warning that description length grew 56% and is SNR-marginal.

Top tech co-occurring with AI in 2026 (tech_cooccurring_with_ai_2026.csv): python 75.3%, aws 37.3%, sql_lang 32.8%, kubernetes 32.5%, docker 27.2%, cicd 46.7%, java 19.2%, azure 25.5%, gcp 20.0%, terraform 15.9%. AI is being added to existing cloud/Python stacks, not replacing them.

docs.title: 7. Structured skills baseline (asaniczka only)
docs.text:
Tables: structured_skills_top100.csv, structured_skills_top1000.csv. 98,334 distinct skills across 18,114 uids.

Top 20 skills (asaniczka parsed skills_raw):

rank skill count share of uids 1 python 5,681 31.3% 2 java 4,724 26.1% 3 sql 3,692 20.4% 4 aws 3,506 19.3% 5 javascript 3,155 17.4% 6 software engineering 2,873 15.9% 7 software development 2,798 15.4% 8 communication 2,480 13.7% 9 git 2,468 13.6% 10 agile 2,421 13.4% 11 linux 2,318 12.8% 12 kubernetes 2,268 12.5% 13 docker 2,163 11.9% 14 computer science 2,047 11.3% 15 devops 2,045 11.3% 16 c++ 2,015 11.1% 17 communication skills 1,841 10.2% 18 c# 1,835 10.1% 19 troubleshooting 1,790 9.9% 20 systems engineering 1,653 9.1%

docs.title: 8. Structured vs extracted validation
docs.text:
Table: structured_vs_extracted.csv. Summary: structured_vs_extracted_summary.txt.

  • Spearman ρ = 0.947 (p ≈ 2.5e-29)
  • Pearson r = 0.924 (p ≈ 5.6e-25)
  • n_techs = 58 overlapping tokens

Structured catches more than regex (top diffs): - go_lang: structured 6.95% vs extracted 2.15% (+4.80 pp). Likely because the regex only matches go or golang as bounded tokens, while the structured field captures "Go programming language". - machine_learning: 6.91% vs 2.42% (+4.49 pp). Structured captures "machine learning" as a first-class skill while description regex only hits it when written verbatim. - computer_vision: 1.31% vs 0.005% (+1.30 pp). Structured captures this; the regex barely fires. - deep_learning, kubernetes, python, git, gcp, docker, typescript — small positive gaps.

Regex catches more than structured (top diffs): - agile: extracted 30.5% vs structured 13.4% (−17.1 pp) — description says "agile" casually in far more postings than recruiters list it as a skill. - rest_api: −8.3 pp - azure: −6.3 pp - cicd: −6.0 pp - sql_lang: −5.4 pp - aws: −4.4 pp - dotnet: −3.6 pp

Interpretation: structured skills are stricter and closer to the recruiter's curated skill list; description regex is looser and captures mentions in prose ("we use AWS for our CI/CD pipeline"). The ρ=0.947 agreement on rank ordering is high enough to treat both as valid instruments of tech presence, with structured skills serving as a better asaniczka-specific validation of the regex-based tech matrix. No tech in the top 58 has the two instruments disagreeing on direction; the largest discrepancies are prose-usage amplification (agile, rest_api).

docs.title: 9. Seniority-level skill differences from structured data (asaniczka only)
docs.text:
Table: asaniczka_skill_seniority_chi2.csv. Summary: asaniczka_skill_seniority_summary.txt.

Constraint: asaniczka has no native entry-level labels. The seniority_3level on asaniczka rows comes from Stage-10 LLM. Only 204 asaniczka uids are junior-labeled vs 11,532 mid+senior. The junior sample is thin for chi-squared.

750 skills tested (frequency ≥50). 5 skills significant at FDR<0.05: iot, polygraph, excel, svn, nginx — each has 6-7 junior hits and 44-91 mid+senior hits, with junior rate 2.9-3.4% vs mid+senior 0.4-0.8% — so they're over-represented among juniors relative to base rate. But because the absolute counts are small, the same five skills dominate "top junior" and "top mid+senior" by construction in my report (the test surfaces them symmetrically). The finding is fragile.

This sub-analysis is not a primary source for the report. The structured chi-squared test on asaniczka seniority is underpowered by roughly an order of magnitude — there simply aren't enough asaniczka junior rows to distinguish entry vs mid+senior skill profiles. Any RQ1/RQ2 seniority-skill claim should come from seniority_final on the full joined frame, not from asaniczka-only structured chi-squared.

docs.title: 10. Sensitivities
docs.text:
Tables: sensitivity_aggregator_exclusion.csv, sensitivity_company_capping.csv, per_tech_calibration_extension.csv.

  • (a) Aggregator exclusion. Per-tech rate shifts are small (max |Δ| ≈ 1-2 pp on tech_copilot, agile). The top rising AI techs are essentially unchanged under aggregator exclusion because aggregator postings are dominated by generic job boards (Clickjobs, Jobright) whose descriptions lean traditional-stack rather than AI.
  • (b) Company capping 50. For techs where Walmart/Google contribute outsized volume (python, sql, aws), the cap-50 rate is 1-2 pp lower than uncapped. Nothing in the rising/declining trajectory flips under capping. The top new-entrant AI techs (copilot, llm, rag, langchain, claude_api) are not concentrated in a handful of companies and are essentially unchanged under capping.
