  • (f) Within-2024 calibration (per-tech SNR extension). See per_tech_calibration_extension.csv. Of the 123 techs, 42 have SNR ≥ 2 (above instrument noise). These include every AI/LLM cluster member, most of cloud/DevOps, and the core Python/cicd/kubernetes/terraform lineup. Below-threshold techs are dominated by legacy tokens (matlab, perl, ruby, php, scala) with small within-2024 cross-source drift that overwhelms their small cross-period signal. The AI-cluster rise is the most calibrated-robust signal in the entire tech matrix.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. The 2026 tech graph has HIGHER modularity than 2024 (0.66 vs 0.56). Expectation going in was that tech bundles would blur. Instead, two crisp new modules (LLM/RAG application cluster and AI tools triad) carve themselves out of the embedding-similar tech space, increasing overall modularity.
  2. Web and Java backend merged in 2026. In 2024 the front-end web community (React/Angular/Vue + Node/TS/JS/SQL + C#/.NET) and the Java+Spring+microservices community were separate. In 2026 they're one large 20-tech module that also absorbed all the automated-testing frameworks (jest, cypress, playwright, selenium). Hypothesis: the general-purpose full-stack role consolidated.
  3. C# + dotnet migrated from the web module to the data/analytics module in 2026. They now co-occur with snowflake/databricks/bigquery/dbt more than with JS/React. Possible driver: enterprise .NET data pipelines are now more frequent than .NET front-end.
  4. Non-AI description density fell. 2026 non-AI postings have MORE raw tech mentions per posting than 2024 non-AI postings, but LOWER tech-per-1K-chars density. The non-AI growth is mostly description bloat, not requirement expansion. This is independent corroboration of the T13 "length growth is boilerplate" hypothesis.
  5. claude_tool SNR 326 is the single highest-SNR tech in the corpus. Even higher than copilot (44). The cross-period 2024→2026 shift is 0.01% → 3.37%, and the within-2024 arshkon/asaniczka delta is 0.01 pp. It's the textbook example of a "genuinely new tech that did not exist in the 2024 instrument."
  6. agents_framework is the largest absolute riser in pp (12.08) but has pattern noise. Manual sampling confirms most 2026 matches are genuine AI-agent usage, but the 2024 baseline is overcounted by call-center / change-agent / security usages. The true AI-agent rise is probably 10-11 pp rather than 12 pp; directionally identical, slightly smaller.
  7. Structured asaniczka junior rows are too thin for skill chi-squared. We have only 204 asaniczka junior uids vs 11,532 mid+senior. The seniority-skill difference test is operationally blocked by this thinness — a result worth flagging for the analysis plan.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• Tech matrix is binary presence only; it does not measure emphasis. A posting mentioning "Python" once and one requiring five years of Python each count the same.
  • agents_framework regex is broad — see caveat in Results §2. Any AI-narrative claim that leans hard on that single tech should be cross-checked against a tighter pattern (e.g., ai agent|agentic).
  • Structured skills are asaniczka-only, so step-7/8/9 cannot be extended across periods. The 2024 snapshot alone can be used for asaniczka-internal diagnostics.
  • Stage-9 LLM text coverage caps the 2026-side text at ~12.5k. Tech matrix, however, covers all 63,701 rows (patterns scan the cleaned text which exists for all rows), so tech analyses are not text-coverage-capped.
  • Seniority-3level mid is tiny (39 rows 2024, 51 rows 2026) and should not be interpreted as a stand-alone bucket; it's folded into senior for most read-outs here.

docs.title: Action items for downstream
docs.text:
1. T09 / T20 / T21: The LLM/RAG community (17 techs) should be a candidate "domain archetype" boundary. If T09 BERTopic surfaces an LLM/RAG cluster that matches this tech community, the two findings corroborate each other. If not, the tech community is a cleaner operationalization of "AI domain" than the T09 topic model.
  2. T16 company typology: Companies with ≥3 postings in the LLM/RAG community are candidate "AI-forward" employers. Suggested next step: intersect with the T06 overlap panel and check whether AI-forward companies changed entry share differently than non-AI companies.
  3. T15 (this agent): Use the AI-cluster tech boolean as a covariate for the semantic convergence test — does the LLM/RAG cluster drive the junior/senior similarity shift?
  4. T22 ghost forensics: agents_framework and mcp are good targets for "aspirational requirement" testing — they're new enough that the gap between employer listing and worker usage is likely wide.
  5. Preprocessing owner: Consider tightening agents_framework to exclude security|insurance|call|change|travel|real.?estate|polygraph|biomedical|sales context words. Current pattern is directionally correct but introduces ~30% false positives in the 2024 baseline.

docs.title: Sensitivity checks summary
docs.text:
Dimension Finding Survives? Notes (a) aggregator exclusion Top AI-rise techs (llm, rag, copilot, claude_tool, langchain, openai_api) YES all rates within 1 pp of inclusive (b) company capping 50 Rising/stable/declining classification YES 26/80/17 counts unchanged; magnitudes within 1-2 pp (f) within-2024 calibration AI cluster rises YES (42 of 123 techs ≥SNR 2; entire AI cluster well above) claude_tool 326, agents_framework 140, copilot 44 (g) SWE tier Not tested per tech; co-occurrence structure not tested Low-risk per T04 — regex tier is clean

All primary findings survive the essential sensitivities. The "agents_framework" magnitude is the weakest link and is flagged with a manual-sample caveat, but directional robustness holds.

docs.title: T15 — Semantic similarity landscape & convergence analysis
docs.text:
Agent: I (Wave 2) Date: 2026-04-15 Inputs: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_embeddings.npy (34,099 × 384 MiniLM-L6-v2, L2-normalized), exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_embedding_index.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet (metadata, filtered to text_source='llm'), data/unified.parquet (for description_core_llm rebuild of TF-IDF). Script: exploration/scripts/T15_semantic_landscape.py

Archetype labels (exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet) landed during execution — the main T15 script ran without them, then a follow-up script (exploration/scripts/T15_archetype_convergence.py) computed per-archetype convergence. Results in §3b below. Of 20 archetypes, only one (Java enterprise) has enough coverage on both 2024 sources to run within-2024 calibration cleanly.

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
1. Junior and senior SWE postings are NOT converging — they are diverging, and the divergence just barely fails the within-2024 calibration check. Embedding junior↔senior centroid cosine dropped from 0.981 (2024 pooled) to 0.959 (2026 scraped), cross-period shift −0.022. Within-2024 noise (|arshkon junior↔senior cosine − asaniczka junior↔senior cosine|) is 0.012. SNR = 1.90, just below the 2.0 threshold. TF-IDF SVD tells the same story with a bigger magnitude: 0.944 → 0.876, cross shift −0.068, within-2024 noise 0.035, SNR 1.94. Neither representation clears the calibration bar. The claim "seniority levels converged" is unsupported; the weaker claim "seniority levels may be slightly diverging, SNR below threshold" is what the data shows.
  2. Period is the dominant axis of variation, not seniority. Within-period mean cosine is 0.976 vs across-period 0.964 (embedding) — a gap of 0.012. Within-seniority mean cosine is 0.972 vs across-seniority 0.969 — a gap of 0.004. TF-IDF tells the same story more dramatically (period gap 0.044 vs seniority gap 0.005). The 2024-vs-2026 story dominates everything. This is independent corroboration of the Gate 1 "instrument/period is the dominant signal" narrative.
  3. 2026 entry postings are closer to 2024 entry postings in TF-IDF (nearest-neighbor excess +12.1 pp over base rate), but only modestly so in embedding space (+3.4 pp). The TF-IDF result is the stronger "2026 juniors still look like 2024 juniors" signal, which is the opposite of what a convergence story would predict.
  4. Within-group dispersion slightly rose across seniority cells in 2026 — mean pairwise cosine within 2026 senior = 0.434 vs 2024 senior = 0.407; within 2026 junior = 0.415 vs 2024 junior = 0.398. 2026 groups are more homogeneous (tighter clusters) than 2024 groups, not less. This also contradicts a convergence reading: if junior and senior were blurring, within-group dispersion should rise, not fall.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
• Embeddings. Loaded shared swe_embeddings.npy (34,099 × 384, L2-normalized MiniLM-L6-v2) and the alignment index. Filtered metadata to text_source='llm' (the artifact is pre-filtered, but the filter is re-asserted).
  • TF-IDF. Rebuilt from description_core_llm for the sampled uids only (9,064 rows). TfidfVectorizer(max_features=20000, min_df=5, max_df=0.8, ngram_range=(1,2), stop_words='english') → TruncatedSVD(n_components=100) → L2-normalized. Cached to exploration/artifacts/t15_tfidf_svd.npz for reruns.
  • Stratified sample. Up to 2,000 per period × seniority_3level. Actual sample composition (sample_composition.csv):
period seniority n_total n_sampled 2024 junior 373 373 2024 mid 39 39 2024 senior 12,822 2,000 2024 unknown 8,409 2,000 2026 junior 601 601 2026 mid 51 51 2026 senior 6,164 2,000 2026 unknown 5,640 2,000

Total 9,064 embeddings sampled (below the 12k cap; junior and mid groups are naturally small).

  • Trimmed centroids. For every group, drop the 10% most-distant points from the initial mean before recomputing the centroid. Reduces outlier pull on similarity estimates.
  • Within-2024 calibration. The analogue of the cross-period "did junior↔senior change?" is the within-source "arshkon junior↔senior cosine vs asaniczka junior↔senior cosine". This gives a direct instrument-noise baseline on the same metric.
  • Archetype sub-step skipped. swe_archetype_labels.parquet does not exist at execution time.

docs.title: 1. Sample and embed
docs.text:
See methodology table above. The 2024 junior bucket (373) and both mid buckets (39, 51) are small and limit the statistical confidence of any junior-specific reading.

text_source distribution in the sampled frame: all llm (the shared embeddings file only contains text_source='llm' rows by construction).

docs.title: 2. Structural map (dominant source of variation)
docs.text:
Tables: centroid_similarity_embedding.csv, centroid_similarity_tfidf.csv. Figure: exploration/figures/T15/centroid_similarity_heatmap.png.

Embedding centroid cosine matrix (trimmed, groups = period × seniority_3level):

2024 J 2024 M 2024 S 2024 U 2026 J 2026 M 2026 S 2026 U 2024 junior 1.000 0.967 0.981 0.984 0.978 0.967 0.942 0.963 2024 senior 0.981 0.975 1.000 0.996 0.961 0.981 0.972 0.982 2026 junior 0.978 0.941 0.961 0.960 1.000 0.959 0.959 0.972 2026 senior 0.942 0.942 0.972 0.966 0.959 0.968 1.000 0.994

Diagnostic of dominant variation:

embedding TF-IDF SVD Within-period mean 0.9757 0.9340 Across-period mean 0.9640 0.8896 Period gap 0.0117 0.0444 Within-seniority mean 0.9723 0.9131 Across-seniority mean 0.9685 0.9079 Seniority gap 0.0038 0.0052 Period/seniority ratio 3.1× 8.5×

Period is the dominant axis of variation in both representations. TF-IDF amplifies the period gap 4× beyond what embeddings show (consistent with TF-IDF being more sensitive to vocabulary drift; embeddings smooth over it via semantic similarity). The seniority gap is near-zero in both representations, which is compatible with two very different stories: (a) junior and senior SWE postings genuinely look alike, or (b) the 2024 junior sample (n=373) is too small to carve out a crisp centroid. Both likely contribute.

docs.title: 3. Convergence analysis (the central test)
docs.text:
Table: convergence_with_calibration.csv. Verdict: convergence_verdict.txt.

| representation | 2024 pooled J↔S cosine | 2026 scraped J↔S cosine | shift | direction | within-2024 |arshkon_JS − asaniczka_JS| | SNR | survives? | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Embedding (MiniLM) | 0.9811 | 0.9587 | −0.0224 | ↓ diverging | 0.0118 | 1.90 | NO | | TF-IDF SVD | 0.9444 | 0.8761 | −0.0682 | ↓ diverging | 0.0352 | 1.94 | NO |

Verdict: there is no seniority convergence. Both representations show modest divergence, and the divergence magnitude is slightly below 2× the within-2024 calibration baseline, which means the signal does not clearly survive instrument noise. The most defensible read is "junior and senior semantic distance is approximately unchanged or very slightly widened, within measurement uncertainty." A strong "convergence" claim is contradicted by the sign of the shift; a strong "divergence" claim is contradicted by the SNR threshold.

Per-source sanity check: - arshkon junior↔senior cosine (2024): 0.969 (n_jr=106, n_sr=1,100 in sample) - asaniczka junior↔senior cosine (2024): 0.981 - scraped junior↔senior cosine (2026): 0.959

Note that arshkon's junior↔senior cosine (0.969) is already lower than asaniczka's (0.981) in 2024. If arshkon is a more "accurate" 2024 instrument (per Gate 1 arshkon has the real junior native labels), then the 2026 scraped value (0.959) represents a further drop of only 0.010 below arshkon, not below the pooled 2024 average. Under the arshkon-only baseline (arshkon → scraped), the shift is just −0.010, which is smaller than the within-2024 noise of 0.012. The picture is even flatter than the pooled-2024 comparison suggests.

docs.title: 3b. Per-archetype convergence (follow-up)
docs.text:
Table: exploration/tables/T15/per_archetype_convergence.csv. Script: exploration/scripts/T15_archetype_convergence.py.

Within-2024 calibration is only computable for archetypes where BOTH arshkon and asaniczka have ≥15 junior AND ≥15 senior rows within that archetype — a high bar that only Java enterprise and Defense archetypes clear. For the rest, the shift is reported without calibration.

archetype n J↔S 2024 J↔S 2026 shift within-2024 noise SNR direction LLM / GenAI / ML 3,618 0.942 0.923 −0.019 — — ↓ diverging Java enterprise (Spring/microservices) 3,400 0.974 0.957 −0.017 0.0074 2.30 ↓ diverging (clears SNR) Data engineering (Spark/ETL) 2,294 0.971 0.957 −0.013 — — ↓ diverging Agile/Scrum generalist 1,515 0.979 0.969 −0.011 — — ↓ diverging DevOps / SRE / platform 4,519 0.973 0.965 −0.008 — — ↓ diverging Embedded / firmware 2,199 0.955 0.950 −0.005 — — flat Defense & cleared 7,102 0.971 0.974 +0.003 0.0057 0.52 flat .NET / ASP.NET full-stack 1,573 0.949 0.954 +0.006 — — flat JS frontend (React/TypeScript) 3,371 0.964 0.972 +0.008 — — ↑ converging (weak) Amazon program boilerplate 462 0.909 0.928 +0.018 — — ↑ converging

Findings: - No archetype shows clean convergence above instrument noise. The only archetype with clean calibration (Java enterprise) has SNR 2.30 and direction ↓ diverging, not converging. This is the strongest single cell in the matrix and it points the same direction as the aggregate. - LLM/GenAI has the largest divergence magnitude (−0.019) and also the largest absolute growth in junior sample (23 juniors 2024 → 190 juniors 2026, an 8.3× rise). Junior and senior postings within the LLM/GenAI archetype are moving apart in embedding space, contradicting the "ML/AI is where entry-senior convergence happens" hypothesis that was flagged as a test in the agent brief. - JS frontend is the only mild-convergence cell (+0.008), but without within-2024 calibration it's indistinguishable from noise. - Defense is flat, which is the expected pattern for a template-driven archetype where language standardization suppresses period shifts.

The archetype-stratified read confirms the aggregate read: seniority convergence is not present in any archetype; the only cells clean enough to calibrate show divergence (Java enterprise) or flatness (Defense). The "convergence within ML/AI" hypothesis is not supported — LLM/GenAI is actually the most divergent archetype.

Archetype caveat (main step): The within-2024 calibration cells are mostly empty because the archetype × source × seniority cross-tabulation is sparse. A stronger test would require pooling archetypes or relaxing the seniority constraint; those are follow-up tasks for T21.

docs.title: 4. Within-group dispersion
docs.text:
Table: within_group_dispersion.csv. Figure: exploration/figures/T15/within_group_dispersion.png.

period seniority n mean pairwise cosine median 2024 junior 373 0.398 0.403 2024 senior 2,000 0.407 0.410 2024 unknown 2,000 0.361 0.364 2026 junior 601 0.415 0.417 2026 senior 2,000 0.434 0.435 2026 unknown 2,000 0.401 0.402

2026 groups are more internally homogeneous than 2024 groups, across junior (+0.017), senior (+0.027), and unknown (+0.040). This contradicts the convergence story: if postings were mixing across seniority lines, within-group dispersion should rise (lower mean pairwise cosine = less homogeneous). Instead each group is tighter. The hypothesis compatible with this data is that the 2026 scraped corpus uses more standardized, template-like requirement language than 2024, which would tighten every group's internal cohesion without moving groups closer together.

docs.title: 5. Visualization
docs.text:
Figures: - exploration/figures/T15/umap_pca_tsne.png — side-by-side UMAP / PCA / t-SNE on ~6K sampled rows, colored by period × seniority. - exploration/figures/T15/umap_density_contours.png — publication-style UMAP with density contours and movement arrows for junior / senior centroids 2024→2026. - exploration/figures/T15/centroid_similarity_heatmap.png — embedding vs TF-IDF SVD centroid cosine matrices.

Visual story (UMAP): the 2024 and 2026 groups form two parallel crescents. Within each period, junior and senior cluster at opposite sides of the same crescent but at substantial overlap. Between periods, the crescents are offset — 2026 sits slightly "up and to the right" of 2024 — which is exactly the period-dominant pattern the centroid table already shows. Junior movement arrow (2024→2026) and senior movement arrow (2024→2026) are parallel, not converging. A convergence story would predict the arrows to rotate toward each other; they don't.

UMAP, PCA, and t-SNE all produce the same qualitative layout: period is the dominant axis, seniority is a secondary axis inside each period. PCA is noisier (linear projection of a 384D space) but the crescents are still visible.

docs.title: 6. Nearest-neighbor analysis (2026 entry → top-5 2024 neighbors)
docs.text:
Table: nearest_neighbor_2026entry_to_2024.csv.

Base rates in the 2024 sample: junior 8.5%, mid 0.9%, senior 45.3%, unknown 45.3%.

representation neighbor seniority NN share base rate excess pp embedding junior 11.8% 8.5% +3.4 embedding mid 1.4% 0.9% +0.5 embedding senior 46.5% 45.3% +1.1 embedding unknown 40.3% 45.3% −5.0 TF-IDF junior 20.5% 8.5% +12.1 TF-IDF mid 0.7% 0.9% −0.2 TF-IDF senior 39.0% 45.3% −6.3 TF-IDF unknown 39.8% 45.3% −5.6

Under TF-IDF, 2026 juniors find 2.4× more 2024 juniors than base rate — a strong anti-convergence signal. TF-IDF captures surface-form vocabulary ("entry-level", "0-2 years", "internship", "bachelor's or equivalent") and these markers persist cleanly from 2024 to 2026. Under embedding, the effect is more diluted (+3.4 pp) because MiniLM smooths over surface form toward semantic gist.

The fact that TF-IDF and embedding agree on direction but disagree on magnitude is consistent with the "period dominates but seniority still matters as surface form" reading.

docs.title: 7. Representation robustness table
docs.text:
Table: robustness_table.csv.

Finding Embedding TF-IDF Agreement junior↔senior similarity rises 2024→2026 (i.e. convergence) ↓ (−0.022) ↓ (−0.068) Both say NO period gap > seniority gap YES (3.1×) YES (8.5×) Strong agreement

Both representations agree that (a) seniority did not converge and (b) period dominates seniority. Embeddings and TF-IDF diverge on magnitude but not direction, which is the ideal robustness pattern.

docs.title: 8. Outlier identification
docs.text:
Table: outliers_per_group.csv. Top-20 most-distant-from-centroid postings per (period, seniority) cell.

Observed patterns (manually scanning the outlier uids and companies):

  • 2024 junior outliers lean toward staffing-agency postings (Accroid Inc, ClickJobs.io) and hardware-chip roles (Renesas Electronics) — domain shift from the pure-software median.
  • 2024 senior outliers are dominated by consultancy/IT-services shops (Ekodus, Visionyle Solutions, Open Systems Technologies) posting generic "senior developer" JDs that describe services rather than engineering.
  • 2026 junior outliers are domain-shift cases: Arena (PLM software), Cadence (EDA/silicon), TikTok (recommender ML).
  • 2026 senior outliers are non-core domains: Vast (space hardware), Robert Half (recruiting), General Motors (automotive software) — each has descriptions that focus on industry context (rocket engines, staffing, vehicle architecture) rather than generic SWE stack.

These are domain outliers, not classification errors. A follow-up lane would be to use domain archetype labels (when T09 labels land) to absorb them into their own archetypes and re-run convergence within each archetype.

docs.title: 9. Sensitivity checks
docs.text:
Table: sensitivity_summary.csv.

Dimension 2024 J↔S 2026 J↔S Notes All rows (primary) 0.981 0.959 −0.022 (a) Exclude aggregators 0.980 0.958 −0.022, essentially identical (c) YOE proxy (jr: yoe≤2 / sr: yoe≥5) 0.995 0.984 −0.011 — still diverging, smaller magnitude (e) Arshkon-only 2024 baseline 0.969 — Arshkon → scraped shift is only −0.010 (g) Exclude swe_tier=title_lookup_llm 0.980 0.958 Unchanged

No sensitivity flips the direction. The YOE-proxy seniority version shows a smaller magnitude divergence (−0.011 vs −0.022), which is still below the calibration baseline. The arshkon-only baseline produces the smallest cross-period shift (−0.010), effectively zero. Across all sensitivities, the directional verdict is the same: not converging, slightly diverging, not clearly above instrument noise.

The robustness table and sensitivity table together are enough to state: the convergence hypothesis does not survive calibration under any representation or any sensitivity, and the direction in every cell is either flat or slightly widening.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Period dominates seniority by 3-8×. I expected seniority to be a meaningful internal structure within each period; it is, but only weakly. The 2024↔2026 gap is so much larger that seniority structure is a second-order effect.
  2. Junior↔senior diverged slightly, not converged. Every narrative about "junior scope inflation" implicitly predicts convergence; the data moves in the opposite direction (slightly) and therefore the null is compatible with "no convergence".
  3. 2026 groups are internally MORE homogeneous than 2024 groups. Mean within-group cosine rose +0.02 to +0.04 across seniority cells. Taken together with the modest divergence of group centroids, this is the "template homogenization" pattern, not the "role blurring" pattern.
  4. TF-IDF and embedding disagree on magnitude but agree on direction. TF-IDF sees bigger junior↔senior gaps in both periods (0.944 → 0.876), consistent with vocabulary-form signals persisting while semantic gist becomes fuzzier. Any paper figure that uses only one representation is vulnerable to the other showing a different-looking quantitative story even with the same direction.
  5. Nearest-neighbor TF-IDF shows 2026 juniors still look very junior. The +12.1 pp excess over base rate is the single strongest "seniority structure still exists" signal in the report. If it were +0 pp, a weak convergence story could be revived; +12 pp is not small.
  6. The arshkon-only 2024 junior↔senior cosine (0.969) is lower than the asaniczka-only (0.981). Arshkon has real junior native labels; asaniczka's junior label comes entirely from Stage-10 LLM. If the more accurate 2024 instrument shows lower junior↔senior cosine, the within-2024 calibration story may be understating how different asaniczka junior rows are from genuinely-entry postings. This is a caveat on any asaniczka-pooled entry claim going forward.
  7. No archetype labels. T15 cannot run the per-archetype convergence sub-step. If T09 delivers labels, a targeted re-run of the step-3 centroid comparison within each archetype is a 5-minute patch to the T15 script.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• 2024 junior n=373 and mid n=39 are small. The centroid for 2024 junior is more noisy than the 2026 junior centroid (n=601). This biases any raw-centroid difference slightly.
  • seniority_3level = "mid" has very few rows in both periods (39 / 51) and is effectively indistinguishable from the senior centroid in the similarity matrix — interpret "junior vs senior" as the binding question, not "junior vs mid".
  • MiniLM-L6-v2 has a 256-token max sequence length; long descriptions are truncated to their first ~256 wordpieces. If scope language lives in the last third of long 2026 descriptions, the embedding may under-weight it. TF-IDF does not have this constraint, which is another reason the two representations disagree on magnitude.
  • Embedding centroid cosines near 0.99 are operating at the tail of what cosine can distinguish meaningfully — small differences (0.02) correspond to substantial angular differences but quantitative interpretation near 1.0 is fragile.
  • TF-IDF was rebuilt from scratch on sampled uids only (9,064), not on the full 63,701 corpus, because the shared artifacts do not contain a TF-IDF matrix. The vocabulary is learned on the sample. This is a minor statistical caveat but could slightly exaggerate period gaps if the sample uid set has more period-specific vocabulary than the full corpus.

docs.title: Action items for downstream
docs.text:
1. T09 archetype labels. When they arrive, re-run step-3 per archetype: does ML/AI show convergence while Frontend stays divergent, or is the null stable across archetypes? This is the single highest-value follow-up for T15. The script has a TODO-friendly hook.
  2. T20 seniority boundary sharpness. The finding "2026 groups are internally more homogeneous" is a direct answer to T20's primary question. T20 should treat the T15 dispersion table as a ready-made input.
  3. T21 senior redefinition. Senior centroid moves ~0.02 in UMAP space 2024→2026 (visible as parallel displacement with junior). The direction and magnitude of that movement (decomposed into the LLM/RAG-direction from the T14 tech graph) is T21's first-order question.
  4. Verification (V1). The verifier should independently re-derive the junior↔senior centroid cosines on a different sample (e.g., stratified by metro instead of period × seniority) and confirm the period-dominance and "no convergence" findings hold.
  5. Paper figure candidate. umap_density_contours.png is publication-ready as a negative-result figure: "the seniority levels did not converge — here is what the semantic landscape actually shows". Combined with the centroid heatmap, the pair tells the story cleanly.

docs.title: Sensitivity checks summary
docs.text:
Dimension Finding Survives? Notes (a) aggregator exclusion No seniority convergence; period dominates YES Magnitude shifts < 0.001 (c) seniority operationalization (YOE proxy) No convergence; slightly smaller magnitude YES −0.011 shift, still diverging (d) description text source Only llm-cleaned text available for embeddings N/A Shared artifact is pre-filtered to text_source='llm' (e) source restriction (arshkon-only 2024) Arshkon→scraped shift is −0.010 YES Smallest cross-period shift; essentially flat (f) within-2024 calibration SNR 1.90 (embedding), 1.94 (TF-IDF) NO (marginal, just below 2.0) Divergence direction stable, magnitude does not cleanly clear calibration (g) SWE tier (exclude title_lookup_llm) Unchanged YES Within 0.001

The ONE sensitivity that matters bites: the within-2024 calibration is right at the threshold (SNR 1.90, 1.94). The verdict is therefore "the seniority convergence hypothesis is unsupported; a slight divergence is the best point estimate but is not cleanly above instrument noise" rather than a hard "no" or a hard "yes".

Combined with the T14 finding that the AI/LLM cluster is the one substantive tech-ecosystem change, the T15 result completes a clean story: the 2024→2026 shift is dominated by AI/LLM content expansion, not by restructuring of seniority boundaries. The RQ1 junior-scope-inflation hypothesis, as a convergence prediction, is not borne out in the semantic landscape.

docs.title: T16 — Company hiring strategy typology
docs.text: Agent: J (Wave 3) · Date: 2026-04-15 Inputs: data/unified.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_tech_matrix.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet Script: exploration/scripts/T16_company_typology.py Panel: companies with >=3 SWE postings in BOTH kaggle_arshkon (2024) and scraped (2026), LinkedIn-only. n = 240 companies (125 at >=5 per T07 feasibility).

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
The clean "tool-stack adopter" cluster exists and is measurable. 46 of 240 overlap-panel companies (19%) form a cluster with ΔAI-any rate of +0.52, Δdesc-length +1,149 chars, and Δscope (end-to-end + cross-functional) +0.33, while their within-company entry-share moved essentially zero under both seniority_final (+0.001) and YOE ≤ 2 (−0.002). This is the strongest direct corroboration of Alt C ("tool-stack restructuring is the real change") in the exploration so far: a quarter of the companies that appear in both periods pivoted hard on AI vocabulary without moving their seniority mix.

The within-company decomposition verdict confirms the Wave 2 finding that the two entry operationalizations disagree directionally on the same 240-company panel: seniority_final within-company change is −0.032 (entry rate falls within-company), YOE ≤ 2 within-company change is +0.015 (entry rate rises within-company). The 0.047 pp gap between these is the specification-dependence finding at the within-company level.

Archetype pivots are pervasive. 179 of 240 (74.6%) companies — or 60 of 82 (73.2%) among those with >=5 archetype-labeled rows per period — changed their dominant archetype between 2024 and 2026. Median per-company archetype total variation distance is 0.629. Companies are not just growing their postings; they are reconfiguring what kind of SWE they post.

New market finding — the uniform within-archetype entry rise from T09 does NOT reproduce in the within-company decomposition stratified by archetype. Under seniority_final, LLM/GenAI within-component is +0.148 but Java enterprise is −0.054, Defense −0.027, Agile-generalist −0.185. The T09 "every large archetype rose" pattern is a between-company composition artifact, not a within-company fact. This is material for the Gate 3 reading of T09/T08.

docs.title: 1. Overlap panel construction
docs.text:
Threshold n companies n rows arshkon rows scraped rows >=3 per period 240 13,291 1,567 11,724 >=5 per period 125 ~11,200 960 10,200

Filter: is_swe = TRUE AND source_platform = 'linkedin' AND is_english = TRUE AND date_flag = 'ok'. Company key: company_name_canonical.

Matches T07 feasibility exactly (240 / 125).

docs.title: 2. Per-company change metrics
docs.text:
For each overlap-panel company we compute 2024 vs 2026 change on:

  • Entry share — seniority_final == 'entry' over known rows (entry/mid-senior/associate/director). Reported in parallel with the YOE ≤ 2 share over yoe_extracted known rows, per Gate 2 correction 5 and the analytical preamble.
  • AI prevalence — binary per-posting any over the T14 broad 24-tech union (llm, machine_learning, rag, copilot, cursor_tool, claude_tool, agents_framework, openai_api, claude_api, langchain, langgraph, mcp, …). Sources from the full shared tech matrix (63,701 rows) — NOT text-coverage-capped.
  • Description length — raw description character count. Length is not boilerplate-sensitive at the character level.
  • Scope language — binary per-posting match to \b(end[- ]to[- ]end|cross[- ]functional)\b on LLM-cleaned text where available, raw text otherwise. Bare ownership is excluded per Gate 2 correction 3 (58% precision, contaminated by "employee-owned", "total cost of ownership", "ownership group" in 2024).

File: exploration/tables/T16/per_company_change_vectors.csv (240 rows × 20+ columns).

docs.title: 3. Cluster typology (k-means, k=4)
docs.text:
Features: ΔentryFinal, ΔYOE≤2, ΔAIany, Δdesclen, Δscope. z-scored before clustering. k=4 produced the cleanest interpretable split; k=3 collapsed clusters 2 and 3, k=5 split cluster 0 without meaningful separation. File: exploration/tables/T16/company_typology_k4.csv.

Cluster n Δentry (sen_fin) Δentry (YOE≤2) ΔAI any Δdesc len Δscope Agg share 2026 Interpretation 3 "tool-stack adopter" 46 +0.001 −0.002 +0.523 +1,149 +0.328 0.19 AI-forward, length grew, scope grew, entry flat. Examples: Adobe, AT&T, American Express, Aditi Consulting, Deloitte, KPG99. 0 "moderate adopter" 99 +0.009 −0.031 +0.103 +1,296 +0.221 0.20 Length and scope rose, AI rose modestly, entry slightly fell (YOE). Examples: Amazon, Akamai, Actalent, Ace Technologies. 2 "length-stagnant entry-riser" 74 +0.076 +0.145 +0.180 +13 −0.029 0.14 AI rose, length flat, entry rose under both operationalizations. Defense/cleared + staffing firms dominate: Anduril, BAE Systems, Ascendion, Amazon Web Services, AMERICAN SYSTEMS. 1 "apparent entry dropper" 21 −0.486 −0.076 +0.162 +1,072 +0.118 0.06 Entry rate collapsed under seniority_final but YOE ≤ 2 barely moved. Almost every company here had 3-12 arshkon rows with a 50-100% entry-label rate that did not reproduce in the larger 2026 sample. This is a small-n composition effect, not a real shift: e.g., JPMorgan Chase 12 → 657 rows, Leidos 13 → 245, Intel 4 → 29 rows. The −0.49 seniority_final flip is almost pure denominator/noise.

Fig: exploration/figures/T16/cluster_heatmap_k4.png.

docs.title: 3a. Why the "tool-stack adopter" cluster is the clean test of Alt C
docs.text:
Cluster 3 is exactly the pattern Alt C predicts: employers adopting AI vocabulary (+0.52 pp AI rate, >10× the panel mean baseline) and writing longer, scope-heavier descriptions, while their seniority distribution moves by less than a rounding step under either operationalization. These 46 companies did not narrow their junior rung; they restructured their tool stack vocabulary.

The companies in cluster 3 skew toward system integrators, consulting, cloud/enterprise software, and staffing firms that rebrand roles quickly — exactly the kinds of employers that would rewrite descriptions to match industry vocabulary shifts. They do NOT skew tech-native (no FAANG here; Adobe is the most tech-native). Gate 2 speculated the tool-stack adopters would be FAANG-adjacent; the data says the opposite — it's the downstream adopters rewriting their templates to look modern.

docs.title: 4. Within-company vs between-company decomposition (overlap panel)
docs.text:
Panel-mean rate decomposition with Oaxaca-style split (symmetric weights):

Metric n 2024 panel mean 2026 panel mean Total Δ Within Between Entry share, seniority_final 197 0.0756 0.0628 −0.0128 −0.0318 +0.0190 Entry share, YOE ≤ 2 222 0.1293 0.1981 +0.0689 +0.0152 +0.0536 AI any (broad 24-term) 240 0.0402 0.2693 +0.2291 +0.2103 +0.0188 Desc len (chars) 240 3,473 5,320 +1,847 +935 +913 Scope (e2e + cross-fn) 240 0.1942 0.3578 +0.1636 +0.1340 +0.0296

Verdict on within-company entry share: - Under seniority_final: panel entry rate falls within-company by 3.18 pp, partially offset by a +1.90 pp between-company composition gain. - Under YOE ≤ 2: panel entry rate rises within-company by 1.52 pp, with a much larger +5.36 pp between-company composition gain.

The two operationalizations disagree directionally on the within-company component (−0.032 vs +0.015), confirming T06's earlier finding on the 115-company panel now on the full 240-company panel. This is the spec-dependence finding. Under EITHER operationalization, the aggregate rise in entry share is dominated by the between-company (new entrant composition) component — a company that's in BOTH 2024 and 2026 is NOT running more entry roles on average. The "entry share rose" story requires the new 2026 entrants to do the work.

AI prevalence is overwhelmingly within-company (+0.2103 of +0.2291 total change, 92%). The same 240 companies are listing AI vocabulary at 6.7× their 2024 rate. This is the cleanest within-company version of the RQ3 AI headline on the overlap panel.

Description length and scope are mostly within-company (desc 51% within, scope 82% within). Companies in both periods are writing longer, scope-heavier descriptions.

docs.title: 4a. Aggregator exclusion sensitivity
docs.text:
Excluding is_aggregator = TRUE (171-213 companies retained):

Metric Total Δ Within Between Entry (seniority_final) −0.0181 −0.0382 +0.0201 Entry (YOE ≤ 2) +0.0687 +0.0067 +0.0620 AI any +0.2354 +0.2151 +0.0203 Scope +0.1630 +0.1305 +0.0325

Direction stable across both operationalizations. Under no-aggregator, the YOE ≤ 2 within-component shrinks to +0.007 (nearly zero) — confirming the YOE-based within-company entry rise is aggregator-driven more than driven by non-aggregator employers. Under no-aggregator, the direction disagreement narrows: seniority_final −0.038 vs YOE ≤ 2 +0.007 — the spec conflict survives in direction but weakens.

File: exploration/tables/T16/decomposition_panel.csv, decomposition_panel_no_aggregator.csv.

docs.title: 5. Archetype-stratified within-company decomposition
docs.text:
New result. Stratify the panel by T09 archetype label (filtering out the "No text / raw-only" coverage-gap rows) and compute the within-company component of each metric within each archetype.

Archetype n rows Entry final total Entry final within YOE≤2 within AI within Scope within LLM / GenAI / ML engineering 837 +0.131 +0.148 +0.137 +0.245 +0.202 Data engineering (Spark/ETL) 401 +0.148 +0.144 −0.001 +0.123 +0.100 JS frontend (React/TypeScript) 353 −0.025 −0.058 +0.184 +0.141 +0.080 DevOps / SRE / platform 758 +0.002 +0.006 +0.043 +0.115 +0.038 Defense & cleared 648 −0.049 −0.027 +0.002 +0.044 +0.113 Embedded / firmware 308 −0.043 +0.018 +0.033 −0.002 +0.171 Java enterprise (Spring) 654 −0.052 −0.054 −0.003 +0.108 +0.131 Amazon program boilerplate 200 −0.234 −0.087 −0.065 +0.089 −0.026 Agile/Scrum generalist 234 −0.100 −0.185 −0.128 +0.126 +0.227

Key observation: the within-company entry-final trend is NOT uniform across archetypes under seniority_final — LLM/GenAI and Data engineering are solidly rising within-company (+0.148, +0.144), while Java enterprise, Defense, and Agile-generalist are falling within-company (−0.054, −0.027, −0.185). JS frontend is near-zero / slightly negative (−0.058).

T09 reported that aggregate within-archetype junior share rose uniformly; that was a between-company effect — the archetype mix shifted toward entry-heavy new entrants. On the within-company slice, the entry rise is concentrated in LLM/GenAI and Data, and the entry fall is concentrated in Java/Agile/Defense. This is consistent with the T09 narrative only if you interpret "within-archetype rise" as composition within the archetype's employer mix, not within the same employers' postings.

This is a material refinement to Gate 2's reading of T09. Recommended for Gate 3.

File: exploration/tables/T16/decomposition_by_archetype.csv.

docs.title: 6. Archetype pivots
docs.text:
For each overlap-panel company we compute the distribution over T09 archetype labels in 2024 and 2026 (excluding the "No text / raw-only" gap bucket), then measure whether the dominant archetype changed and compute total variation distance (TVD) between the two period distributions.

  • 179 of 240 companies (74.6%) changed dominant archetype between 2024 and 2026.
  • Restricted to companies with >=5 archetype-labeled rows per period: 60 of 82 (73.2%) still pivoted.
  • Median per-company TVD = 0.629. Half the companies have more than 63% of their archetype mass redistributed between periods.

This is unexpectedly high. Even with a minimum-5 filter the pivot rate holds, so it's not a small-sample artifact. The most likely mechanism is that companies are posting different kinds of SWE roles across the two snapshots — consistent with Cluster 3's "tool-stack adopter" pattern, but it applies broadly beyond cluster 3.

Caveat: T09 archetype labels cover 53.5% of rows (arshkon 99.9%, scraped 30.7% because text_source='llm' gate). For overlap companies, the arshkon side is well-labeled and the scraped side is text-coverage-capped. A company's 2026 labeled sample may be a non-random subset of its 2026 postings (Stage 9 router may favor certain titles). So the pivot rate may overstate true reshuffling if Stage 9 favored AI/LLM-looking titles.

Fig: exploration/figures/T16/archetype_tvd_hist.png. Files: exploration/tables/T16/archetype_dominant_pivot.csv, archetype_tvd_per_company.csv.

docs.title: 7. New market entrants (2026-only companies)
docs.text:
Companies posting on scraped 2026 that were not present in arshkon 2024. These are NOT in the overlap panel — this is the contrast.

Bucket n companies n rows Entry (sen_fin) Entry (YOE≤2) AI any Desc len Scope Agg share Returning (in both) 1,004 17,422 0.061 0.196 0.267 5,233 0.372 0.152 New in 2026 only 6,936 23,459 0.072 0.152 0.288 4,673 0.387 0.177 T08 entry specialists (6 firms) 6 2,083 0.128 0.208 0.206 5,448 0.361 0.869

Findings: - The 6 T08-flagged entry-specialist intermediaries (SynergisticIT, WayUp, Lensa, Emonics, Leidos, Jobs via Dice, Jobs via eFinancialCareers, Dice — in the data) have entry rates 2.1× the returning-company baseline (0.128 vs 0.061) and 87% aggregator-flagged. They confirm T08's concentration finding. - The broader pool of 6,936 new-in-2026 companies has entry rates only marginally higher than returning companies (0.072 vs 0.061 under seniority_final; 0.152 vs 0.196 under YOE ≤ 2 — lower). - Under YOE ≤ 2, new entrants look LESS junior than returning employers. This is the opposite direction from seniority_final. It means YOE-based entry is concentrated in returning (older panel) employers, while seniority_final-labeled entry is modestly concentrated in new-2026 entrants. The two operationalizations select different sub-populations at the company level. - AI prevalence is slightly HIGHER among new entrants (0.288 vs 0.267). The tool-stack explosion is not confined to the overlap panel.

Fig: exploration/figures/T16/new_vs_returning.png. File: exploration/tables/T16/new_entrants_profile.csv.

docs.title: 8. Aggregator vs direct employer (change dimension)
docs.text:
Within the 240-company panel, compare change vectors for companies whose 2026 aggregator share is >=0.5 vs <0.5.

Pool n Δentry (sen_fin) Δentry (YOE≤2) ΔAI any Δdesc len Direct (agg<0.5) 204 −0.029 +0.024 +0.212 +840 Aggregator (agg>=0.5) 36 +0.009 +0.039 +0.215 +925

The two subsets look remarkably similar on ΔAI and Δdesc len. Aggregators are NOT running a different AI narrative than direct employers. Their Δentry under seniority_final is slightly positive (+0.009) vs direct employers' slightly negative (−0.029), but both are within rounding of zero at this panel size. The aggregator vs direct distinction does NOT refine the cluster typology. File: exploration/tables/T16/aggregator_vs_direct.csv.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Tool-stack adopters are NOT tech-native. The 46-company cluster 3 is dominated by consulting firms, system integrators, and enterprise software, not FAANG / AI-labs. The companies with the biggest ΔAI are the ones rewriting their templates to match industry vocabulary, not the ones actually building the AI systems.
  2. Archetype pivot rate is 73% at >=5 per period. Companies are reconfiguring what SWE roles they post, not just growing their post count. This is the cleanest within-company evidence for "employer-side restructuring" — but the restructuring is in archetype, not in seniority.
  3. Within-company entry-final trend is NOT uniform across archetypes. LLM/GenAI and Data engineering rise within-company (+0.148, +0.144); Java enterprise, Defense, Agile-generalist fall within-company (−0.054 to −0.185). T09's aggregate uniform rise was between-company composition, not within-company.
  4. The YOE ≤ 2 within-component collapses to +0.007 under aggregator exclusion. The modest YOE-based within-company entry rise is entirely an aggregator phenomenon on the overlap panel. Real direct-employer companies in the overlap panel have roughly flat YOE ≤ 2 rates.
  5. New-in-2026 entrants look LESS junior under YOE ≤ 2 (0.152 vs 0.196 returning), but MORE junior under seniority_final (0.072 vs 0.061). Specification dependence re-emerges at the new-entrant vs returning cut.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• seniority_final denominator drift. 53% of scraped rows are seniority_final = 'unknown' (Stage 10 LLM budget exhausted). The 240-company panel is small enough that denominator drift at the company level produces unstable entry_final rates for companies with few known-seniority rows. Cluster 1 (the −0.49 entry dropper cluster) is dominated by companies whose 2024 "entry rate" is 50-100% off a sample of 3-12 rows. It is a small-n denominator artifact, not a real flip. The decomposition's 197-company denominator (rather than 240) reflects exclusion of companies where one period had zero known-seniority rows.
  • Archetype labels have 30.7% coverage on the scraped side (vs 99.9% on arshkon). Per-company archetype distributions on 2026 may be biased by which titles Stage 9 routed to LLM extraction. The 73% pivot rate should be treated as an upper bound until the router's title-selection bias can be characterized.
  • Desc_len uses raw description char count. Gate 1's length growth finding includes this metric. Characters are not boilerplate-sensitive at the count level, but the interpretation of "scope from desc length" IS — desc grew +1,767 chars on average in panel companies, of which T13 shows ~37% is responsibilities/role_summary and 0% is requirements.
  • Scope uses V1-validated patterns only (end-to-end + cross-functional). Expected precision ~96-100%. Bare ownership is excluded.

docs.title: Sensitivity checks summary
docs.text:
Dimension Applied Direction-changing? (a) Aggregator exclusion ✓ No direction flip for entry (sen_fin), YOE ≤ 2, AI, scope. YOE ≤ 2 within shrinks to near-zero (materially sensitive on magnitude). (b) Company capping (50/co) Not applied as primary (240 companies × small avg n per period). Impact would be on cluster-3 Adobe/Deloitte-type companies whose 2026 n reaches ~60-130 — capped to 50 would dampen their Δ values but not change cluster membership. (c) Seniority operationalization ✓ (both seniority_final and YOE ≤ 2 throughout) Direction-flip on within-company entry component — this IS the headline.

Material sensitivities: - YOE ≤ 2 within-company entry change under aggregator exclusion (+0.015 → +0.007) — magnitude sensitive, direction stable. - seniority_final within-company entry change direction differs from YOE ≤ 2 direction — structural spec dependence (RQ5 evidence).

docs.title: Action items for downstream
docs.text:
• Gate 3 note (for synthesis): T16 Cluster 3 is the cleanest cross-validation of Alt C. 19% of overlap-panel companies pivoted hard on AI vocabulary without moving seniority mix.
  • For T28 (domain-stratified scope): The within-company archetype decomposition (Section 5) shows LLM/GenAI within-company entry rose +0.148 while Java/Defense within-company entry fell. This refines T09's "uniform rise" interpretation — the uniformity was between-company. T28 should adopt per-archetype within-company cuts where possible.
  • For T24 (hypothesis generation): The 73% archetype pivot rate at >=5 per period is novel. It suggests companies are reconfiguring what SWE roles they post at much higher frequency than prior literature assumes, and motivates an RQ around employer-side role reconfiguration.
  • For V2 verification: Re-derive the cluster-3 ΔAI mean (+0.523) under an independent script, and verify that the archetype pivot rate at >=5 holds with random alternative random seeds in the K-means (it should — the archetype pivot computation does not involve clustering).
  • For preprocessing owner: The seniority_final = 'unknown' share on scraped is now the dominant binding constraint for company-level analysis. Stage 10 LLM budget priority for scraped should be raised ahead of any further company-level analytical work.

docs.title: T17 — Geographic market structure
docs.text: Agent: J (Wave 3) · Date: 2026-04-15 Inputs: data/unified.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_tech_matrix.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet Script: exploration/scripts/T17_geographic_structure.py Sample: 18 metros qualifying with n >= 50 SWE postings in BOTH kaggle_arshkon 2024 and scraped 2026. Total metro-assigned rows: 1,962 (2024) + 24,220 (2026) = 26,182. Multi-location / remote rows excluded (see Section 1).

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
Metro-level AI surge vs entry-share change: Pearson r = −0.28, p = 0.255 (n=18). Spearman ρ = −0.441, p = 0.067. Under aggregator exclusion, Pearson r strengthens to −0.38 but remains non-significant (p=0.13). Under 50/company capping, r = −0.30.

Verdict: The correlation is weakly negative but does not reject the null of "no metro-level relationship between the AI surge and entry-share change." This is consistent with Alt C's prediction that the AI surge is metro-uniform and orthogonal to entry-share movement. The point estimate leans toward "metros with larger AI surges had SLIGHTLY larger entry-share DECLINES" — a direction that is NOT what the simple junior-scope-inflation story predicts — but it is below the SNR threshold for a Wave 3 claim.

AI surge is broadly uniform. 18 of 18 metros saw ΔAI any between +0.07 and +0.30 (mean +0.19, std 0.06). Every qualifying metro's AI rate at least tripled. The smallest surge (Detroit +0.07) is 2× the 2024 baseline; the largest (Seattle +0.30) is 4×. Geography is NOT a hiding place for AI prevalence change.

Entry-share movement is NOT concentrated in tech hubs. The three biggest AI-surge metros (Seattle +0.30, SF Bay +0.28, Austin +0.24) had minimal entry-share movement under seniority_final (Seattle −0.028, SF Bay −0.026, Austin +0.001). The metros with the biggest entry-share drops are Phoenix (−0.13) and Houston (−0.12) — both modest-AI-surge markets.

Archetype geography is real and shifting. SF Bay's LLM/GenAI share grew from 15.7% to 35.6% (+19.9 pp, relative index 1.78 vs global). Austin's JS frontend share collapsed from 25.3% to 9.1% while its LLM/GenAI share rose from 3.2% to 15.9%. Washington DC's SCI-cleared-SWE share nearly quadrupled (3.2% → 11.8%). Defense archetype is concentrated in LA (index 1.87), Denver (1.75), Detroit (1.70) — not what most people would guess. These are first-class geographic findings and they complicate the national-level narrative.

docs.title: 1. Metro qualification and multi-location handling
docs.text:
Per-T07 feasibility and the task preamble, multi-location postings are excluded from the metro rollup. is_multi_location = TRUE rows have metro_area = NULL by construction (Stage 4 of preprocessing collapses syndicated postings). Remote postings also carry metro_area = NULL.

Subset n rows Multi-location SWE (2024+2026, all sources) 4,100 Multi-location SWE on scraped 2026 3,090 Metro-assigned SWE on scraped 2026 30,560 Remote-only (is_remote=TRUE, metro=NULL) scraped 2026 8,128

Qualifying metros at n >= 50 per period: 18 metros, matching T07's feasibility table. File: exploration/tables/T17/metro_metrics.csv.

docs.title: 2. Per-metro metric table (primary)
docs.text:
Rows sorted by ΔAI any descending.

Metro n 2024 n 2026 Δ entry (sen_fin) Δ entry (YOE≤2) ΔAI any ΔAI tools Δ scope Δ desc len (med) Seattle 173 2,201 −0.028 +0.048 +0.303 +0.217 +0.113 +1,478 SF Bay Area 359 5,012 −0.026 +0.106 +0.283 +0.182 +0.185 +1,509 Austin 95 1,129 +0.001 −0.048 +0.243 +0.147 +0.123 +1,471 Dallas-Ft Worth 245 2,058 −0.034 −0.001 +0.238 +0.091 +0.125 +1,434 Atlanta 139 1,087 −0.057 −0.004 +0.228 +0.082 +0.180 +1,367 Charlotte 75 1,061 −0.099 −0.058 +0.224 +0.084 +0.161 +1,370 Boston 132 1,541 +0.019 +0.041 +0.220 +0.101 +0.117 +1,406 San Diego 53 492 −0.018 +0.133 +0.205 +0.131 +0.304 +1,391 Chicago 152 1,032 +0.048 −0.018 +0.204 +0.060 +0.173 +1,376 Raleigh-Durham 51 558 +0.103 +0.013 +0.203 +0.046 +0.128 +1,323 Phoenix 75 706 −0.131 −0.002 +0.170 +0.054 +0.209 +1,333 Houston 65 526 −0.117 −0.079 +0.167 +0.037 +0.135 +1,299 Philadelphia 80 831 +0.044 +0.158 +0.149 +0.053 +0.157 +1,321 Denver 80 741 +0.018 +0.078 +0.147 +0.032 +0.113 +1,295 Minneapolis 54 558 +0.001 −0.043 +0.137 +0.039 +0.213 +1,322 Washington DC 250 3,036 −0.003 −0.037 +0.133 +0.042 +0.066 +1,272 Los Angeles 121 1,660 +0.036 +0.098 +0.107 +0.058 +0.162 +1,343 Detroit 67 622 +0.065 −0.145 +0.070 +0.024 +0.097 +1,354

Observations: - AI any rose monotonically by at least +0.07 in every metro. Tech hubs (Seattle, SF, Austin) lead; Detroit, LA, DC lag — but the gap is 4× not 100×. AI adoption is a national phenomenon, not a coastal one. - AI tools-specific (copilot, cursor, claude tool, openai_api, claude_api, mcp) shows bigger tech-hub concentration — Seattle +0.22, SF +0.18, Austin +0.15 — but rises everywhere (minimum Detroit +0.02). Tool prevalence is more coastal than broad AI prevalence. - Entry share (seniority_final) is highly variable: +0.103 (Raleigh-Durham) to −0.131 (Phoenix). Five metros show declines >= 0.05 pp, two show increases >= 0.05 pp, and 11 are within ±0.05. - Entry share (YOE ≤ 2) has a different geographic pattern: +0.158 (Philadelphia), +0.133 (San Diego), +0.106 (SF Bay), versus −0.145 (Detroit), −0.079 (Houston). Again the two operationalizations do not align at the metro level. - Scope (end-to-end + cross-fn) rose in all 18 metros. San Diego leads (+0.304), DC lags (+0.066). Within-metro scope growth is universal. - Description length rose +1,272 to +1,509 median chars in every metro. Narrow +240-char spread.

Fig: exploration/figures/T17/metro_heatmap.png.

docs.title: 3. Metro-level correlation test (primary Alt C test)
docs.text:
Pearson and Spearman correlations between metro-level Δ metrics (n=18):

x y Pearson r p Spearman ρ p ΔAI any Δ entry (sen_fin) −0.283 0.255 −0.441 0.067 ΔAI any Δ entry (YOE≤2) +0.236 0.345 +0.164 0.515 ΔAI any Δ desc len −0.158 0.531 −0.092 0.717 ΔAI any Δ scope +0.117 0.643 +0.135 0.593 ΔAI tools Δ entry (sen_fin) −0.117 0.644 −0.226 0.367 Δ desc len Δ entry (sen_fin) +0.047 0.853 +0.154 0.542 Δ scope Δ entry (sen_fin) −0.258 0.301 −0.273 0.272

Alt C prediction test: Under Alt C, metro-level AI surge should be uniform (confirmed: std 0.06 around mean 0.19) and uncorrelated with metro-level entry-share movement (confirmed at conventional thresholds — neither Pearson nor Spearman is significant at p<0.05).

Note: the Spearman ρ of −0.441 (p=0.067) for ΔAI any vs Δentry(sen_fin) is close to significance. If taken at face value, it would indicate that metros with larger AI surges had larger entry-share DECLINES under seniority_final — the direction expected under the "AI restructuring pushes juniors out" hypothesis. But under YOE ≤ 2, the rank correlation flips to +0.164 (positive). The spec dependence of the entry metric makes the metro-level correlation uninformative about the "AI restructuring pushes juniors out" claim: you can get either direction depending on which entry operationalization you use.

Interpretation: the correlation result does NOT overturn Alt C. It is consistent with "uniform AI surge, instrument-driven entry-share noise." It is also technically consistent with a weak direction-unstable AI → entry relationship, but the spec dependence dissolves that interpretation.

docs.title: 3a. Sensitivities on the correlation
docs.text:
Spec n Pearson r (ΔAI × Δentry sen_fin) Spearman ρ All rows (primary) 18 −0.283 −0.441 (p=0.067) Aggregator-excluded 17 −0.382 (p=0.130) −0.449 (p=0.071) Capped 50/company 18 −0.296 −0.447 (p=0.063)

The correlation strengthens slightly under aggregator exclusion but never reaches significance at p<0.05.

Fig: exploration/figures/T17/correlation_ai_vs_entry.png. File: exploration/tables/T17/metro_correlations.csv.

docs.title: 4. Archetype geography
docs.text: Distribution of T09 archetype labels by metro × period, with archetype_name = "No text / raw-only" excluded. Coverage is 53.5% on average (arshkon 99.9% / scraped 30.7%), so scraped archetype shares reflect the router-selected Stage 9 sample — interpret cautiously.

docs.title: 4a. Over-indexing archetypes by metro (2026 scraped, relative to global 2026)
docs.text:
Metro Top over-indexed (index > 1.4) SF Bay Area LLM/GenAI (1.78), JS frontend (1.53) Seattle LLM/GenAI (1.55), Data engineering (1.21) San Diego Embedded (3.27), Defense (1.94) Detroit Embedded (2.22), Defense (1.70) LA Embedded (2.49), Defense (1.87) Houston Data engineering (1.91), DevOps (1.36), Defense (1.49) DC DevOps (1.39), US gov SCI-cleared (ratio not shown but ~5x) Denver Defense (1.75), DevOps (1.33) Raleigh-Durham Data engineering (1.49), Defense (1.15) Minneapolis Data engineering (1.38), Defense (1.64)

Surprises: 1. LLM/GenAI is concentrated in SF (1.78×) and Seattle (1.55×) and only marginally present elsewhere. At the metro level, the LLM revolution is visibly geographically concentrated, even though the broad AI keyword surge is national. 2. Defense & cleared archetype is disproportionately LA (1.87), San Diego (1.94), Denver (1.75), Detroit (1.70), Minneapolis (1.64), Philadelphia (1.57), Houston (1.49). Not DC. DC is actually below-index (0.90) for "Defense & cleared" but above-index for the more specific "US gov SCI-cleared SWE" sub-archetype. The sub-archetype geography is sharper than the broad archetype geography. 3. Embedded / firmware is heavily concentrated: San Diego 3.27×, LA 2.49×, Detroit 2.22×, Austin 1.58×. These are the physical-hardware metros. 4. DevOps / SRE / platform is geographically uniform (most metros 0.8-1.4×). Platform work is everywhere.

docs.title: 4b. Archetype mix SHIFTS 2024 → 2026 (examples)
docs.text:
SF Bay Area (top 5 archetypes by 2026 share): | Archetype | 2024 | 2026 | Δ | |---|---:|---:|---:| | LLM / GenAI / ML engineering | 0.157 | 0.356 | +0.199 | | JS frontend (React/TS) | 0.157 | 0.144 | −0.013 | | DevOps / SRE / platform | 0.118 | 0.111 | −0.007 | | Embedded / firmware | 0.079 | 0.069 | −0.010 | | Data engineering | 0.076 | 0.060 | −0.016 | | Java enterprise | 0.118 | 0.047 | −0.071 | | Defense & cleared | 0.101 | 0.060 | −0.041 |

SF Bay shifted 20 pp of its archetype mass into LLM/GenAI in two years, primarily by contracting Java enterprise and Defense. The JS-frontend share held steady.

Austin: | Archetype | 2024 | 2026 | Δ | |---|---:|---:|---:| | DevOps / SRE / platform | 0.158 | 0.165 | +0.007 | | LLM / GenAI / ML engineering | 0.032 | 0.159 | +0.127 | | Java enterprise | 0.137 | 0.126 | −0.011 | | Embedded / firmware | 0.116 | 0.112 | −0.004 | | JS frontend | 0.253 | 0.091 | −0.162 |

Austin's JS-frontend share collapsed by 16 pp and its LLM/GenAI share gained 12.7 pp. This is the sharpest archetype-shift in any metro I looked at. Austin's "we're the Silicon Hills of frontend" narrative did not survive two years.

Washington DC: | Archetype | 2024 | 2026 | Δ | |---|---:|---:|---:| | DevOps / SRE / platform | 0.156 | 0.207 | +0.051 | | LLM / GenAI / ML | 0.068 | 0.123 | +0.055 | | US gov SCI-cleared SWE | 0.032 | 0.118 | +0.086 | | Defense & cleared | 0.188 | 0.110 | −0.078 | | Java enterprise | 0.136 | 0.111 | −0.025 |

DC is rebalancing from the broad "Defense" label into the specific SCI-cleared label (probably a BERTopic resolution issue + real fed-contract posting growth). DevOps and LLM both grew.

Files: exploration/tables/T17/archetype_by_metro.csv, archetype_shares_2024.csv, archetype_shares_2026.csv, archetype_rel_index_2026.csv. Fig: exploration/figures/T17/archetype_by_metro.png.

docs.title: 5. Remote work dimension
docs.text:
In this schema, is_remote = TRUE rows carry metro_area = NULL (they are not assigned to a metro). So "remote share by metro" mechanically returns 0 for every metro. The valid test is to compare the remote-only pool vs the metro-assigned pool on 2026 SWE LinkedIn rows.

Bucket n entry (sen_fin) entry (YOE≤2) AI any AI tools Scope Desc len (med) Tech count (med) Metro-assigned (2026) 30,560 0.065 0.173 0.274 0.093 0.375 4,611 6 Remote-only (2026) 8,128 0.074 0.159 0.273 0.105 0.387 5,458 6

Findings: - AI any is identical (0.273 vs 0.274) between remote and metro-assigned pools. Remote hiring is not more AI-forward than in-metro hiring. - AI tools-specific is slightly higher in remote (+0.012). Small. - Entry share under seniority_final is slightly HIGHER in remote (0.074 vs 0.065). Entry under YOE ≤ 2 is slightly LOWER in remote (0.159 vs 0.173). Opposite directions again — spec dependence at the remote/in-metro cut. - Remote descriptions are ~850 chars longer on median than metro-assigned. Scope rate slightly higher.

Interpretation: The remote pool and the metro-assigned pool are structurally similar on the primary metrics. Remote hiring is not a distinct labor market segment for these metrics.

Fig: exploration/figures/T17/remote_vs_metro_profile.png. File: exploration/tables/T17/remote_vs_metro_profile.csv.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Austin's JS-frontend share collapsed from 25.3% to 9.1% while LLM/GenAI share quadrupled. This is the sharpest single metro × archetype shift in the data. Did not expect it.
  2. LLM/GenAI archetype is visibly geographically concentrated (SF 1.78×, Seattle 1.55×, everywhere else ~1×), even though the broad AI keyword surge is metro-uniform. There are TWO AI signals: the broad AI vocabulary that rose everywhere, and the LLM/GenAI job category that only rose sharply in SF and Seattle.
  3. Defense-related work is LA/San Diego/Detroit/Denver, not DC. DC is below-index on Defense-broad but above-index on SCI-cleared. The standard "DC = defense tech" intuition is wrong at the metro level and gets corrected when you split Defense into general-cleared vs SCI-cleared.
  4. Phoenix and Houston had the biggest entry-share drops (seniority_final) despite modest AI surges (+0.17, +0.17). The metros with the biggest entry drops are NOT the ones with the biggest AI surges.
  5. Raleigh-Durham had the biggest entry-share INCREASE (+0.10 under seniority_final) — but entry under YOE ≤ 2 barely moved (+0.013). This is another example of the spec dependence being live at the metro level. Something about Raleigh-Durham's 2026 sample relabeled toward seniority_final = entry.
  6. Remote vs metro-assigned pools are nearly identical on AI any (0.273 vs 0.274). Rhetoric about "remote engineering is the AI-native labor market" is not supported by the data — AI prevalence is orthogonal to remote/in-metro status.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• Small 2024 per-metro n. The 18 qualifying metros have arshkon counts from 51 (Raleigh-Durham) to 359 (SF Bay), averaging 129. With 10% entry rates, that's ~5-36 entry rows per metro in 2024. Per-metro rate estimates are noisy.
  • Archetype coverage asymmetry. Arshkon archetype labels cover 99.9%; scraped 30.7%. Per-metro 2026 archetype shares reflect the Stage 9 LLM router's selection, which may favor certain title patterns. The Austin JS-frontend collapse and SF LLM/GenAI surge should be verified against title-level distributions (not in T17 scope).
  • Entry-share metro rates have denominator drift — scraped seniority_final = unknown averages 53%, varying by metro. Per-metro entry-share comparisons carry residual denominator risk; the YOE ≤ 2 proxy is label-independent and carries no such risk.
  • Remote pool detected via is_remote. This is an explicit scraper-side flag (not the 0% 2024 data-artifact flag that Gate 1 warned about). Remote pool n=8,128 for scraped 2026.

docs.title: Sensitivity checks summary
docs.text:
Dimension Applied Result (a) Aggregator exclusion ✓ (metro_metrics_no_aggregator.csv) Correlation direction preserved, magnitude slightly larger. No metros drop out (17 of 18 retained). (b) Company capping (50/company) ✓ (metro_metrics_cap50.csv) Direction preserved across all metrics. SF Bay ΔAI any shrinks from 0.283 to ~0.27 under cap (small companies like Adobe get capped).

Not materially sensitive on any headline. The metro rankings by ΔAI are stable; the correlation is robust to direction but non-significant under every spec.

docs.title: Action items for downstream
docs.text:
• For T28 (domain-stratified scope): The metro-level LLM/GenAI concentration in SF+Seattle (1.55-1.78×) is informative for domain stratification. T28 should verify that the within-archetype scope/requirement change it finds is not just an SF+Seattle effect.
  • For Gate 3 (V2 verification): Re-derive the metro-level Pearson r for ΔAI × Δentry under an independent script — verify the −0.28 and its Spearman analog.
  • For the paper: The metro-uniform AI surge is publishable as a geographic robustness claim. "AI adoption in SWE postings rose at least 2× in every one of the 18 metros qualifying at n≥50 per period" is a clean sentence. The entry-share geographic heterogeneity argues against a simple "juniors getting pushed out" reading that would predict entry declines proportional to AI surge.
  • For preprocessing owner: Remote rows are being flagged on scraped but carry metro_area = NULL. This is fine structurally but means per-metro analyses cannot measure remote share. Consider a metro_source = 'company_headquarters' fill for remote rows as a diagnostic option (not as a primary metro label).
