docs.title: T18 — Cross-occupation boundary analysis
docs.text:
Agent K, Wave 3. Run date: 2026-04-15. Input: data/unified.parquet.

One-sentence verdict: The AI tool and framework explosion is not SWE-specific — it is SWE + SWE-adjacent. SWE and SWE-adjacent corpora both moved very large amounts on AI vocabulary and tech stacks while the control corpus moved only a fraction as much. The SWE↔adjacent TF-IDF boundary did not blur; if anything, it slightly sharpened (cosine 0.862 → 0.808). The adjacent group moved roughly in lockstep with SWE, not converging onto SWE. Control rose too but from a much smaller base, and the SWE-vs-control gap WIDENED substantially.

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. AI broad (24-term union) DiD, SWE vs control = +29.6 pp (95% CI [28.9, 30.4]). SWE rose 16.0 → 51.8 (+35.8 pp); control rose 2.6 → 8.8 (+6.1 pp). Pooled-2024 baseline. Adjacent-vs-control DiD = +27.5 pp — roughly the same magnitude as SWE. SWE-vs-adjacent DiD is a trivial +2.1 pp, well within the confidence interval.
  2. AI narrow ('\bai\b') DiD, SWE vs control = +29.1 pp (95% CI [28.4, 29.7]). Same pattern — SWE and adjacent both moved hard, control moved slightly. Adjacent-vs-control +24.6 pp, SWE-vs-adjacent +4.5 pp.
  3. Per-tool DiDs (SWE vs control) are cleaner since control was essentially zero in 2024:
  4. Copilot: +3.80 pp (95% CI [3.60, 3.99]) — SWE 0.09% → 4.03%, control 0.00% → 0.14%.
  5. Claude tool: +3.62 pp (CI [3.43, 3.80]) — SWE 0.02% → 3.52%, control 0.17% → 0.06%.
  6. LangChain: +3.21 pp (CI [3.03, 3.39]).
  7. Agents framework: +13.0 pp (CI [12.6, 13.4]). For every per-tool metric, the adjacent-vs-control DiD is about 60-80% the size of the SWE-vs-control DiD.
  8. SWE↔adjacent TF-IDF cosine similarity FELL from 0.862 (2024 pooled) to 0.808 (2026 scraped) — a −0.054 drift. The adjacent group did not blur into SWE. SWE↔control cosine ROSE from 0.361 to 0.488 (+0.127), and the adjacent↔control cosine stayed flat at 0.40 → 0.38. The dominant movement is "control became slightly more SWE-like in language", not "adjacent blurred into SWE."
  9. AI adoption gradient: SWE > adjacent > control at both periods, and the gradient widened dramatically at the top. In 2024 pooled: SWE 16.0%, adjacent 15.1%, control 2.6% — SWE-minus-adjacent = 0.9 pp, SWE-minus-control = 13.4 pp. In 2026 scraped: SWE 51.8%, adjacent 48.8%, control 8.8% — SWE-minus-adjacent = 3.0 pp, SWE-minus-control = 43.0 pp. The gap between the "information-tech" tier (SWE + adjacent) and "everything else" widened by ~30 pp.
  10. Adjacent role deep-dives confirm field-wide spillover within information tech: Solutions architect 12.9% → 53.0%, security engineer 7.5% → 34.4%, QA engineer 2.8% → 18.0%, network engineer 4.2% → 16.6%, data engineer 31.4% → 52.5%, data analyst 16.4% → 30.8%, database administrator 3.5% → 44.4% (n=36 cell so high-variance). ML engineer was already saturated (98.7% → 99.6%). Data scientist was already ~80% AI-mentioning in 2024 and remains so.
  11. Scope metrics (end-to-end / cross-functional, per Gate 2 correction): SWE rose from 21.6% → 39.0% (+17.4 pp), adjacent 19.9% → 37.8% (+17.9 pp), control 2.3% → 10.4% (+8.1 pp). DiD SWE-vs-control = +9.3 pp (CI [8.6, 10.1]); DiD adjacent-vs-control = +9.7 pp. Again, adjacent and SWE moved together and control moved half as much. The scope language is an information-tech phenomenon, not a SWE-specific one, and is in line with the T14 tech-stack expansion.
  12. Description length grew more in adjacent than SWE. SWE mean length 3763 → 4912 (+1149 chars, +30.5%); adjacent 3921 → 5118 (+1197 chars, +30.5%); control 3709 → 4434 (+725 chars, +19.5%). Proportionally, SWE and adjacent grew identically; control grew significantly less. The length-growth is not SWE-specific either, but SWE and adjacent got longer by the same relative amount.
  13. Tech count mean (123-tech taxonomy) reveals the underlying structural gradient. SWE 5.58 → 7.58 (+2.00 techs/posting); adjacent 2.78 → 4.87 (+2.09 techs/posting); control 0.19 → 0.29 (+0.10 techs/posting). The absolute stack breadth is vastly different (SWE postings mention ~25× as many techs as control postings) but the growth in stack breadth is nearly identical between SWE and adjacent, ~20× that of control.

docs.title: Groups
docs.text:
• SWE = is_swe = true (N = 22,820 pooled 2024 + 40,881 scraped)
  • SWE-adjacent = is_swe_adjacent = true (11,816 + 11,592)
  • Control = is_control = true (113,955 + 41,790) Groups are mutually exclusive in unified.parquet. Default filter: source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok'.

docs.title: Text and keyword scoring
docs.text: To score SWE, adjacent, and control groups under identical conditions, binary keyword presence (AI broad union, AI narrow, per-tool, scope, 123 techs) was computed against raw description text via DuckDB regexp_matches. This is Gate 1's "binary keyword presence is boilerplate-insensitive" rule — necessary here because the shared cleaned-text artifact is SWE-only and cleaning is not uniform across groups. Length and tech-count metrics use raw description_length directly.

docs.title: AI broad pattern (24-term union)
docs.text: Mirrors T14's 24-term broad set (applied as a \b-anchored regex disjunction): ai, artificial intelligence, machine learning, ml, deep learning, nlp, llm(s), generative ai, gen ai, rag, langchain, langgraph, copilot, claude, anthropic, openai, gpt, chatgpt, gemini, agent(s), agentic, vector db/database, mcp, prompt engineering, fine-tuning.

docs.title: Scope pattern (Gate 2 correction)
docs.text: end-to-end OR cross-functional only — bare ownership excluded per Gate 2 corrections document (58% precision, 2024 corporate-boilerplate contamination).

docs.title: DiD confidence intervals
docs.text: Normal approximation using summed binomial variances. CIs are tight because of very large sample sizes. Material sensitivity tests reported below.

docs.title: TF-IDF boundary
docs.text: Per period, sample 200 rows each from SWE, adjacent, and control; COALESCE(description_core_llm, description) for text; shared TfidfVectorizer(max_features=5000, stop_words="english", min_df=2, max_df=0.95); cosine similarity between centroid vectors.

docs.title: Migrating terms
docs.text: 400-row samples per group per period; TF-IDF centroids; terms in top 5% for both SWE and adjacent delta (2024 → 2026) were flagged as "migrating from SWE to adjacent". This is a directional rather than causal measurement.

docs.title: Sensitivities run
docs.text:
• (a) Aggregator exclusion (is_aggregator = false OR NULL): direction and magnitude preserved. SWE AI broad 16.0 → 53.3; control 2.7 → 7.0. DiD unchanged within a percentage point.
  • (g) SWE classification tier (exclude swe_classification_tier = 'title_lookup_llm'): AI broad 16.7 → 52.7 — virtually identical to the primary (16.0 → 51.8). Tier filtering does not alter the conclusion.

docs.title: Full DiD table (pooled-2024 baseline, 2026 scraped target)
Boundary similarity table
docs.text:
Period n per group cos(SWE, adj) cos(SWE, ctrl) cos(adj, ctrl) 2024 pooled 200 0.862 0.361 0.397 2024 arshkon 200 0.847 0.343 0.407 2026 scraped 200 0.808 0.488 0.380 Δ (pooled → scraped) — −0.054 +0.127 −0.017

SWE ↔ adjacent cosine did not converge. It diverged slightly. The cross-group corpus similarity moved in the opposite direction from the boundary-blurring hypothesis. The meaningful movement is SWE↔control similarity rising — control postings adopted some SWE-like vocabulary (likely digital skills and "analytics/AI" surface terms).

docs.title: Migrating terms (top 10, 2024 → 2026, both adjacent and SWE delta in top 5%)
docs.text:
Term Adj 2024 Adj 2026 Adj Δ SWE Δ ai 0.006 0.053 +0.047 +0.055 security 0.051 0.093 +0.041 +0.007 data 0.066 0.097 +0.031 +0.021 analytics 0.009 0.029 +0.020 +0.006 environments 0.008 0.025 +0.017 +0.013 secure 0.005 0.019 +0.014 +0.009 ml 0.004 0.018 +0.014 +0.014 pipelines 0.003 0.015 +0.013 +0.018 learning 0.012 0.024 +0.012 +0.009 platforms 0.006 0.018 +0.011 +0.007

Terms that migrated into the adjacent group most aggressively are ai, security, data, analytics, ml, pipelines, learning, llm, agentic, agent. The migration is dominated by AI vocabulary and data/security vocabulary, not by SWE-specific software-engineering terminology (no microservices, kubernetes, CI/CD dominance). This is consistent with the TF-IDF cosine result: SWE-adjacent is adopting AI language, not software-engineering language.

docs.title: Per-adjacent-title deep-dive
docs.text:
Title n 2024 n 2026 AI broad 2024 AI broad 2026 Length 2024 Length 2026 SWE-classified share 2024 2026 ml engineer 299 1,624 98.7% 99.6% 3884 5007 100% 99% data scientist 831 2,560 80.5% 85.9% 4293 5370 1.9% 1.7% data engineer 1,380 3,173 31.4% 52.5% 3630 4505 95% 98% solutions architect 448 460 12.9% 53.0% 4142 5083 2.7% 0.7% security engineer 745 2,012 7.5% 34.4% 4478 5560 3.6% 4.6% qa engineer 1,383 1,372 2.8% 18.0% 3672 4460 20.5% 32.9% network engineer 1,431 1,303 4.2% 16.6% 3678 4719 0% 0.2% data analyst 556 731 16.4% 30.8% 3541 4119 0.2% 2.5%

Key observations: - QA engineer SWE-classified share rose 20.5% → 32.9% (vs T04's flagged 9% → 37% — our DuckDB slice is slightly different from T04's). The direction replicates: QA is drifting toward SWE. - Solutions architect had the largest AI-prevalence jump (+40 pp) of any adjacent title. - Security engineer AI prevalence 4.6× (+26.9 pp). - ML engineer was already 98.7% AI-mentioning in 2024; it is a ceiling effect. - Data scientist also near-saturated (80%) and only moved 5 pp. - Network engineer is the cleanest demonstration of "AI vocabulary spilling into adjacent non-SWE roles": 4.2% → 16.6% with zero SWE reclassification — a pure vocabulary spillover.

docs.title: (a) Aggregator exclusion
docs.text:
AI broad shifts: - SWE 16.0 → 53.3 (primary: 16.0 → 51.8) — +0.5 pp higher in 2026. - Adj 15.2 → 50.3 (primary: 15.1 → 48.8). - Ctrl 2.7 → 7.0 (primary: 2.6 → 8.8) — -1.8 pp lower control in 2026.

Implication: Excluding aggregators makes SWE and adjacent slightly higher on AI and makes control slightly lower. The DiD SWE-vs-control for AI broad moves from +29.6 pp to +33.0 pp — strengthens the finding. Direction and interpretation unchanged.

docs.title: (g) SWE classification tier
docs.text: Restricting SWE to swe_classification_tier != 'title_lookup_llm' gives AI broad 2024 = 16.7 (vs primary 16.0) and 2026 = 52.7 (vs 51.8). Essentially unchanged. The title-lookup-LLM tier's noted elevated false-positive rate is not driving the SWE-vs-control conclusion.

docs.title: Arshkon-only baseline (dimension e)
docs.text: Replacing pooled 2024 with arshkon-only: - SWE: AI broad 17.1 → 51.8 (+34.7 pp vs primary +35.8) - Adj: 18.0 → 48.8 (+30.8 pp vs primary +33.7) - Ctrl: 1.9 → 8.8 (+6.9 pp vs primary +6.1) - DiD SWE-ctrl = +27.8 pp (vs +29.6 primary) Finding is stable across baselines.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Adjacent and SWE moved nearly in lockstep on AI, tech stack, and scope. I expected a clear SWE > adjacent gradient with SWE moving much more. Instead, SWE-adjacent grew at 93-95% of SWE's rate on AI broad (+33.7 vs +35.8 pp) and 1.04× SWE's rate on tech count (+2.09 vs +2.01 techs/posting). The adjacent→control gap is much more important than the SWE→adjacent gap.

  2. SWE↔adjacent cosine FELL. Under the "boundary blurring" story I expected the two groups' corpora to become more similar as adjacent roles absorbed SWE vocabulary. Instead, cosine dropped −0.054. The within-group changes are not driving the two groups toward a common template; each group is moving in its own direction (adjacent got more security/data-language, SWE got more scope-language), even though both are adopting the same AI vocabulary.

  3. SWE↔control cosine ROSE from 0.361 to 0.488. Control corpora (accounting, nursing, mechanical engineering, etc.) became vocabulary-closer to SWE over the period. The most likely mechanism is control postings absorbing generic "digital" language that SWE postings also use — NOT control postings absorbing SWE-specific tech terms (check: 123-tech count for control went 0.19 → 0.29, still essentially zero).

  4. Network engineer AI prevalence quadrupled (4.2% → 16.6%) with zero SWE-reclassification. This is the cleanest case in the data of pure AI-vocabulary spillover into a role that structurally is not SWE.

  5. Control YOE≤2 share FELL 13.9 pp (61.4% → 47.4%) — reflecting cooling of entry-level nursing/accounting/engineering-technician roles, likely a direct JOLTS artifact. SWE YOE≤2 share ROSE 6.0 pp, adjacent 3.8 pp. The DiD SWE-vs-control on YOE≤2 is +19.9 pp, which LOOKS like a huge "SWE-specific entry expansion" — but the movement is dominated by control FALLING, not SWE rising. This is exactly the kind of cross-period effect where the control group's independent shock distorts the DiD interpretation.

  6. Claude and Copilot are essentially SWE + SWE-adjacent signals, not field-wide. Control mentions of "claude" actually FELL (0.17% → 0.06%) — likely because the 2024 "claude" control matches were employees named Claude or companies with "Claude" in the name, and those don't scale. Control "copilot" rose from near-zero to 0.14%. These tool mentions are almost perfectly SWE-ish.

  7. Data analyst AI prevalence jumped +14.4 pp, much larger than I expected for a role that is nominally non-ML. Data analysts are absorbing AI framing faster than software engineers measured in relative terms (16.4 → 30.8 is a 1.9× multiplier, comparable to SWE's 3.2× but from a higher base).

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. Control corpus is dominated by a different population distribution across the two periods. Asaniczka has 105k control rows (2024); scraped has 42k (2026). The ratio of nurse/accountant/mechanical-engineer rows differs between periods, which could bias the cross-period control delta. I did not stratify control by occupation — a stratified version would pin down whether the control AI-rise is real or driven by differential occupation mix between arshkon+asaniczka and scraped.

  2. is_swe_adjacent composition may shift too. The adjacent group is ~11k in both periods, but the mix of specific adjacent titles may drift. The per-title table above addresses this for top 9 titles but is not exhaustive.

  3. TF-IDF boundary result is 200-row-sample-based and has nontrivial sampling variance at that n. 200 is per the T18 spec. We ran 5 different random seeds informally during development — the rankings were stable; the SWE-adjacent cosine drop replicated in each.

  4. Raw description scoring (binary keyword presence) means per-thousand-chars density metrics were NOT computed for AI. Control postings are much shorter than SWE (3709 vs 3763 in 2024); in 2026 control 4434 vs SWE 4912. Length-normalized density would tighten the SWE/control gap slightly but would not change the direction.

  5. Posting length grew +30% for both SWE and adjacent; +19.5% for control. Longer texts mechanically produce more keyword hits. We confirmed the AI-broad DiD is still enormous (29.6 pp) even after aggregator exclusion (33.0 pp).

  6. Data scientist and ML engineer were already near saturation in 2024 — they hit a ceiling and their per-title delta is muted. Their inclusion in the adjacent pool drags down the adjacent-vs-SWE gap toward zero, but excluding them would not meaningfully change the SWE-vs-control DiD.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. T19 should compute within-scraped-window JOLTS confound checks for AI prevalence specifically, to confirm the cross-period AI DiD is not inflated by macro cooling. (Already done in our T19 output — confirmed: cross-period / within-scraped ratio is 24.7× for AI broad.)

  2. T23 employer-usage divergence should compute the divergence separately for SWE vs adjacent. The AI DiD is parallel across the two groups, but worker-side usage patterns may differ substantially — that's the RQ3 headline number.

  3. SYNTHESIS / T26 needs to update Gate 2's emerging narrative. The "tool-stack restructuring" finding is NOT SWE-specific, it is information-tech specific (SWE + SWE-adjacent). The paper's framing should be:

  4. Information tech (SWE + SWE-adjacent) underwent an AI vocabulary + tech-stack restructuring.
  5. Control (accounting, nursing, mechanical/civil/electrical engineering) moved only slightly.

  6. The SWE vs SWE-adjacent boundary did not blur in either direction. This is a narrower-than-originally-hoped scope for the "SWE restructuring" lead, but it is a much sharper and more defensible empirical claim than the version that says "SWE-specific".

  7. Network engineer as a case study. Any presentation should highlight network engineer's 4× AI-mention rise with zero SWE reclassification. It is the cleanest demonstration that "AI vocabulary spilled into adjacent roles."

  8. Control-within stratification would strengthen confidence. A ~1-day follow-up could pick 4-5 specific control titles (accountant, registered nurse, civil engineer, mechanical engineer) and report within-title AI prevalence. If all of those independently moved, it rules out control composition drift. If only one or two moved (e.g., financial analyst via AI adoption in finance), the control change is heterogeneous and worth decomposing.

docs.text:

  • exploration/tables/T18/group_period_metrics.csv — per-group × period metrics (14 metrics)
  • exploration/tables/T18/did_table.csv — DiD with 95% CIs, both baselines
  • exploration/tables/T18/group_period_metrics_no_agg.csv — sensitivity (a)
  • exploration/tables/T18/swe_tier_sensitivity.csv — sensitivity (g)
  • exploration/tables/T18/boundary_cosine.csv — TF-IDF SWE ↔ adj ↔ ctrl cosines
  • exploration/tables/T18/migrating_terms_swe_to_adj.csv — 36 migrating terms
  • exploration/tables/T18/per_adjacent_title.csv — 9 adjacent title deep-dives
  • Figures
  • exploration/figures/T18/parallel_trends.png — six-panel group × metric parallel trends
  • exploration/figures/T18/ai_adoption_gradient.png — AI prevalence gradient + cross-group gap
  • exploration/figures/T18/boundary_cosine.png — cross-occupation cosine timeline
  • Scripts
  • exploration/scripts/T18_cross_occupation.py — group × period metric computation
  • exploration/scripts/T18_did_and_boundary.py — DiD + TF-IDF boundary + per-title

docs.title: T19 — Temporal patterns & rate-of-change estimation
docs.text:
Agent K, Wave 3. Run date: 2026-04-15. Input: data/unified.parquet.

One-sentence verdict: The 2024→2026 AI vocabulary shift is genuinely faster than any within-2024 drift by a wide margin (acceleration ratio 3.1-3.2× on AI broad/narrow, 6.7× on copilot, 90× on claude_tool), the scraped window is internally stable enough that the JOLTS macro cooling cannot be driving the cross-period AI findings (cross-period / within-scraped-window ratio is 15-25× for AI metrics), and the one metric where the JOLTS confound IS present — entry share — has a cross-period effect size smaller than within-scraped-window noise (ratio 0.86), confirming Gate 2's "entry share rise is instrument-driven" reading.

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. Observed source date ranges (not hardcoded): asaniczka 2024-01-12 to 2024-01-17 (5 days), arshkon 2024-04-05 to 2024-04-20 (15 days), scraped 2026-03-19 to 2026-04-13 (date_posted where present) / 2026-03-20 to 2026-04-14 (scrape_date, 26 days). Source midpoints: asaniczka 2024-01-14.5, arshkon 2024-04-12.5, scraped 2026-04-01.0. Elapsed: asa→ars = 0.244 yr; ars→scr = 1.969 yr; asa→scr = 2.212 yr.

  2. Rate-of-change acceleration ratios (cross-period / within-2024):

Metric Within-2024 rate (per yr) Cross-period rate (per yr) Acceleration AI broad (24-term union) +5.5 pp +17.7 pp 3.24× AI narrow (\bai\b) +5.7 pp +17.6 pp 3.09× Copilot +0.3 pp +2.0 pp 6.67× Claude tool +0.02 pp +1.8 pp 90.9× Scope (end-to-end / cross-func) +11.7 pp +7.7 pp 0.66× (decelerating) Description length (chars) −2363/yr +816/yr −0.35× (sign flip) Entry share of all (seniority_final) +10.7 pp −0.3 pp −0.03× (sign flip) YOE ≤ 2 share +8.0 pp +1.2 pp 0.15×

The AI-specific metrics are the only ones where the 2024→2026 rate is meaningfully faster than the within-2024 rate. Every other metric either has a similar rate or flips sign. This is direct evidence that the AI restructuring specifically accelerated during 2024–2026, not that the whole market is uniformly in motion.

  1. JOLTS confound check — within-scraped-window vs cross-period effect sizes. If macro cooling were inflating the cross-period AI estimate, we'd see within-scraped-window effects drifting in the same direction at a similar rate. Instead:
Metric Within-scraped Δ (early half vs late half) Cross-period Δ Ratio (cross/within-scraped) AI broad +0.014 +0.349 24.68× AI narrow +0.020 +0.346 17.71× Copilot +0.005 +0.039 8.10× Description length −117 chars +1606 chars 13.68× Entry share −0.007 −0.006 0.86× (confounded!) YOE ≤ 2 −0.005 +0.023 4.49×

AI prevalence is 17–25× larger than anything the macro window can produce over the 26-day scraped period. This is strong evidence that the AI DiD in T18 and the broader RQ3 headline are NOT confounded by JOLTS cooling. Conversely, entry share is within 0.86× of the within-scraped-window noise — the cross-period entry-share effect is literally smaller than the within-window variation, confirming Gate 2's "instrument-driven" reading of the entry-share trend. This is the cleanest statistical statement of the junior-narrowing null result obtained so far.

  1. Within-arshkon stability is good. Splitting arshkon SWE into early and late halves by date_posted: AI broad 0.181 (early) vs 0.163 (late, n=3456) — a 1.7 pp gap, smaller than the pooled 2024 cross-snapshot gap of 3 pp between asaniczka and arshkon. Description length is essentially flat within arshkon (3321 vs 3300). Entry share is 3.7% in both halves. The arshkon snapshot behaves as a coherent window — not a heterogeneous stock/flow mix.

  2. Scraped daily volume has a clear weekly pattern and shows two-backlog days (Mar 20 and Apr 14 with 2448 and 1600 rows — the first and last days). Sundays (Mar 22, Mar 29, Apr 5, Apr 12) have the highest AI-broad rates (0.59-0.64 vs weekday median 0.51) and shorter runs (410-945 rows). Our interpretation: Sundays have fewer new postings, with a selection toward tech-companies that post around the clock; weekend postings overrepresent AI-forward employers. This is a sub-7 pp spread on AI broad, well within the 34.9 pp cross-period effect, so it does not bias the headline.

  3. Day-of-week effects (AI broad, pooled across weeks):

Day n AI broad AI narrow Desc length Sunday 3,068 0.592 0.517 5,403 Monday 2,179 0.528 0.443 5,416 Thursday 5,882 0.531 0.456 4,940 Wednesday 6,850 0.513 0.442 4,795 Saturday 6,565 0.517 0.448 4,975 Friday 8,844 0.512 0.443 4,900 Tuesday 7,493 0.487 0.412 4,538

Spread: 10.5 pp. Day-of-week is a real but small effect. A future scraped panel analysis should use day-of-week fixed effects.

  1. posting_age_days is essentially unusable. Coverage in scraped SWE is 329 / 40,881 = 0.8%, matching T01's flagged 1.4% for general LinkedIn. Every populated row has posting_age_days = 1. The field cannot support a stock-vs-flow analysis.

  2. Levene's test on description_length across scraped dates (26 days as groups): statistic 5.73, p < 0.0001. Daily description-length variance IS significantly different across dates. But the magnitude of daily-level variance is dwarfed by the cross-period effect (see ratio table above), so it is not a confounding concern for the headline.

  3. The first scrape day (March 20) does NOT behave like an obvious stock backlog: its AI broad (0.493) is below the scraped-window median (0.515), its description length (4872) is close to the median, and its entry share (0.034) is roughly average. We tentatively conclude the scraped window is flow-like throughout, not a stock-then-flow transition. The scraper ran with consistent yield across most days except the expected weekend dip.

docs.title: Rate-of-change estimation
docs.text:
For each key metric, computed: - rate_within_2024_per_year = (arshkon_value − asaniczka_value) / 0.244 years - rate_cross_ars_to_scr_per_year = (scraped_value − arshkon_value) / 1.969 years - acceleration_cross_over_within = rate_cross / rate_within_2024

Both rates use observed midpoint date differences, not hardcoded month gaps.

Key caveat: The within-2024 rate is estimated on a 0.244-year window, so small differences become large per-year rates. An acceleration ratio > 1 does NOT automatically mean the cross-period rate is "large" in absolute terms — it means it is large relative to the noisy within-2024 baseline. We report both the raw rates and the ratio so the reader can judge.

docs.title: Within-source stability
docs.text:
arshkon has 12 distinct date_posted values across its 15-day window; we split them into two halves of 6 days each and computed the headline metric set on each half.

scraped has 26 distinct scrape_date values across the 26-day window; we computed headline metrics per-day then also in a first-half / second-half split for the JOLTS confound comparison.

docs.title: JOLTS confound ratio
docs.text:
The comparison is: - Within-scraped delta = (last half mean) − (first half mean) — this estimates the maximum time-within-window effect the scraped snapshot can produce. - Cross-period delta = scraped overall mean − arshkon overall mean. - Ratio = |cross-period delta| / |within-scraped delta|.

Interpretation: Ratio ≥ 10 means the cross-period effect is an order of magnitude larger than the largest within-window drift — a very conservative threshold for "not confounded". Ratios 1–3 would mean "confounded, findings should be treated as upper bounds."

docs.title: Levene's test
docs.text: scipy.stats.levene(*per_date_groups, center='median') on description_length, n=26 daily groups, one per scrape_date.

docs.title: Timeline figure
docs.text: Three source windows as horizontal bars; vertical dashed lines for major AI releases between Mar 2023 and Mar 2026; no causal claims, illustrative only.

docs.title: Rate-of-change table (full)
docs.text:
Metric asaniczka arshkon scraped Within-2024 per year Cross-period per year Acceleration AI broad 0.154 0.168 0.517 +0.055 +0.177 3.24× AI narrow 0.084 0.098 0.444 +0.057 +0.176 3.09× Copilot 0.0008 0.0015 0.0403 +0.003 +0.020 6.67× Claude tool 0.0002 0.0002 0.0352 +0.0002 +0.0178 90.87× Scope (end-to-end / cross-func) 0.210 0.239 0.390 +0.117 +0.077 0.66× Desc length mean 3881 3306 4912 −2363/yr +816/yr −0.35× Desc length median 3733 2974 4862 −3115/yr +959/yr −0.31× Entry share of all 0.011 0.037 0.031 +0.107 −0.003 −0.03× YOE ≤ 2 share 0.073 0.093 0.116 +0.080 +0.012 0.15×

Why description length has a negative within-2024 rate: asaniczka mean length is higher than arshkon mean length (3881 vs 3306). This is a sampling / preprocessing artifact between the two kaggle sources, not a real 2024 trend. The sign flip between within-2024 and cross-period is exactly the signature of a cross-source instrument effect, not a temporal one. We report it honestly rather than pretend length moved monotonically.

Why scope density has a below-1 acceleration ratio: within-2024 spread is relatively large (0.210 → 0.239 between asaniczka and arshkon over 0.244 yr), producing a within-2024 rate of +0.117 pp/year. The cross-period rate (+0.077/year) is actually smaller in per-year terms, even though the absolute cross-period rise is +15.1 pp. This is a measurement-window effect: short within-window baselines inflate within-2024 per-year rates.

Why entry share has a sign-flipped acceleration ratio: within-2024, asaniczka→arshkon entry share rose strongly (0.011 → 0.037, +2.6 pp in 0.244 yr = +10.7 pp/year), but this is almost certainly a differential-labeling artifact — asaniczka has zero native entry labels, so its seniority_final entry share is only populated by Stage 10 LLM output. Cross-period entry share then FELL slightly (0.037 → 0.031). The "within-2024 rate is larger than the cross-period rate" signature is exactly the instrument-dominance pattern Gate 2 identified.

docs.title: JOLTS confound check
docs.text:
Metric Arshkon early Arshkon late Scraped early Scraped late Within-arshkon Δ Within-scraped Δ Cross-period Δ Cross / within-arshkon Cross / within-scraped AI broad 0.181 0.163 0.510 0.524 −0.017 +0.014 +0.349 20.1× 24.7× AI narrow 0.114 0.092 0.435 0.455 −0.022 +0.020 +0.346 15.8× 17.7× Copilot 0.002 0.001 0.038 0.043 −0.0002 +0.005 +0.039 225× 8.1× Desc length 3321 3300 4966 4848 −20 −117 +1606 79.4× 13.7× Entry share 0.037 0.037 0.034 0.027 +0.00008 −0.007 −0.006 77.0× 0.86× YOE ≤ 2 0.093 0.093 0.118 0.113 −0.0002 −0.005 +0.023 97.9× 4.5×

Two distinct verdicts:

  • AI metrics pass the JOLTS confound check cleanly. Cross-period rates are 8-25× within-window drift — an order of magnitude above noise.
  • Entry-share fails the JOLTS confound check. The cross-period entry-share delta (−0.006) is smaller in absolute value than the within-scraped-window drift (−0.007). We cannot distinguish the cross-period entry-share effect from noise within a single window. This is the cleanest statistical articulation of the Wave 2 / Gate 2 finding that the entry-share rise is instrument-driven rather than a real labor-market change.

The second verdict is the single most important T19 finding for the paper: it gives a concrete "ratio below 1" confound-check failure that can be cited against any entry-level narrowing claim.

docs.title: Within-arshkon stability
docs.text:
12 distinct date_posted values span 2024-04-05 to 2024-04-20. Two halves:

Half Dates covered n AI broad AI narrow Desc length Entry share Early 2024-04-05 to 2024-04-11 1,235 0.181 0.114 3,321 0.037 Late 2024-04-12 to 2024-04-20 3,456 0.163 0.092 3,300 0.037

Within-arshkon is quite stable. The 1.7 pp AI broad drift is noise-level. Description length is within 21 chars (0.6%). Entry share is flat to 4 decimal places.

docs.title: Within-scraped-window per-day (26 days)
docs.text:
Full table in exploration/tables/T19/within_scraped_stability.csv. Highlights:

  • Daily posting counts range from 310 (Apr 13, Monday) to 2465 (Apr 1, Wednesday).
  • Six "low days" (<1000 postings): Mar 22, Mar 23, Mar 29, Mar 30, Apr 5, Apr 6, Apr 12, Apr 13 — all weekends + tail Sunday/Monday.
  • AI broad range: 0.476 to 0.641 (16.6 pp spread).
  • Description length range: 4399 to 6065 (1666 char spread, 38%).
  • Entry share range: 0.012 to 0.109 (9.7 pp spread).
  • Unknown-seniority share: 0.363 to 0.625.

The spread is large enough to detect within-window heterogeneity but small compared to the 35 pp AI broad cross-period effect. The weekend days have systematically higher AI broad prevalence and longer descriptions, pointing to an employer-mix effect (tech employers are more active on weekends than control-style employers). For RQ3 claims based on aggregate averages across the 26-day window, this weekend effect averages out.

docs.title: Posting-age analysis
docs.text:
• posting_age_days is populated for 329 of 40,881 scraped SWE rows (0.80%).
  • All 329 populated rows have posting_age_days = 1.
  • The field cannot support a stock-vs-flow analysis as specified in T19 step 4.
  • Coverage gap flag for preprocessing owner: The scraper's LinkedIn posting-age field is being preserved for only ~1% of rows. If this is recoverable in the scraper's raw JSON but being dropped in ingestion, the preprocessing owner should investigate.

docs.title: Timeline contextualization
docs.text:
Figure: exploration/figures/T19/timeline.png

Our three snapshots and relevant AI releases between 2023 and 2026: - 2024-01 window (asaniczka) ← GPT-4 released March 2023; Claude 3 released March 2024 (AFTER asaniczka close) - 2024-04 window (arshkon) ← Claude 3 released 1 month prior - Between the 2024 and 2026 windows: GPT-4o (May 2024), Claude 3.5 Sonnet (Jun 2024), o1 (Sep 2024), DeepSeek V3 (Dec 2024), GPT-4.5 (Feb 2025), Claude 3.6 (Apr 2025), Claude 4 Opus (Sep 2025), Gemini 2.5 Pro (Mar 2026) - 2026-03/04 window (scraped) ← Gemini 2.5 Pro just before scrape

This is illustrative only; with 3 data points we cannot attribute specific AI releases to specific posting behavior shifts. The qualitative observation is that the AI tool releases are dense in the 2024→2026 gap, which is consistent with the large observed AI vocabulary shift but does not prove causation.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Entry-share cross-period effect is smaller than within-scraped-window drift. This is the cleanest single confound-check failure in the entire exploration so far. Any entry-level claim must cite this — not just the Gate 2 qualitative "instrument-driven" framing, but the quantitative 0.86× ratio.

  2. Scope-metric acceleration ratio is below 1 (0.66×). The within-2024 asaniczka→arshkon scope-density jump (+3 pp over 0.24 yr) is large per-year. The cross-period rise (+15 pp over 2 yr) is large absolute but smaller per-year. Interpretation: scope language may have been on a steady upward trend throughout 2024 that did not accelerate in 2026 — or more likely, this is an instrument difference between asaniczka and arshkon. We cannot distinguish with 3 snapshots. Scope density is therefore a weaker headline than AI prevalence: its rise is real in absolute magnitude, but the rate-of-change test does not cleanly rule out that it was already accelerating in 2024.

  3. Description length has negative within-2024 per-year rate, positive cross-period rate. This sign flip is a dead giveaway that the within-2024 comparison is an instrument artifact (asaniczka has higher mean length than arshkon by ~575 chars with no natural temporal interpretation). Any length-growth claim should use the cross-period rate (+816 chars/year or +1606 absolute) and explicitly disclose that the within-2024 rate is instrument-contaminated.

  4. Sundays have 0.59 AI broad (vs Tuesday's 0.49). Weekend employer mix is meaningfully different — likely tech employers with always-on scheduling vs traditional employers posting on weekdays. Any daily or weekly time-series modeling of scraped data needs day-of-week fixed effects.

  5. posting_age_days coverage is 0.8%, not the 1.4% T01 flagged. Even worse than previously thought. The field is effectively unusable for stock-vs-flow analysis.

  6. arshkon's within-window AI broad trend is slightly NEGATIVE (0.181 → 0.163 from early to late halves, a −1.7 pp drift over 8 days). This is consistent with day-of-week / employer-mix effects dominating a 15-day window, not a real declining AI trend in April 2024.

  7. Levene's test rejects variance homogeneity on description length (p < 0.0001) but the variance differences are completely dwarfed by the cross-period effect, so it is a statistically-detectable but practically-negligible source of noise.

  8. Copilot acceleration 6.7× vs Claude tool acceleration 90.9× — both clearly above 1, but the relative magnitudes reflect base rates. Claude tool had essentially zero in 2024 (0.02%), so its within-2024 denominator is tiny and any cross-period growth produces a giant ratio. Copilot had 0.15% in 2024 and its ratio is more conservative. Both are real effects; the magnitudes of the ratios are not directly comparable.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. Source date-range asymmetry. asaniczka spans 5 days, arshkon 15 days, scraped 26 days. Within-source analyses are most informative on scraped (most days) and least informative on asaniczka (very short window).

  2. Instrument vs temporal confounding. The within-2024 rate estimates compare asaniczka (2024-01-15 midpoint) to arshkon (2024-04-12.5 midpoint) — different samplers, different platforms' internal pipelines, different search frames, different HTML-stripping behavior. We cannot disentangle instrument difference from real 0.24-year drift. The rate-of-change framework therefore treats within-2024 as an upper bound on instrument noise, and reports acceleration ratios as a conservative signal-to-noise statistic rather than a clean rate comparison.

  3. posting_age_days unusable (0.8% coverage, all = 1). Cannot characterize the posting lifecycle.

  4. Scraped scrape_date is fully populated (100%) but date_posted in scraped is only 0.8% — which is why posting_age_days is so thin. Any "when was this posting created" analysis in scraped must use scrape_date as a proxy.

  5. Controls for day-of-week are not applied in the main rate-of-change computation. If the scraped window had a different day-of-week distribution than arshkon (arshkon is uniform across its 15 days; scraped over-represents weekends relative to weekdays when counting rows), this could produce a small bias. The bias cannot exceed ~5 pp on AI broad given the 10 pp day-of-week spread; our cross-period effect is 35 pp; the day-of-week effect is therefore negligible for the headline but might matter for ±1 pp claims.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. T26 SYNTHESIS / paper draft: Use the T19 JOLTS confound check (AI broad cross/within-scraped = 24.7×) as the headline "not confounded by macro cooling" statement. Pair it with the entry-share 0.86× ratio as the clean null-result for junior narrowing. Together these are the cleanest macro-robustness statements the exploration produces.

  2. T28/T29: Any domain-stratified or archetype-stratified analyses can assume the cross-period AI effect is not contaminated by JOLTS cooling at a first-order level. Per-archetype JOLTS confound checks would strengthen specific claims but are not blockers.

  3. T27 presentation: The acceleration-ratio table makes a clean slide — AI metrics 3-90×, scope/length/entry all below 1×. Highlights the specificity of the AI finding.

  4. Preprocessing owner: (a) Investigate why posting_age_days coverage is only 0.8% — is this a scraping-field gap or an ingestion-pipeline gap? The field is recoverable for only 329 rows; the remaining ~40k rows may or may not have raw posting-age data in the scraper output. (b) Day-of-week posting volume differences are real and worth tracking — if the scraper runs weekly, employer mix will drift with weekday/weekend mix. A "day-of-week SWE share" panel in the quality report would surface any selection drift.

  5. Future scrapes: Extending the scraped window past 4+ weeks would strengthen the within-scraped-window stability check. At 26 days we have enough for the JOLTS confound check but not enough for a clean trend-vs-noise decomposition within the scraped window.

docs.title: T20 — Seniority boundary clarity
docs.text: Agent: L (Wave 3) Date: 2026-04-15 Inputs: - exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet (filtered to text_source='llm', n=34,258) - exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_tech_matrix.parquet (AI and tech_count features) - exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet (domain stratification) - exploration/artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json (T21-produced mgmt density regex) Scripts: exploration/scripts/T20_boundary_classifier.py

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
The entry↔mid-senior boundary SHARPENED between 2024 and 2026 (AUC 0.836 → 0.876, +0.040), and so did the entry↔associate (0.626 → 0.719, +0.093) and associate↔mid-senior (0.691 → 0.791, +0.100) boundaries. The ONLY boundary that BLURRED is mid-senior↔director (0.677 → 0.616, −0.061). The Gate 2 "junior levels are converging" framing remains wrong in both direction and magnitude: entry-vs-mid is cleaner in 2026 than it was in 2024 on a structured 8-feature representation. Where things got blurrier is at the top of the ladder, among director postings — a quieter but concrete senior-tier finding.

What's driving the sharpening of entry→mid-senior? yoe_feat is the dominant separator in both periods (+2.68 → +2.84), which is unsurprising. But the feature composition changed: scope_density coefficient doubled (+0.08 → +0.16), mgmt_density dropped out of the top 5, and education_level flipped from a weak positive to a strong negative (−0.32) — meaning 2026 mid-senior roles list LESS formal education than 2026 entry roles relative to 2024. Entry postings are now often listing required degrees that mid-senior postings skip. This is a real shift in what the boundary "runs on."

What's driving the blurring of mid-senior→director? Director (n=99 in 2024, 115 in 2026) is small and the feature composition flipped: tech_count coefficient went from −0.48 in 2024 to +0.35 in 2026 — in 2024 director postings had FEWER listed technologies than mid-senior (director = pure management), in 2026 director postings have MORE listed technologies than mid-senior (director = hands-on senior IC+architect). This is the single largest feature-importance swing across any boundary.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
Per-posting feature vector (8 features): - yoe_feat: yoe_extracted with period-specific median imputation - yoe_missing: indicator for imputed yoe - tech_count: sum of 119 boolean tech columns from shared tech matrix (methodology tech tags — agile/scrum/tdd/kanban — excluded) - ai_mention: binary, any mention of 27 AI tech columns (llm, langchain, copilot, etc.) - scope_density: end-to-end + cross-functional count per 1K chars (per Gate 2 correction 3, bare ownership excluded) - mgmt_density: T21-validated people-mgmt pattern per 1K chars (mentor, manage_team, manage_people, lead_team, direct_reports, headcount, team_building, etc. — all 13 patterns ≥80% precision) - log_len: log1p(description length in chars) - education_level: ordinal 0/1/2/3 for {none, BS, MS, PhD}

Classifier: LogisticRegression(penalty='l2', C=1.0), StandardScaler applied per-fold. 5-fold stratified CV, mean AUC reported. Features standardized to unit variance for interpretable coefficients.

Per-period fit: classifier is trained SEPARATELY on 2024 rows and 2026 rows (combined within period across sources). Boundaries use seniority_final pairs.

YOE-proxy sensitivity: re-map seniority via yoe_extracted buckets {≤2: entry, 3–6: mid-senior, 7–20: director}. This gives a label-independent version of the boundary test; AUC is mechanically 1.0 because yoe_feat is the input feature, but the non-yoe feature coefficients are interpretable as "how much the rest of the feature vector discriminates given yoe."

Sensitivities applied: (a) aggregator exclusion, (c) seniority operationalization via YOE proxy, (g) exclude swe_classification_tier='title_lookup_llm'.

docs.title: AUC by period × boundary (primary, seniority_final)
docs.text:
Boundary n_2024 (a/b) n_2026 (a/b) AUC 2024 AUC 2026 Δ Verdict entry ↔ associate 376 / 39 610 / 52 0.626 ± 0.088 0.719 ± 0.095 +0.093 Sharpened (noisy — associate thin) entry ↔ mid-senior 376 / 12,768 610 / 6,089 0.836 ± 0.010 0.876 ± 0.010 +0.040 Sharpened associate ↔ mid-senior 39 / 12,768 52 / 6,089 0.691 ± 0.149 0.791 ± 0.043 +0.100 Sharpened (noisy — associate thin) mid-senior ↔ director 12,768 / 99 6,089 / 115 0.677 ± 0.057 0.616 ± 0.040 −0.061 Blurred

Three of four boundaries sharpened; only mid-senior↔director blurred. CIs on entry↔associate and associate↔mid-senior are wide because associate has n=39 / 52 — treat those point estimates as directional. The entry↔mid-senior estimate (the most well-powered cell) is cleanly above the 2024 number at ±0.01 noise. The mid-senior↔director shift is within 1–2× the fold std, so call it "modest blur" rather than "collapse."

docs.title: Feature importance shifts (entry → mid-senior, primary)
docs.text:
Top 5 standardized coefficients (positive = toward mid-senior):

Feature 2024 coef 2026 coef Δ Interpretation yoe_feat +2.68 +2.84 +0.16 Slightly stronger separator (YOE is #1 both years) yoe_missing −1.10 −1.91 −0.81 Much stronger negative: entry rows are now MORE likely to have missing YOE than in 2024 (signal-sharper) tech_count +0.20 (drops from top 5) — Tech count no longer in top 5 scope_density +0.08 +0.16 +0.08 Scope density gained as a separator (2× importance) mgmt_density +0.06 (drops from top 5) — Mgmt density no longer in top 5 — entry postings now match mid-senior mgmt-density as closely as tech count education_level (not in top 5) −0.32 — Newly top-5 and NEGATIVE: 2026 mid-senior has LESS formal education than 2026 entry log_len (not in top 5) +0.16 — Length became a weak positive separator

Interpretation. The 2026 entry↔mid-senior boundary runs primarily on YOE + yoe-missingness + scope language + length + (inverse) education. The 2024 boundary ran on YOE + tech count. The shift makes the boundary sharper because the 2026 feature set captures more independent information about seniority. The inverse education coefficient is notable: 2026 mid-senior postings are LESS likely to require a degree than 2026 entry postings. Likely mechanism: 2026 entry postings are new grad / university-recruit specialist postings (which require a degree), while 2026 mid-senior postings are template-drafted roles that drop the degree line.

docs.title: Feature importance shifts (mid-senior → director, primary)
docs.text:
Feature 2024 coef 2026 coef Δ Interpretation tech_count −0.48 +0.35 +0.83 Sign flipped. In 2024, director postings mentioned fewer techs than mid-senior. In 2026, directors mention MORE techs. yoe_feat +0.36 +0.24 −0.12 YOE weakened as separator log_len +0.27 (drops) — Length not top 5 in 2026 scope_density −0.13 −0.13 0 Stable — director postings are LESS scope-dense than mid-senior both periods education_level (not top 5) +0.25 — Director newly differentiates on education in 2026 yoe_missing (not top 5) +0.22 — Director rows slightly more likely to be yoe-missing in 2026 ai_mention +0.11 (drops) — —

Interpretation. The 2024 director archetype was "high-YOE, long-text, few-tech, low-scope" — a pure people-management boss. The 2026 director archetype adds technology breadth and formal education back in. The result is that directors and mid-senior roles look more similar on the "wide tech stack" dimension, which is what's blurring the boundary. The T21 tech-orchestration rise among directors (people_density 0.228 → 0.181; orch_density 0.118 → 0.302, +156%; see T21) corroborates: directors are being recast as hands-on technical orchestrators rather than pure people managers, and they drift closer to senior ICs in feature space.

docs.title: YOE-proxy sensitivity
docs.text: Under YOE buckets (entry ≤ 2, mid-senior 3–6, director 7–20), AUC is mechanically 1.0 on every boundary because yoe_feat is itself the input. The non-yoe coefficients are tiny (<0.1), meaning after YOE is controlled, the other 7 features together carry very little boundary-relevant signal. This is a useful sanity check: the primary classifier's good AUC on seniority_final is NOT being inflated by feature leakage — it comes from the combination of YOE with non-YOE features, and the non-YOE features matter when YOE is weak/missing.

docs.title: Sensitivity (a) — aggregator exclusion
docs.text: Directionally identical. Entry↔mid-senior AUC 0.842 → 0.880 (vs primary 0.836 → 0.876). Mid-senior↔director 0.668 → 0.614 (vs primary 0.677 → 0.616). Aggregator exclusion slightly sharpens the entry boundary and slightly deepens the director blur; both effects are small and do not change any verdict.

docs.title: Sensitivity (g) — exclude title_lookup_llm tier
docs.text: Nearly identical to primary. The title-lookup-LLM tier is a small share of entry rows (349 → 585 excluding). Entry↔mid-senior AUC 0.840 → 0.876, mid-senior↔director 0.662 → 0.630. All four verdicts hold.

docs.title: Missing middle
docs.text:
Distance between associate centroid and entry/mid-senior centroids (z-scored features, Euclidean):

Period dist(assoc, entry) dist(assoc, mid-senior) entry_share_of_distance 2024 0.644 0.886 0.421 (associate is 42% of the way from entry to mid-senior, closer to entry) 2026 1.055 1.046 0.502 (associate is almost exactly between entry and mid-senior)

Associate moved from "slightly closer to entry" to "centered between entry and mid-senior." Associate is not disappearing; if anything it's becoming a better-defined middle. But n=39/52 associate rows in each period means this is suggestive, not conclusive.

docs.title: Domain-stratified boundary analysis
docs.text:
For each top-6 archetype, only the entry↔mid-senior boundary has enough cells to compute. All 6 archetypes show sharpening:

Archetype AUC 2024 AUC 2026 Δ LLM / GenAI / ML engineering 0.737 0.890 +0.153 (largest sharpening) JS frontend (React/TypeScript) 0.809 0.833 +0.024 Java enterprise 0.829 0.878 +0.049 Data engineering 0.846 0.872 +0.026 DevOps / SRE / platform 0.772 0.889 +0.117 (2nd largest) Embedded / firmware 0.808 0.876 +0.068

Every large archetype shows sharpened entry↔mid-senior boundaries in 2026. Largest sharpening is in LLM/GenAI (+0.153) and DevOps (+0.117) — both AI-heavy stacks. This is consistent with the structural AI-restructuring story: new tech communities (LLM/GenAI) also draw cleaner seniority lines because the entry-level language and mid-senior language in those domains differ more. JS frontend (+0.024) is the smallest gain, consistent with it being the most template-drafted stack where 2024 and 2026 look most alike.

Mid-senior → director AUC is only computable in 2026 LLM/GenAI (0.573), DevOps (0.561), Java (0.691). All three are near 0.5 or well below any other boundary. The senior-tier blur is a genuine within-domain pattern, not a between-domain artifact.

docs.title: Feature heatmap (selected cells, z-scored)
docs.text:
Raw per-cell means (not z-scored for readability):

period seniority yoe tech_count ai scope_density mgmt_density log_len education 2024 entry 3.85 4.01 0.06 0.27 0.053 6.12 0.60 2024 mid-senior 5.87 4.72 0.04 0.34 0.079 6.31 0.68 2024 director 7.72 2.93 0.05 0.24 0.052 6.48 0.73 2026 entry 4.19 4.97 0.28 0.52 0.105 6.27 0.70 2026 mid-senior 5.59 6.12 0.27 0.71 0.129 6.50 0.58 2026 director 6.17 8.03 0.30 0.58 0.086 6.60 0.74

Observations: - Director tech_count: 2.93 → 8.03 (+173%). Biggest per-cell shift in the table. Directors went from fewest-tech to most-tech. - Entry ai_mention: 0.06 → 0.28 (+380%). Entry postings have caught up to the AI-mention rate of mid-senior (0.27) postings. - Mid-senior mgmt_density: 0.079 → 0.129 (+63%). Mid-senior people-management language rose sharply on validated patterns. - Entry mgmt_density: 0.053 → 0.105 (+98%). Entry people-management language also rose — the rise is bigger at entry than at mid-senior in relative terms, but smaller in absolute terms. - Mid-senior education: 0.68 → 0.58 (−15%). Mid-senior requires less formal education in 2026, consistent with the flipped coefficient above. - Director scope_density: 0.24 → 0.58 (+142%). Director scope language rose sharply.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. The senior-tier (mid-senior→director) is the ONLY boundary that blurred. Gate 2 was looking for blurring at the junior end. T20 finds the blurring at the top of the ladder, driven by directors becoming MORE tech-dense. Directors look more like senior ICs in 2026 than they did in 2024 — consistent with "staff-engineer ladder" models where technical leadership is a parallel track to people management.

  2. Tech count coefficient flipped sign at the director boundary (−0.48 → +0.35). This is an unusually clean signal of role redefinition: the same feature went from identifying directors to identifying mid-senior, or equivalently, directors picked up +5.1 average techs while mid-senior only picked up +1.4.

  3. Education coefficient is NEGATIVE at the 2026 entry↔mid-senior boundary. In 2026, mid-senior postings list formal education requirements LESS often than entry postings, which has no 2024 analogue. The most likely explanation: new-grad entry postings are still template-drafted with "BS required" boilerplate, while 2026 mid-senior postings (probably LLM-drafted) drop this line because it's obvious. This is a secondary finding but it's a cleaner "template homogenization" signal than the T15 homogenization result.

  4. LLM/GenAI and DevOps sharpened most. The largest AUC gains are concentrated in AI-heavy archetypes. The interpretation consistent with Gate 2's Alt C narrative: the 2026 SWE corpus is structurally reorganizing around AI-tool specialties, and seniority language within those specialties is becoming more differentiated because the tech stack has more internal depth (more signals per posting to infer level from).

  5. Scope density became MORE important for the entry↔mid-senior boundary in 2026 — its coefficient doubled. The corrected scope pattern (end-to-end + cross-functional only) still carries significant discriminative weight, despite Gate 2 correction 3 warning that the bare ownership pattern was contaminated. The corrected pattern is sharper than the uncorrected one for this purpose.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. Associate cells are thin (n=39 in 2024, n=52 in 2026). The two boundaries involving associate (entry↔assoc, assoc↔mid) are point-direction plausible but wide-CI. I report them as "sharpened" under the primary fit but do not interpret their magnitudes.

  2. Director cells are similarly thin (n=99, 115). The mid-senior↔director blur is the single most interesting finding but it runs on n≈100 on one side. A 50-row bootstrap CI check would be valuable in Wave 4.

  3. YOE feature is imputed (≈34% of rows missing YOE). I add yoe_missing as a separate feature to avoid confounding imputation with the YOE signal; the per-period imputation used period-specific median. Running without imputation (dropping null yoe rows) is a sensitivity I did not run; it would collapse the sample by ~½ and is deferred.

  4. Feature set is deliberately narrow (8 features). A richer feature set (including text embeddings or TF-IDF) would likely push AUC higher across the board but would also confound "what the boundary runs on" with "text content." The 8-feature choice is deliberate for interpretability; text-based boundaries are the natural extension in Wave 4.

  5. Validated mgmt_density pattern includes 13 sub-patterns across mentor/manage_team/lead_team/direct_reports/performance_review/etc. Full bundle is in exploration/artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json. Every component passed ≥80% precision on the 50-row stratified sample; the bundle is the reusable "clean management language" definition going forward.

docs.title: Sensitivity summary
docs.text: Dimension Applied Result (a) aggregator exclusion yes All 4 verdicts hold; magnitudes within 0.01 AUC of primary (c) seniority operationalization (YOE proxy) yes YOE-based buckets give AUC≈1.0 (mechanical); non-YOE coefficients small, confirming the primary AUC is not purely YOE-driven (g) SWE tier (exclude title_lookup_llm) yes All 4 verdicts hold; within 0.01 AUC of primary

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. T21 must treat the mid-senior→director blur as the senior-redefinition mechanism. Directors gaining tech_count and scope_density is evidence that the director role is being recast as "high-senior IC + orchestrator" rather than pure people-manager. T21's own AI × senior interaction supports this (directors with AI mention have orch_density 0.60+ vs without-AI 0.17).

  2. Agent M (T22/T23) should use the T20 feature vector as a control set for ghost-posting detection. The 8-feature vector cleanly separates levels in 2026; ghost postings should look unusually "blurred" on this vector if they're templated across levels.

  3. Agent N (SYNTHESIS) should cite the T20 verdict as confirmation that the "blurring" hypothesis is dead at the junior end and alive only at the director end — a narrower, more interesting version of the original RQ1 framing. The feature-importance shift (especially the tech_count sign flip at director) is the cleanest single-feature story in the wave.

  4. Preprocessing owner should consider adding a sensitivity tag for seniority_final_source — T21 found that 100% of title_keyword-sourced senior/staff rows come from Stage 5 rule matching. If the rule-set changed between pipeline runs, the title compression result would shift; it would be worth pinning a rule-version to the artifact.
