docs.title: T21 — Senior role evolution deep dive
docs.text: Agent: L (Wave 3) Date: 2026-04-15 Inputs: - data/unified.parquet (filtered to SWE LinkedIn labeled-llm-text rows; description_core_llm used directly) - exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet (for company_name_canonical join) - exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_tech_matrix.parquet (for ai_mention) - exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet Scripts: exploration/scripts/T21_validate_patterns.py, exploration/scripts/T21_senior_analysis.py Artifact: exploration/artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json (saved for T20 and Agent M)

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
Senior SWE roles are redefining toward technical orchestration, not away from people management, and NOT toward strategic scope. On validated ≥80%-precision patterns only:

Profile Mid-senior 2024 Mid-senior 2026 Δ Director 2024 Director 2026 Δ People management (per 1K) 0.186 0.232 (+25%) ↑ 0.228 0.181 (−21%) ↓ Technical orchestration 0.168 0.332 (+98%) ↑↑ 0.118 0.302 (+156%) ↑↑↑ Strategic scope 0.045 0.053 (+17%) ~flat 0.035 0.076 (+117%) ↑

The rebuilt pattern set overturns T11's "manage fell −47%" finding. On the validated narrow set — which requires object-noun-phrase after manage (manage_team, manage_people) and drops hire/hiring and bare manage — people-management density ROSE +25% at mid-senior. T11's −47% was a pattern artifact caused by the standalone-noun exclusion and HR boilerplate filtering. The mentor +55% claim survives as a clean component. The broader point: strict people-management language did not fall; it rose, but the rise is dominated by mentoring/team-leading rather than headcount/direct-reports.

The dominant shift, though, is technical orchestration (+98% at mid-senior, +156% at director). This is the cleanest "new senior archetype" signal: 2026 senior postings describe architecture review, system design, code review, agent workflows, prompt engineering, evals/guardrails, technical vision, and "setting engineering standards" at twice the 2024 density.

Strategic scope is the smallest shift and it is NOT a field-wide finding. Bare stakeholder, roadmap, prioritization, and cross-functional all FAILED the ≥80% precision check (they are used as soft-skill/collaboration filler, not as strategic-ownership markers). The narrow strategic patterns that passed (business_impact, product_strategy, resource_allocation, budget_ownership, drive_revenue, executive_partner, strategic_initiative, influence_direction, business_requirements) show ~flat density at mid-senior but a real rise at director (+117%). Strategic language is concentrated in the director band and that's where it moved.

Senior title compression verdict. "Senior" in titles dropped 41.7% → 28.9% (−12.8 pp). "Staff" rose only 2.7% → 5.8% (+3.1 pp). Staff DOES NOT explain the senior drop. Within the 150-company overlap panel (n≥10 in both periods), mean senior_delta is −9.1 pp per company; staff_delta is +1.6 pp. The senior drop is real and not a between-company composition artifact. The most likely mechanism: employer template rewriting under LLM-drafting tools that remove the "Senior" prefix from generic SWE postings — all 100% of "senior"-titled rows are Stage 5 keyword-matched (seniority_final_source='title_keyword') and 99.5% of them still classify as seniority_final='mid-senior' in both periods, so this is not a classifier drift. It is a literal vocabulary shift in the title field.

Cross-seniority management comparison. Mid-senior people-management density rose (0.186 → 0.232, +25%), but entry-level people-management density FELL (0.064 → 0.056, −13%), and director fell (0.228 → 0.181, −21%). This is NOT a field-wide template shift — it is a mid-senior-specific rise. If management language were being boiler-plated into every posting by LLM-drafting tools, entry would have risen with mid-senior. Instead, mid-senior specifically is being redefined as the mentoring/team-leading rung.

docs.title: Pattern validation (50-row stratified precision check)
docs.text:
Source text: description_core_llm (raw LLM-cleaned prose, NOT the stopword-stripped description_cleaned from the shared artifact, because validation requires natural prose for context checks). Filter: is_swe = true AND source_platform='linkedin' AND is_english=true AND date_flag='ok' AND llm_extraction_coverage='labeled' AND seniority_final IN ('mid-senior','director'). n=19,018 senior LLM-text rows.

Per pattern: sample up to 25 matches per period (25/25 balanced), compute rule-based acceptance using pattern-specific validators. Precision = accepted / sampled. Threshold: ≥0.80. Validators are conservative — when in doubt, reject — so they produce a lower bound on true precision. The validator code is in T21_validate_patterns.py and each validator's logic is documented inline.

44 patterns tested. 36 passed, 8 dropped.

docs.title: Density computation
docs.text: After validation, each profile (people_mgmt, tech_orch, strategic) is encoded as a disjunction of its kept patterns. Per posting: count matches, divide by max(desc_len_chars, 200) / 1000. The 200-char floor prevents single-word short descriptions from inflating density.

docs.title: Senior sub-archetype clustering
docs.text: k-means (k=4) on z-scored (people_density, orch_density, strat_density). Cluster labels assigned by which raw-density center is largest (or "generic (low all)" if total density < 0.3).

docs.title: Sensitivity
docs.text: • (a) aggregator exclusion — applied to the density table.

docs.title: People management (narrow) — 13 kept, 3 dropped
docs.text:
Pattern n_2024 n_2026 Precision Verdict mentor 3454 2426 1.00 kept (Gate 2 baseline surviving) direct_reports 60 15 1.00 kept performance_review 15 8 1.00 kept one_on_one 17 18 1.00 kept headcount 1 2 1.00 kept team_building 79 52 1.00 kept grow_team 49 46 1.00 kept develop_talent 4 7 1.00 kept manage_team (manage {a/the/your/our team, team of, engineering team}) 59 19 1.00 kept — narrow version of T11's failed manage manage_people (manage {people, direct reports, engineers, the engineering}) 11 4 1.00 kept lead_team (lead {a/the/your/our team, engineering, group}) 483 120 1.00 kept people_management_phrase 250 73 1.00 kept line_manage 13 6 1.00 kept coach 650 352 0.60 dropped (life/wellness/career coaching + player-coach noise) hiring_decisions 14 16 0.33 dropped career_development 76 51 0.10 dropped (mostly "career development opportunities" boilerplate)

Note: direct_reports, performance_review, one_on_one, and headcount all have small n but pass 100% precision — they are clean but rare. The dominant kept components by count are mentor (5,880), lead_team (603), people_management_phrase (323), manage_team (78), team_building (131).

docs.title: Technical orchestration — 14 kept, 1 dropped
docs.text:
Pattern n_2024 n_2026 Precision Verdict architecture_review 55 72 1.00 kept code_review 1517 1245 1.00 kept system_design 782 676 1.00 kept technical_direction 288 450 1.00 kept technical_leadership 867 825 1.00 kept tech_lead 456 291 1.00 kept agent_workflow 6 259 1.00 kept (the largest new-in-2026 pattern) workflow_automation 19 55 1.00 kept quality_gate 16 39 1.00 kept prompt_engineering 26 212 1.00 kept evals (evals / evaluation framework / eval harness) 1 110 1.00 kept design_review 560 425 1.00 kept technical_vision 54 124 1.00 kept set_technical_standards 13 61 1.00 kept guardrails 43 176 0.62 dropped (too often refers to security/compliance guardrails not AI)

agent_workflow, prompt_engineering, and evals all showed >10× growth in absolute counts — these are the T14 AI-explosion patterns landing in senior language specifically.

docs.title: Strategic scope — 9 kept, 4 dropped
docs.text:
Pattern n_2024 n_2026 Precision Verdict business_impact 334 437 1.00 kept product_strategy 75 44 1.00 kept resource_allocation 25 29 1.00 kept budget_ownership (own/manage/plan the budget) 12 2 1.00 kept drive_revenue 9 14 1.00 kept executive_partner 6 20 1.00 kept business_requirements 820 297 1.00 kept strategic_initiative 27 31 1.00 kept influence_direction 17 28 1.00 kept roadmap 704 658 0.70 dropped (often "roadmap" as feature/ideation, not strategic ownership) cross_functional_alignment 2029 1695 0.70 dropped (often used as soft-skill adjective, not strategic-scope noun) stakeholder 2190 1653 0.50 dropped (confirms Gate 2 risk — mostly communication-filler "work with stakeholders") prioritization 878 392 0.12 dropped (mostly "strong prioritization skills" boilerplate)

The three highest-n strategic candidates (stakeholder, prioritization, cross-functional) all failed precision. Any strategic-language finding that depends on them is a measurement artifact. After dropping them, the strategic profile is driven mostly by business_impact, business_requirements, and strategic_initiative. This is narrower than Gate 1 / Gate 2 assumed.

Full precision table: exploration/tables/T21/validated_patterns.csv. Validated regex bundle: exploration/artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json.

docs.title: Density by profile × period × seniority (primary)
docs.text:
seniority period n people_density orch_density strat_density people_any orch_any strat_any mid-senior 2024 12,745 0.186 0.168 0.045 30.2% 28.6% 9.6% mid-senior 2026 6,062 0.232 0.332 0.053 41.0% 48.9% 13.2% director 2024 99 0.228 0.118 0.035 37.4% 26.3% 8.1% director 2026 112 0.181 0.302 0.076 42.9% 58.0% 23.2%

Binary (share with at least one match) confirms direction. Mid-senior any-people-mgmt rose 30.2 → 41.0 pp (+10.8 pp); any-tech-orch rose 28.6 → 48.9 pp (+20.3 pp). Tech-orchestration is the dominant shift at both seniority levels.

docs.title: Senior sub-archetype clustering
docs.text:
Four k-means clusters on z-scored densities, labeled by dominant raw-density component:

cluster people orch strat 2024 share 2026 share Δ share generic (low all) 0.05 0.08 0.01 70.2% 59.8% −10.4 pp tech-lead 0.20 1.05 0.02 7.8% 16.9% +9.1 pp people-manager 0.86 0.25 0.01 14.5% 14.7% flat strategist 0.24 0.23 0.52 7.5% 8.6% +1.1 pp

The "tech-lead" sub-archetype more than doubled (7.8% → 16.9%, +9.1 pp), at the expense of the "generic" bucket (70.2% → 59.8%, −10.4 pp). The people-manager sub-archetype is nearly unchanged. The strategist sub-archetype grew slightly. The senior redefinition is a tech-lead specialization story: postings that used to be bucket-generic are being written with more system design / code review / agent workflow / tech vision language, shifting into the tech-lead cluster.

docs.title: AI × senior interaction
docs.text:
period ai_mention n people orch strat 2024 no_ai 12,269 0.188 0.168 0.045 2024 ai 575 0.163 0.155 0.050 2026 no_ai 4,482 0.231 0.274 0.049 2026 ai 1,692 0.231 0.482 0.064

In 2024, AI-mentioning senior postings were indistinguishable from non-AI senior postings on every profile. In 2026, AI-mentioning senior postings have tech_orch density +0.21 over non-AI (0.482 vs 0.274, 76% higher). People-management density is identical whether or not the posting mentions AI. This is the single sharpest domain-specific signal in T21: the technical orchestration rise is specifically concentrated in AI-mentioning postings. The AI senior is the "tech lead / orchestrator" senior, not the "people manager" senior.

docs.title: Director-specific deep dive
docs.text:
2024 director 2024 mid-senior 2026 director 2026 mid-senior n 99 12,745 112 6,062 people_density 0.228 0.186 0.181 0.232 orch_density 0.118 0.168 0.302 0.332 strat_density 0.035 0.045 0.076 0.053

In 2024, director was {higher people, lower orch, lower strat} than mid-senior — the classic "people boss" director. In 2026, director is {lower people, ~equal orch, higher strat} than mid-senior — the director profile flipped. Directors went from "more mgmt than mid-senior" to "more strategic than mid-senior." Orchestration matched mid-senior (both ≈0.30). The director role is narrowing toward strategic + orchestration and dropping people management. T20's mid-senior→director boundary BLUR is the structural reflection of this — directors became so close to mid-senior on orchestration that the two levels are less discriminable on a structured feature vector.

docs.title: Cross-seniority management comparison
docs.text:
seniority period n people orch strat people_any share entry 2024 376 0.064 0.105 0.050 10.9% entry 2026 607 0.056 0.159 0.037 9.9% mid-senior 2024 12,745 0.186 0.168 0.045 30.2% mid-senior 2026 6,062 0.232 0.332 0.053 41.0% director 2024 99 0.228 0.118 0.035 37.4% director 2026 112 0.181 0.302 0.076 42.9%

The people-management rise is ONLY at mid-senior. Entry fell −13% (0.064 → 0.056), director fell −21% (0.228 → 0.181), mid-senior rose +25% (0.186 → 0.232). This is not a field-wide template shift and not downward migration; it is a specific rewiring of the mid-senior posting to emphasize mentoring/team-leading. If recruiter LLM-drafting tools were inserting "mentor the team" into every posting, entry would have risen with mid-senior; instead entry fell.

Note: people_any (share with ≥1 mention) shows entry 10.9% → 9.9% (−1.0 pp), mid-senior 30.2% → 41.0% (+10.8 pp), director 37.4% → 42.9% (+5.5 pp). Binary rose at mid-senior and director but fell at entry, while density fell at director (because 2026 directors have longer descriptions diluting the mentions). The "any mention" signal agrees with the density signal on direction at mid-senior and entry; director is the only cell where density and binary disagree.

docs.title: Senior title compression deep dive
docs.text:
Raw title markers across ALL SWE rows (n=22,820 in 2024, 40,881 in 2026):

marker 2024 share 2026 share Δ pp senior 41.7% 28.9% −12.8 staff 2.7% 5.8% +3.1 principal 5.2% 3.7% −1.6 lead 9.3% 6.1% −3.2

Within-company (150 companies with n≥10 in both periods): - Mean senior_delta = −9.1 pp (senior share fell at the average company too) - Mean staff_delta = +1.6 pp - 90/150 companies had senior share drop - 34/150 companies had staff share rise - Only ~22% of the senior drop is even potentially absorbed by staff rises

Mapping titles to seniority_final: - 100% of senior-titled rows and staff-titled rows are from seniority_final_source='title_keyword' (Stage 5 rule-match). No LLM drift. - 99.5% of senior-titled rows classify as seniority_final='mid-senior' in BOTH periods. - 99.6% of staff-titled rows classify as seniority_final='mid-senior' in both periods. - Director rows are a tiny fraction (33/2024, 65/2026) of senior-titled. - Classifier is stable; vocabulary is what changed.

The drop is real (both aggregate and within-company), is not explained by staff rising (only 22% absorbed), is not a classifier drift, and is not a seniority-band redefinition inside seniority_final. Best-supported mechanism: 2026 posters are dropping the "Senior" prefix from plain-SWE postings. Possible reasons: (a) Staff ladder adoption — but staff only gained ~1.6 pp within-company on average, insufficient. (b) LinkedIn title-normalization drift — the scraped source uses the raw LinkedIn field; could be that LinkedIn's own title display changed formatting. (c) Employer LLM-drafting — titles like "Senior Software Engineer" are being rewritten to "Software Engineer" or to AI-variant titles like "AI Engineer", "Software Engineer [multiple positions available]" (the #1 new 2026 title per T10).

Most-supported explanation: (c). T10 documented that the #1 new title in 2026 is "software engineer [multiple positions available]" (n=88) — a template-sounding compound title — and 13 of the top 20 new titles are AI-compound variants. This is consistent with a vocabulary-level rewriting that bypasses the "Senior" prefix. The within-company magnitude (−9.1 pp) is larger than can be explained by the staff rise (+1.6 pp), so staff ladder adoption is NOT the primary driver.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. The management finding is RESURRECTED but narrower. T11 said manage −47% and got overturned by V1. I said that finding was an artifact of pattern exclusion rules and rebuilt with object-noun-phrase requirements. The rebuilt patterns show people management rose +25% at mid-senior on valid narrow patterns. This is a notable correction of the Gate 2 narrative: "general management rose" was contradicted; "narrow people management rose at mid-senior" is the corrected surviving claim.

  2. Technical orchestration nearly doubled at mid-senior and 2.5×'d at director. Tech orchestration (+98% mid-senior, +156% director) is by far the largest profile shift. The composition — code_review, system_design, agent_workflow, prompt_engineering, evals, technical_vision — is unmistakably the "AI-era tech lead" vocabulary.

  3. Strategic scope is NOT rising at mid-senior. This is the biggest single null I found. Gate 1 / T11 assumed strategic scope was rising ("stakeholder", "roadmap", "prioritization", "cross-functional"); but the high-n candidates all FAILED precision validation, and the surviving narrow strategic pattern is essentially flat at mid-senior. Strategic scope rose only at director (+117%) where it was already the rarest profile. The "senior archetype shift" is mostly orchestration, not strategic scope.

  4. The tech-lead sub-archetype more than doubled (7.8% → 16.9%) while the people-manager sub-archetype was flat (14.5% → 14.7%). If the question is "which senior sub-type grew?", the answer is unambiguously tech-lead / orchestrator, not people-manager.

  5. Cross-seniority: people management FELL at entry and director but rose at mid-senior. This non-monotonic pattern rules out "field-wide template shift" as the mechanism; it specifically points to mid-senior redefinition. The mechanism candidates narrow to:

  6. LLM-drafted senior postings that insert mentoring language specifically for the senior level (not templated across levels), or

  7. real market shift where senior positions are being hired to mentor/team-lead in ways they weren't in 2024.

  8. AI × senior interaction is entirely in the orchestration profile. AI-mentioning senior postings have the same people-management density as non-AI senior postings, but their tech_orch density is 0.482 vs 0.274 — nearly double. The orchestration rise is concentrated among AI-mentioning postings. This is the cleanest single finding that ties T14's AI-explosion to T21's senior redefinition.

  9. Director people-management density FELL. Directors are LESS about people management in 2026 than in 2024. That's a reversal of the typical "higher = more management" gradient and is the single largest deviation from the 2024 baseline among senior levels. It pairs with T20's mid-senior→director boundary blur — directors became less of a distinct "people-manager" archetype.

  10. The senior-title drop is 5.7× larger than the staff-title rise. Staff is not "the new senior" at company level. The senior compression is about something else — probably title-field rewriting under LLM drafting tools, which also produces the T15 template homogenization finding.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. Validators are rule-based and conservative. The "dropped" patterns (stakeholder, prioritization, roadmap, cross-functional) might be only weakly boilerplate-contaminated and a human coder would save some borderline cases. My rule-based validator rejected them and I elected to treat that as a precision floor. Rerunning with human-coded samples is a Wave 4 V2 task.

  2. Director cells are small (99 in 2024, 112 in 2026). Every director-specific finding is directionally plausible but should not be cited with precision. The director-orchestration rise is large enough (+156%) that it would survive a much larger sample, but the director people-mgmt fall (−21%) could noise out.

  3. Density uses description_core_llm natural text, NOT the stopword-stripped description_cleaned from the shared artifact. This is deliberate for validation (needed for context checks). For comparability with T11 which also used natural text proxies, the numbers should be roughly comparable; for comparability with T20's mgmt_density (which uses description_cleaned from the shared artifact, stopwords removed) there is a constant-factor difference. T20's mgmt_density scale is different but T20's verdicts (sign of coefficients, direction of AUC change) are unaffected.

  4. Entry sample (376 / 607) is thin. The "entry people management fell −13%" claim has small-n error bars. Direction is likely correct but magnitude is uncertain. The flat-to-falling entry result is consistent with T11's earlier entry-level findings, so I do not think this is a fluke.

  5. Within-company overlap panel is 150 companies, no top-cap applied. Per T06, the top-50 2024 employers account for ~40% of 2024 LinkedIn SWE postings; within-company comparisons inherit this concentration. A cap-50 per company sensitivity would be the natural follow-up but was not run.

  6. The validated regex bundle is saved at exploration/artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json with the full per-pattern precision table and kept/dropped lists. Agent M (T22/T23) should load this bundle rather than re-deriving patterns.

docs.title: Sensitivity summary
docs.text: Dimension Applied Result (a) aggregator exclusion yes Mid-senior: people_density 0.186→0.232 (primary) vs 0.161→0.236 (no agg). Orch_density 0.168→0.332 (primary) vs 0.178→0.337 (no agg). Direction and magnitude hold; aggregators are not driving the senior finding. All others n/a or deferred No other sensitivities materially relevant (density is a per-posting metric; source restriction is implicit via period split; SWE tier is already the artifact filter)

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. Agent M (T22/T23) must load exploration/artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json. The bundle's profiles.people_mgmt.regex, profiles.tech_orch.regex, profiles.strategic.regex are the only patterns I would recommend using for senior-language metrics going forward. In particular, T22's scope_density rebuild should use the strategic profile (which already excludes stakeholder, prioritization, roadmap, cross_functional).

  2. Agent N (SYNTHESIS / Gate 3) should revise Gate 2's senior-archetype story:

  3. OVERTURN: "mentor +55%, manage −47%" → replace with "mentor rose and narrow people-management rose ~25% at mid-senior."
  4. NEW HEADLINE: Technical orchestration language nearly doubled at mid-senior and 2.5×'d at director. This is the dominant senior-redefinition signal and is concentrated in AI-mentioning postings.
  5. NARROW: Strategic scope is NOT a mid-senior story; it is a narrow director-tier shift.
  6. ADD: The "staff is the new senior" hypothesis is falsified at the within-company level. The senior-title drop is a vocabulary-level template shift, not a band redefinition.

  7. CONFIRM: T20's mid-senior→director boundary blur is the structural dual of T21's director people→orch shift. Directors are becoming "high-senior tech-lead + strategist" and less "people boss."

  8. Agent O (T29 LLM-authorship) should use the mid-senior-specific nature of the management rise as a test: if LLM-drafting tools are template-inserting mentoring language, it should appear at all seniority levels, not just mid-senior. Since T21 finds mid-senior is the only level where people-mgmt rose, either (a) the LLM templates are seniority-aware (plausible) or (b) the rise is a real hiring-market shift in what mid-senior roles are asked to do. T29 should probe this directly on LLM-authorship scores.

  9. Preprocessing owner should consider tracking seniority_final_source distribution over time. The T21 finding that 100% of senior-titled rows are Stage 5 rule-matched is a nice audit — but if the Stage 5 rule changes, the title compression claim could shift. Pinning the rule version to the artifact would be ideal.

  • exploration/figures/T21/cross_seniority_mgmt.png

docs.title: T22. Ghost & aspirational requirements forensics
docs.text: Agent M (Wave 3). Run: 2026-04-15. Input: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet (for row selection) joined with description_core_llm from data/unified.parquet (for section headers, aspiration cues, firm cues — the shared swe_cleaned_text.parquet text is tokenized/stopword-stripped and cannot carry section headers). Script: exploration/scripts/T22_ghost_forensics.py. Sample frame: text_source='llm' (n=34,161 SWE LinkedIn rows with llm_extraction_coverage='labeled').

docs.title: Headline findings
docs.text:
1. Scope pattern validation: 9 of 11 patterns pass the 80% precision threshold. ownership_bare (60%) and vision (74%) fail. end_to_end, cross_functional, stakeholder, strategy, roadmap, drive_impact, initiative are 100% clean; autonomous is 86%. Corrected scope_density (per 1K chars, validated patterns only) rises +64.7% between 2024 and 2026 (0.357 → 0.589), exactly inside V1's +60-70% prediction and about 20 pp below T11's original +85% claim.

  2. Aspiration-heavy share of SWE postings doubled 2024 → 2026 (13.0% → 25.9%). Mean aspiration-to-firm ratio rose 0.61 → 1.00. The rise is consistent across seniority and holds under aggregator exclusion. This is the single strongest ghost-forensics signal.

  3. AI ghostiness test — the most important result for the paper. AI token mentions are dramatically under-represented in the requirements section compared to non-AI tech mentions, in both periods:

  4. 2024: AI in requirements 23.6% vs non-AI tech 39.4% (lift −15.8 pp).
  5. 2026: AI in requirements 21.0% vs non-AI tech 34.3% (lift −13.3 pp).
  6. Instead, AI mentions land in responsibilities (24-26%) and role_summary (28-29%) much more than non-AI tech does (non-AI responsibilities 19-24%, role_summary 15-16%).
  7. AI in preferred section: 2.4% → 7.9%, tripled — AI moved toward "preferred" as its prevalence exploded.

  8. Interpretation: employers describe AI as what you'll do far more than as what you must have. This is a structural qualifier on any RQ3 divergence headline: a substantial share of the posting-side "AI requirement" signal is actually "AI will be part of the job" framing, not a hard requirement. This is the key cross-check for T23.

  9. Kitchen-sink score (tech_count × scope_count) rose 2.4× (4.39 → 10.59). Entry-level: 2.95 → 6.12 (2.1×). Mid-senior: 5.29 → 13.50 (2.6×). Postings are getting broader on both axes simultaneously. This matches T11's breadth/credential-stack findings but is more dramatic because it's multiplicative.

  10. Aggregators are NOT more ghost-like than direct employers — in fact, slightly less. In 2026, direct-employer kitchen-sink score is 10.99 vs aggregators' 8.66; aspiration ratio 1.02 vs 0.92; scope_count 1.69 vs 1.22. T11's hint that Jobs via Dice dominates the top-1% requirement_breadth outlier list is real, but at the corpus level aggregators are less templated-ghost than direct employers — their ghost signature is high tech-count short text, not high scope × high tech.

  11. Credential impossibility is rare (<0.3%). The construct fires almost exclusively on YOE-extraction artifacts (Nutanix "Software Engineer 1 / Early Career" with yoe=18 is the canonical example — "18+ years" phrases in boilerplate confused the YOE extractor). Entry-level credential_impossible rate is 0.27% in 2024, 0.00% in 2026. This indicator should be dropped as a ghost signal; too sparse.

  12. YOE-scope mismatch (entry-level with yoe≥5 OR ≥3 senior scope terms) fires on 5.3% of 2024 entry vs 6.7% of 2026 entry — a small rise, but concentrated almost entirely on AI/ML grad internships at big companies (Visa, General Motors, Adobe, TikTok). Entry-level AI/ML roles with scope language are the dominant "ghost" pattern, and they may be real roles — PhD + many techs + AI is a real 2026 entry-level category.

docs.title: Step 1 — Scope pattern validation
docs.text:
50-row stratified sample per candidate pattern (25 from 2024, 25 from 2026). Automated precision heuristic flags contamination based on known bad contexts derived from V1's manual audit and this task's inspection. Precision ≥ 0.80 = pass.

Pattern Hits (all rows) Sample n Precision Verdict end_to_end 3,646 50 1.00 pass cross_functional 6,171 50 1.00 pass ownership_bare 3,067 50 0.60 FAIL (V1 saw 58%; re-confirmed) ownership_qualified 1,373 50 1.00 pass stakeholder 6,320 50 1.00 pass autonomous 1,584 50 0.86 pass (contamination: "autonomous vehicle/driving/robot") strategy 4,450 50 1.00 pass (engineering contexts dominate) roadmap 1,377 50 1.00 pass drive_impact 1,155 50 1.00 pass vision 2,076 50 0.74 FAIL (26% computer-vision / vision-insurance boilerplate) initiative 4,081 50 1.00 pass

Kept scope patterns (validated_mgmt_patterns.json scope block): end_to_end, cross_functional, ownership_qualified, stakeholder, autonomous, strategy, roadmap, drive_impact, initiative. Total 9 patterns.

Dropped: ownership_bare, vision.

Corrected scope_density (per 1K chars, kept patterns, mean over rows):

Period Validated scope density % change 2024 0.357 — 2026 0.589 +64.7%

T11 reported +85% on a less-filtered pattern set. The corrected rise is 20 pp smaller but directionally identical and still a strong signal. Cite +64.7%, not +85%, as the validated scope density rise.

Caveat on precision methodology: the automated heuristic flags contamination via keyword-based rules (e.g., "autonomous vehicle" → drop). It is stricter than V1's manual coding in some cases (ownership_bare at 60% here vs 58% at V1) and is NOT a substitute for human coding. Sample CSVs are saved in tables/T22/scope_pattern_samples.csv for downstream audit. The direction of every pass/fail call agrees with V1's independent audit where both overlap.

Cross-wave disagreement with T21: T21 (Agent L) published its own validated_mgmt_patterns.json in parallel and reported stakeholder at 0.50 precision as strategic management language and roadmap at 0.70. T22's automated heuristic rated both at 1.0 as org scope language. The patterns fire on the same strings but measure different semantic targets (strategic vs scope). For downstream scope_density use, treat stakeholder and roadmap as PROVISIONAL. Re-running scope_density with the pessimistic set (drop stakeholder, roadmap, vision, ownership_bare — keep end_to_end, cross_functional, ownership_qualified, autonomous, strategy, drive_impact, initiative) gives 2024 = 0.261, 2026 = 0.455 → +74.5%. The original T22 kept-set gives +64.7%. The directional finding ("scope density rose 60-75%") is robust across the specification range; T11's original +85% remains on the high end, V1's predicted +60-70% is on the low end.

docs.title: Step 2 — Per-posting ghost indicators
docs.text:
Five indicators computed per row (see script for exact formulas):

  • kitchen_sink_score = tech_count × scope_count (kept patterns only). High = posting asks for everything.
  • aspiration_ratio = aspiration cues / max(firm cues, 1). Aspiration cues: nice to have, preferred, ideally, bonus, a plus, familiarity with, exposure to, would be a plus, desirable, helpful. Firm cues: must have, required, requirement, minimum, mandatory, essential, basic qualifications.
  • aspiration_heavy = binary indicator: aspiration cues > firm cues AND total ≥ 2.
  • yoe_scope_mismatch = binary: seniority_final='entry' AND (yoe_extracted >= 5 OR senior_scope_count >= 3). Senior scope terms = end_to_end, cross_functional, strategy, roadmap (excluding vision which failed).
  • credential_impossible = binary: entry AND (yoe≥10 OR no degree required paired with MS required). Sparse.

docs.title: Period-level prevalence
docs.text: Metric 2024 mean 2026 mean Δ kitchen_sink_score 4.39 10.59 +141% scope_count (kept) 0.87 1.61 +85% aspiration_ratio 0.61 1.00 +65% aspiration_heavy share 13.0% 25.9% +12.9 pp yoe_scope_mismatch share (all) 0.09% 0.33% +0.24 pp credential_impossible share 0.00% 0.00% ~0 tech_count 4.75 6.11 +29%

docs.title: Period × seniority
docs.text:
Period Seniority kitchen_sink scope_count asp_ratio asp_heavy yoe_mismatch 2024 entry 2.95 0.56 0.63 14.1% 5.3% 2024 mid-senior 5.29 0.98 0.61 12.5% 0.0% 2026 entry 6.12 1.05 1.23 33.1% 6.7% 2026 mid-senior 13.50 1.98 1.00 25.7% 0.0%

Entry-level aspiration-heavy share more than doubled (14.1% → 33.1%), as did mid-senior (12.5% → 25.7%). The aspiration shift is corpus-wide but slightly larger at entry.

docs.title: Aggregator vs direct
docs.text:
Period Is aggregator kitchen_sink scope_count asp_ratio asp_heavy 2024 direct 4.61 0.92 0.61 13.6% 2024 aggregator 3.04 0.62 0.60 9.2% 2026 direct 10.99 1.69 1.02 26.0% 2026 aggregator 8.66 1.22 0.92 25.2%

Aggregators are less ghost-like by this construction, not more. Their tech_count is comparable (5.85 in 2024, 6.49 in 2026) but their scope_count and aspiration cues are systematically lower. This contradicts the "aggregators template-spam everything" intuition at the corpus level. Jobs via Dice drives the T11 top-1% outlier list, but in expectation aggregators are cleaner.

docs.title: Step 3 — Template saturation per company
docs.text:
Per-company pairwise cosine similarity on requirements+preferred section text, restricted to companies with ≥ 5 LLM-cleaned postings in the full frame. 713 companies eligible. 6 companies have mean pairwise cosine > 0.8 — genuine template saturation.

See tables/T22/template_saturation_per_company.csv. The 6 saturated companies account for a tiny fraction of total postings; template homogenization (T15's finding) is corpus-wide, not concentrated in a handful of employers running literal copies.

docs.title: Step 4 — AI ghostiness test (THE KEY RESULT)
docs.text: Two complementary tests.

docs.title: (A) Window-level cue test
docs.text:
For each AI keyword mention (regex: ai, artificial intelligence, llm, gpt, chatgpt, copilot, cursor, claude, agentic, generative, rag, langchain, prompt engineering, machine learning, deep learning, nlp, computer vision, transformer, embedding, fine-tuning, ml, pytorch, tensorflow, openai, anthropic, gemini, mcp), compute the share landing within 120 chars of an aspiration cue vs a firm cue. Same for a non-AI tech regex (python/java/aws/kubernetes/etc).

Period AI asp-window share AI firm-window share Non-AI asp-window share Non-AI firm-window share AI asp lift 2024 12.9% 9.9% 19.8% 16.3% −6.9 pp 2026 15.2% 7.5% 21.3% 10.3% −6.2 pp

AI mentions are less likely to have aspiration cues OR firm cues nearby — they are more likely to appear in neutral (responsibility / role summary) text. Both periods.

docs.title: (B) Section-level test (more direct)
docs.text:
For each posting, segment with the T13 section classifier, then count AI and non-AI tech mentions inside each segment.

Period AI in requirements Non-AI in requirements AI lift (pp) AI in preferred Non-AI in preferred AI lift (pp) AI in responsibilities Non-AI in responsibilities 2024 23.6% 39.4% −15.8 2.4% 3.6% −1.2 24.3% 19.5% 2026 21.0% 34.3% −13.3 7.9% 7.4% +0.5 25.8% 24.1%

Punchline: AI mentions are hugely under-represented in the requirements section (−13 to −16 pp vs non-AI tech) and over-represented in responsibilities + role_summary. This holds in both periods.

What moved 2024 → 2026: - AI preferred-section share tripled (2.4% → 7.9%) and roughly caught up to non-AI (7.4%). - AI requirements-section share stayed similar (23.6% → 21.0%), even as the total AI mention count quadrupled (12,512 → 43,481 window-level / 12,330 → 42,988 section-level).

Interpretation for T23 (CRITICAL):

The paper's headline "employer AI requirement rate" (which T05 / T14 / T23 all compute as "any AI keyword in description") is genuinely capturing AI-related content, but only ~21-24% of that content is in the requirements section — most of the AI mentions land in responsibilities ("you will build LLM features") or role summary ("we are building an AI platform"). Non-AI tech mentions, by contrast, land in requirements 34-39% of the time.

This does NOT invalidate the divergence story. But it reframes it: - AI-as-topic is ~2× over-represented in SWE postings between 2024 and 2026 (RQ3 lead, confirmed elsewhere). - AI-as-hard-requirement is a smaller, still-real subset of that signal. If we restrict the AI prevalence measure to hits in the requirements section only, the rate is roughly 21-24% of the all-description rate. A 28.6% broad-union rate implies ~6-7% of SWE postings have an AI mention in the requirements section.

T23 should present the all-description number as the headline (consistent with prior work) and the requirements-only number as a ghost-check lower bound.

docs.title: Step 5 — 20 most ghost-like entry-level postings
docs.text:
Ranked by (kitchen_sink_score normalized) + (aspiration_ratio clipped) + yoe_scope_mismatch + credential_impossible.

Rank Title Company Period YOE Tech Scope Ghost? 1 Software Engineer, New College Grad — 2026 Visa 2026-04 — 23 6 Real big-tech new grad posting (23 techs is real for Visa) 2 Software Engineer, New College Grad — Atlanta Visa 2026-04 — 23 6 Same template as #1 3 Software Engineer 1 / Early Career Nutanix 2024-01 18 7 0 YOE artifact — "18+ years" from boilerplate 4-6, 10-12 2026 AI/ML Intern / Grad General Motors / Adobe / TikTok 2026 — 5-8 4-9 Real PhD AI/ML grad roles 7 Builder — Software Engineer (New Grad) Reevo 2026-03 — 7 5 Real 8 Software Development Engineer I Optimum 2026-04 1 8 6 Real 9 Software Engineer, 3D Modeling Hover 2026-04 1 5 1 Real 16 Software Engineer, Product Frontend (2-8 YOE) Airtable 2026-03 2 6 5 Real ("2-8 YOE" is hedge range, not entry) 19 Junior Systems Engineer JRC Integrated Systems 2026-04 2 0 5 Defense/cleared boilerplate

Verdict: 18 of 20 top-ghost entry postings are real roles, just concentrated in AI/ML grad pipelines at large enterprises. The ghost rank is dominated by yoe_scope_mismatch (which fires for any entry-labeled posting with yoe=NaN AND scope cues, and AI/ML grad roles typically have both). One is a YOE extraction artifact (Nutanix). The rest are real and reflect the 2026 reality that entry-level AI/ML roles now ask for PhDs + full AI stacks. This is a feature of the market, not an artifact of the posting text. See tables/T22/top20_ghost_entry.csv.

docs.title: Step 7 — Industry patterns (arshkon-only)
docs.text: company_industry is only available in arshkon (and some scraped rows with compound labels). Restricted to arshkon. Top 20 industries by posting count are dominated by IT Services, Staffing, Software Dev, Financial Services, Higher Education. See tables/T22/ghost_by_industry_arshkon.csv. Ghost prevalence varies modestly across industries; no single industry dominates as "ghost-heavy" — the rise is corpus-wide.

docs.title: Sensitivities applied
docs.text:
Dimension Applied Result (a) aggregator exclusion yes Direct-employer subset has higher kitchen-sink and aspiration ratio than aggregator subset. Exclusion STRENGTHENS the ghost findings slightly. (d) description text source partial Primary uses description_core_llm (section-header-preserving). The shared swe_cleaned_text.parquet is tokenized and cannot be used for aspiration/firm cue counts. A raw-text sanity check is deferred; the boilerplate-sensitivity risk is highest on aspiration patterns, which live in preferred/benefits boilerplate in some 2024 postings. (f) within-2024 calibration NOT applied Would require recomputing within-arshkon-vs-asaniczka on these metrics. Added to action items.

Materiality: the AI section-level finding is robust to aggregator exclusion (AI → requirements lift stays negative in both periods). Aspiration-heavy doubling is also robust.

docs.title: Surprises
docs.text:
1. Aggregators are less ghost-like than direct employers. I expected the reverse. The scope_count gap is the biggest surprise: direct-employer scope_count is ~35% higher than aggregator scope_count in both periods. The "aggregators are template sludge" intuition does not survive this corpus-level test. Jobs via Dice is a top-1% outlier generator but not a corpus-average driver.

  2. AI mentions live in responsibilities + role_summary, not requirements. This is the opposite of what a naive reading of "the AI requirement explosion" implies. Employers describe AI as part of the work more than as a hard requirement. The section-level split should be cited everywhere the divergence story is discussed.

  3. vision fails the scope pattern precision check (74%) because "computer vision" is an AI domain that is genuinely in the data, plus vision-insurance boilerplate from benefits sections. Gate 2's pattern list did not flag this. Dropped.

  4. Aspiration-heavy share doubled corpus-wide, not just in AI-heavy subsets. Both entry (14.1 → 33.1%) and mid-senior (12.5 → 25.7%) roughly doubled. This is consistent with a "recruiter tooling adoption softens language" reading (T29 territory).

  5. Credential impossibility is nearly non-existent (<0.3%). I expected ghost postings to be full of "10+ YOE for entry-level" contradictions. They aren't. The construct catches only one YOE-extraction artifact in the top 20. Drop from ghost indicator list for downstream use.

  6. The +64.7% corrected scope_density matches V1's +60-70% prediction almost exactly. V1's independent audit and this task's automated re-validation converge. The scope rise is real, the magnitude is 20 pp below T11's original claim, and everyone in the agent chain agrees now.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. Automated precision heuristic, not human coding. The validation rules for scope patterns (dropping ownership when in corporate-boilerplate contexts, dropping vision when preceded by computer or insurance) are keyword-based and approximate. V2 should run a human sample if the paper publishes exact precision numbers. Sample CSVs in tables/T22/ support this.

  2. Section classifier precision. The T13 classifier was audited at ≥95% precision on mixed kaggle+scraped text. The AI ghostiness section-level finding inherits that precision. A ~5% classifier error rate cannot explain a 13-16 pp AI-vs-non-AI requirements lift.

  3. Aspiration cue list is not exhaustive. I used 12 patterns. True aspiration language in job postings is broader ("you might", "we'd love to see", "experience with X is ideal"). The aspiration_heavy metric is a conservative lower bound.

  4. firm_count uses required/requirement as cues, but these words also appear inside "required qualifications" headers. This inflates firm cue counts in postings with structured section headers. The aspiration ratio may be biased downward (aspiration understated) in 2024 more than 2026 because 2024 sources have fewer markdown-structured headers.

  5. Text frame is capped at text_source='llm' (34,161 rows). Ghost indicators are only computed on the LLM-cleaned subset. The 2026 scraped side is 12,481 rows — the Gate 1 binding constraint applies.

  6. Entry-level n is thin. 376 entry in 2024, 607 entry in 2026 under seniority_final. Gate 1 MDE 8.2 pp applies. The aspiration-heavy entry-level doubling (14.1 → 33.1%) is well above MDE.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. T23 (this agent): use the AI section-level finding to qualify the divergence headline. Report both the all-description and requirements-only AI prevalence rates.
  2. T28 (Agent O): the aspiration_heavy share by archetype would sharpen the ghost story — is it concentrated in any domain?
  3. T29 (Agent O): the aspiration_heavy doubling is a strong candidate signature of recruiter LLM drafting — LLMs hedge more than humans. Cross-reference with T29's authorship score.
  4. T24 (Agent N synthesis): the "AI lives in responsibilities, not requirements" finding is a narrative hook. It reframes the divergence as "employers are building AI work into the role description, not demanding AI skills up front."
  5. V2 verification: independently validate the vision and autonomous pattern drop/keep decisions with a human sample, and independently validate the section-level AI ghostiness lift using an alternative section classifier if available.

docs.title: T23. Employer-requirement / worker-usage divergence
docs.text: Agent M (Wave 3). Run: 2026-04-15. Input: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_tech_matrix.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet, data/unified.parquet (for description_core_llm). External benchmarks from Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2024/2025 and Anthropic's Labor Market Impact research (fetched 2026-04-15). Script: exploration/scripts/T23_divergence.py.

docs.title: Headline finding — the divergence points the OPPOSITE way
docs.text:
Worker-side AI usage (80.8% of professional developers, Stack Overflow 2025) is 2-15× higher than the rate at which SWE postings list AI requirements in 2026. The gap is robustly negative across every benchmark assumption tested (50% → 85% worker usage) and across every specification (narrow regex / broad 24-term union / tool-specific).

Measure 2026 SWE posting rate Worker rate (SO 2025) Gap (pp) Ratio (posting / worker) Narrow AI ("ai" / "artificial intelligence" regex, T05-style) 34.6% 80.8% any-use −46.2 pp 0.43 Broad AI (24 tech-term union, T14-style) 28.6% 80.8% any-use −52.2 pp 0.35 AI-as-tool (copilot/cursor/claude/chatgpt/prompt-eng) 6.9% 80.8% any-use −73.9 pp 0.09

The paper's lead RQ3 narrative must be inverted. The original framing assumed employer AI requirements outpace worker adoption ("anticipatory restructuring"). The data says the opposite: developers are already using AI tools at 2-15× the rate that employers ask for them in job postings.

What the paper CAN still say (and should): the posting-side AI rate rose 5.6× between 2024 and 2026 (51.3% → 28.6% broad-union → broad AI grew +23.5 pp), and the worker-side rose more slowly (63.2% → 80.8% in SO's panel → +17.6 pp). Posting-side growth (+23.5 pp) outpaces worker-side growth (+17.6 pp), so employers are catching up, not falling further behind. But the absolute level of AI usage remains much higher on the worker side than the employer side.

docs.title: By period (all SWE LinkedIn, default filters)
docs.text:
period n narrow_ai (LLM text denom) broad_ai (full corpus) ai_tool ai_domain ai_generic 2024 22,820 5.96% 5.13% 0.11% 3.90% 2.02% 2026 40,881 34.6% 28.6% 6.87% 12.77% 20.33% Δ — +28.7 pp +23.5 pp +6.8 pp +8.9 pp +18.3 pp Ratio — 5.8× 5.6× 62.7× 3.3× 10.0×

Consistency with Gate 2: the broad rate (28.6%) matches T14's reported broad-union 2026 rate. The narrow rate (34.6%) differs from T05's 18.78% because T05 used a narrower pattern on raw text; here I use description_core_llm which has different coverage. Both are valid operationalizations of "narrow AI."

docs.title: By period × seniority (entry vs mid-senior)
docs.text:
Period Seniority n narrow_ai broad_ai ai_tool ai_domain ai_generic 2024 entry 379 7.2% 6.3% 0.3% 4.8% 2.4% 2024 mid-senior 13,490 6.3% 5.2% 0.1% 4.1% 2.1% 2026 entry 1,275 31.8% 29.1% 6.4% 18.1% 18.0% 2026 mid-senior 17,418 37.4% 31.2% 7.5% 14.0% 22.1%

Mid-senior 2026 AI posting rates slightly exceed entry-level on broad/narrow/tool metrics (31-37% vs 29-32%). Entry-level has the higher ai_domain share (18.1% vs 14.0%), reflecting AI/ML grad internship pipelines (Visa/GM/Adobe/TikTok from T22).

docs.title: By aggregator (sensitivity a)
docs.text:
Period Is aggregator n narrow_ai broad_ai ai_tool 2024 direct 19,447 5.8% 5.2% 0.11% 2024 aggregator 3,373 6.9% 4.5% 0.09% 2026 direct 34,081 36.0% 29.4% 6.94% 2026 aggregator 6,800 28.3% 24.6% 6.51%

Aggregators have LOWER AI posting rates than direct employers in 2026 (24.6% vs 29.4% broad). Exclusion strengthens the direct-employer AI rate by ~1 pp but does not change the divergence direction.

docs.title: Step 2 — External benchmarks
docs.text:
Fetched 2026-04-15 from:

Benchmark Value Source Notes Stack Overflow 2024 Pro, currently using 63.2% survey.stackoverflow.co/2024/ai +13.5% plan to soon → total awareness ~77% Stack Overflow 2025 Pro, daily 50.6% survey.stackoverflow.co/2025/ai — Stack Overflow 2025 Pro, any use (daily+weekly+monthly) 80.8% survey.stackoverflow.co/2025/ai Self-reported Stack Overflow 2025 all, using or planning 84.0% stackoverflow.blog/2025/12/29 Includes learners Anthropic Computer Programmer occ exposure 75% anthropic.com/research/labor-market-impacts "Observed exposure" share of tasks Accenture Copilot deployed-dev daily 67% github.blog 5+ days/week among licensed External Copilot surveys midpoint ~33% 2024-2025 range 26-40% Unconditional Copilot users total 20M GitHub official July 2025 SO 2025 Copilot share AMONG AI-users 67.9% survey.stackoverflow.co/2025/ai → 54.9% unconditional (× 80.8%) SO 2025 ChatGPT share AMONG AI-users 81.7% survey.stackoverflow.co/2025/ai → 66.0% unconditional SO 2025 Claude Code share AMONG AI-users 40.8% survey.stackoverflow.co/2025/ai → 33.0% unconditional

Choice of primary benchmark: Stack Overflow 2025 professional-developer "any use" (80.8%). This is the best-matched number for "do professional developers use AI in their day job." It is self-selected (respondents who bother with SO surveys skew toward engaged developers), so sensitivity 50-85% is essential.

docs.title: Headline (2026 posting vs SO 2025 80.8% worker any-use)
docs.text:
Metric Posting rate Worker rate Gap (pp) Ratio Narrow AI posting 34.6% 80.8% −46.2 0.43 Broad AI posting 28.6% 80.8% −52.2 0.35 AI-as-tool posting 6.87% 80.8% −73.9 0.09

The gap is negative in every case — worker usage is above posting requirement. This is the opposite of the anticipatory-restructuring hypothesis.

docs.title: Sensitivity (step 6)
docs.text:
Under every worker-rate assumption from 50% to 85%, the direction holds:

Worker assumption Narrow gap Broad gap AI-tool gap 50% (lowest plausible) −15.4 pp −21.4 pp −43.1 pp 65% −30.4 pp −36.4 pp −58.1 pp 75% −40.4 pp −46.4 pp −68.1 pp 80.8% (SO 2025) −46.2 pp −52.2 pp −73.9 pp 85% −50.4 pp −56.4 pp −78.1 pp

The only scenario where employer rate matches or exceeds worker rate is worker < 29% for broad AI, worker < 35% for narrow AI, or worker < 7% for tool-specific. All three are implausible — every benchmark we found puts worker any-use at 50%+.

Materiality: the qualitative finding (worker-above-employer) is robust across the full sensitivity range. Magnitudes differ by up to 40 pp across the range, but direction does not flip.

docs.title: Step 4 — Temporal divergence (how fast are they moving?)
docs.text:
Metric 2024 2026 Δ pp Ratio Narrow AI posting 5.96% 34.6% +28.7 5.8× Broad AI posting 5.13% 28.6% +23.5 5.6× AI-tool posting 0.11% 6.87% +6.8 62.7× AI-domain posting 3.90% 12.77% +8.9 3.3× AI-generic posting 2.02% 20.33% +18.3 10.0× Worker any-use (SO pro, 2024→2025) 63.2% 80.8% +17.6 1.28×

Posting-side is growing FASTER in absolute points than the worker-side (+23.5 pp broad vs +17.6 pp worker any-use). In ratio terms, the posting side grew 5.6× while the worker side grew 1.28×. Employers are catching up, but from a much lower base. If the current growth rates held constant, it would take roughly 5-8 more years for posting broad AI to match SO 2025's 80.8% worker rate — and by then the worker rate will be even higher.

Important caveat: the worker-side is approaching saturation (80%+ of professional developers), so its absolute growth is naturally slowing. The posting-side growth from 5% to 28% looks large partly because of the low base.

docs.title: Step 5 — Divergence by specificity (per-tool)
docs.text:
Tool Posting rate 2026 Worker rate (est.) Gap (pp) Ratio Benchmark note GitHub Copilot 3.77% 54.9% −51.1 0.07 67.9% among AI-users × 80.8% any-use ChatGPT 0.61% 66.0% −65.4 0.01 81.7% among AI-users × 80.8% any-use Claude Code 3.37% 33.0% −29.6 0.10 40.8% among AI-users × 80.8% any-use Cursor 1.91% — — — No published benchmark

Punchline: employers list specific AI tools in 1-4% of postings, while 33-66% of workers use them. The tool-specific divergence is 10-100× larger than the broad-AI divergence. Employers are not asking for Copilot / ChatGPT / Claude by name — they're asking for AI literacy broadly (if at all) and leaving tool choice to the developer.

This is the single most important per-tool finding for the paper. Copilot alone has 20M users (GitHub) and is used by ~55% of pro developers, but shows up in only 3.8% of 2026 SWE postings. The ratio of worker usage to posting requirement is 14.5× for Copilot.

Claude Code has the smallest (but still large) gap: 10% posting-to-worker ratio. Its absolute posting rate (3.4%) is the highest relative to its worker base (33%), which suggests Claude Code is somewhat more likely to be named in job postings than Copilot or ChatGPT.

docs.title: Step 7 — Ghost cross-check (from T22)
docs.text:
Using T22's section-level AI ghostiness finding: AI mentions land in the requirements section only 21% of the time in 2026 (vs 34% for non-AI tech), so the "hard AI requirement" rate is a small subset of the all-description AI rate.

Measure 2024 2026 Δ pp Broad AI (all sections) 5.13% 28.61% +23.5 Broad AI (requirements section only, adjusted) 1.21% 6.00% +4.8 AI mention share in requirements section 23.6% 21.0% −2.6 AI mention share in preferred section 2.4% 7.9% +5.5 AI mention share in responsibilities section 24.3% 25.8% +1.5

When we restrict the AI posting rate to mentions in the requirements section only, the 2026 rate drops from 28.6% to about 6.0%. That is a plausible "hard AI requirement" estimate for the market.

Worker usage at 80.8% vs requirements-only AI at 6.0% gives a gap of −74.8 pp, and a ratio of 0.07 — the divergence is even LARGER when restricted to hard requirements. This is the defensible version of the divergence claim: not "employers want AI skills they don't have" (wrong direction) but "developers are using AI tools in their jobs whether or not the posting says so — by a factor of 10-15×."

The AI-mention share in preferred sections TRIPLED (2.4% → 7.9%) while the requirements share held steady (23.6% → 21.0%). Growth of posted AI content is disproportionately landing in "preferred" / nice-to-have language, not in hard requirements. This confirms T22's ghostiness finding: as AI mentions explode on the posting side, they're increasingly hedged.

docs.title: Sensitivities applied
docs.text:
Dimension Applied Result (a) aggregator exclusion yes Direct employers have 29.4% broad AI vs 24.6% aggregator. Exclusion SLIGHTLY strengthens the posting rate; does not change the divergence direction. (c) seniority operationalization yes Entry and mid-senior both show large negative gaps. Mid-senior broad AI (31.2%) is slightly above entry (29.1%). Under both seniority_final and label-independent YOE proxy, the divergence direction holds. (d) description text source partial Narrow AI rate computed on description_core_llm (LLM subset, n=12,483 2026 rows). Broad AI via tech matrix on all rows. Both point the same direction. (f) within-2024 calibration via T14 T14 already calibrated broad AI: SNR 13.3 (well above threshold).

The divergence direction is robust to every sensitivity dimension I can apply. Magnitudes shift modestly (±5 pp) under aggregator exclusion and seniority operationalization but never flip.

docs.title: Surprises
docs.text:
1. The divergence points the opposite of RQ3's original hypothesis. The paper's prior framing (employer AI requirements outpace worker usage as "anticipatory restructuring") must be inverted. The new framing: worker AI usage is so broad and early that employers are effectively describing the job to developers who already use AI every day — and the posting side is slowly catching up.

  2. Per-tool divergence is 10-100× larger than aggregate divergence. The Copilot gap (ratio 0.07) is over 5× larger than the broad AI gap (ratio 0.35). This tells us employers are increasingly AI-literate in broad terms but very rarely name specific tools. This could be intentional (leaving tool choice to the developer) or it could be a lag (HR/recruiters don't know which tool to ask for).

  3. ChatGPT has the largest tool gap (ratio 0.01 — posting rate 0.6% vs worker rate 66%). It's the most-used tool by developers but the least-listed in postings. This is likely because ChatGPT is seen as a general productivity tool, not a named engineering skill — employers don't list it the way they don't list "Google Docs."

  4. The 2026 posting-side AI rate is 28.6% (broad) but only 6% lands in the requirements section. Most of the posting-side AI content is in responsibilities / role summary, not hard requirements. When we restrict to hard requirements, the 6.0% hard-requirement rate against 80.8% worker rate is the cleanest single number for the paper: a 13.5× gap between worker AI usage and posting AI hard requirements.

  5. Aggregators have LOWER AI rates than direct employers (24.6% vs 29.4% broad). Consistent with T22's finding that aggregators are less ghost-like at the corpus level.

  6. Mid-senior 2026 AI rate slightly exceeds entry-level (31.2% vs 29.1% broad). The "employers demand more AI at the junior rung" hypothesis is not supported; if anything, senior postings mention AI slightly more. Entry-level is higher only on the ai_domain (ML/DL/CV) subset.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
1. Self-selection bias in SO 2025: Stack Overflow's 80.8% figure is self-reported by developers who respond to the survey. Respondents likely skew more AI-engaged than the average professional developer. The Anthropic Labor Market Impact study reports Computer Programmer observed exposure at 75%, consistent with SO 2025, and the sensitivity range from 50% to 85% covers the plausible range. Even at the lowest plausible worker rate of 50%, broad posting rate (28.6%) is 21 pp below worker rate.

  2. Posting rate is a LINGUISTIC signal, not a hiring intention. The 28.6% broad AI posting rate means "the posting text mentions an AI term somewhere." It does NOT mean "the employer will reject applicants without AI skills." T22's section analysis (only 21% of AI mentions are in the requirements section) suggests the actual "hard requirement" rate is ~6%. The apples-to-apples comparison with "workers use AI tools in their day job" is closer to the 6% requirements-only figure — but even then, the direction of divergence is worker-above-employer by more than 10×.

  3. Worker usage includes general tools (ChatGPT, Copilot). A developer using ChatGPT to look up a regex counts as "using AI" in SO's survey. This inflates the worker rate. But developers at 80.8% self-reported any-use is still enormous compared to 28.6% posting mention rate — the direction holds even if we halve the worker rate.

  4. AI-tool vs AI-domain vs AI-generic categories are imperfect. langchain and rag could be AI-tool OR AI-generic depending on context. The categorization follows the Gate 2 guidance and is approximate.

  5. Narrow AI regex has false positives on boilerplate (e.g., "we use AI responsibly", "no AI tools allowed"). T05 reported 925 SNR on this pattern, which validates its discriminative power — but the absolute level is inflated by boilerplate to some unknown degree. The 34.6% narrow rate should be read as an upper bound; the broad 28.6% is a tighter measure.

  6. The tech-matrix regexes were validated in the shared artifact build. c++, c#, .NET were flagged with markdown-escape bugs (Gate 2 note 9); AI tech patterns were not affected.

docs.title: Action items for downstream tasks
docs.text:
1. T24 (Agent N synthesis) — CRITICAL: the divergence direction is OPPOSITE to RQ3's original hypothesis. The paper needs a major narrative update. The new lead is: "Worker AI tool usage (80.8% SO 2025) is 10-15× the rate at which employers list AI tools in job postings, and tool-specific naming is almost entirely absent from postings." This is still a RQ3 win — it's just a different win.

  2. T26 (Agent N SYNTHESIS.md): integrate the per-tool gap (Copilot 14.5×, ChatGPT ~100×, Claude 10×) as the headline divergence number rather than the narrow/broad aggregate. The tool-specific gap is the cleanest signal.

  3. T25 (Agent N interview protocol): add a probe: "Employers rarely name specific AI tools like Copilot or Cursor in job postings, even though most developers use them daily. Why do you think that is?" This is a high-value qualitative question.

  4. V2 verification: independently validate the 28.6% broad posting rate using a different AI term list and the 80.8% worker benchmark against a non-SO source. Also verify the per-tool rates on the 2026 scraped subset only (arshkon has ~0 AI-tool mentions in 2024).

  5. T18 (cross-occupation): if the worker-above-employer pattern is specific to SWE, it's a strong RQ3 finding. If it also holds for SWE-adjacent and control occupations, the finding becomes "posting AI rate lags worker AI use in knowledge work generally" — a broader and arguably more important claim.
