docs.title: T28 — Domain-stratified scope changes by archetype
docs.text: Date: 2026-04-15 Agent: Wave 3 O Input: exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_archetype_labels.parquet, swe_cleaned_text.parquet, swe_tech_matrix.parquet, data/unified.parquet

docs.title: Headline
docs.text:
Aggregate scope and content changes are almost entirely within-domain, not between-domain composition shifts. The Kitagawa decomposition of the 2024→2026 entry-share change puts 85% (seniority_final) to 107% (yoe_le2_of_known) of the total change inside the within-domain component; between-domain composition contributes 2-10%. The T09 "uniform within-archetype junior-share rise" result survives replacement of seniority_final with the label-independent YOE ≤ 2 proxy — 15 of 16 large archetypes rise under YOE ≤ 2 (of all), 13 of 13 rise under YOE ≤ 2 (of known). Alt A's "instrument-dominated apparent rise" reading WEAKENS because the rise survives under a proxy that does not depend on any seniority classifier.

Within-archetype junior/senior content comparison reveals convergence that the T15 corpus-level null missed: credential_stack_depth gap closes (and often flips) in ALL 10 large archetypes; requirement_breadth gap closes in 5/10. LLM/GenAI is the exception — there the junior/senior gap in requirement_breadth and tech_count WIDENS (+1.4 and +2.0 respectively), i.e., LLM/GenAI senior postings are pulling away from LLM/GenAI junior postings on content breadth.

LLM/GenAI deep dive: 68% of 2026 volume comes from companies NOT present in the 2024 sample. Only 138 of 1,174 2026 LLM/GenAI companies overlap with 2024. Entry share rose under both operationalizations (seniority_final 2.2% → 7.4%, YOE≤2 8.8% → 11.6%). Description length +30% (2054 → 2664 chars). The LLM/GenAI explosion is mostly NEW entrants, not existing employers pivoting.

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
• Archetype coverage: 34,099 of 63,701 SWE LinkedIn rows are labeled (53.5%). Coverage is 94.8% in 2024 but only 30.5% in 2026, because scraped text goes through a smaller Stage-9 LLM window (text_source='llm' rows only). Every 2026 archetype-level figure should be read with this coverage constraint in mind.
  • Used a conservative subset of the Gate-2 / T21-validated clean pattern set: scope is end-to-end + cross-functional (no bare ownership), mentoring is \bmentor(?:ing|ship|s|ed)?\b only. The T21 validated bundle at exploration/artifacts/shared/validated_mgmt_patterns.json contains 13 people-mgmt patterns (all precision=1.0), 14 tech-orch patterns, 9 strategic patterns — T28 uses only mentor from the people-mgmt set to preserve apples-to-apples with Gate 2's narrow mentor finding. A fuller pattern set would strengthen the magnitudes but the qualitative direction of the mentor rise is already clear here. Follow-up with T21's full pattern bundle recommended.
  • AI mention is reported narrow (\bai\b|\bartificial intelligence\b) and broad (union of 17 tech-matrix AI columns). Both pass SNR ≥ 2.
  • Density metrics (scope_density, credential_stack_depth, requirement_breadth) computed on cleaned LLM text only (llm_extraction_coverage='labeled'). Binary metrics allow raw-text fallback for recall.
  • Kitagawa-style decomposition: agg_2026 - agg_2024 = Σ w₂₀₂₄Δr + Σ r₂₀₂₄Δw + Σ Δw·Δr. Restricted to labeled archetypes (excludes archetype=-2 "no text"). See exploration/scripts/T28_archetype_decomposition.py.

docs.title: 1. Archetype distribution by period
docs.text:
Labeled archetype counts only. Asaniczka is distributed across many archetypes in 2024; scraped 2026 is concentrated in Defense/cleared, DevOps, LLM/GenAI, JS frontend, Java enterprise, Data, Embedded.

# Name n_2024 n_2026 Δshare (pp of labeled) 0 LLM / GenAI / ML engineering 1,059 2,559 +1.6 1 Defense & cleared regulated work 5,676 1,426 −21.4 2 DevOps / SRE / platform 2,576 1,943 −6.5 3 JS frontend (React/TypeScript) 2,031 1,340 −5.6 4 Java enterprise (Spring/microservices) 2,254 1,146 −7.1 5 Data engineering (Spark/ETL) 1,347 947 −3.6 6 Embedded / firmware 1,476 723 −4.7 7 .NET / ASP.NET full-stack 1,250 323 −4.7 8 Agile/Scrum generalist 1,081 434 −3.7

LLM/GenAI is the ONLY archetype that rises in share of labeled rows. Share shifts are dominated by the 2024 Defense/cleared oversample (itself largely an asaniczka composition artifact per Gate 2) and cannot be interpreted as real cross-period composition change without an archetype classifier trained on the raw 2026 text — which we don't have.

See exploration/tables/T28/step1_archetype_distribution.csv.

docs.title: 2. Domain × seniority decomposition of the entry-share change
Aggregate decomposition (labeled archetypes only)
docs.text:
Metric 2024 2026 Δ Within (pp) Between (pp) Interaction Within % Between % seniority_final entry 1.72% 4.83% +3.10 pp +2.64 +0.11 +0.36 85.0% 3.4% yoe_le2 of known 11.23% 17.16% +5.92 pp +6.34 +0.56 −0.98 107.0% 9.5% yoe_le2 of all 7.78% 11.63% +3.86 pp +4.34 +0.07 −0.56 112.6% 1.9%

Interpretation. Between-domain market-share shifts contribute at most 9.5% of the entry-share change. The overwhelming share of the apparent change is happening inside archetypes, not as a reallocation across them. Alt B ("ML/AI eats frontend") is decisively ruled out — as Gate 2 already flagged, ML/AI is not entry-poor, and the ML/AI growth shifts the between-domain component only marginally.

See exploration/tables/T28/step2_entry_decomposition_summary.csv and exploration/figures/T28/fig1_decomposition_summary.png.

docs.title: Uniform-rise check under YOE ≤ 2
docs.text:
Does the T09 "within-archetype entry share rose uniformly in 9/10 large archetypes" result hold under the label-independent YOE proxy?

Spec large archetypes rising flat/down seniority_final 16/16 large archetypes 0 yoe_le2_of_all 15/16 1 (Azure data platform, tiny: −0.58 pp) yoe_le2_of_known 13/13 large archetypes (with n_known ≥ 100 in both periods) 0

Conclusion — Alt A weakens. The uniform within-archetype rise is robust to replacing seniority_final with YOE ≤ 2, a metric that does not depend on any seniority classifier. Both operationalizations produce the same qualitative pattern: within-archetype entry share rose broadly. Under seniority_final the biggest risers are Agile/Scrum generalist (+5.9 pp), LLM/GenAI (+5.3 pp), .NET (+4.3 pp), JS frontend (+4.0 pp). Under YOE ≤ 2 of known the biggest risers are Defense/cleared (+10.5 pp) and Embedded (+8.5 pp).

Under seniority_final, GPU/CUDA (the Gate-2-corrected exception) has n < 100 in both periods and drops out of the "large archetype" set — so we do not replicate V1's exact "9/10 with GPU/CUDA falling" claim here; we see 16/16 rising in the n ≥ 100 set under seniority_final, and 15/16 rising under YOE ≤ 2 of all. This is a stricter version of the Gate 2 corrected statement.

The "mostly instrument artifact" reading is not sufficient. If Stage 10 LLM labeling drift were the main driver, we would expect the uniform rise to weaken or disappear under YOE ≤ 2, which does not depend on the LLM. It doesn't weaken. This does not rule out Alt A entirely — the uniform pattern is still more consistent with a "category-wide shift" than with a specific-archetype restructuring — but the mechanism is no longer about LLM label quality.

See exploration/tables/T28/step2_uniform_rise_check.csv, step2_uniform_rise_counts.csv, and exploration/figures/T28/fig2_uniform_rise.png.

docs.title: Requirement breadth (clean text; number of the 14 breadth categories present)
docs.text:
Archetype 2024 2026 Δ Java enterprise 7.40 8.64 +1.24 .NET / ASP.NET 6.75 8.42 +1.67 LLM/GenAI 5.60 6.64 +1.04 Python web backend 6.08 7.47 +1.39 JS frontend 6.64 7.40 +0.76 Embedded / firmware 4.42 5.25 +0.83 Data engineering 6.58 7.07 +0.49 DevOps / SRE 6.36 7.27 +0.91 Defense / cleared 4.62 5.10 +0.48 Agile/Scrum generalist 7.26 8.02 +0.76

Every large archetype rose on requirement breadth. Scope inflation is within-domain, uniform, and not a composition effect.

docs.title: AI mentions (broad) by archetype
docs.text:
Archetype 2024 2026 Δ pp LLM/GenAI 41.7% 85.9% +44.2 JS frontend 2.5% 17.2% +14.7 .NET / ASP.NET 1.0% 14.6% +13.6 Java enterprise 0.75% 12.4% +11.6 DevOps / SRE 1.4% 11.7% +10.2 Data engineering 3.1% 12.4% +9.2 Agile/Scrum generalist 2.0% 9.2% +7.2 Defense / cleared 1.1% 2.9% +1.8 Embedded / firmware 1.0% 3.7% +2.7

AI vocabulary migrated into traditionally non-AI archetypes. Frontend JS, .NET full-stack, and Java enterprise posted +11-15 pp rises in broad AI mention, indicating that the AI layer T14 detected is sprinkling across every domain, not just the native ML archetype. Defense/cleared and Embedded/firmware — classically conservative — lag at +2-3 pp, consistent with clearance and domain-specific tooling barriers. This is a novel finding not surfaced in T09.

docs.title: Credential stack depth
docs.text: Credential stack depth fell across most archetypes in this rebuild (using our pattern set: years-experience, BS/MS/PhD, certification counts). This contradicts Gate 2's "credential_stack_depth rose" headline — but note my pattern set differs from T11's and may be less comprehensive. The direction is sensitive to pattern choice. Material sensitivity: flagged. Gate 2 should not elevate this to a Wave 3 confirmation; T28's data is consistent with credential stack depth being pattern-dependent.

docs.title: Mentoring mentions
docs.text:
Archetype 2024 2026 Δ Java enterprise 25.4% 41.5% +16.1 LLM/GenAI 24.8% 40.5% +15.7 JS frontend 28.8% 42.5% +13.7 DevOps / SRE 24.0% 36.6% +12.7 Defense / cleared 19.2% 29.3% +10.1 Data engineering 23.8% 35.2% +11.4 .NET / ASP.NET 24.4% 44.3% +19.9

Mentoring rise is broad across all archetypes — not concentrated in LLM/GenAI. This is important because the Gate 2 narrative suggested mentoring could be a senior-shift signal. T28 shows it is a corpus-wide text feature growth, not a domain-specific restructuring. Combined with T29's authorship-style evidence (below), the mentoring rise is a strong candidate for a style artifact of LLM drafting, not a labor-market change.

See exploration/tables/T28/step3_domain_scope_changes.csv and exploration/figures/T28/fig3_archetype_scope_changes.png.

docs.title: 4. Junior vs senior content within each archetype
docs.text: For each (archetype, metric, period), computed the mid_senior − entry mean gap, then Δgap = gap_2026 − gap_2024. Negative Δgap ⇒ convergence.

docs.title: Credential stack depth gap — CONVERGING in all 10 large archetypes
docs.text:
Archetype gap_2024 gap_2026 Δ gap Data engineering +0.56 −0.31 −0.88 Java enterprise +0.42 −0.32 −0.74 AWS cloud SDE +0.08 −0.61 −0.68 JS frontend +0.33 −0.35 −0.68 Defense / cleared +0.70 +0.08 −0.62 LLM/GenAI +0.53 −0.07 −0.60 .NET / ASP.NET +0.19 −0.40 −0.59 Agile/Scrum generalist +0.80 +0.22 −0.58 Embedded / firmware +0.66 +0.30 −0.35 DevOps / SRE +0.06 −0.24 −0.30

In 7 of 10 large archetypes, the credential stack gap FLIPS SIGN — entry postings in 2026 stack MORE credential categories than mid-senior postings in the same archetype. This is a within-domain convergence finding that the T15 corpus-level null completely hides. The mechanism is consistent with entry postings accumulating years-experience + degree + AI/tool requirements together, while senior postings drop overt credential language.

docs.title: Requirement breadth gap
docs.text: 5 of 10 large archetypes converged (Defense, AWS cloud SDE, Embedded, Agile generalist, JS frontend); 4 diverged (DevOps, Data, Java, LLM/GenAI). LLM/GenAI DIVERGED the most (+1.4) — LLM/GenAI senior postings are asking for 1.6 more requirement categories than LLM/GenAI entry postings in 2026 vs only 0.2 more in 2024. This is the clearest case where the mid_senior/entry gap widened.

docs.title: tech_count gap
docs.text: Similar pattern. LLM/GenAI's gap widened +2.0, Data engineering's +1.1, DevOps +1.1. AWS cloud SDE's gap collapsed from +2.15 to −1.51 (junior now has more tech mentions than senior — artifact of very few junior rows in that archetype).

docs.title: Mentor gap — DIVERGING in every archetype
docs.text:
All 10 large archetypes show a WIDER mentor gap in 2026 than 2024 (senior postings adopted mentor language faster than entry postings). This is consistent with a "senior redefinition" reading — except that T29's authorship analysis shows mentor is among the style tokens that correlate with LLM authorship. Read with T29.

See exploration/tables/T28/step4_junior_senior_gap.csv and exploration/figures/T28/fig4_junior_senior_gap_change.png.

docs.title: 5. Senior mentoring shift by archetype
docs.text:
Mentor mentions rose substantially in mid_senior rows of all large archetypes (+4.3 to +24.3 pp). The top risers: Azure data platform (+24.3), AWS cloud SDE (+24.1), QA (+21.4), .NET (+20.2), Java enterprise (+16.2), LLM/GenAI (+16.2). The finding is broad, not LLM/GenAI-specific. Table: exploration/tables/T28/step5_mentoring_by_domain.csv.

Caveat. T29's correlation analysis shows mentor mentions correlate positively with LLM-authorship score (corr via intermediate metrics, see T29 section 6). A recruiter LLM template that includes "we mentor junior engineers" can produce this rise without any real senior-role change.

docs.title: Period profile
docs.text: Metric 2024 2026 n 1,059 2,559 seniority_final entry rate 2.2% 7.4% YOE ≤ 2 rate 8.8% 11.6% Mean cleaned length (chars) 2,054 2,664 Mean tech count 4.6 6.7 Mean requirement breadth 5.6 6.6 any-AI broad rate 41.7% 85.9% any-AI narrow rate 58.7% 87.5% (approx, see corr matrix) Mean mentor rate 18.6% 25.4% Credential stack (this rebuild) 1.67 1.18 Aggregator rate 13.4% 12.6%

docs.title: Companies: new entrants or existing pivot?
docs.text:
Metric value Companies in 2024 LLM/GenAI cluster 616 Companies in 2026 LLM/GenAI cluster 1,174 Shared companies (both periods) 138 New companies in 2026 only 1,036 Companies only in 2024 478 % of 2026 LLM/GenAI volume from new-in-2026 companies 68.2%

The LLM/GenAI growth is driven by NEW entrants, not existing-employer pivoting. Only 138 companies appear in both periods' LLM/GenAI cluster. 68% of 2026 LLM/GenAI volume comes from companies that did NOT post an LLM/GenAI-cluster role in 2024.

This is consistent with T08's earlier TikTok/ByteDance intern-pipeline finding, and extends it: the LLM/GenAI archetype is structurally different from the other archetypes because its employer base is largely fresh. The T16 overlap panel data was not available at run time, but this test can be run again on the panel once T16 publishes.

docs.title: Top 2026 employers
docs.text:
See exploration/tables/T28/step6_llm_genai_top_employers.csv. The top 2026 LLM/GenAI employers include Anthropic, Microsoft AI, Intel, Intuit, Alignerr, Honeywell, Harvey, Cognizant, LinkedIn, Walmart. Many of these are NOT in the 2024 top list.

See exploration/figures/T28/fig5_llm_genai_profile.png.

docs.title: (a) Aggregator exclusion
docs.text:
Effect on aggregate metric deltas when excluding is_aggregator=true rows:

| Metric | Include agg Δ | Exclude agg Δ | |Δrel| | |---|---|---|---| | requirement_breadth | +0.91 | +1.06 | +16% | | tech_count | +1.36 | +1.47 | +8% | | any_ai_broad | +0.195 | +0.203 | +4% | | any_mentor | +0.054 | +0.082 | +53%† | | credential_stack | −0.52 | −0.41 | −21% |

† Mentor delta is materially sensitive — excluding aggregators increases the mentor rise by ~53%. This means aggregators were actually flat on mentor while non-aggregators rose. Aggregators appear to use different (boilerplate) language that does not include mentoring specifically.

All direction-preserving; magnitudes within tolerance except mentor. See exploration/tables/T28/sensitivity_aggregator.csv.

docs.title: (b) Company capping
docs.text: Not applied to entry-share decomposition (per-row rate). For within-archetype term analyses (scope / AI mention), the 50-posting cap is recommended by the T06 concentration prediction table. This rerun did not re-cap; the LLM/GenAI deep dive is particularly exposed to a few large employers (Alignerr n=52 in 2026, Walmart n=69, Microsoft n=100 across clusters). The direction of the LLM/GenAI findings is strong enough that capping is expected to preserve them, but re-running capped is a Wave-3.5 to-do.

docs.title: (c) Seniority operationalization
docs.text: Already primary — both seniority_final and YOE ≤ 2 reported side by side throughout Step 2.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Credential stack depth gap closes in 10/10 large archetypes and flips in 7/10. The T15 corpus-level null on convergence masks a clean within-domain convergence on this specific metric. Entry postings in 2026 are stacking more credentials than mid-senior postings in the same archetype. This is a genuine, novel RQ2-adjacent finding.

  2. AI vocabulary has spread into traditionally non-AI archetypes. JS frontend +14.7 pp broad AI, .NET +13.6, Java +11.6. This is not an ML/AI-archetype story; this is broad AI-layer-stacking across every SWE domain. T14's network analysis found it structurally; T28 locates it within archetypes.

  3. LLM/GenAI growth is 68% from brand-new employers. The cluster is not formed by existing companies pivoting into AI — it's formed by a wave of new employers entering the posting market. Only 138 companies appear in both 2024 and 2026 LLM/GenAI clusters.

  4. Mentor rise is broad across ALL large archetypes (+10 to +24 pp), not concentrated in LLM/GenAI. Combined with T29's correlation evidence, this points to mentoring language being downstream of LLM drafting style, not senior-role change.

  5. Requirement breadth diverges (junior vs senior) in LLM/GenAI but converges in older archetypes. The structural "senior pulling away" story holds specifically in LLM/GenAI, not as a general corpus pattern. If the paper wants to say "ML/AI seniors are becoming harder to match against juniors," this is the evidence.

  6. Credential stack depth fell aggregate in this rebuild. My pattern set is narrower than T11's and may miss pattern types; Gate 2's "credential_stack rose" headline is not reproduced under this rebuild. This is a pattern-dependence flag that T22 (scope pattern validation) should resolve.

  7. The seniority_final vs yoe_le2 uniform-rise disagreement is minimal. Both produce the same qualitative "every archetype rises" story. Gate 2's Alt A reading depended on the rise being an artifact of LLM label drift. Since the rise holds under YOE (which doesn't use LLM labels for its entry definition), Alt A has to propose a mechanism that affects both classifiers — e.g., LinkedIn-side posting behavior or employer-mix shift inside archetypes. The label-drift story alone is no longer sufficient.

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
• T22 (scope / credential pattern validation): my credential_stack_depth direction disagrees with Gate 2's T11 headline. T22 should rebuild the credential pattern and re-test the direction. If T11's was correct, my pattern is under-covering; if T28's is correct, the Gate 2 headline needs revision.
  • Downstream analysis: LLM/GenAI's within-archetype junior/senior divergence on requirement_breadth and tech_count is a high-priority follow-up — why is this archetype showing the opposite of the corpus pattern? Hypothesis: LLM/GenAI senior postings are long-tail research roles (higher req breadth), while LLM/GenAI entry postings are prompt-engineering / fine-tuning internships. T08's TikTok/ByteDance entry concentration supports this.
  • Paper framing: rephrase Gate 2's "uniform within-archetype rise is a signature of measurement change" to "uniform within-archetype rise survives YOE proxy, so it's either a broad category-wide shift OR a posting-side instrument change that affects both classifiers equally." The mechanism is no longer specifically about Stage 10 LLM label drift.
  • Overlap panel: once T16 publishes the overlap panel, re-run Step 6 (entrants-vs-pivoters) on the panel and test whether the LLM/GenAI pattern holds within companies that posted in both periods.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• Scraped 2026 archetype coverage is only 30.5% — the 2026 side of every archetype cross-tab is non-random (biased toward the kind of 2026 postings that passed the Stage-9 LLM text cleaning filter).
  • The pattern sets here are mine, not T11's. Pattern drift may explain small magnitude differences with Gate 2 headlines.
  • Archetype = −2 ("no text / raw-only") is 69.9% of the 2026 sample and 1.7% of the 2024 sample. My step 2 decomposition excludes these rows. An alternative that assigns them to their own bucket would give a slightly different decomposition but would not flip the within-vs-between conclusion because the unlabeled rows pass through both the 2024 and 2026 denominators.
  • Mentor pattern is mentor|mentoring|mentorship — simpler than the T11 strict management pattern. It is consistent with Gate 2 Correction 2 ("only mentor is clean").

docs.title: T29 — LLM-authored description detection
docs.text: Date: 2026-04-15 Agent: Wave 3 O (promoted from exploratory to primary) Input: data/unified.parquet + exploration/artifacts/shared/swe_cleaned_text.parquet Primary sample: 34,161 SWE LinkedIn rows with llm_extraction_coverage='labeled'

docs.title: Verdict — supported
docs.text:
The LLM-authorship signals discriminate 2024 from 2026 strongly, and the unifying-mechanism test shows that the Wave-2 length growth and requirement_breadth rise are largely mediated by authorship style, while the AI mention explosion is NOT. The paper's Gate 2 headlines split cleanly along this line: tool-stack restructuring (RQ3 headline) is independent of authorship and holds as a real labor-market signal; length growth, requirement-breadth rise, and mentoring shift are partially or mostly downstream of recruiter LLM drafting.

Headline numbers:

  • Authorship-score median shifts +0.33 std units from 2024 (−0.139) to 2026 (+0.192) — a large effect.
  • 88.7% of 2026 postings score above the 2024 median, and only 3.9% of 2026 postings score below 2024 p25 — by 2026, human-style drafting is the minority behavior.
  • Style-matched cross-period delta (sampling 2024 rows whose authorship score matches each 2026 row's nearest-neighbor score): the any_ai_broad delta attenuates only 2.1%, the any_ai_narrow delta attenuates 0%, but the char_len delta FLIPS sign (+377 chars full → −411 chars matched) and the requirement_breadth delta attenuates 62% (+0.92 full → +0.35 matched).

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
• Built a per-posting authorship score from seven style features on cleaned text:
  • LLM tell density per 1K chars — 42 classic LLM vocabulary tokens (delve, tapestry, leverage, robust, unleash, embark, navigate, cutting-edge, comprehensive, seamless, furthermore, notably, pivotal, harness, dynamic, vibrant, intricate, meticulous, plethora, foster, multifaceted, underscore, paramount, testament, realm, bespoke, empower, orchestrate, spearhead, transformative, synergy, holistic, etc.). Deliberately excluded AI-domain tokens (ai, ml, llm, neural, model) so AI-topic postings are not labeled as LLM-authored by content.
  • Em-dash density (U+2014, U+2013, and " -- ") per 1K chars.
  • Sentence-length mean and SD (words/sentence).
  • Type-token ratio (vocabulary diversity).
  • Bullet density per 1K chars.
  • Paragraph length mean.
  • Z-scored each feature on the pooled sample, applied LLM-directional signs (+1 for tell density, em-dash, sent_mean, bullet, para_mean; −1 for sent_std and type-token ratio), and averaged.
  • Primary text column: description_core_llm (filtered to llm_extraction_coverage='labeled'). n=34,161 (2024=21,680, 2026=12,481).
  • Sensitivity: re-ran on raw description text for 15k sampled rows.

Script: exploration/scripts/T29_authorship_detection.py and T29_matched_analysis.py.

docs.title: 1. Per-feature distribution shifts
docs.text:
Feature 2024 median 2026 median Direction Authorship score −0.139 +0.192 +0.33 std LLM tell density (per 1K) 0.00 (mean 0.24) 0.00 (mean 0.34) Mean +41% Em-dash density (per 1K) 0.00 (mean 0.094) 0.00 (mean 0.187) Mean +98% Mean sentence length (words) 40 38 Slightly shorter Sentence length std 28.3 29.5 Slightly longer tail Type-token ratio 0.599 0.589 Slightly less diverse Bullet density (per 1K) 0.40 6.33 ~16× increase Mean paragraph length (chars) 2,234 2,612 Longer paragraphs

The bullet density feature dominates. This reflects a format change: 2024 Kaggle text is unformatted (HTML-stripped), 2026 scraped text preserves markdown bullet points. This is the "Kaggle vs scraped instrument" composition confound flagged in the analytical preamble. I flag this explicitly: the bullet-density component is part LLM style, part instrument difference. The raw-text sensitivity below helps bound how much of the shift is purely format.

See exploration/figures/T29/fig1_score_distribution.png and fig2_per_feature_shift.png.

docs.title: 2. Distribution by period
docs.text:
Period n median p25 p75 std IQR 2024 21,680 −0.139 −0.331 +0.065 0.331 0.396 2026 12,481 +0.192 +0.008 +0.393 0.339 0.385

Share of 2026 above 2024 median: 88.7%. Share of 2026 below 2024 p25: 3.9%.

Variance does NOT drop between periods (std 0.331 → 0.339, IQR 0.396 → 0.385). 2026 is not more uniform in raw authorship score — rather, the entire distribution shifted upward without compressing. This is a notable finding: the homogenization story from T15 is not about all postings collapsing to one style. Instead, the median posting moved into a more LLM-styled regime while the spread stayed similar.

However, the within-score-decile analysis (see section 7) shows that WITHIN each style class, 2026 postings are more homogeneous in content metrics like char_len and requirement_breadth than 2024 postings were. So the T15 homogenization may be partially a style-class shift and partially a within-style-class compression.

See exploration/tables/T29/period_stats.csv.

docs.title: 3. Distribution by company
docs.text: Companies with ≥10 postings in a period.

docs.title: Top 2026 LLM-score companies (recruiter tooling adoption signals)
docs.text:
Company n score_mean Alignerr 52 0.826 BlackRock 13 0.627 Sigma 10 0.555 Walmart 69 0.505 LinkedIn 17 0.499 Intuit 31 0.467 Klaviyo 12 0.466 Intel 10 0.455 Ford Motor Company 15 0.454 Rippling 17 0.439 Microsoft AI 23 0.417 Cognizant 18 0.399 Harvey 10 0.397 Anthropic 40 0.389 Microsoft 100 0.378

Notable: Anthropic, Microsoft AI, Intel, Harvey, LinkedIn, Intuit — high-velocity tech hirers — all score as high-LLM-authorship 2026. Also Cognizant (a consultancy with known bulk posting) and Alignerr (AI data labeler) score highest.

docs.title: Top 2024 LLM-score companies (early adopters)
docs.text:
Company n score_mean Verticalmove, Inc 10 0.302 Rose International 10 0.215 Visa 15 0.206 Astrion 10 0.197 Pinnacle Group, Inc. 14 0.187 Shield AI 12 0.185 Walmart 163 0.097

Walmart is already high-score in 2024 (+0.097) and rises further in 2026 (+0.505). This is a 2024-LLM-adoption signal — some companies were templating before ChatGPT became ubiquitous.

See exploration/tables/T29/top_llm_companies_2024.csv, top_llm_companies_2026.csv, low_llm_companies_2026.csv, and exploration/figures/T29/fig5_company_examples.png.

docs.title: 4. Correlation with Wave 2 findings
docs.text:
Per-posting correlation matrix (Pearson):

authorship tell emdash char_len req_breadth tech_count cred_stack ai_narrow ai_broad scope_density authorship_score 1.00 0.42 0.38 0.59 0.33 0.19 0.09 0.25 0.21 0.12 char_len 0.59 0.07 −0.03 1.00 0.43 0.19 0.31 0.12 0.11 0.02 requirement_breadth 0.33 0.15 −0.03 0.43 1.00 0.69 0.17 0.18 0.13 0.10 tech_count 0.19 0.11 0.01 0.19 0.69 1.00 −0.02 0.16 0.23 0.04 any_ai_broad 0.21 0.11 0.07 0.11 0.13 0.23 −0.08 0.50 1.00 0.11 credential_stack_depth 0.09 0.00 −0.10 0.31 0.17 −0.02 1.00 −0.08 −0.08 −0.07
  • char_len ↔ authorship_score: r=0.59. The strongest correlation. Length and LLM style are tightly coupled.
  • requirement_breadth ↔ authorship_score: r=0.33. Substantial but not overwhelming.
  • any_ai_broad ↔ authorship_score: r=0.21. Weaker — AI mentions are partly independent of authorship style.
  • any_ai_narrow ↔ authorship_score: r=0.25. Similar.
  • credential_stack_depth ↔ authorship_score: r=0.09. Essentially uncorrelated — credential language is not LLM-style driven.

See exploration/tables/T29/correlation_matrix.csv and exploration/figures/T29/fig3_correlation_heatmap.png.

docs.title: Naive subset (2024 p25 cutoff = −0.331)
docs.text: Only 485 of 12,481 2026 postings fall below the 2024 p25 threshold. On this thin and likely-weird subsample, char_len delta flips (−277 chars vs +377 full), requirement_breadth flips (−0.55 vs +0.92), and tech_count attenuates 88% (+0.16 vs +1.36). AI mention rates still rise (+0.10 vs +0.19, 44% attenuation). But the 485-row subsample is atypical.

docs.title: Style-matched delta (sampled nearest-neighbor pairs)
docs.text:
For each 2026 row, find the 2024 row with the nearest authorship score. Compute metric delta on matched pairs.

Metric Full delta Matched delta Attenuation clean_len +377 −411 209% (flip) requirement_breadth +0.92 +0.35 62% tech_count +1.36 +1.04 23% credential_stack_depth −0.51 −0.76 −49% (strengthens) any_ai_broad +0.195 +0.190 2% any_ai_narrow +0.367 +0.370 0% scope_density +0.078 +0.065 17%

docs.title: Within-decile delta (pooled score deciles, pure within-decile comparison)
docs.text:
Metric Overall Δ Bottom-3 deciles Δ Top-3 deciles Δ clean_len −424 −373 −305 requirement_breadth −0.01 −0.59 +0.62 tech_count +0.59 −0.08 +1.42 any_ai_broad +0.159 +0.128 +0.226 scope_density +0.053 +0.037 +0.071

Reading both tests together:

  1. AI mention explosion (any_ai_broad, any_ai_narrow) is real and independent of authorship style. Matched delta within 2% of full delta. Within-decile, the AI rate rises in every decile, even the bottom (+0.09 pp). The T14 AI-tools-network finding and the RQ3 headline survive.

  2. Length growth is almost entirely LLM-authorship-mediated. Within same style class, 2026 postings are shorter, not longer. The corpus-level length growth is purely a composition shift toward higher-style deciles. This is the most striking unifying-mechanism result.

  3. Requirement breadth rise is partially LLM-authorship-mediated (~62% attenuation). About 1/3 of the breadth rise is real within-style; the rest is style migration.

  4. Tech count rise is mostly within-style (23% attenuation). Within a given style decile, tech count still rises ~1.04 on matched pairs — consistent with the structural tech-network finding from T14.

  5. Credential stack depth FALL is robust and even strengthens under matched analysis. Independent of style.

  6. Mentoring rise is correlated with LLM style but not directly tested in the matched analysis. Correlation pattern suggests it is at least partially style-mediated.

See exploration/tables/T29/unifying_mechanism_test.csv, matched_decile_analysis.csv, style_matched_delta.csv, and exploration/figures/T29/fig4_unifying_mechanism.png.

docs.title: 6. Sensitivity: raw-text re-run
docs.text:
Re-computed all features on raw description (15k sampled rows).

period n score_mean score_median score_std 2024 5,296 −0.047 −0.055 0.354 2026 9,704 +0.025 +0.016 0.272

On raw text, the shift is smaller (+0.07 median) but still present and positive. The cleaned-text magnitude (+0.33 median) overstates the style shift because cleaned LLM text is itself more formatted than raw. The direction is stable; the magnitude is inflated by the text-source composition.

Interpretation: roughly half of the apparent "authorship shift" on cleaned text is the text-source instrument change (Kaggle HTML-stripped vs scraped-markdown, plus LLM-section-classifier formatting). The remaining half (the +0.07 raw-text shift) is a genuine LLM-authorship signal. The qualitative conclusion survives; the magnitude is halved.

See exploration/tables/T29/sensitivity_raw_text.csv.

docs.title: 7. Sensitivity: aggregator exclusion
docs.text:
Excluding is_aggregator=true barely changes the median or std in either period. The authorship shift is not driven by aggregators.

period include_agg n score_median score_std 2024 true 21,680 −0.139 0.331 2024 false 18,578 −0.139 0.334 2026 true 12,481 +0.192 0.339 2026 false 10,343 +0.211 0.327

docs.title: 8. Homogenization re-test on low-LLM subset
docs.text:
T15 reported "2026 postings are more internally homogeneous than 2024" based on average within-group pairwise cosine. Is that homogenization downstream of recruiter tooling?

On matched within-decile comparison:

Metric (std) 2024 2026 Δ std requirement_breadth (across all deciles) 2.67 2.57 −0.10 tech_count 4.26 4.76 +0.50 clean_len 1246 1266 +20

WITHIN each style decile, 2026 shows lower std for requirement_breadth and dramatically lower std for char_len in the top deciles (top decile char_len std drops 681 chars). The homogenization is partially within-decile (content compression inside a style class) and partially between-decile (everyone moving to similar style classes).

Reading: part of T15's homogenization is style migration (people using LLMs write more uniformly) and part is within-style content compression (postings are converging on similar content within the same style). Both point at template-driven drafting.

See exploration/tables/T29/homogeneity_test.csv.

docs.title: Surprises & unexpected patterns
docs.text:
1. Anthropic scores 0.389 — its own postings are highly LLM-styled. Strong face validity for the signal.

  2. Walmart scored +0.097 in 2024 — higher than most companies. Walmart was templating before the ChatGPT era; its score rises to +0.505 in 2026. Some companies were early.

  3. The length growth delta completely flips direction under style matching (+377 full → −411 matched). Within same style class, 2026 postings are shorter. This is the single most striking unifying-mechanism result — the entire Gate 2 length-growth finding is a style migration artifact, not a content expansion.

  4. AI mention rates rise even in the lowest style decile (+0.09 pp any_ai_broad in bottom decile). AI vocabulary is not an LLM-authorship tell — it's a real content signal that survives every subset restriction.

  5. credential_stack_depth fell AND is uncorrelated with authorship style (r=0.09). This confirms it's an independent content signal, and contradicts the Gate 2 T11 "credential stack rose" headline. T22 should resolve the pattern-set disagreement.

  6. Variance of authorship score does NOT drop from 2024 to 2026 (0.331 → 0.339). The entire distribution shifts upward without compressing. Homogenization is about the median, not the spread.

  7. Matched requirement_breadth delta (+0.35) is still positive after style matching, meaning roughly 1/3 of the T11 breadth rise is genuine within-style content signal. The paper should claim this narrow residual, not the full +0.92.

  8. Bullet density is the strongest feature (16× rise) but also the most instrument-confounded. Excluding bullet density from the composite score would reduce the +0.33 shift to roughly +0.16 (estimated — not fully re-run). The raw-text sensitivity puts this at ~+0.07 median, suggesting bullet density inflates the cleaned-text signal substantially.

docs.title: Verdict breakdown per Gate 2 headline
docs.text: Headline Survives low-LLM / matched? Attenuation Verdict AI tool/framework explosion (narrow + broad) Yes 0-2% Real content signal Tech network modularity rise (implied by tech_count) Mostly 23% Real with modest style component Length growth in responsibilities/role_summary No — flips 209% Style-mediated artifact Requirement breadth rise Partial 62% 1/3 real, 2/3 style Credential stack depth Robust (T28 rebuild shows fall, not rise) — Pattern-dependent Within-group homogenization Partial — Part style-migration, part within-style compression Mentor +55% Not directly tested — Correlation suggests style-mediated

docs.title: Action items
docs.text:
• Paper framing: the RQ3 AI-requirement explosion headline is now even stronger — it survives every sensitivity including style matching. The length-growth and requirement-breadth rise must be rephrased: "2026 postings adopted a more LLM-stylized drafting voice which is longer and lists more requirement categories; the within-style residual on requirement breadth is +0.35 (real), and on length is negative (style migration entirely)."
  • Mechanism claim: the Gate 2 "template homogenization" reading is supported with a specific mechanism — recruiter LLM drafting. The T29 authorship score should be offered as a per-posting control variable in any regression-style analysis.
  • T28 re-read: the T28 credential_stack_depth fall, which disagreed with Gate 2, is corroborated by T29's low correlation between credential_stack and authorship style. Both point at the credential stack depth as a separate content signal that may genuinely be falling.
  • Interview probe (RQ4): per Gate 2's recommendation, the T29 result formalizes the interview question — "Did you write this posting, or did a tool draft it?" Given that 88.7% of 2026 postings score above the 2024 median, most senior-engineer interview respondents in 2026 will be describing postings they did not personally write.
  • T24 synthesis input: T29 provides the causal mechanism linking T15 homogenization to T11 length growth. These findings should be unified in the Wave 4 T24 synthesis.

docs.title: Data quality caveats
docs.text:
• Text-source confound is the biggest threat to the cleaned-text estimates. Bullet density is part format, part LLM style. The raw-text sensitivity shows the true magnitude is roughly half the cleaned-text magnitude. I report both and recommend citing the raw-text version in any paper claims ("+0.07 median on raw text" vs "+0.33 on cleaned text").
  • The per-feature signs on type_token_ratio and sent_std_words are weak-discriminator. Dropping them would not materially change the composite score, because tell_density, emdash_density, bullet_density, and para_mean_chars dominate.
  • No pre-2023 reference corpus. The signals are corpus-internal; without a known-human baseline we cannot calibrate absolute discrimination accuracy. The within-corpus shifts are strong enough to report without this calibration, but they are not classifier-grade outputs.
  • Feature reliance on sentence segmentation is approximate. The regex [.!?]+ fails on abbreviations like "U.S." and "etc.". This is acceptable for population-level means.
  • Only the clean-text sample of 34,161 rows enters the primary analysis. The 29,540 raw-only rows (mostly scraped 2026) cannot be scored on the primary pipeline. Since 2026 is already heavily reduced on cleaned-text coverage, the 2026 side is a non-random sample (biased toward the kind of scraped postings that passed Stage-9 LLM cleaning). This is the same caveat as T28.

docs.title: V1 — Gate 2 Adversarial Verification
docs.text: Agent: V1 (verification, adversarial) Date: 2026-04-15 Inputs: data/unified.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/* Scripts: exploration/scripts/V1_*.py Tables: exploration/tables/V1/V1_*.csv Frame: SWE LinkedIn, is_english=true AND date_flag='ok'. 22,820 rows 2024 / 40,881 rows 2026.

docs.title: Summary verdict
docs.text: Gate 2 clears with three specific corrections and one keyword-validation failure. The three rock-solid headlines (AI explosion, tech modularity, length-in-responsibilities-not-requirements) all reproduce independently and all four alternative explanations fail under my tests. The junior-share flip on arshkon-only also holds under the most aggressive denominator sensitivity I could apply. The two problems are: (i) a conflated SNR citation in the Gate 2 memo ("5.15% → 28.63%, SNR 925"), and (ii) the T11 strict-management precision audit is a tautology — on a genuine people-management-interpretation precision audit, strict management also fails the 80% threshold. Scope-density is a mixed signal — end_to_end and cross_functional are clean patterns, but ownership fails with 58% precision and is partially instrument-driven.

docs.title: 1. Re-derivation pass/fail table
docs.text:
Independent re-derivation (my SQL/Python, no code reuse from prior agents).

# Finding Gate 2 number V1 re-derived Verdict 1a claude_tool SNR 326 326.0 (0.013% → 3.37%) PASS exact match 1b copilot SNR 44 44.3 (0.061% → 3.77%) PASS 1c langchain SNR 36 36.4 (0.105% → 3.10%) PASS 1d agents_framework SNR 140 139.8 (0.61% → 12.70%) PASS 1e embedding SNR 123 123.4 (0.15% → 2.82%) PASS 2 Aggregate AI-any rise 5.15% → 28.63% (+23.5 pp) 5.01% → 28.57% (+23.56 pp) PASS (within 0.1 pp) 2* Aggregate AI SNR "SNR 925" (Gate 2) SNR 13.3 on the same 5.01%/28.57% cells FAIL — Gate 2 cross-wires two operationalizations. See §1a. 3a T13 responsibilities Δ +196 chars (52% of growth) +163 chars (43%) on 2k/period sample PASS (direction holds, magnitude slightly smaller) 3b T13 role_summary Δ +139 (37%) +132 (35%) PASS 3c T13 requirements Δ −2 (flat) −17 (−4.4%) PASS (strengthens) — requirements slightly fell 3d T13 preferred Δ +111 (29%) +117 (31%) PASS 4 Within-archetype uniform junior rise "rose in every archetype" See §4 PARTIAL PASS — holds for all 7 large archetypes cited by name in Gate 2; fails for small archetype 15 (GPU/CUDA, 4.7% → 3.4% → 3.4%) 5 arshkon-only entry-share flip (of known) 7.72% → 6.70% (−1.0 pp) 7.72% → 6.70% (−1.02 pp) PASS exact

Re-derivation pass rate: 5 out of 5 headline targets (the one apparent failure — aggregate AI SNR 925 — is a citation conflation in Gate 2 rather than a computation error, see §1a).

docs.title: 1a. The "SNR 925" citation conflation
docs.text:
Gate 2 §What we learned, bullet 3 writes: AI-any | 5.15% → 28.63% | +23.5 | **925** (Gate 1, reconfirmed).

On independent re-derivation this citation is internally inconsistent:

  • The 5.15% → 28.63% cells are T14's ai_any broad-union definition (union of llm, rag, agents_framework, copilot, claude_api, openai_api, prompt_engineering, fine_tuning, mcp, embedding, transformer_arch, machine_learning, deep_learning, pytorch, tensorflow, langchain, langgraph, chatgpt, claude_tool, cursor_tool, gemini_tool, codex_tool, nlp, huggingface — 24 tech columns).
  • The "SNR 925" figure comes from T05's ai_mention_rate which is a narrower LIKE-pattern: copilot | cursor | llm | claude | chatgpt | gpt-4 on the raw description (2.81% → 18.78%, within-2024 delta 0.0002).
  • Under T14's broad union, V1 independently gets within-2024 = 0.01766 and cross-period = 0.2356, so SNR = 13.3, not 925.
  • Under T05's narrow LIKE, V1 independently gets within-2024 = 0.0002 and cross-period = 0.1597, so SNR ≈ 798-925 (depending on rounding).

The two operationalizations are both defensible, but they cannot be combined in a single table cell. The Gate 2 memo as written attaches the narrow-pattern SNR to the broad-union prevalence cells, which overstates the SNR attached to the 23.5 pp rise by roughly 70×.

What to report instead:

Operationalization 2024 rate 2026 rate Δ pp within-2024 SNR T05 narrow (copilot\|cursor\|llm\|claude\|chatgpt\|gpt-4) 2.81% 18.78% +16.0 0.0002 ~925 T14 broad union (24 AI techs) 5.01% 28.57% +23.56 0.0177 13.3

Both are above-threshold. The narrow metric has the extreme SNR because the denominator (within-2024 noise) is nearly zero; the broad metric has a larger absolute effect but is diluted by the traditional ML subset (pytorch/tensorflow/machine_learning) which has real within-2024 drift between arshkon and asaniczka.

This is a citation issue, not a finding issue. The lead claim "AI explosion" is even more robust than Gate 2 reports — it passes both operationalizations with SNR ≥ 13, which is still >1 order of magnitude above the next-strongest cross-period signal.

docs.title: 1b. Target 4: within-archetype junior share rise (with ID→name mapping)
docs.text:
ID Archetype 2024 2026-03 2026-04 Gate 2 cites Match? 0 LLM/GenAI/ML 3.72% 17.17% 8.52% 3.7 → 8.5 EXACT 1 Defense & cleared 2.98% 8.08% 9.09% (not cited by Gate 2) — 2 DevOps/SRE 1.65% 4.44% 4.98% 1.6 → 5.0 EXACT 3 JS frontend 2.67% 10.43% 9.81% 2.7 → 9.8 EXACT 4 Java enterprise 3.70% 7.27% 4.06% 3.7 → 4.1 EXACT 5 Data engineering 3.26% 8.92% 4.20% 3.3 → 4.2 EXACT 6 Embedded/firmware 2.35% 8.86% 7.69% 2.4 → 7.7 EXACT 7 .NET/ASP.NET 2.62% 12.35% 8.91% 2.6 → 8.9 EXACT 15 GPU/CUDA 4.69% 3.45% 3.45% "every archetype" FALSE — GPU/CUDA FELL 16 Python web backend 4.85% 3.12% 7.69% "every archetype" mixed (2026-03 below 2024) 12 Azure data platform 3.00% 8.00% 0.00% "every archetype" mixed (2026-04 is 0%)

Verdict: The Gate 2 narrative "junior share rose in every archetype" is overstated. Among the 7 large archetypes Gate 2 cites by name, every one rose and the numbers are exact matches. But for smaller archetypes GPU/CUDA (n_known 64 → 29 → 29) saw junior share FALL 4.69% → 3.45%. This is a minor qualification — the claim is defensible if restricted to the ~7 large archetypes with adequate sample size, but "every archetype" is literally false.

For Wave 3, the correct phrasing is "every archetype with n_known ≥ 100 showed a junior-share rise" (or similar).

docs.title: 2a. T14 agents_framework regex — more FP in 2024 than T14 reported
docs.text:
Pattern: (?:^|\s)(agent|agents|agentic)(?:$|\s) — sampled 30 rows per period. My gradings (based on semantic AI-vs-non-AI interpretation):

Period n AI-agent TP Non-AI (change/security/real-estate/call-center/travel/staffing) Precision 2024 30 ~3 27 (change agents, trusted agents, phone agents, travel agent, RMM agents, insurance agents) ~10% 2026 30 ~27 3 (legal "agents & contracted partners", change agent, insurance agents) ~90%

T14 reported: 2024 ~30% AI (70% FP), 2026 ~80% AI (20% FP), adjusted true rise ~+10-11 pp.

V1 finds 2024 precision lower than T14 stated (~10% vs 30%). This strengthens the AI-explosion finding: the true 2024 AI-agent rate is ~10% × 0.6135% = 0.06%, and the true cross-period rise is 0.06% → ~90% × 12.70% = 11.43%. So the true pp rise is ~+11.4 pp, close to T14's conservative estimate. Directional robustness holds.

Verdict: The T14 agents_framework caveat is correct in spirit; the magnitude adjustment should be slightly larger (true rise ~+11 pp not ~+12 pp). This does not affect the per-tool SNR ranking or the community finding. PASS (with tightened caveat).

docs.title: 2b. T11 strict management pattern — FAILS ≥80% precision on a semantic audit
docs.text:
Pattern: manag(e|es|ed|ing) | mentor* | coach* | hir(e|es|ed|ing) | direct reports | performance review | headcount. Sampled 25 rows per period. My semantic grading:

Period n People-mgmt TP Ambiguous (project/task mgmt) Corporate/legal boilerplate FP (e.g., "we're hiring", "date of hire", "E-Verify hiring practices", "IoT sensors to manage", "employee-owned") Precision 2024 25 9 4 12 36% (TP only) / 52% (TP+ambiguous) 2026 25 10 2 13 40% (TP only) / 48% (TP+ambiguous) Combined 50 50 19 6 25 38% (TP only) / 50% (TP+ambiguous)

T11 reported: strict precision 100%. This is a tautology: the T11 "audit" simply checked whether a row in the strict-pattern sample actually contains a strict-pattern keyword (yes, by construction). It did not grade whether the match has the meaning of "people management".

On a real semantic audit, strict management fails the 80% precision threshold. The biggest contaminants are:

  1. hire / hiring matches company boilerplate: "we're hiring", "date of hire", "uses E-Verify in its hiring practices", "hiring process", "all persons hired will be required to verify identity". These are almost always HR/legal language, not people management.
  2. manage matches task/product language: "manage web development", "manage the electric motors", "manage a fleet of IoT sensors", "manage the full life cycle of business opportunities".
  3. coach matches "Career Coach" as a benefit.
  4. mentor is the cleanest sub-pattern — it fires mostly on genuine mentoring references ("mentor engineers", "mentor junior engineers").

Implication for Gate 2 claims:

  • The narrower "mentor +55% / manage −47%" finding that Gate 2 flagged as "moderate" should be elevated — mentor is the clean sub-pattern and its cross-period rise is not contaminated.
  • The "management rate rose" is doubly contradicted: broad fails precision (T11), strict fails precision (V1, this audit), AND strict fails SNR (0.35).
  • A clean replacement pattern for "people management" should include mentor|mentor(ship|ing)|people manager|team lead|technical lead|engineering manager|manager|head of engineering|direct reports|1:1|one-on-one|hire and retain|hire and mentor|headcount and exclude hire / hiring as standalone (too noisy).

docs.title: 2c. T11 scope_density — mixed: two clean patterns, one dirty pattern
docs.text:
Sampled 25 per period × 3 top scope patterns = 150 rows total.

Pattern 2024 precision 2026 precision Combined Verdict ownership/own/owning/owned/owns ~36% (9/25) ~80% (20/25) 58% FAILS 80% end-to-end (incl. "end to end") ~96% (24/25) ~96% (24/25) 96% PASSES cross-functional (incl. "cross functional") ~100% (25/25) ~100% (25/25) 100% PASSES

Verdict on the +85% scope_density claim: Two of the top-three scope patterns are clean and show genuine cross-period rise. The third (ownership) has:

  1. Precision inflating between periods (36% → 80%), which means part of the "rise" in that sub-pattern is an instrument effect, not a content effect. 2024's contamination set is heavy with corporate boilerplate ("employee-owned company", "total cost of ownership", "on your own terms", "minority-owned", "diversity-owned", "wholly owned subsidiary"); 2026's corporate-boilerplate rate looks lower.
  2. 2026 postings genuinely say "own the delivery", "own end-to-end", "take ownership of complex problems" more often — this is real scope language, not purely an artifact.

Net effect: the +85% scope_density rise is directionally real but the magnitude is likely inflated by the ownership sub-pattern. A cleaner composite using only end-to-end + cross-functional + stakeholder + autonom* + initiative + high-impact (dropping the ownership glob) would give a smaller but defensible rise. Wave 3 T22 should tighten the ownership sub-pattern to require a noun-phrase following (e.g., own(s|ed|ing)?\s+(?:the|end-to-end|delivery|development|design|implementation)) or drop it entirely.

docs.title: 2d. T12 agentic distinct-company claim — verified and stronger
docs.text:
T12 reports 495 distinct 2026 companies mention agentic. T12's number came from the LLM-cleaned text subset (llm_extraction_coverage='labeled'); V1 reproduces 503 on that same subset.

On the full scraped corpus (raw description, no LLM subset filter): V1 finds 3,164 matches across 1,351 distinct companies — nearly three times T12's reported figure. The agentic term is spreading across an even broader employer base than T12 reported.

Precision spot-check (20 matches, 2026): 20/20 (100%) — every single match references AI/LLM agentic systems, workflows, or platforms. The pattern is clean.

Verdict: T12 finding holds and strengthens.

docs.title: Alt 1 — AI tool/framework explosion driven by top-10 AI companies?
docs.text:
Ruled out. Top 10 AI-mentioning 2026 companies: Jobs via Dice, Google, Microsoft, JPMorgan, AWS, OpenAI, Amazon, Wells Fargo, Uber, Capital One.

Dropping all 10 of these companies from the entire frame:

  • Primary aggregate AI-any: 5.01% → 28.57% (+23.56 pp, 5.70×)
  • Drop-top-10: 4.92% → 27.84% (+22.92 pp, 5.66×)

The 5.7× rise survives the exclusion essentially unchanged. The AI explosion is broad-based, not concentrated in a handful of AI-first employers. Alt 1 is dead.

docs.title: Alt 2 — Modularity rise sensitive to company-cap choice?
docs.text:
Ruled out. Re-ran the full T14 community detection at cap=20 and cap=50:

Cap 2024 nodes 2024 edges 2024 mod 2026 nodes 2026 edges 2026 mod Δ mod 20 67 190 0.557 92 277 0.650 +0.093 50 66 189 0.554 93 271 0.659 +0.105 T14 (cap 50) 66 188 0.560 92 266 0.657 +0.097

Both caps agree on the directional rise of ~+0.10. Node and edge counts are similar. The community count also rises in both (12/13 → 14/15). Modularity rise is robust to cap choice.

docs.title: Alt 3 — T13 section classifier misclassifies requirements as responsibilities?
docs.text:
Ruled out. Audited 30 random 2026 "responsibilities" segments (full script: V1_alt3_t13_responsibilities_audit.py). Manual grading:

  • 28/30 are clean responsibilities content (imperatives describing duties: "Design, develop, and maintain", "Build and evolve", "Architect and build", "Plans, conducts, and coordinates").
  • 2/30 are borderline role-summary phrasings ("We are seeking a Staff Software Engineer to lead", "You are a passionate software engineer").
  • 0/30 are misclassified requirements content.

Precision ≥93%. The T13 "requirements is flat" finding does NOT depend on classifier failure mode; it is robust. Alt 3 is dead.

docs.title: Alt 4 — asaniczka rows disproportionately in employer-template topics?
docs.text:
Ruled out. Asaniczka occupies 55.5% of rows in the four employer-template topics (10, 13, 17, 19) vs 49.8% of all archetype-labeled rows. Enrichment ratio: 1.11×.

This is essentially negligible. Asaniczka is NOT disproportionately in the template topics, so the Gate 2 claim of uniform within-archetype junior-share rise cannot be blamed on an asaniczka template skew. Alt 4 is dead.

docs.title: Alt 5 — arshkon-only entry-share flip driven by denominator drift?
docs.text:
Ruled out. Recomputed both entry_of_all and entry_of_known under the arshkon-only baseline:

Slice n_total pct_unknown entry_of_all entry_of_known arshkon_only_2024 4,691 51.7% 3.73% 7.72% scraped_2026 40,881 53.4% 3.12% 6.70% Δ — +1.7 pp −0.61 pp −1.02 pp

Key finding: under the arshkon-only 2024 baseline, pct_unknown is essentially the same in both periods (51.7% → 53.4%). The "denominator drift 61% → 47%" that T08 flagged applies only to the pooled-2024 baseline — under the arshkon-only baseline, there is no denominator drift and the entry-share flip holds under both of_all and of_known denominators. The direction is robust.

This actually strengthens the Gate 2 claim that the arshkon-only baseline is the cleaner comparator. Alt 5 is dead.

docs.title: 4. Specification-dependence inventory
docs.text:
The Gate 2 memo and Wave 2 reports flag the following as specification-dependent. For each I record the primary and alternative specifications and the current defensibility of the choice.

Finding Primary spec Alternative spec(s) Direction flips? Gate 2 treatment Defensible? Junior share cross-period seniority_final of-known YOE ≤ 2 of-known; arshkon-only vs pooled 2024 YES (arshkon-only sf flips; YOE ≤ 2 always rises) Reported as Alt-A confirmed ✅ Gate 2's "instrument-dominated" framing is defensible; V1 corroborates T08 arshkon-only entry-share flip seniority_final of-known of-all, of-known-excluding-top-specialists NO (all three agree: -1.02 pp / -0.61 pp / -2.05 pp) Lead evidence for Alt A ✅ Robust across specs T12 Fightin' Words term list Full-text Section-filtered (requirements+responsibilities) Partial (84/100 overlap) Reported: "84 of 100 2026-heavy terms appear in both" ✅ T11 tech_count vs tech_density tech_density flat (primary) tech_count rises NO direction flip — different claims Gate 2 writes both; reports tech_density as "flat, SNR 0.7" and tech_count as "rose but length-driven" ✅ Honest T15 junior↔senior convergence Embedding cosine; TF-IDF cosine Both NO (both say diverging) Reported as "no convergence, both just below SNR 2" ✅ Honest T15 convergence SNR (1.90-1.94) Cohen's d on cosine shifts — — "fails SNR 2.0 by a hair" ⚠️ Dangerous: a claim "no convergence" that depends on failing a robustness threshold should not be cited as strong evidence; it should be cited as "sample cannot distinguish convergence from divergence at SNR ≥ 2". The Gate 2 ranking table correctly labels this Moderate. T09 ML/AI junior share 3.7 → 17.2 → 8.5 Pooled 2026 (8.5 is 2026-04) 2026-03 = 17.2 Magnitude varies 2× Gate 2 correctly flags as "intern-cycle volatile" ⚠️ Dangerous: which month is cited in the paper will materially change the headline. Solution: cite both months explicitly or use cell-weighted average. T13 section decomposition 2k/period stratified sample Full corpus Minor — V1 gets 43% vs T13's 52% on responsibilities share T13 reports 52% ⚠️ Report as "40-52% depending on sample"; both still make responsibilities the #1 driver T14 agents_framework magnitude +12.08 pp raw +10-11 pp after FP adjustment (T14), V1 finds ~+11.4 pp NO direction flip, 10% magnitude change Gate 2 correctly footnotes the caveat ✅ T09 "uniform within-archetype rise" All archetypes Only archetypes with n_known ≥ 100 YES — GPU/CUDA (n=64/29) actually FELL Gate 2 writes "rose in every archetype" ⚠️ Overstatement. Correct phrasing: "rose in every archetype with adequate sample size".

Most dangerous specification-dependent findings to paper claims:

  1. T15 convergence SNR 1.90-1.94. Gate 2 cites the "no convergence" finding as Moderate evidence. Because the SNR just barely fails the threshold, this is a negative result — the sample cannot rule out mild divergence OR mild convergence. The paper should frame this as "no evidence for convergence detected; sample cannot distinguish mild divergence from null at 95% confidence" rather than "levels are diverging".

  2. T09 ML/AI junior share volatility (3.7 → 17.2 → 8.5). The 2× month-to-month volatility means any paper cell that cites a single 2026 number will be cherry-picked. Wave 3 T28 must decide how to cite this — weighted pooled, or both months explicitly.

  3. Gate 2's "5.15% → 28.63%, SNR 925" conflation (see §1a). This is technically a citation error not a specification-dependence, but it is the worst kind of error because it falsely implies the broad AI metric passes the highest SNR bar. Fix: report SNR 13.3 next to the 23.5 pp rise, and SNR ~925 next to the separate narrow-pattern 2.81% → 18.78% rise.

docs.title: 5. Verdict on each Gate 2 finding
docs.text: Gate 2 finding V1 verdict AI tool/framework explosion (aggregate + per-tool SNR) VERIFIED (all 5 per-tool SNRs reproduce within 1%; aggregate survives drop-top-10; narrow and broad operationalizations both pass SNR ≥ 2) Tech network modularity rise 0.56 → 0.66 VERIFIED (cap=20 and cap=50 agree; +0.09 to +0.11) Two new tech communities (LLM/RAG, AI-tools triad) VERIFIED (structurally reproduced in both cap settings) Length grew in responsibilities/role_summary, not requirements VERIFIED (direction holds; magnitudes 43% vs T13's 52% for responsibilities, slightly smaller but same rank) Requirements section is flat VERIFIED STRONGER (V1 finds −17 chars vs T13's −2 — slight decrease, same conclusion) Domain dominance of clustering (NMI 0.412) NOT INDEPENDENTLY RE-DERIVED (would need to rerun BERTopic; trusted by V1 because T09 artifact is used directly) ML/AI growth +15.6 pp NOT INDEPENDENTLY RE-DERIVED (archetype label dependency, trusted) Within-archetype uniform junior-share rise PARTIAL — true for 7/8 large archetypes by exact match; false for GPU/CUDA; Gate 2 should restrict claim to "every archetype with n_known ≥ 100" Arshkon-only entry share flip (7.72 → 6.70) VERIFIED (exact match; robust to denominator choice) Credential-stack depth rise (breadth, SNR 10.2-10.5) NOT INDEPENDENTLY RE-DERIVED — did not test Entry-level credential stacking (both ops agree) NOT INDEPENDENTLY RE-DERIVED — did not test Mentor +55% / manage −47% VERIFIED (via sample precision) — mentor is the clean sub-pattern per my 50-row audit; the +55% should be elevated from Moderate to Strong because the sub-pattern is clean Strict management precision 100% OVERTURNED — real semantic precision is ~38% (people-mgmt) or ~50% (incl. task/project mgmt). The T11 "precision audit" was a tautology. Broad management precision 26% VERIFIED (consistent with V1 findings) Scope density +85% WEAKENED — ownership sub-pattern is contaminated (58% precision) and contributes disproportionately; end-to-end and cross-functional are clean. Direction robust, magnitude overstated. T15 seniority divergence SNR 1.90-1.94 NEEDS-MORE-WORK — reframe as "null result at SNR ≥ 2" not "diverging" Title-level senior compression (−12.8 pp) NOT INDEPENDENTLY RE-DERIVED — did not test 2026 template homogenization NOT INDEPENDENTLY RE-DERIVED — did not test Original "junior share declined at aggregate" CONTRADICTED (confirmed by V1 §1b/Alt 5) Alt B "ML/AI eats frontend" mechanism CONTRADICTED (confirmed — frontend +1.4 pp, ML/AI not entry-poor) Gate 1 "arshkon native entry 26% YOE ≥ 5" Gate 2 correction (42.6%) verified earlier — V1 does not re-derive T12 agentic 495 distinct companies VERIFIED AND STRONGER (1,351 on raw corpus; 503 on LLM subset matches T12 within 1.6%; precision 100% on 20 samples)

docs.title: Issue 1 — Gate 2's "5.15% → 28.63%, SNR 925" must be split into two cells before any paper draft
docs.text: The current table cross-wires T14's broad-union prevalence (23.5 pp rise) with T05's narrow-pattern SNR (925) and creates a misleading combined row. The fix is mechanical: cite the T05 narrow-pattern number (2.81% → 18.78%, SNR ~925) separately from the T14 broad-union number (5.01% → 28.57%, SNR 13.3). Both pass threshold. The combined row as written in Gate 2 is not defensible. Highest-priority correction because this is the lead empirical headline of the paper.

docs.title: Issue 2 — Strict management precision is 38-50%, not 100%. Reframe all management claims around mentor only.
docs.text:
T11's strict-management "precision 100%" is a tautological audit (fires match because they contain strict keywords by construction). On a real semantic people-management audit, strict management matches only 19/50 semantic-true people-management instances, driven mostly by corporate boilerplate around hire / hiring and task-management manage. The ONLY clean management sub-pattern in the strict family is mentor, which V1 graded at high precision and rose 3.8% → 5.9%. Wave 3 should:

  1. Drop the "management rate rose" framing entirely.
  2. Elevate "mentor mentions rose +55%" from Moderate to Strong.
  3. Construct a new tight people-management pattern that excludes hire as a standalone keyword and requires the manage verb to co-occur with team|engineers|people|staff|employees|reports|direct reports.
  4. Re-validate the new pattern on a fresh 50-row stratified sample before using it in any claim.

T21 / T28 / V2 should all be told about this. Agent L's T21 task (mentor/ manage swap validation) is now directly dependent on this.

docs.title: Issue 3 — Scope-density +85% is partially instrument-driven; ownership sub-pattern needs to be tightened or dropped
docs.text:
The top-3 scope patterns break down as:

  • ownership (50 samples, 58% precision, precision rises 36% → 80% between periods) — instrument-contaminated
  • end-to-end (50 samples, 96% precision) — clean
  • cross-functional (50 samples, 100% precision) — clean

The cross-period rise in the ownership sub-pattern is partly due to decreasing contamination from 2024-side corporate boilerplate (employee- owned, minority-owned, on-your-own-terms, total cost of ownership) rather than real growth in scope language. Wave 3 T22 should:

  1. Build a tightened ownership pattern that requires an object noun phrase (e.g., own(s|ed|ing)?\s+(?:the|end-to-end|delivery|development| design|implementation|quality|outcome|initiative)) OR drop the keyword entirely.
  2. Recompute scope_density under the clean sub-pattern set (end-to-end
  3. cross-functional + stakeholder + autonom* + initiative + high- impact, excluding ownership).
  4. Report the clean-subset magnitude as the primary. Gate 2's +85% is probably overstated by 15-30%.

docs.title: Verification of Gate 2 clearance
docs.text:
Per Gate 2 memo §Decisions item 7: "Gate 2 clears, conditional on V1 verification of (a) per-tool SNRs in the calibration table, (b) section-anatomy decomposition in T13, (c) within-archetype uniform entry rise in T09, and (d) management pattern precision claims in T11."

Condition V1 verdict (a) per-tool SNRs in calibration table VERIFIED EXACT — all five reproduce within 1% (b) section-anatomy decomposition in T13 VERIFIED — direction and rank order hold on 2k/period independent sample (c) within-archetype uniform entry rise in T09 VERIFIED for large archetypes; fails for small archetypes — Gate 2 should narrow the phrasing (d) management pattern precision claims in T11 STRICT PRECISION IS OVERTURNED — see Issue 2 above

Gate 2 clears on conditions (a)-(c) with minor caveats. Condition (d) DOES NOT CLEAR as written. The T11 management precision number is overstated because the audit method was tautological. The paper should not cite "strict management precision 100%". Wave 3 must rebuild the management pattern before the finding can be promoted.

Overall: Gate 2 clears for Waves 3 dispatch, subject to the three corrections listed above being communicated to Wave 3 agents. The AI-explosion lead, the modularity rise, the length-in-responsibilities finding, the arshkon-only entry-share flip, and the alternative- explanation survivals all reproduce cleanly. The corrections are narrative / citation level, not computational.

docs.title: V2 — Gate 3 Adversarial Verification
docs.text: Agent: V2 (verification, adversarial) Date: 2026-04-15 Frame: SWE LinkedIn default filter (is_english=true AND date_flag='ok'), 22,820 rows 2024 / 40,881 rows 2026. Inputs: data/unified.parquet, exploration/artifacts/shared/* Scripts: exploration/scripts/V2_*.py Tables: exploration/tables/V2/V2_*.csv

docs.title: Summary verdict
docs.text:
Gate 3 clears with qualifications on three headline numbers. The paper's lead finding (T23 RQ3 inversion) and the co-headline (T21 senior orchestration shift) both reproduce independently with very tight tolerances. T16's within-company decomposition reproduces within ~2%. T18's DiD is essentially exact. But two findings do not reproduce cleanly:

  1. T28's "credential-stack gap flips sign in 7 of 10 large archetypes" is strongly pattern-dependent. Under an independently defined 6-category credential stack, only 2 of 10 large archetypes show a sign flip, though all 10 converge in the expected direction. The 7/10 number should not be cited in the paper without specifying the exact pattern set.

  2. T29's "length-flip under style matching" (−411 chars) is very sensitive to which authorship features enter the matcher. Under a simpler 3-feature composite (tell-density, em-dash, bullet), the matched delta is +473 chars (no flip). Under em-dash-only matching it is −264 chars (flips). Under bullet-only it is +704 chars (strengthens). The flip reproduces under em-dash and tell-leaning compositions but NOT under a bullet-weighted composite, and T29 itself flagged bullet density as a heavy instrument/format confound. The length-flip finding is specification-dependent in its direction, and the paper should state the exact feature set used.

Nothing else in Gate 3 is in trouble.

docs.title: Re-derivation pass rate
docs.text: 8 of 10 headline numbers PASS within tolerance. 1 partial (T16 within-%), 1 spec-dependent (T28 7/10 flip count).
